<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Flow Measurement

**Flow measurement** is one of the most critical measurements in industrial process control. It quantifies the movement of fluids (liquids, gases, or steam) through pipes, channels, and open systems. In process industries such as oil & gas, petrochemicals, power generation, water treatment, and pharmaceuticals, accurate flow measurement directly affects product quality, safety, billing, and regulatory compliance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, flow is fundamentally measured in two distinct ways:
- **Rate of Flow (Flow Rate)** ‚Üí *How fast* fluid is moving (instantaneous)
- **Total Flow (Cumulative Flow)** ‚Üí *How much* fluid has moved over time (integrated)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A flowmeter measures the *rate* of flow, whereas a *totalizer* gives the *total* (integrated) flow. Many industrial flowmeters combine both functions (e.g., magnetic flowmeters with built-in totalizers).

---

## 1.2 Volumetric Flow Rate ($Q$)

**Definition:** The *volumetric flow rate* is defined as the volume of fluid passing through a given cross-section per unit time.

$$Q = \frac{dV}{dt}$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m¬≥/s)
- $V$ = volume of fluid (m¬≥)
- $t$ = time (s)

It can also be expressed as the product of the cross-sectional area and the fluid velocity:

$$Q = A \cdot \bar{v}$$

where $A$ is the pipe cross-sectional area (m¬≤) and $\bar{v}$ is the average fluid velocity (m/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Units:** m¬≥/s, L/s, m¬≥/h, gallons/min (GPM), cubic feet per minute (CFM), standard cubic feet per minute (SCFM) ‚Äî for gases.

### üåä Intuitive Analogy

Think of a **garden hose filling a bucket**. If the bucket has a capacity of 10 liters and it fills in 20 seconds, the volumetric flow rate is $10/20 = 0.5$ L/s. The hose *displaces* a volume every second ‚Äî that *displaced volume per second* is the volumetric flow rate.

---

## 1.3 Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}$)

**Definition:** The *mass flow rate* is the mass of fluid passing through a given cross-section per unit time.

$$\dot{m} = \frac{dm}{dt} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot \bar{v}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $\rho$ = density of the fluid (kg/m¬≥)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m¬≥/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why mass flow matters in industry:** In chemical reactions, *mass* is conserved, not volume. A reaction requiring 100 kg of feedstock per hour needs a **mass flowmeter**, not a volumetric one, because density changes with temperature and pressure.

### ü©∏ Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **blood circulation system**. Doctors care about how much *blood mass* reaches organs per minute, not just the volume. If the blood thickens (higher density), the same volume delivers more mass ‚Äî that's why mass flow is the chemically meaningful quantity.

---

## 1.4 Rate of Flow vs. Total Flow

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Typical Unit | Example |
|----------|--------|------------|--------------|---------|
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | Volume per unit time | m¬≥/h | 50 m¬≥/h of water |
| Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | Mass per unit time | kg/h | 1500 kg/h of steam |
| **Total Flow** | $V_{tot}$ or $m_{tot}$ | Cumulative quantity over time | m¬≥ or kg | Daily refinery throughput |

$$\text{Total Volume} = V_{tot} = \int_{0}^{T} Q(t)\, dt \qquad \text{Total Mass} = m_{tot} = \int_{0}^{T} \dot{m}(t)\, dt$$

---

## 1.5 Classification of Flow Meters

Industrial flowmeters are classified based on the physical principle exploited to infer flow. The major categories in the **KTU 2024 syllabus** are:

1. **Differential Pressure Flowmeters** ‚Äî Orifice plate, Venturi, Flow nozzle, Pitot tube
2. **Positive Displacement Flowmeters** ‚Äî Reciprocating piston, Oval gear, Nutating disc
3. **Velocity Flowmeters** ‚Äî Turbine, Vortex shedding, Ultrasonic, Electromagnetic
4. **Mass Flowmeters** ‚Äî Coriolis, Thermal mass
5. **Open Channel Flowmeters** ‚Äî Weirs, Flumes
6. **Variable Area Flowmeters** ‚Äî Rotameter
7. **Miscellaneous** ‚Äî Target, Thermal anemometer

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** A question on "classify flowmeters with one example each" carries high weightage in Module 1. Memorize the principle behind *each* category ‚Äî the examiner tests understanding, not just rote memorization.

---

## 1.6 Fundamental Fluid Mechanics Concepts

Before diving into flowmeter design, four foundational fluid mechanics concepts must be mastered:

### (a) Viscosity ($\mu$)
**Viscosity** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow ‚Äî its "stickiness" or "thickness."

### (b) Reynolds Number ($Re$)
A **dimensionless number** that predicts whether flow will be laminar or turbulent by comparing inertial forces to viscous forces.

### (c) Flow Regime
The *character* of the flow ‚Äî **Laminar**, **Transitional**, or **Turbulent** ‚Äî determined by the Reynolds number.

### (d) Law of Continuity
States that for an incompressible fluid in a steady-state, closed conduit, the volumetric flow rate is constant everywhere.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚Äî Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Viscosity ‚Äî The Molecular Origin

Viscosity arises from **momentum exchange between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities**. Newton's law of viscosity (for Newtonian fluids) is:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (N/m¬≤ or Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa¬∑s or N¬∑s/m¬≤)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s‚Åª¬π), also called *shear rate* $\dot{\gamma}$

### Dynamic vs. Kinematic Viscosity

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

where $\nu$ = **kinematic viscosity** (m¬≤/s) and $\rho$ = density (kg/m¬≥).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian:** Water, air, oil, gasoline are **Newtonian** (linear $\tau$‚Äì$\dot{\gamma}$). Blood, paint, ketchup, slurries are **Non-Newtonian** (non-linear). Most KTU flowmeter equations assume Newtonian behavior.

---

## 2.2 Reynolds Number ‚Äî The Flow Regime Predictor

Osborne Reynolds (1883) defined the dimensionless number that captures the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{v \cdot D}{\nu}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m¬≥)
- $v$ = average velocity (m/s)
- $D$ = pipe internal diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa¬∑s)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m¬≤/s)

### Derivation Insight (from Force Ratio)

$$\text{Inertial force} \sim \rho v^2 D^2 \qquad \text{Viscous force} \sim \mu v D$$

$$Re = \frac{\text{Inertial}}{\text{Viscous}} = \frac{\rho v^2 D^2}{\mu v D} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

---

## 2.3 Flow Regimes ‚Äî The Three States

| Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Characteristics | Velocity Profile |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 2300$ | Smooth, layered, predictable; viscous forces dominate | Parabolic (Poiseuille) |
| **Transitional** | $2300 \leq Re \leq 4000$ | Unstable; oscillates between laminar & turbulent | Distorted parabola |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic eddies, mixing; inertial forces dominate | Flat (logarithmic "plug-like") |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** The values 2300 and 4000 are for flow in **circular pipes**. For non-circular ducts (e.g., annuli, rectangular ducts), the **hydraulic diameter** $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P}$ must replace $D$ in the Reynolds formula.

---

## 2.4 Law of Continuity

For a **steady, incompressible flow** through a varying cross-section:

$$\boxed{A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q = \text{constant}}$$

For compressible flow (gases at high Mach), the **mass continuity** applies:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2 = \text{constant}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Where the pipe narrows (smaller $A$), velocity must *increase* to maintain constant volumetric flow ‚Äî this is why a thumb over a hose makes water spray faster and farther.

---

## 2.5 üìã KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | $Q = A \bar{v}$ | Volumetric flow rate | m¬≥/s | Incompressible, steady |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho A \bar{v}$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s | Universal |
| 3 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Pa | Newtonian fluids only |
| 4 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | m¬≤/s | Pure fluid property |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | Pipe flow |
| 6 | $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P}$ | Hydraulic diameter | m | Non-circular ducts |
| 7 | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Continuity (incompressible) | m¬≥/s | Steady flow |
| 8 | $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$ | Continuity (compressible) | kg/s | Mass conservation |
| 9 | $V_{tot} = \displaystyle\int_0^T Q(t)\, dt$ | Total volume | m¬≥ | Totalizer operation |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{tot} = \displaystyle\int_0^T \dot{m}(t)\, dt$ | Total mass | kg | Totalizer operation |
| 11 | Laminar: $Re < 2300$ | Flow regime criterion | ‚Äî | Circular pipe |
| 12 | Turbulent: $Re > 4000$ | Flow regime criterion | ‚Äî | Circular pipe |
| 13 | Transitional: $2300 \leq Re \leq 4000$ | Flow regime criterion | ‚Äî | Unstable |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industry Application |
|---------|---------------------|
| Volumetric flow ($Q$) | Custody transfer of liquids (water, oil) where billing is volumetric |
| Mass flow ($\dot{m}$) | Chemical reactor feed, combustion air-fuel ratio control, steam mass for energy balance |
| Reynolds number | Selecting flowmeter type (e.g., Coriolis works at any $Re$, orifice needs $Re > 10^4$ for accuracy) |
| Viscosity | Crude oil pipeline design, lubrication systems, paint manufacturing |
| Law of continuity | Venturi/nozzle design (acceleration ‚Üí pressure drop ‚Üí flow inference) |

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚Äî Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (Law of Continuity)

**Statement:** *For an incompressible fluid flowing steadily through a pipe with varying cross-section, the volumetric flow rate at every cross-section is constant.*

### Setup

Consider a pipe segment with two cross-sections:
- Section 1: Area $A_1$, velocity $v_1$
- Section 2: Area $A_2$, velocity $v_2$

In a small time interval $\Delta t$:
- Fluid entering section 1 travels a distance $v_1 \Delta t$
- Volume entering = $A_1 \cdot v_1 \Delta t$
- Fluid leaving section 2 travels a distance $v_2 \Delta t$
- Volume leaving = $A_2 \cdot v_2 \Delta t$

### Mass Conservation (Incompressible)

Since the fluid is **incompressible**, density $\rho$ is constant, and mass conservation requires *volume in = volume out*:

$$A_1 \cdot v_1 \cdot \Delta t = A_2 \cdot v_2 \cdot \Delta t$$

Cancel $\Delta t$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q}$$

> **Interpretation:** Where area decreases, velocity increases proportionally. For compressible flow, multiply both sides by local density to get mass continuity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Reynolds Number from Dimensional Analysis

The Reynolds number is derived by forming a dimensionless group from the governing variables of the flow.

### Governing Variables

| Variable | Symbol | Dimensions |
|----------|--------|------------|
| Pipe diameter | $D$ | $L$ |
| Average velocity | $v$ | $L T^{-1}$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |

We seek a dimensionless group $\pi$ of the form:

$$\pi = D^a \, v^b \, \rho^c \, \mu^d$$

### Dimensional Equation

$$[M^0 L^0 T^0] = [L]^a \, [L T^{-1}]^b \, [M L^{-3}]^c \, [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]^d$$

### Equating Powers

**For $M$:**
$$0 = c + d \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = -c$$

**For $T$:**
$$0 = -b - d \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = -d = c$$

**For $L$:**
$$0 = a + b - 3c - d = a + c - 3c - (-c) = a - c$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad a = c$$

### Choosing $c = 1$

Then $a = 1$, $b = 1$, $d = -1$. Substituting:

$$\pi = D^1 \, v^1 \, \rho^1 \, \mu^{-1} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = Re$$

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}}$$

> **Physical Meaning:** It is the *only* independent dimensionless group formed from $D$, $v$, $\rho$, $\mu$ (Buckingham $\pi$-theorem with 4 variables and 3 base dimensions ‚Üí 1 $\pi$-group). This is why Reynolds number is so fundamental.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Parabolic Velocity Profile (Laminar Flow)

For fully developed laminar flow in a horizontal circular pipe (Hagen-Poiseuille flow), the Navier-Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}$$

where $\dfrac{dp}{dz}$ is the constant pressure gradient (negative, since pressure drops in the flow direction).

### Integration Steps

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $r$:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{r}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate once with respect to $r$:

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r^2}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dz} + C_1$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $r$:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dz} + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

**Step 4:** Apply **boundary condition 1** ‚Äî axisymmetry at the centerline ($r = 0$), velocity must be finite. This forces $C_1 = 0$.

**Step 5:** Integrate again:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz} r^2 + C_2$$

**Step 6:** Apply **boundary condition 2** ‚Äî no-slip at the wall ($r = R$, $v_z = 0$):

$$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz} R^2 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{R^2}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back:

$$\boxed{v_z(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)}$$

> **This is the parabolic (Poiseuille) profile.** Maximum velocity occurs at the centerline ($r = 0$):
> $$v_{max} = -\frac{R^2}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}$$
> Average velocity is half of maximum:
> $$\bar{v} = \frac{v_{max}}{2} = -\frac{R^2}{8\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem Solved Step-by-Step

**Problem:** Water at 20 ¬∞C flows through a horizontal smooth pipe of diameter $D = 50$ mm at an average velocity of $\bar{v} = 2$ m/s. Determine:
1. The volumetric flow rate
2. The mass flow rate
3. The Reynolds number
4. The flow regime

**Given Data (water at 20 ¬∞C):**
- $\rho = 998$ kg/m¬≥
- $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa¬∑s
- $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m¬≤/s
- $D = 0.050$ m
- $\bar{v} = 2$ m/s

### Solution

**Part (1): Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = A \cdot \bar{v} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 = 3.927 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting:
$$Q = 3.927 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 14.14 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Part (2): Mass Flow Rate**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 998 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-3} = 3.919 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m} = 3.919 \times 3600 = 14{,}108 \text{ kg/h} \approx 14.11 \text{ ton/h}$$

**Part (3): Reynolds Number**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 2 \times 0.050}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{99.8}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 99{,}601 \approx 9.96 \times 10^{4}$$

**Part (4): Flow Regime**

Since $Re = 9.96 \times 10^{4} \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At such high Reynolds number, the velocity profile is "flat" (plug-like) with a thin viscous sublayer near the wall. This is the typical regime in industrial pipelines.

# SECTION 5 ‚Äî KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## üìù PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define volumetric flow rate and mass flow rate. Derive the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Volumetric Flow Rate ($Q$):** It is defined as the volume of fluid flowing past a given cross-section per unit time.
$$Q = \frac{dV}{dt} = A \cdot \bar{v} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}$$

**Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}$):** It is defined as the mass of fluid flowing past a given cross-section per unit time.
$$\dot{m} = \frac{dm}{dt} \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

**Relationship:** Since mass = density √ó volume ($m = \rho \cdot V$), differentiating:
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot \bar{v}$$

For a fluid of constant density (incompressible), $\rho$ is constant and the proportionality is direct. For compressible fluids (gases), local density must be used at the measurement point.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Reynolds number? State its significance in flow measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Reynolds number ($Re$) is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a flowing fluid.

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

**Significance in flow measurement:**

1. **Flow regime prediction:** Determines whether flow is laminar ($Re < 2300$), transitional ($2300 < Re < 4000$), or turbulent ($Re > 4000$) in circular pipes.
2. **Flowmeter selection:** Different flowmeters are valid for different $Re$ ranges (e.g., orifice plates need $Re > 10^4$ for predictable discharge coefficient).
3. **Calibration transfer:** Laboratory calibrations at one $Re$ can be applied to field installations at the same $Re$ (dynamic similarity).
4. **Pressure drop estimation:** Friction factor $f$ is a function of $Re$ and pipe roughness ‚Äî needed for DP-type flowmeter design.

---

## üìù PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚Äî Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A ‚Äî Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the classification of flow meters with at least one example for each category. Discuss the working principle of any two flow meters. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Define Reynolds number and explain the three flow regimes with neat sketches. A fluid of density 900 kg/m¬≥ and viscosity 0.5 Pa¬∑s flows through a 10 cm diameter pipe at 2 m/s. Determine the Reynolds number and identify the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

---

### ‚úÖ Model Answer ‚Äî Part (a) [7 marks]

**Classification of Flow Meters (with examples and working principles):**

**(i) Differential Pressure (DP) Flowmeters:**
- *Example:* Orifice plate
- *Principle:* Flow through a constriction creates a pressure drop proportional to the square of the flow rate. From Bernoulli's and continuity equations: $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$, where $\beta = D_2/D_1$.
- *Other examples:* Venturi tube, flow nozzle, Pitot tube.

**(ii) Positive Displacement Flowmeters:**
- *Example:* Oval gear meter
- *Principle:* Fluid mechanically displaces rotating gears; each rotation corresponds to a fixed volume. Pulses counted ‚Üí total volume.
- *Other examples:* Reciprocating piston, nutating disc.

**(iii) Velocity Flowmeters:**
- *Example:* Turbine flowmeter
- *Principle:* Fluid impinges on a multi-bladed rotor; rotor angular velocity is proportional to fluid velocity. Magnetic pickup senses blade passing frequency.
- *Other examples:* Vortex shedding (K√°rm√°n vortices), Ultrasonic (Doppler/transit-time), Electromagnetic (Faraday's law).

**(iv) Mass Flowmeters:**
- *Example:* Coriolis mass flowmeter
- *Principle:* Fluid flowing through a U-shaped vibrating tube induces Coriolis forces proportional to mass flow; tube twist is sensed.
- *Other example:* Thermal mass flowmeter.

**(v) Open Channel Flowmeters:**
- *Example:* V-notch weir
- *Principle:* Flow is computed from upstream head $h$ measured above the notch: $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g}\, h^{5/2}$.

**(vi) Variable Area Flowmeters:**
- *Example:* Rotameter
- *Principle:* A float rises in a tapered tube to a height where buoyancy + weight balance drag force; height is proportional to flow.

**Working Principle ‚Äî Venturi Tube (one of the two):**
A Venturi tube has a converging section (to throat) and a diverging section. Using Bernoulli's equation and continuity at inlet (1) and throat (2):

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

Combined with $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$:

$$Q = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1-P_2)}{\rho}} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$

**Working Principle ‚Äî Magnetic Flowmeter (the second):**
Based on **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**. When a conductive fluid flows through a magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to flow direction, an EMF is induced across electrodes perpendicular to both:

$$\varepsilon = B \cdot v \cdot D$$

where $D$ = pipe diameter (electrode spacing). Since $v = Q/A = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$, the EMF is directly proportional to volumetric flow rate and **independent of fluid density, viscosity, and turbulence** ‚Äî making it ideal for slurries and corrosive chemicals.

---

### ‚úÖ Model Answer ‚Äî Part (b) [7 marks]

**Definition:** Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces in a fluid:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

**Three Flow Regimes:**

| Regime | Range | Sketches (described) |
|--------|-------|---------------------|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 2300$ | Streamlines parallel; parabolic velocity profile; viscous forces dominate; pressure drop ‚àù $v$ |
| **Transitional** | $2300 \leq Re \leq 4000$ | Unstable; intermittent bursts of turbulence; designers avoid operating here |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic eddies; flat velocity profile; inertial forces dominate; pressure drop ‚àù $v^2$ |

**Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- $\rho = 900$ kg/m¬≥
- $\mu = 0.5$ Pa¬∑s
- $D = 10$ cm $= 0.10$ m
- $\bar{v} = 2$ m/s

**Solution:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 2 \times 0.10}{0.5} = \frac{180}{0.5} = 360$$

**Flow Regime Identification:** Since $Re = 360 < 2300$, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state units explicitly and check the regime thresholds (2300, 4000) before concluding. A common mistake: students write "turbulent" when $Re$ is just above 2300 but the result is still transitional.

---

### **Question B ‚Äî Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Define viscosity. Differentiate between dynamic and kinematic viscosity. Explain Newton's law of viscosity with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** State and derive the Law of Continuity. A pipe of diameter 20 cm carries water at a velocity of 3 m/s. The pipe narrows to 10 cm diameter. Find the velocity in the narrow section and the volumetric flow rate. **(7 marks)**

---

### ‚úÖ Model Answer ‚Äî Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition of Viscosity:**
Viscosity is the property of a fluid that resists the relative motion of its layers. It quantifies internal friction due to molecular momentum transfer.

**Dynamic vs. Kinematic Viscosity:**

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Units |
|----------|--------|------------|-------|
| Dynamic (Absolute) viscosity | $\mu$ | Resistance to shearing | Pa¬∑s or N¬∑s/m¬≤ |
| Kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ | Ratio $\mu/\rho$ ‚Äî resistance to flow under gravity | m¬≤/s |

**Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$

The shear stress $\tau$ between two adjacent fluid layers is proportional to the velocity gradient (shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$) perpendicular to the flow.

**Diagram description (Newton's Law setup):**
A fluid is placed between two parallel plates. The bottom plate is stationary; the top plate moves with velocity $U$. The fluid layers between develop a linear velocity profile $u(y) = Uy/h$. Shear stress at any layer:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

A force $F = \tau \cdot A$ must be applied to maintain motion ‚Äî this force overcomes the viscous resistance.

**Newtonian Fluid Definition:** A fluid that obeys Newton's law (linear $\tau$-$\dot{\gamma}$ relationship). Examples: water, air, gasoline. Non-Newtonian fluids (blood, paint, polymer melts) have non-linear $\tau$-$\dot{\gamma}$ curves.

---

### ‚úÖ Model Answer ‚Äî Part (b) [7 marks]

**Statement of Law of Continuity:**
*For an incompressible fluid flowing steadily through a pipe with varying cross-sectional area, the volumetric flow rate is constant at every cross-section:*
$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q = \text{constant}$$

**Derivation:**

Consider two cross-sections 1 and 2 of a pipe carrying an incompressible fluid ($\rho = $ constant). In time $\Delta t$:
- Volume entering at section 1: $V_1 = A_1 v_1 \Delta t$
- Volume leaving at section 2: $V_2 = A_2 v_2 \Delta t$

For steady flow, by conservation of mass (and incompressibility, conservation of volume):

$$A_1 v_1 \Delta t = A_2 v_2 \Delta t$$

Canceling $\Delta t$:

$$\boxed{A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2}$$

**Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 20$ cm $= 0.20$ m ‚Üí $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.20)^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-2}$ m¬≤
- $v_1 = 3$ m/s
- $D_2 = 10$ cm $= 0.10$ m ‚Üí $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m¬≤

**Step 1 ‚Äî Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = A_1 v_1 = 3.142 \times 10^{-2} \times 3 = 9.425 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 0.09425 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 339.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Velocity at narrow section (using continuity):**

$$v_2 = \frac{A_1}{A_2} v_1 = \frac{D_1^2}{D_2^2} v_1 = \left(\frac{0.20}{0.10}\right)^2 \times 3 = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ m/s}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Halving the diameter quadruples the velocity ‚Äî this is the physical basis for the **Venturi effect**, used in carburetors, aircraft wings, and DP flowmeters.

---

## ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 3‚Äì5 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up volumetric and mass flow rate formulas** ‚Äî especially in compressible gas problems. Always multiply by *local* $\rho$, not standard-state $\rho$.
> 2. **Forgetting the hydraulic diameter substitution** in non-circular ducts ‚Äî examiners specifically test this in 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Wrong Reynolds number threshold** ‚Äî using $Re = 2000$ or $Re = 3000$ as the laminar-turbulent boundary will be marked wrong. KTU strictly expects **2300** and **4000**.
> 4. **Not specifying units** ‚Äî every numerical answer must include SI units (m¬≥/s, kg/s). Bare numbers are penalized.
> 5. **Confusing the continuity equation forms** ‚Äî for compressible flow, the *mass* form $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$ applies, not the volume form.
> 6. **Newtonian assumption** ‚Äî when applying $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$, students often forget to mention that the fluid is Newtonian (a 1-mark loss).

---

## üéØ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ‚úÖ **Volumetric flow rate:** $Q = A\bar{v}$ (m¬≥/s); **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ (kg/s).
- ‚úÖ **Total flow** = time-integral of flow rate; **flow rate** = derivative of total flow.
- ‚úÖ **Viscosity** is internal fluid friction; **Newtonian fluids** obey $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$; **kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu/\rho$.
- ‚úÖ **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ is dimensionless ‚Äî predicts flow regime.
- ‚úÖ **Laminar:** $Re < 2300$ (parabolic profile, viscous-dominated); **Transitional:** $2300 \le Re \le 4000$; **Turbulent:** $Re > 4000$ (flat profile, inertia-dominated).
- ‚úÖ **Law of Continuity:** For incompressible steady flow, $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$; for compressible, $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$.
- ‚úÖ **Classification of flowmeters:** DP type (orifice, Venturi, nozzle, Pitot), Positive Displacement (oval gear, piston), Velocity (turbine, vortex, ultrasonic, magnetic), Mass (Coriolis, thermal), Open channel (weir, flume), Variable area (rotameter).
- ‚úÖ **Hydraulic diameter** $D_h = 4A/P$ is used for non-circular ducts.
- ‚úÖ **Parabolic velocity profile** in laminar pipe flow: $v_z(r) = -\dfrac{1}{4\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dz}(R^2 - r^2)$; average velocity = ¬Ω √ó centerline velocity.
- ‚úÖ **Pressure drop‚Äìvelocity scaling:** $\Delta P \propto v$ (laminar); $\Delta P \propto v^2$ (turbulent).
- ‚úÖ **High $Re$ ‚Üí turbulent** means mixing is good ‚Äî desirable for heat exchangers, mixers, reactors.
- ‚úÖ **Low $Re$ ‚Üí laminar** means viscous layers matter ‚Äî desirable for lubrication, microfluidics.
- ‚úÖ In **SI units**, always express flow in m¬≥/s, kg/s, or m¬≥/h ‚Äî never in GPM or CFM without conversion in board exams.
- ‚úÖ **Coriolis meter** is the gold standard for mass flow ‚Äî works on any flow regime; **Magnetic meter** is ideal for conductive slurries; **Orifice plate** is cheapest but introduces permanent pressure loss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bernoulli's Equation. Linearization and Compensation of Flow Meters - Linear and Nonlinear Flow Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bernoulli's Equation. Linearization and Compensation of Flow Meters - Linear and Nonlinear Flow Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bernoulli's Equation ‚Äî The Engineering Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Theorem (KTU 2024 Definition):** It is the principle of conservation of energy applied to a steady, incompressible, non-viscous, irrotational flow of fluid along a streamline. It states that the sum of the pressure head, the velocity head, and the elevation head at any point in such a flow remains constant.

Mathematically, for two points **1** and **2** on the same streamline:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{\,2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{\,2}}{2g} + z_2$$

Where:
- $P$ = Static Pressure of the fluid (**Pa** or **N/m¬≤**)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (**kg/m¬≥**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s¬≤**)
- $v$ = Velocity of the fluid at that section (**m/s**)
- $z$ = Elevation of the section above an arbitrary datum (**m**)
- $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ = **Pressure head** (energy per unit weight due to pressure)
- $\dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ = **Velocity head** (kinetic energy per unit weight)
- $z$ = **Elevation head** (potential energy per unit weight)

Multiplying the entire equation by $\rho g$ yields the energy form:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^{\,2} + \rho g z_1 + P_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^{\,2} + \rho g z_2 + P_2$$

Here, each term has units of **Pascal (Pa)** and represents mechanical energy per unit volume.

## 1.2 Assumptions Underlying Bernoulli's Equation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask this as a 3-mark question. Memorize it thoroughly.

1. The flow is **steady** (velocity at a point does not change with time).
2. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$).
3. The fluid is **non-viscous** (frictionless; no energy losses).
4. The flow is along a **single streamline** (or irrotational for the extended form).
5. No energy is added to or removed from the fluid between the two sections (no pump, no turbine in between).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî "The Hill, the Pipe, and the Speeding Car"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine water flowing inside a horizontal pipe that has a narrow constriction (a Venturi throat). The water speeds up at the throat. Where does the extra kinetic energy come from? It comes from the **pressure energy** ‚Äî the pressure must drop there. This trade-off between *pressure* and *velocity* is the very essence of Bernoulli's principle.

If we climb uphill along a streamline, the elevation increases and pressure falls (think of a hilltop having lower atmospheric pressure). The equation simply says: *as one form of energy rises, the others must fall to keep the total constant*. This is the engineering mantra that underpins **every differential-pressure flow meter** on the planet.

## 1.4 Flow Meters ‚Äî Linear vs Nonlinear Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification by Output Characteristic (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> - **Linear Flow Meters:** Output (voltage, current, frequency) is **directly proportional** to flow rate $Q$. Equation of the form $y = mQ$.
> - **Nonlinear Flow Meters:** Output is a **nonlinear (often square-root)** function of flow rate. Equation of the form $y = k\sqrt{Q}$ or $y = kQ^{n}$ where $n \neq 1$.

| Type | Examples | Transfer Function | Nature |
|------|----------|--------------------|--------|
| Differential Pressure (DP) | Orifice, Venturi, Flow Nozzle, Pitot | $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$ | **Nonlinear** |
| Variable Area | Rotameter | $h \propto Q$ (approx.) | Approximately Linear |
| Turbine | Turbine flow meter | $f \propto Q$ | **Linear** (over range) |
| Electromagnetic | Magmeter | $E \propto v \propto Q$ | **Linear** |
| Ultrasonic | Transit-time / Doppler | $\Delta t \propto 1/v \propto 1/Q$ | **Nonlinear** |
| Vortex | Vortex shedding | $f \propto Q$ | **Linear** |
| Coriolis | Coriolis mass flow meter | $\Delta t \propto Q_{m}$ | **Linear** |

## 1.5 Why Linearization and Compensation Are Needed

> [!WARNING]
> Most industrial processes require a controller (PLC/DCS) input that is **linear in flow**. A nonlinear sensor forces extra mathematical gymnastics on the controller, reduces accuracy, and complicates totalization (flow total = ‚à´Q dt). Hence we **linearize** the sensor output.

Similarly, **compensation** corrects for environmental changes (temperature, pressure, viscosity) that alter the sensor's calibration. Without compensation, a flow meter calibrated at 25 ¬∞C will read wrongly at 200 ¬∞C because density has changed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept of Bernoulli's Equation ‚Äî Step by Step

1. **Select a control volume** bounded by two cross-sections (1) and (2) of a pipe carrying fluid.
2. **Apply Newton's second law** to a fluid element moving along the streamline. The net pressure force does work on the fluid, increasing its kinetic energy.
3. **Integrate** the resulting Euler's equation of motion along the streamline from section (1) to section (2).
4. **The integral reduces** to the Bernoulli equation provided the assumptions listed in ¬ß1.2 hold.
5. **Real fluids** introduce a head-loss term $h_L$ (due to viscous friction, fittings, sudden expansions) on the right-hand side:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{\,2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{\,2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

This extended form is what is used in **industrial flow metering**, since real flow meters always incur some loss.

## 2.2 Application of Bernoulli's Equation to the Orifice Plate

> [!NOTE]
> The orifice plate is the most widely used primary flow element. KTU examiners test this derivation almost every cycle.

Consider an orifice plate installed in a horizontal pipe of diameter $D$. Fluid approaches with velocity $v_1$ at section (1) and contracts to a **vena contracta** of area $A_2$ with velocity $v_2$ at section (2). Applying Bernoulli between (1) and (2) with $z_1 = z_2$:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^{\,2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{\,2}}{2}$$

Rearranging for the differential pressure $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\rho}{2}\,(v_2^{\,2} - v_1^{\,2})$$

Using the **continuity equation** $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ (mass conservation for incompressible flow):

$$v_1 \;=\; \frac{A_2}{A_1}\,v_2 \;=\; \beta^{2}\,v_2$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{d}{D}$ is the **diameter ratio** ($d$ = orifice diameter). Substituting back:

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\rho\,v_2^{\,2}}{2}\,(1 - \beta^{4})$$

Solving for the theoretical velocity at the vena contracta:

$$v_2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}}\,\sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P}{\rho}}$$

The actual (averaged) velocity is lower due to **vena contraction**, so we introduce a **discharge coefficient** $C_d$ (typically 0.6 ‚Äì 0.65 for standard orifice). The volumetric flow rate is:

$$\boxed{\;Q \;=\; C_d\,A_2\,\sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho\,(1-\beta^{4})}}\;}$$

Or, using the pipe cross-section:

$$Q \;=\; C_d\,A_o\,Y\,\sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho\,(1-\beta^{4})}}$$

where $Y$ is the **expansion factor** (‚âà 1 for liquids, < 1 for gases) and $A_o = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize these formulas. They are the **highest-weightage** equations for any Module 1 question on flow meters.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli's Equation | m (of fluid) |
| 2 | $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{\rho}{2}(v_2^{\,2} - v_1^{\,2})$ | Pressure drop across constriction | Pa |
| 3 | $Q = C_d\,A_2\sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^{4})}}$ | Orifice / nozzle flow rate | m¬≥/s |
| 4 | $\beta = \dfrac{d}{D}$ | Diameter ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$ | Square-law of DP meters | Pa |
| 6 | $v_{\text{true}} = \sqrt{2g\,\Delta h}$ | Pitot-static velocity (liquid) | m/s |
| 7 | $\rho_{T,P} = \dfrac{\rho_{0}\,P\,T_{0}}{P_{0}\,T}$ | Ideal-gas density compensation | kg/m¬≥ |
| 8 | $V_{o} = -\sqrt{\dfrac{R_1}{K R_2 R_3 R_4}}\,V_{i}$ | Square-root extractor output | V |
| 9 | $Q_{m} = \rho\,Q$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 10 | Reynolds No. $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime check | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Linear vs Nonlinear Flow Meters ‚Äî Detailed Engineering Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output of a **nonlinear flow meter** is generally a power-law function of flow:

$$y \;=\; k\,Q^{\,n}$$

where $y$ is the meter output (e.g., $\Delta P$ for orifice) and $n$ is the **flow exponent** ($n = 2$ for orifice/Venturi, $n = 1$ for turbine, $n \approx 0.5$ for rotameter with float displacement, etc.).

- **Linear meter ($n = 1$):** Calibration curve $y$ vs $Q$ is a straight line passing through the origin. No signal conditioning required.
- **Nonlinear meter ($n \neq 1$):** The curve must be *linearized* before feeding the controller. For DP meters with $n = 2$, we must extract the **square root** of $\Delta P$ to recover $Q$ linearly.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Bernoulli-Based Flow Meters

- **Orifice plates** dominate the oil & gas industry because they are cheap, rugged, and standards-based (ISO 5167).
- **Venturi meters** are preferred where low permanent pressure loss matters (e.g., large water mains, HVAC).
- **Pitot-static tubes** are used in aircraft airspeed indicators.
- **Flow nozzles** are used in high-velocity steam service.

Without Bernoulli's equation, none of these could be designed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation

### Starting Point: Euler's Equation of Motion

Consider a fluid element of length $ds$, cross-sectional area $dA$, moving along a streamline. The net pressure force on it equals its mass $\times$ acceleration:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}\,ds\,dA \;=\; (\rho\,ds\,dA)\,\frac{dv}{dt}$$

Dividing both sides by $\rho\,ds\,dA$:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} \;=\; \frac{dv}{dt}$$

But $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{dv}{ds}\,\dfrac{ds}{dt} = v\,\dfrac{dv}{ds}$. Substituting:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} \;=\; v\,\frac{dv}{ds} + g\,\frac{dz}{ds}$$

(where the gravity component $g\,dz/ds$ comes from the weight component along the streamline.)

Multiplying both sides by $ds$:

$$-\frac{1}{\rho}\,dP \;=\; v\,dv + g\,dz$$

Multiplying through by $\dfrac{1}{g}$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{dP}{\rho g} + \frac{d(v^{2})}{2g} + dz \;=\; 0$$

### Integration Along the Streamline

For an **incompressible** fluid ($\rho = \text{const}$), integrate from point (1) to point (2):

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2}\frac{dP}{\rho g} + \int_{v_1}^{v_2}\frac{d(v^{2})}{2g} + \int_{z_1}^{z_2}dz \;=\; 0$$

Carrying out the integrations:

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{\,2} - v_1^{\,2}}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1) \;=\; 0$$

Rearranging gives the **final form**:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{\,2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{\,2}}{2g} + z_2\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each term has units of length (meters of fluid) and is referred to as a **head**. The equation is therefore often called **Bernoulli's Head Equation**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Orifice Flow Equation (Detailed)

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli across the orifice (horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_2$):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^{\,2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{\,2}}{2}$$

**Step 2: Solve for the pressure difference:**

$$P_1 - P_2 \;=\; \frac{\rho}{2}\,(v_2^{\,2} - v_1^{\,2})$$

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\rho}{2}\,(v_2^{\,2} - v_1^{\,2}) \qquad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3: Apply continuity equation between pipe and vena contracta:**

$$A_1\,v_1 \;=\; A_2\,v_2$$

$$v_1 \;=\; \frac{A_2}{A_1}\,v_2 \;=\; \beta^{2}\,v_2 \qquad \text{...(ii)}$$

where $\beta = d/D$ and $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ (orifice area = vena contracta ideal area).

**Step 4: Substitute (ii) into (i):**

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\rho}{2}\,(v_2^{\,2} - \beta^{4}\,v_2^{\,2}) \;=\; \frac{\rho\,v_2^{\,2}}{2}\,(1 - \beta^{4})$$

**Step 5: Solve for the theoretical $v_2$:**

$$v_2^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho\,(1 - \beta^{4})}$$

$$v_2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}}\,\sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 6: Convert to volumetric flow rate $Q = A_2 v_2$:**

$$Q_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; A_2\,\sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho\,(1 - \beta^{4})}}$$

**Step 7: Introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for real-fluid effects (vena contraction, viscosity):**

$$\boxed{\;Q \;=\; C_d\,A_2\,\sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho\,(1 - \beta^{4})}}\;}$$

**Step 8: Express the square-law nature explicitly:**

Since all terms except $\Delta P$ are constant for a given installation, we can write:

$$Q \;=\; K\,\sqrt{\Delta P}$$

$$\Rightarrow \Delta P \;=\; \frac{Q^{2}}{K^{2}} \;\propto\; Q^{2}$$

This proves the **square-law (nonlinear) nature** of the orifice meter.

## 3.3 Derivation of Square-Root Extractor Output (Op-Amp Circuit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the classic analog linearization circuit that KTU expects you to derive.

A square-root extractor can be built using a **log-antilog amplifier pair**:
1. Take logarithm of input: $\log(V_i)$
2. Multiply by 2: $2\log(V_i) = \log(V_i^{2})$
3. Take antilog: $V_i^{2}$
4. Use a square-root op-amp stage with a multiplier in the feedback loop.

The classical op-amp square-root extractor uses a **multiplier (IC AD534 / AD633)** in the feedback path. The governing equation of the op-amp with multiplier in feedback is:

$$V_o \;=\; -\frac{V_i}{\sqrt{K\,V_x}}$$

where $K$ is the multiplier constant (typically $\dfrac{1}{10}\,\text{V}^{-1}$ for AD534) and $V_x$ is set to a precise reference $V_{\text{ref}}$.

Choosing $V_x = V_{\text{ref}}$:

$$\boxed{\;V_o \;=\; -\sqrt{\frac{R_1}{K\,R_2\,R_3\,R_4}}}\,\sqrt{V_i}\;$$

If $V_i$ is the differential-pressure transducer output (proportional to $\Delta P$) and the constant is chosen such that the proportionality factor equals the meter's $K$ factor from ¬ß3.2, then:

$$V_o \;\propto\; \sqrt{\Delta P} \;\propto\; Q$$

The output voltage is now **linear in flow rate $Q$**.

## 3.4 Compensation ‚Äî Temperature & Pressure Correction

For a gas, the density varies with operating temperature and pressure:

$$\rho_{T,P} \;=\; \rho_{0}\,\frac{P}{P_{0}}\,\frac{T_{0}}{T}$$

where subscript 0 denotes *reference (calibration) conditions*. Since $Q_{m} = \rho Q$, the mass flow rate computed from a DP meter must be corrected:

$$Q_{m,\text{true}} \;=\; Q_{m,\text{cal}}\,\sqrt{\frac{\rho_{T,P}}{\rho_0}} \;=\; Q_{m,\text{cal}}\,\sqrt{\frac{P\,T_0}{P_0\,T}}$$

In modern DCS systems, $T$ and $P$ are measured by separate transmitters and the square-root extractor stage is followed by a **multiplier block** performing this compensation digitally.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Orifice | Venturi | Turbine | Magnetic | Coriolis |
|-----------|---------|---------|---------|----------|----------|
| Transfer function | $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$ | $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$ | $f \propto Q$ | $E \propto Q$ | $\Delta t \propto Q_{m}$ |
| Linearity | Nonlinear | Nonlinear | Linear | Linear | Linear |
| Linearization needed? | **Yes** (‚àö) | **Yes** (‚àö) | No | No | No |
| Pressure loss | Moderate | **Low** | Moderate | None | Moderate |
| Suitable for slurries | Limited | Limited | Poor | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Accuracy (¬±%FS) | 2 ‚Äì 4 | 1 ‚Äì 2 | 0.5 ‚Äì 1 | 0.5 ‚Äì 1 | 0.1 ‚Äì 0.5 |
| Cost | **Low** | Moderate | Moderate | High | **Very High** |
