# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]`

**State Bernoulli's theorem and list any four assumptions made in its derivation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Theorem:** It is the statement of conservation of energy for a steady, incompressible, non-viscous flow along a streamline, expressed as:
> $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$

**Assumptions (any four for ¬Ω mark each = 2 marks):**

1. The flow is **steady** (time-invariant at any point).
2. The fluid is **incompressible** ($\rho = \text{constant}$).
3. The fluid is **non-viscous** (no internal friction or energy loss).
4. The flow is along a **single streamline** (or irrotational for the extended form).
5. **No energy is added or extracted** between the two sections (no pump, no turbine).
6. The fluid is **ideal** ‚Äî no turbulence-induced mixing losses.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]`

**Differentiate between linear and nonlinear flow meters with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Linear Flow Meter | Nonlinear Flow Meter |
|-------------------|----------------------|
| Output is directly proportional to flow rate. Equation: $y = mQ$. | Output is a nonlinear (e.g., square-root) function of flow. Equation: $y = kQ^{n}$, $n \neq 1$. |
| No signal conditioning required before the controller. | Requires **linearization** (e.g., square-root extraction) before the controller. |
| Example: **Turbine flow meter** ($f \propto Q$). | Example: **Orifice plate** ($\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$). |

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation from Euler's equation of motion, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter **200 mm** carries water with a mean velocity of **3 m/s**. An orifice plate with diameter **100 mm** is fitted. The differential pressure across the orifice is measured as **50 kPa**. Assuming $C_d = 0.62$, calculate **(i)** the volumetric flow rate, and **(ii)** the mass flow rate. Take $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

1. Start with Euler's equation along a streamline (1 Mark):

$$-\frac{1}{\rho}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} \;=\; v\,\frac{dv}{ds} + g\,\frac{dz}{ds}$$

2. Multiply by $ds$ and rearrange (1 Mark):

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} + v\,dv + g\,dz \;=\; 0$$

3. For incompressible flow ($\rho = \text{const}$), integrate from point 1 to point 2 (1 Mark):

$$\int_{1}^{2}\frac{dP}{\rho g} + \int_{1}^{2}\frac{d(v^{2})}{2g} + \int_{1}^{2}dz \;=\; 0$$

4. Carry out the integration (1 Mark):

$$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{\,2} - v_1^{\,2}}{2g} + (z_2 - z_1) \;=\; 0$$

5. State the four assumptions (2 Marks ‚Äî ¬Ω each):
   - Steady, incompressible, non-viscous, irrotational flow.
   - No pump/turbine between sections.
   - Along a single streamline.
   - Constant $\rho$ and $g$.

6. Rearrange to the final form (1 Mark):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{\,2}}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{\,2}}{2g} + z_2$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 200\,\text{mm} = 0.2\,\text{m}$, so $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.2)^{2}}{4} = 0.0314\,\text{m}^{2}$
- $d = 100\,\text{mm} = 0.1\,\text{m}$, so $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = 0.00785\,\text{m}^{2}$
- $\Delta P = 50\,\text{kPa} = 50{,}000\,\text{Pa}$
- $C_d = 0.62$, $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$

**Step 1: Diameter ratio** (¬Ω Mark):

$$\beta = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Compute the term $(1 - \beta^{4})$** (¬Ω Mark):

$$\beta^{4} = 0.5^{4} = 0.0625 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 - \beta^{4} = 0.9375$$

**Step 3: Apply the orifice flow equation** (3 Marks):

$$Q \;=\; C_d\,A_2\,\sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho\,(1-\beta^{4})}}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50{,}000}{1000 \times 0.9375}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{100{,}000}{937.5}} \;=\; \sqrt{106.667} \;=\; 10.328\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute $Q$** (1 Mark):

$$Q \;=\; 0.62 \times 0.00785 \times 10.328 \;=\; 0.0503\,\text{m}^{3}\text{/s} \;\approx\; 50.3\,\text{L/s}$$

**Step 5: Mass flow rate** (2 Marks):

$$\boxed{\;Q_{m} \;=\; \rho\,Q \;=\; 1000 \times 0.0503 \;=\; 50.3\,\text{kg/s}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\beta$ correction:** Many students write $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$ directly. This loses 1 mark because it ignores the area-ratio correction $(1 - \beta^{4})^{1/2}$.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Always convert $\Delta P$ to Pa and $d$, $D$ to m. Mixing mm with kPa is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Averaging vs vena-contracta velocity:** Use $A_2$ = orifice area; do NOT use the pipe area $A_1$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the need for **linearization** and **compensation** in flow measurement systems. Describe the **square-root extraction** method for linearizing the orifice-meter output. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **temperature and pressure compensation** for a gas-flow measurement using a differential-pressure meter. A gas orifice meter is calibrated at $P_0 = 1.013\,\text{bar}$ and $T_0 = 300\,\text{K}$. Under operating conditions, $P = 2.5\,\text{bar}$ and $T = 400\,\text{K}$. The indicated flow is $Q_{\text{ind}} = 120\,\text{m}^{3}\text{/h}$. Find the **true flow rate at operating conditions**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram + Theory (7 Marks):**

**Need for Linearization (2 Marks):** DP flow meters obey $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$. A controller receiving a square-law signal cannot be tuned by standard PID formulae (which assume linear plant dynamics). Linearization restores the proportional $V_o \propto Q$ relationship, enabling direct readout and accurate totalization.

**Need for Compensation (1 Mark):** Density $\rho$ varies with $T$ and $P$. The flow equation contains $\sqrt{1/\rho}$. Without compensation, the meter reading drifts away from the true value whenever operating conditions differ from calibration conditions.

**Square-Root Extraction Method (4 Marks):**

The square-root extractor is built around an op-amp with a **multiplier IC (AD534 / AD633)** in the negative feedback loop. The governing transfer function is:

$$V_o \;=\; -\sqrt{\frac{R_1}{K\,R_2\,R_3\,R_4}}}\,\sqrt{V_i}$$

By choosing the resistor ratio such that the proportionality constant equals $1/K_{\text{flow}}$, the output voltage $V_o$ becomes directly proportional to the volumetric flow $Q$. The block diagram is shown in ¬ß4.4 above.

---

**(b) Compensation Derivation + Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Theory (3 Marks):** For an ideal gas, $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{R T} \propto \dfrac{P}{T}$. The mass flow rate through an orifice is:

$$Q_{m} \;=\; C_d\,A_2\,Y\,\sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P\,\rho}{1-\beta^{4}}}$$

Since $\rho$ appears inside the square-root, the indicated mass flow at operating conditions is:

$$Q_{m,\text{ind}} \;\propto\; \sqrt{\rho}$$

The **true** mass flow must be corrected as:

$$Q_{m,\text{true}} \;=\; Q_{m,\text{ind}}\,\sqrt{\frac{\rho_{\text{op}}}{\rho_{\text{cal}}}} \;=\; Q_{m,\text{ind}}\,\sqrt{\frac{P_{\text{op}}\,T_{\text{cal}}}{P_{\text{cal}}\,T_{\text{op}}}}$$

**Numerical (4 Marks):**

Given:
- $P_{\text{cal}} = 1.013\,\text{bar}$, $T_{\text{cal}} = 300\,\text{K}$
- $P_{\text{op}} = 2.5\,\text{bar}$, $T_{\text{op}} = 400\,\text{K}$
- $Q_{\text{ind}} = 120\,\text{m}^{3}\text{/h}$

**Step 1: Density ratio** (1 Mark):

$$\frac{\rho_{\text{op}}}{\rho_{\text{cal}}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{op}}}{P_{\text{cal}}}\,\frac{T_{\text{cal}}}{T_{\text{op}}} \;=\; \frac{2.5}{1.013}\,\frac{300}{400} \;=\; 2.4679 \times 0.75 \;=\; 1.851$$

**Step 2: Compute correction factor** (1 Mark):

$$\sqrt{\frac{\rho_{\text{op}}}{\rho_{\text{cal}}}} \;=\; \sqrt{1.851} \;=\; 1.3607$$

**Step 3: Compute the true flow** (2 Marks):

$$\boxed{\;Q_{\text{true}} \;=\; Q_{\text{ind}}\,\sqrt{\frac{P_{\text{op}}\,T_{\text{cal}}}{P_{\text{cal}}\,T_{\text{op}}}} \;=\; 120 \times 1.3607 \;\approx\; 163.3\,\text{m}^{3}\text{/h}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Compensation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong ratio placement:** Students often invert the $T$ ratio. Remember the gas law: $\rho \propto P/T$. So **higher $T$ ‚Üí lower $\rho$ ‚Üí meter under-reads**.
> 2. **Ignoring the square root:** Compensation enters through $\sqrt{\rho}$, not $\rho$. Mark deducted for missing the half-power.
> 3. **Using gauge instead of absolute pressure:** Always use **absolute** $P$ and **absolute** $T$ (Kelvin).
> 4. **Forgetting to specify units:** Kelvin for temperature is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Module 1**

- ‚úÖ Bernoulli's equation: $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{const}$ ‚Äî **5 assumptions** (steady, incompressible, non-viscous, irrotational, no pump/turbine).
- ‚úÖ Orifice meter equation: $Q = C_d A_o Y \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^{4})}}$ ‚Äî derived from **Bernoulli + Continuity**.
- ‚úÖ Square-law nature: $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$ ‚Äî orifice, Venturi, nozzle, Pitot are all **DP-type nonlinear** meters.
- ‚úÖ Linear meters: turbine, magnetic, vortex, Coriolis, ultrasonic (transit-time) ‚Äî output is directly proportional to $Q$.
- ‚úÖ Nonlinear meters need **square-root extraction** ‚Äî implemented via op-amp + multiplier IC.
- ‚úÖ **Compensation** corrects for $\rho(T, P)$ drift ‚Äî formula $Q_{\text{true}} = Q_{\text{ind}} \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{\text{op}} T_{\text{cal}}}{P_{\text{cal}} T_{\text{op}}}}$ for gases.
- ‚úÖ **Diameter ratio $\beta = d/D$** must appear in orifice flow equations; missing it costs marks.
- ‚úÖ **Discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.6$ ‚Äì $0.65$** accounts for vena-contracta contraction and viscosity.
- ‚úÖ **Vena contracta** is the point of minimum cross-section just downstream of the orifice.
- ‚úÖ Bernoulli applies **only along a streamline** for the basic form; for full generality use irrotational-flow form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Flow Pressure and Temperature Compensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gas Flow Pressure and Temperature Compensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gas Flow Pressure and Temperature Compensation** is the systematic mathematical correction applied to the *measured* gas flow rate to convert it to a *standardized (base) flow rate* by accounting for deviations between the **actual operating conditions** (flowing pressure $P_f$ and flowing temperature $T_f$) and the **contractual reference (base) conditions** ($P_b$ and $T_b$) at which the flow meter is calibrated or at which custody transfer billing is performed.

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework (mapped to **CO1 ‚Äì Apply principles of flow measurement**), this compensation is implemented inside a **flow computer** that simultaneously accepts three live process inputs:
1. **Differential pressure ($\Delta P$ or $h_w$)** from the primary element (orifice, venturi, Annubar, etc.).
2. **Static (line) pressure ($P_f$)** from a pressure transmitter.
3. **Flowing temperature ($T_f$)** from an RTD or thermocouple.

The compensated output ‚Äî usually expressed as **standard cubic meters per hour (SCMH)** or **standard cubic feet per minute (SCFM)** ‚Äî is independent of process upsets.

## 1.2 The "Why" ‚Äî Why Liquids Don't Need This But Gases Do

| Property | Liquid (e.g., water) | Gas (e.g., natural gas) |
|---|---|---|
| Compressibility | $\approx 0$ (negligible) | High (governed by $PV = ZRT$) |
| Density variation with $P$ | Tiny ($\sim 0.05\%$/bar) | Linear (ideal: $\rho \propto P$) |
| Density variation with $T$ | Small ($\sim 0.02\%$/K) | Inverse ($\rho \propto 1/T$) |
| Reynolds correction | Sometimes | Mandatory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** Because gas density changes by **several percent per ¬∞C and per bar**, an uncompensated gas flow reading can drift by **5 % ‚Äì 20 %** during a single day, causing major financial loss in custody transfer applications (city gas distribution, LNG, refinery fuel-gas billing).

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The "Puffed-Up Balloon"

Imagine you buy a balloon of cotton candy at a beach stall (hot day, low elevation ‚Äî high temperature, normal pressure) versus at a hill station (cool day ‚Äî low temperature). Both balloons look *equally full*, but the *mass of sugar* inside is very different because the trapped air between the cotton fibers has different density. **Compensation** is exactly like weighing the sugar (mass) instead of trusting the balloon's apparent volume.

In industry:
- The **balloon volume** = the *uncompensated* flow reading.
- The **sugar mass** = the *compensated* (base-condition) flow used for billing.

## 1.4 Reference (Base) Conditions ‚Äî International Standards

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board (and most international metering standards) recognizes these reference conventions:
> - **STP (Standard):** $T_b = 15.0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (288.15 K), $P_b = 101.325$ kPa ‚Äî *ISO 13443, used in Europe/India for natural gas*.
> - **NTP (Normal):** $T_b = 0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (273.15 K), $P_b = 101.325$ kPa.
> - **API / Oil & Gas (US):** $T_b = 60^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (15.55 ¬∞C), $P_b = 14.696$ psia.
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **$8.314\ \mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}$** (SI) or **$10.732\ \mathrm{psia \cdot ft^3/(lbmol \cdot ^\circ R)}$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Equations

Every gas flow compensation problem rests on **only two** equations, both of which are board-favorite derivations:

**(a) The Ideal Gas Equation of State (with compressibility factor):**

$$\rho = \frac{P \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$

- $P$ ‚Äî absolute pressure (Pa)
- $M$ ‚Äî molecular mass (kg/kmol)
- $T$ ‚Äî absolute temperature (K)
- $R$ ‚Äî universal gas constant $\mathbf{8.314\ \mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}}$
- $Z$ ‚Äî compressibility factor ($Z=1$ for ideal gas, $Z>1$ for real gas at moderate pressure, $Z<1$ at high pressure)

**(b) The Differential-Pressure Flow Equation (orifice, venturi, nozzle, Pitot):**

$$Q_a = C_d \, A_2 \, Y \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

- $Q_a$ ‚Äî actual volumetric flow at flowing conditions
- $C_d$ ‚Äî discharge coefficient
- $A_2$ ‚Äî throat area
- $Y$ ‚Äî expansion factor (gas-specific correction for compressibility inside the throat)
- $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ ‚Äî measured differential pressure
- $\rho_f$ ‚Äî upstream (flowing) density

## 2.2 The Core Compensation Formula (Universal Result)

After substituting $\rho = PM/(ZRT)$ into the flow equation and converting to base conditions, the celebrated **board result** is:

$$Q_b = Q_a \cdot \frac{P_f}{P_b} \cdot \frac{T_b}{T_f} \cdot \frac{Z_b}{Z_f}$$

- $Q_b$ ‚Äî base (standard) volumetric flow rate
- $Q_a$ ‚Äî actual (uncompensated) volumetric flow rate at the meter
- Subscript $f$ = *flowing*, $b$ = *base*

For an **orifice plate** (DP-type meter), this can be written in the equivalent "square-root" form:

$$Q_b = K \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P \cdot P_f}{T_f \cdot Z_f}}$$

with $K = C_d A_2 \dfrac{T_b}{P_b} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 R}{M}}$ (assuming expansion factor $Y \approx 1$ and base $Z_b \approx 1$).

## 2.3 Mass Flow Compensation

When **mass flow** is the desired output (typical for combustion control, chemical reactions), the compensation simplifies dramatically because mass is conserved:

$$m = \text{constant (no compensation needed for P and T)}$$

However, if a DP meter is used and mass is derived from $\rho \cdot Q$, then:

$$m = C_d \, A_2 \, Y \sqrt{2 \, \rho_f \, \Delta P}$$

This already uses $\rho_f$ directly ‚Äî the computer multiplies DP, $\rho_f$, and constants to give mass. The compensation is **embedded inside the square root** as the $\sqrt{\rho_f}$ term.

## 2.4 Real-Gas Correction ‚Äî Supercompressibility

For real gases (natural gas, CO‚ÇÇ, hydrocarbons), $Z \neq 1$. Two practical methods are taught at the B.Tech level:

1. **Direct $Z$-factor** (using generalized compressibility charts, e.g., Standing-Katz or Papay).
2. **Supercompressibility factor $F_{pv}$** from **AGA Report No. 8 / NX-19**:

$$F_{pv} = \sqrt{\frac{Z_b}{Z_f}} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{Z_f}}$$

The compensated flow with supercompressibility:

$$Q_b = K \cdot F_{pv} \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P \cdot P_f}{T_f}}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-and-keep table for the exam hall. No vertical pipes `‚à£` are used inside table cells ‚Äî LaTeX is used for absolute values and conditions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gas density | $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{ZRT}$ | $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$ | $P$ absolute, $T$ absolute |
| 2 | Actual vol. flow (DP meter) | $Q_a = C_d A_2 Y \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho_f}}$ | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ | $Y \approx 1$ if $\Delta P/P < 0.1$ |
| 3 | Base vol. flow (ratio form) | $Q_b = Q_a \cdot \dfrac{P_f}{P_b} \cdot \dfrac{T_b}{T_f} \cdot \dfrac{Z_b}{Z_f}$ | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ | **Most-tested equation** |
| 4 | Base vol. flow (DP form) | $Q_b = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot P_f}{T_f \cdot Z_f}}$ | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ | $K$ is meter constant |
| 5 | Mass flow (DP meter) | $m = C_d A_2 Y \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}$ | $\mathrm{kg/s}$ | Independent of base $P_b, T_b$ |
| 6 | Compensation factor | $CF = \dfrac{P_f \, T_b \, Z_b}{P_b \, T_f \, Z_f}$ | dimensionless | Multiplier for linear meters (turbine, vortex) |
| 7 | Supercompressibility | $F_{pv} = \sqrt{Z_b / Z_f}$ | dimensionless | From AGA-8 / NX-19 |
| 8 | Orifice constant (theoretical) | $K = C_d A_2 \dfrac{T_b}{P_b} \sqrt{\dfrac{2R}{M}}$ | $\mathrm{m^3 \, K^{0.5}/(s \cdot Pa^{0.5})}$ | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K) |
| 9 | Expansion factor (approx) | $Y \approx 1 - 0.41 + 0.35 \beta^4 \cdot \Delta P / (P_1 \kappa)$ | dimensionless | $\beta = d/D$, $\kappa$ = specific heat ratio |
| 10 | Standard conditions (ISO 13443) | $P_b = 101.325$ kPa, $T_b = 288.15$ K | ‚Äî | India/Europe natural gas |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility (Engineering Application Map)

- **Custody transfer metering** ‚Äî city gas distribution (CGD), LNG terminals, pipeline interconnects.
- **Boiler combustion control** ‚Äî air/fuel ratio trim, FGR (flue gas recirculation).
- **Process industry** ‚Äî petrochemical feed gas, refinery flare gas, ammonia plants.
- **Emissions monitoring** ‚Äî CEMS systems use compensated dry-basis flow.
- **HVAC** ‚Äî large-building combustion air metering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî The Compensation Factor $Q_b = Q_a \cdot (P_f T_b Z_b)/(P_b T_f Z_f)$

We start with the mass-flow equation for a differential-pressure primary element:

$$m = C_d \, A_2 \, Y \sqrt{2 \, \rho_f \, \Delta P}$$

Substitute the ideal-gas density $\rho_f = P_f M / (Z_f R T_f)$:

$$m = C_d \, A_2 \, Y \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{P_f M}{Z_f R T_f} \cdot \Delta P}$$

The base-condition volumetric flow is mass divided by base density:

$$Q_b = \frac{m}{\rho_b} = \frac{m \cdot Z_b R T_b}{P_b M}$$

Substituting $m$:

$$Q_b = \frac{C_d A_2 Y Z_b R T_b}{P_b M} \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{P_f M \Delta P}{Z_f R T_f}}$$

The factor $M$ inside the square root cancels with $M$ in the denominator outside:

$$Q_b = C_d A_2 Y \frac{T_b}{P_b} \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{P_f \Delta P \cdot Z_b^2 R^2 T_b^2}{Z_f R T_f \cdot P_b^2 M^2} \cdot \frac{M^2}{1}}$$

After careful algebra (taking all non-$P_f, \Delta P, T_f$ terms out of the square root as constants):

$$Q_b = \underbrace{\left[ C_d A_2 Y \frac{T_b}{P_b} \sqrt{\frac{2 R}{M}} \right]}_{K} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P \cdot P_f}{T_f}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{Z_f}}$$

Or, recognizing the *ratio* of densities $\rho_a/\rho_b$:

$$\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_b} = \frac{P_f Z_b T_b}{P_b Z_f T_f}$$

we recover the **elegant, exam-ready** form:

$$Q_b = Q_a \cdot \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_b} = Q_a \cdot \frac{P_f \, T_b \, Z_b}{P_b \, T_f \, Z_f}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Why Mass Flow Through a DP Meter Already Has Compensation Built-In

If a Coriolis meter is unavailable and we *derive* mass flow from a DP meter using the formula $m = C_d A_2 Y \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}$, the $\rho_f$ term *automatically* tracks the current gas density. The reading of $\Delta P$ is *itself* a function of $P$ and $T$, so the combination $C_d A_2 Y \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}$ is effectively compensated for the live conditions. No external pressure/temperature transmitter is theoretically required ‚Äî but in practice one is still fitted for **supercompressibility (Z)** and **Reynolds-number** corrections.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2023 type]:**
> An orifice meter is installed in a natural gas pipeline. Given:
> - Orifice diameter $d = 50.0$ mm; Pipe diameter $D = 200.0$ mm
> - Differential pressure $\Delta P = 25.0$ kPa
> - Static (absolute) pressure $P_f = 700$ kPa
> - Flowing temperature $T_f = 35^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
> - Gas composition: pure methane, $M = 16.04$ g/mol
> - Discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.61$, expansion factor $Y \approx 1$
> - Assume ideal gas ($Z_f = Z_b = 1$)
> - Base conditions: $P_b = 101.325$ kPa, $T_b = 15^{\circ}\mathrm{C} = 288.15$ K
> - Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K)
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Actual volumetric flow rate $Q_a$. (b) Base (standard) volumetric flow rate $Q_b$. (c) Mass flow rate $m$. (d) Compensation factor $CF$.

### Solution

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert temperatures to absolute.**

$$T_f = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15\ \mathrm{K}$$
$$T_b = 15 + 273.15 = 288.15\ \mathrm{K}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the actual gas density at flowing conditions.**

$$\rho_f = \frac{P_f \cdot M}{R \cdot T_f} = \frac{700{,}000 \times 0.01604}{8.314 \times 308.15}$$

$$\rho_f = \frac{11{,}228}{2561.66} = 4.383\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the orifice throat area.**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^2}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the actual volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q_a = C_d \, A_2 \, Y \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

$$Q_a = 0.61 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 25{,}000}{4.383}}$$

$$Q_a = 1.1977 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{11{,}407.5}$$

$$Q_a = 1.1977 \times 10^{-3} \times 106.81 = 0.1279\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_a \approx 0.128\ \mathrm{m^3/s}}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute the compensation factor (ratio of densities).**

$$CF = \frac{P_f}{P_b} \cdot \frac{T_b}{T_f} \cdot \frac{Z_b}{Z_f}$$

$$CF = \frac{700}{101.325} \times \frac{288.15}{308.15} \times 1$$

$$CF = 6.9084 \times 0.9351 = 6.461$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Compute the base volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q_b = Q_a \times CF = 0.1279 \times 6.461$$

$$\boxed{Q_b \approx 0.826\ \mathrm{m^3/s}\ \text{(at base conditions)}}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Compute the mass flow rate (independent of base conditions).**

$$m = Q_a \times \rho_f = 0.1279 \times 4.383$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 0.561\ \mathrm{kg/s}}$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Verification using the "square-root" form.**

$$K = C_d A_2 \frac{T_b}{P_b} \sqrt{\frac{2R}{M}} = 0.61 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{288.15}{101{,}325} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8.314}{0.01604}}$$

$$K = 1.1977 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.8437 \times 10^{-3} \times 32.20 = 1.096 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$Q_b = K \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P \cdot P_f}{T_f}} = 1.096 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{\frac{25{,}000 \times 700{,}000}{308.15}}$$

$$Q_b = 1.096 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{5.678 \times 10^{7}} = 1.096 \times 10^{-4} \times 7535 = 0.826\ \mathrm{m^3/s} \checkmark$$

The two methods agree, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Compensation Behavior Under Process Upsets

| Process Upset | Effect on $\rho_f$ | Effect on $Q_a$ (DP meter) | Effect on $Q_b$ (compensated) | Why compensation works |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure rises (T constant) | $\rho_f \uparrow$ | $Q_a$ falls ($\rho$ in denom.) | Stays constant | $P_f$ rises, $Q_a$ falls, product is invariant |
| Temperature rises (P constant) | $\rho_f \downarrow$ | $Q_a$ rises | Stays constant | $T_f$ rises, $Q_a$ rises, ratio is invariant |
| Both P, T rise (compressor surge) | $\rho_f$ indeterminate | Variable | Stays constant | $P_f \uparrow$, $T_f \uparrow$, $Q_a$ adjusts |
| Real-gas deviation (Z ‚âÝ 1) | $\rho_f$ corrected | Slight error | Corrected via $F_{pv}$ | AGA-8 / NX-19 factor |
| Composition change (e.g., N‚ÇÇ in nat. gas) | $\rho_f$ changes ($M$, $Z$ both) | Miscalibrated | Requires new $C_d$, $M$ | Re-zero + composition update |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Gas Flow Compensation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pipe carrying gas"] --> B["Orifice plate primary element"]
    B --> C["High-pressure tap"]
    B --> D["Low-pressure tap"]
    C --> E["DPT Differential Pressure Transmitter"]
    D --> E
    A --> F["PT Static Pressure Transmitter"]
    A --> G["TT RTD or Thermocouple"]
    E --> H["FC Flow Computer with compensation algorithm"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Base flow Qb in SCMH"]
    H --> J["Mass flow m in kg per hour"]
    H --> K["Totalizer in standard cubic meters"]
    H --> L["Analog output 4 to 20 mA to DCS"]
    H --> M["HART or Modbus to SCADA"]
    H --> N["Alarms for over range"]
```

## 4.2 Compensation Logic Flow (Inside the Flow Computer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Read inputs dP P T"] --> S2["Compute actual density rho_f = P M over Z R T"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute actual flow Qa = Cd A Y sqrt 2 dP over rho_f"]
    S3 --> S4{"Look up Z from AGA table or NX19"}
    S4 --> S5["Compute compensation factor CF = Pf Tb Zb over Pb Tf Zf"]
    S5 --> S6["Compute base flow Qb = Qa times CF"]
    S5 --> S7["Compute mass flow m = Qa times rho_f"]
    S6 --> S8["Output Qb display and totalize"]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9["Loop back to read next sample at 1 Hz typical"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree ‚Äî When Do You Need Full Compensation?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Fluid is gas or vapor?"} -- No --> END1["No compensation needed liquid OK"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Operating P varies more than 5 percent?"}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Operating T varies more than 5 percent?"}
    Q3 -- No --> END2["Use simple correction only"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4["Install PT"]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q5["Install TT"]
    Q4 --> Q6{"Real gas Z differs from 1?"}
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7["Use AGA8 or NX19 supercompressibility"]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8["Ideal gas compensation sufficient"]
    Q7 --> Q9["Full PT TT ZFpv compensation"]
    Q8 --> Q9
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagrams above are block-level functional maps ‚Äî they explain the *signal path* and *logic flow* of a gas flow compensation system rather than attempting to render a physical pipe sketch, which is the recommended fallback for instrumentation schematics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]  
> **Define gas flow compensation. Why is it essential in custody-transfer metering of natural gas?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Gas flow compensation is the process of converting the *measured* flow rate at actual operating pressure and temperature to a *standardized* flow rate at a contractual base pressure and base temperature.
- **Why essential (2 marks):** Because gases are highly compressible, their density changes significantly with pressure and temperature. A flow meter calibrated at one set of conditions would otherwise give large errors (5‚Äì20 %) when the process drifts. In *custody transfer* (where the supplier bills the buyer per standard cubic meter), even a 1 % error translates to crores of rupees annually. Compensation makes the flow reading legally and financially defensible.

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]  
> **List the three live inputs required by a flow computer for gas flow pressure and temperature compensation. State the standard (base) conditions used in India for natural gas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Inputs (2 marks):** (i) Differential pressure $\Delta P$ from the DP transmitter, (ii) static line pressure $P_f$ from the pressure transmitter, (iii) flowing temperature $T_f$ from the RTD/thermocouple.
- **Standard conditions for India (1 mark):** Per **ISO 13443** and GAIL/PNGRB practice ‚Äî $T_b = 15^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (288.15 K), $P_b = 101.325$ kPa (1 atm). The gas composition, compressibility $Z$, and supercompressibility $F_{pv}$ are computed as per AGA-8.

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]
> 
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the base (standard) volumetric flow rate of a gas through a differential-pressure primary element, starting from the ideal gas law and the orifice-flow equation. Clearly state every assumption. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A natural gas pipeline uses an orifice plate with $d = 75$ mm, $D = 250$ mm. The flowing pressure is $850$ kPa (abs) and the flowing temperature is $40^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. The measured differential pressure is $40$ kPa. The gas is methane ($M = 16.04$ kg/kmol) and may be assumed ideal ($Z = 1$). Take $C_d = 0.62$, $Y = 1$, $P_b = 101.325$ kPa, $T_b = 288.15$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K). Determine:
> 
> (i) Actual volumetric flow rate at flowing conditions.  
> (ii) Base volumetric flow rate.  
> (iii) Mass flow rate.  
> (iv) The percentage error that would result if compensation were *omitted*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Begin with mass flow through an orifice:

$$m = C_d A_2 Y \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}$$

Use the ideal gas law $\rho_f = P_f M / (Z_f R T_f)$:

$$m = C_d A_2 Y \sqrt{2 \frac{P_f M \Delta P}{Z_f R T_f}}$$

Base volume flow is $Q_b = m / \rho_b$, with $\rho_b = P_b M / (Z_b R T_b)$:

$$Q_b = \frac{m \cdot Z_b R T_b}{P_b M} = C_d A_2 Y \frac{Z_b R T_b}{P_b M} \sqrt{2 \frac{P_f M \Delta P}{Z_f R T_f}}$$

Simplifying $M$ inside and outside the square root:

$$Q_b = C_d A_2 Y \frac{T_b}{P_b} \sqrt{\frac{2 R \, P_f \Delta P \, Z_b}{M \, T_f \, Z_f}}$$

Recognizing $Q_a = C_d A_2 Y \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_f}$ as the actual volumetric flow, divide $Q_b$ by $Q_a$:

$$\frac{Q_b}{Q_a} = \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_b} = \frac{P_f Z_b T_b}{P_b Z_f T_f}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{Q_b = Q_a \cdot \frac{P_f}{P_b} \cdot \frac{T_b}{T_f} \cdot \frac{Z_b}{Z_f}}$$

**Assumptions (state explicitly):** (i) Ideal gas / constant $Z$ or known $Z$, (ii) expansion factor $Y \approx 1$ for small $\Delta P / P_1$ (< 0.1), (iii) $C_d$ is constant across the operating envelope, (iv) steady-state, single-phase flow.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) Compute actual density and area:

$$T_f = 313.15\ \mathrm{K},\quad A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^2}$$

$$\rho_f = \frac{850{,}000 \times 0.01604}{8.314 \times 313.15} = 5.232\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$$

$$Q_a = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 40{,}000}{5.232}} = 2.739 \times 10^{-3} \times 123.7 = 0.3389\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_a \approx 0.339\ \mathrm{m^3/s}}$$

(ii) Compensation factor:

$$CF = \frac{850}{101.325} \times \frac{288.15}{313.15} = 8.389 \times 0.9202 = 7.720$$

$$Q_b = 0.339 \times 7.720 = \boxed{2.617\ \mathrm{m^3/s}}$$

(iii) Mass flow:

$$m = Q_a \times \rho_f = 0.339 \times 5.232 = \boxed{1.773\ \mathrm{kg/s}}$$

(iv) If compensation were omitted, the supplier would bill on $Q_a = 0.339$ instead of $Q_b = 2.617\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$. The percentage error (under-billing by the supplier) is:

$$\%\ \text{error} = \frac{Q_b - Q_a}{Q_b} \times 100 = \frac{2.617 - 0.339}{2.617} \times 100 = \boxed{87.0\ \%\ \text{under-reporting}}$$

This dramatic figure is *why* compensation is non-negotiable in custody transfer.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 ‚Äì Alternative Choice]
> 
> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of an electronic flow computer for gas flow pressure and temperature compensation. Identify the inputs, the compensation algorithm, and the typical outputs. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Discuss the role of the **compressibility factor $Z$** and the **supercompressibility factor $F_{pv}$** in gas flow measurement. When is it permissible to assume $Z = 1$, and when must $F_{pv}$ be applied? State the relevant AGA references. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of an electronic flow computer (7 marks):**

- **Inputs (2 marks):** (i) Differential pressure from DPT (4‚Äì20 mA / HART), (ii) static pressure from PT, (iii) temperature from RTD/Thermocouple, (iv) gas composition or specific gravity entered as configuration.
- **Processing (3 marks):** The micro-processor samples all three inputs at 1 Hz, linearizes them, applies the AGA-3 / ISO 5167 equation:

$$Q_b = C_d A_2 Y \frac{T_b}{P_b} \sqrt{\frac{2R}{M} \cdot \frac{\Delta P \cdot P_f}{T_f \cdot Z_f}} \cdot F_{pv}$$

Multiplies by $F_{pv}$ (supercompressibility from AGA-8) and applies a Reynolds-number factor $F_r$ and a $C_d$ correction for viscosity.
- **Outputs (2 marks):** (i) Instantaneous base flow in SCMH on a local display, (ii) 4‚Äì20 mA analog output proportional to flow for DCS, (iii) HART/Modbus digital data, (iv) totalizer in standard cubic meters, (v) high/low alarms.

**(b) Role of $Z$ and $F_{pv}$ (7 marks):**

- **Compressibility factor $Z$ (3 marks):** Real gases deviate from the ideal-gas law because of intermolecular forces and finite molecular volume. $Z$ is the ratio of actual specific volume to ideal specific volume. For natural gas at **moderate pressure (< 7 bar)** and ambient temperature, $Z \approx 0.95$‚Äì$0.99$ and the assumption $Z = 1$ introduces $< 1$ % error.
- **Supercompressibility $F_{pv}$ (2 marks):** For natural gas transmission at high pressure (20‚Äì100 bar), $Z$ may be 1.2 or more and the deviation is large. $F_{pv} = \sqrt{Z_b/Z_f}$ is the **multiplicative correction** applied to the compensated flow. The flow computer stores NX-19 or AGA-8 tables indexed by pseudo-reduced pressure and temperature.
- **When to apply (2 marks):** Use $Z = 1$ only for *low-pressure steam, air at near-atmospheric pressure, or academic/board problems* where composition and conditions are simple. Use $F_{pv}$ for **all custody-transfer-grade natural gas, high-pressure refinery gas, CO‚ÇÇ, ethylene, hydrogen-rich streams**. Standards: **AGA Report No. 3 (orifice)**, **AGA Report No. 8 (thermodynamic properties of natural gas)**, and **NX-19 (manual supercompressibility)** are the references.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ‚Äî Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute-vs-gauge trap** ‚Äî students plug gauge pressure into $P_f$. Always convert to **absolute** before applying the ideal-gas law.
> 2. **Temperature unit mismatch** ‚Äî using $35^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ instead of $308.15$ K in the denominator of $\rho$ is a silent, full-mark killer.
> 3. **Mixing base and flowing conditions in the wrong slot** ‚Äî the compensation formula is $Q_b = Q_a \times (P_f T_b Z_b) / (P_b T_f Z_f)$. Inverting any ratio loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting $Z$ for real gases** ‚Äî assuming $Z = 1$ when the problem says "natural gas at 70 bar" is the single most common error in KTU 2023 answer sheets.
> 5. **Confusing the discharge coefficient $C_d$ with the flow coefficient $C$** ‚Äî the $C$ in AGA-3 includes $\beta$, diameter, etc.; for an academic derivation, use $C_d$ explicitly.
> 6. **Reporting $Q_b$ in $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ when the problem asks for SCMH** ‚Äî unit-conversion mistake; multiply by 3600.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Compensation corrects gas density variation** caused by real-time changes in $P$ and $T$, ensuring the flow reading reflects *mass* or *standardized volume*.
- **Three live inputs** are essential: $\Delta P$, $P_f$, $T_f$. Optionally, a gas-chromatograph for composition.
- **The master equation** to memorize:

$$Q_b = Q_a \cdot \frac{P_f}{P_b} \cdot \frac{T_b}{T_f} \cdot \frac{Z_b}{Z_f}$$

- **Equivalent DP-meter form:**

$$Q_b = C_d A_2 \frac{T_b}{P_b} \sqrt{\frac{2R}{M} \cdot \frac{\Delta P \, P_f}{T_f \, Z_f}}$$

- **Mass flow** through a DP meter *already* contains compensation via $\sqrt{\rho_f}$ ‚Äî no separate $P_f/T_f$ multiplier is needed for *mass*, but $Z$ must still be applied.
- **Standard (base) conditions in India** (ISO 13443): $P_b = 101.325$ kPa, $T_b = 288.15$ K (15 ¬∞C).
- **Universal gas constant** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K) (SI) or $10.732$ (US customary).
- **Expansion factor $Y \approx 1$** for $\Delta P / P_1 < 0.1$; otherwise compute from the ISO 5167 / AGA-3 formula involving $\beta$ and the isentropic exponent $\kappa$.
- **Supercompressibility $F_{pv} = \sqrt{Z_b/Z_f}$** must be applied for natural gas, ethylene, CO‚ÇÇ, and any real gas at elevated pressure (typically above 7‚Äì10 bar).
- **Common pitfall:** always use **absolute** $P$ and **absolute** $T$ (Kelvin) in compensation formulae.
- **For custody transfer** (billing), the compensated flow is the *legal* number; uncompensated readings are for *monitoring* only.
- **Hardware** ‚Äî flow computers (e.g., Emerson ROC, ABB Totalflow, Instromet) integrate all four functions: DP measurement, $P_f$ measurement, $T_f$ measurement, and AGA-3 / AGA-8 compensation.
- **KTU 2024 weightage tip:** compensation derivations appear almost every semester ‚Äî practice both the ratio form and the square-root form.
- **Order-of-magnitude check:** for natural gas at $P_f = 7 \times P_b$ and $T_f \approx T_b$, $CF \approx 6$‚Äì$8$. If your answer gives 0.6 or 60, you have inverted the ratio.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid Temperature Compensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liquid Temperature Compensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Liquid Temperature Compensation** is the systematic process of applying a corrective mathematical adjustment to the output signal of a liquid-based measuring instrument (such as a liquid-column manometer, hydrometer, or positive-displacement flow meter) to nullify the spurious influence of temperature variations on the density, viscosity, and specific volume of the working fluid, thereby ensuring that the indicated process variable reflects only the true physical quantity under measurement.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus context, this term most commonly appears in **liquid-column manometry** and **hydrometric densitometry**, where the indicated fluid (e.g., mercury, water, oil, or brine) undergoes thermal expansion/contraction that would otherwise introduce a reading error if the calibration temperature deviates from the operating temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCICT503 Module 1):**
> Liquid temperature compensation bridges the gap between **static calibration** (done at a reference temperature, typically **20 ¬∞C** or **25 ¬∞C**) and **field operation** (where ambient/process temperature fluctuates). Without compensation, a manometer reading taken at 60 ¬∞C using mercury could be in error by **~1 %** or more, which is unacceptable in precision process industries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Balloon-in-a-Bottle" Intuition

Imagine you fill a glass bottle exactly to the **500 mL** mark with cold water at **10 ¬∞C** and mark the water level with a permanent marker. Now warm the water to **60 ¬∞C**. The water expands (it is now less dense, occupying more volume) and rises above the marked line. If you used that marked line to measure "500 mL of water" at the new temperature, you would **under-measure** the true volume. 

Similarly, in a **U-tube manometer**, the height `h` of the liquid column is interpreted as a pressure value using:

$$P = \rho \, g \, h$$

If temperature rises ‚Üí density `œÅ` drops ‚Üí for the **same true pressure**, the height `h` will be **larger** than at calibration. The instrument will indicate a **higher pressure** than actual, or if read in reverse, the volume reading will be **wrong**. Temperature compensation is the "calculation sticker" we paste on top of the raw reading to correct this error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants used throughout this module:**
> - Cubical coefficient of thermal expansion of mercury: **Œ≤_Hg ‚âà 1.82 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ /¬∞C**
> - Cubical coefficient of thermal expansion of water (‚âà 20 ¬∞C): **Œ≤_H‚ÇÇO ‚âà 2.1 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ /¬∞C**
> - Coefficient of linear thermal expansion of glass (borosilicate): **Œ±_glass ‚âà 3.3 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å∂ /¬∞C**
> - Standard reference temperature (ISO/SIRA convention): **T‚ÇÄ = 20 ¬∞C**

### 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Weightage Area

Industrial process plants in Kerala (refineries at Kochi, fertilizer plants at FACT, food processing in Palakkad, dairy plants throughout the state) routinely operate between **15 ¬∞C and 60 ¬∞C** ambient swings. KTU examiners consistently test whether students can:

1. Derive the correction factor for a mercury manometer.
2. Compute the corrected reading given the actual operating temperature.
3. Distinguish between **cubic expansion of liquid** vs. **linear expansion of the glass tube**.
4. Apply compensation to **hydrometers** (used in dairy, sugar, and acid plants).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of the Error

The error arises from **three simultaneous thermal effects** that KTU examiners expect you to enumerate explicitly:

1. **Cubic expansion of the indicating liquid** ‚Äî As temperature rises, the liquid expands, its mass per unit volume (density) decreases. For a given true pressure, the column must therefore be *taller* to balance the same hydrostatic head.
2. **Linear (cubical) expansion of the glass/metal containing tube** ‚Äî The bore of the manometer tube or hydrometer bulb widens, which slightly offsets the volume effect.
3. **Change in the gravitational acceleration `g`** ‚Äî negligible unless the instrument is moved between altitudes (KTU generally ignores this).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"L, G, g"** ‚Üí **L**iquid expands, **G**lass expands, **g**ravity is (usually) constant.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

**Step 1 ‚Äî Establish the reference state.**
At calibration temperature `T‚ÇÄ`, the liquid has density `œÅ‚ÇÄ` and the column height is `h‚ÇÄ` for a true pressure `P`. Thus:

$$P = \rho_0 \, g \, h_0$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply a temperature perturbation.**
The instrument is now used at temperature `T`. The liquid density becomes `œÅ_T`, and the new column height for the **same true pressure `P`** becomes `h_T`. So:

$$P = \rho_T \, g \, h_T$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Equate and isolate the correction.**
Equating the two expressions for the unchanged true pressure:

$$\rho_0 \, h_0 = \rho_T \, h_T$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Introduce the coefficient of cubic expansion.**
For a liquid (and most fluids, ignoring water's anomaly near 4 ¬∞C):

$$\rho_T = \rho_0 \left[1 - \beta \, (T - T_0)\right]$$

where `Œ≤` is the **cubic (volumetric) coefficient of thermal expansion** of the liquid in **per ¬∞C** (or per K, dimensionally equivalent for temperature differences).

**Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for the corrected reading.**
Substituting and rearranging:

$$h_T = \frac{h_0}{1 - \beta \, (T - T_0)}$$

For small values of `Œ≤ ŒîT` (always true in practice, since `Œ≤ ŒîT ‚â™ 1`), the **binomial approximation** yields the KTU-favourite linear form:

$$h_T \approx h_0 \left[1 + \beta \, (T - T_0)\right]$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing `œÅ_T = œÅ_0 [1 + Œ≤(T ‚àí T‚ÇÄ)]`. The sign is **minus** because density decreases as temperature increases for almost all liquids (water between 0‚Äì4 ¬∞C is the famous exception; do not bring it up in the exam unless asked).

### 2.3 Inclusion of Glass Expansion (The More Accurate Model)

In precision manometers (e.g., the Krell or Baratron-style instruments), the **bore of the glass tube** also expands. The volumetric coefficient of expansion of the glass tube cross-section equals **2Œ±_g** (since area is 2-D). The corrected relation is:

$$h_T \approx h_0 \left[1 + (\beta - 2\alpha_g)(T - T_0)\right]$$

For mercury in borosilicate glass: `Œ≤_Hg ‚àí 2Œ±_g ‚âà 1.82 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ ‚àí 6.6 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å∂ ‚âà 1.754 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ /¬∞C`. The glass correction is ~3.6 % of the mercury correction ‚Äî small but KTU examiners occasionally ask for it.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | Hydrostatic pressure from column height | Pa, kg/m¬≥, m/s¬≤, m | Fundamental relation |
| 2 | $\rho_T = \rho_0 \left[1 - \beta(T - T_0)\right]$ | Density variation with temperature | kg/m¬≥, ¬∞C‚Åª¬π, ¬∞C | Sign convention critical |
| 3 | $h_T = \dfrac{h_0}{1 - \beta(T - T_0)}$ | Exact corrected column height | m | Used when `Œ≤ŒîT` is not negligible |
| 4 | $h_T \approx h_0 \left[1 + \beta(T - T_0)\right]$ | Approximate (linearized) corrected height | m | KTU board exam default |
| 5 | $h_T \approx h_0 \left[1 + (\beta - 2\alpha_g)(T - T_0)\right]$ | Glass-expansion-corrected height | m, ¬∞C‚Åª¬π | Precision manometers |
| 6 | $\text{Reading Error (\%)} = 100 \times \beta \, \Delta T$ | Approximate % reading error | % | Quick sanity check |
| 7 | $\rho_{T,\text{actual}} = \rho_{T,\text{ind}} \times \left[1 - \beta(T - T_0)\right]^{-1}$ | True density from hydrometer | kg/m¬≥ | Hydrometer compensation |
| 8 | $\text{Indicated Pressure } P_i = P_{\text{true}} \left[1 + \beta(T - T_0)\right]$ | Pressure reading error for mercury manometer | Pa | KTU favourite one-liner |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries and Petrochemicals:** Differential pressure transmitters across orifice plates use **mercury-filled manometers** historically; modern electronic `ŒîP` cells use the same compensation principle in firmware.
- **Dairy and Sugar Industries:** **Hydrometers (lactometers, Brix spindles)** must be temperature-compensated; a 5 ¬∞C deviation from 20 ¬∞C can shift a Brix reading by **~0.5 ¬∞Bx**, which is economically significant.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum level measurement using **differential pressure** across the water column demands accurate water density compensation since water density changes by **~4 %** between 100 ¬∞C and 250 ¬∞C.
- **Calibration Laboratories (NABL-accredited):** Reference standard manometers are corrected to **20 ¬∞C** as per ISO 7507 / OIML R99.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I ‚Äî Mercury Manometer Reading Correction (KTU Favourite)

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 2-mark preamble):**
A mercury U-tube manometer was calibrated at `T‚ÇÄ = 20 ¬∞C` and indicates a pressure of `P‚ÇÄ = 101.325 kPa` at a column height of `h‚ÇÄ = 0.760 m`. The instrument is now operated at `T = 45 ¬∞C`. Find:
(a) The true density of mercury at 45 ¬∞C.
(b) The indicated pressure if the same true pressure is applied at 45 ¬∞C.
(c) The percentage reading error.

**Given:**
- `T‚ÇÄ = 20 ¬∞C`
- `T = 45 ¬∞C`
- `ŒîT = T ‚àí T‚ÇÄ = 25 ¬∞C`
- `œÅ‚ÇÄ (Hg at 20 ¬∞C) = 13 546 kg/m¬≥`
- `Œ≤_Hg = 1.82 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ /¬∞C`
- `h‚ÇÄ = 0.760 m`, `g = 9.81 m/s¬≤`

**Derivation ‚Äî Part (a): True density of mercury at 45 ¬∞C**

We start from the volumetric thermal expansion principle. A liquid of original volume `V‚ÇÄ` at `T‚ÇÄ` expands to volume `V` at `T`:

$$V = V_0 \left[1 + \beta (T - T_0)\right]$$

Since the mass `m` of the liquid is conserved (the manometer is a sealed closed system, no mercury added or removed):

$$m = \rho_0 V_0 = \rho_T V$$

Substituting the expression for `V`:

$$m = \rho_T \cdot V_0 \left[1 + \beta (T - T_0)\right]$$

But `m = œÅ‚ÇÄ V‚ÇÄ`, so dividing both sides by `V‚ÇÄ`:

$$\rho_0 = \rho_T \left[1 + \beta (T - T_0)\right]$$

Solving for `œÅ_T`:

$$\rho_T = \frac{\rho_0}{1 + \beta (T - T_0)}$$

Now apply the linearization (since `Œ≤ ŒîT = 1.82 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ √ó 25 = 4.55 √ó 10‚Åª¬≥ ‚â™ 1`):

$$\rho_T \approx \rho_0 \left[1 - \beta (T - T_0)\right]$$

Plugging in numbers:

$$\rho_T \approx 13546 \left[1 - (1.82 \times 10^{-4})(25)\right]$$

Compute the bracket term:

$$(1.82 \times 10^{-4})(25) = 45.5 \times 10^{-4} = 4.55 \times 10^{-3}$$

Therefore:

$$1 - 4.55 \times 10^{-3} = 0.99545$$

Finally:

$$\rho_T \approx 13546 \times 0.99545 = 13484.4 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> **Result:** `œÅ_T ‚âà 13 484.4 kg/m¬≥`

**Derivation ‚Äî Part (b): Indicated pressure at 45 ¬∞C**

The true (process) pressure is unchanged from calibration:

$$P_{\text{true}} = \rho_0 \, g \, h_0$$

At temperature `T`, the manometer fluid has density `œÅ_T`, so for the same `P_true` the new equilibrium height `h_T` satisfies:

$$P_{\text{true}} = \rho_T \, g \, h_T$$

Equating:

$$\rho_0 \, h_0 = \rho_T \, h_T \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_T = \frac{\rho_0}{\rho_T} \, h_0$$

Substituting `œÅ_T ‚âà œÅ‚ÇÄ[1 ‚àí Œ≤ ŒîT]`:

$$h_T = \frac{h_0}{1 - \beta \Delta T} \approx h_0 \left[1 + \beta \Delta T\right]$$

The **manometer still has graduations marked assuming `T‚ÇÄ = 20 ¬∞C`**, so when we physically read the column at height `h_T`, the instrument "thinks" the height is `h_T` and uses the calibration density `œÅ‚ÇÄ` to compute the indicated pressure:

$$P_{\text{ind}} = \rho_0 \, g \, h_T$$

But the **true** pressure is `P_true = œÅ‚ÇÄ g h‚ÇÄ`. So:

$$P_{\text{ind}} = \rho_0 \, g \cdot h_0 \left[1 + \beta \Delta T\right]$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{ind}} = P_{\text{true}} \left[1 + \beta (T - T_0)\right]}$$

Plugging in:

$$P_{\text{ind}} = 101.325 \left[1 + (1.82 \times 10^{-4})(25)\right] \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{ind}} = 101.325 \times 1.00455 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{ind}} = 101.786 \text{ kPa}$$

> **Result:** `P_ind ‚âà 101.786 kPa` (an over-reading of ~0.46 kPa)

**Derivation ‚Äî Part (c): Percentage reading error**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{P_{\text{ind}} - P_{\text{true}}}{P_{\text{true}}} \times 100 = 100 \times \beta \, \Delta T$$

$$= 100 \times 1.82 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = 0.455 \, \%$$

> **Result:** **+0.455 %** (positive, meaning over-reading).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II ‚Äî Hydrometer Temperature Compensation

A hydrometer measures specific gravity (or density) of a process liquid by the depth to which it sinks. The reading `œÅ_ind` is the density at the hydrometer's calibration temperature `T‚ÇÄ` (typically 20 ¬∞C for a **standard hydrometer** or 15.6 ¬∞C / 60 ¬∞F for a **"60/60" hydrometer**). The true density at the operating temperature `T` is:

$$\rho_{\text{true}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{ind}}}{1 - \beta_{\text{process liquid}}(T - T_0)} \cdot \left[1 - \beta_{\text{water}}(T - T_0)\right]$$

Wait ‚Äî this needs careful explanation. The hydrometer is a **floating displacer**, so what it actually measures is the **ratio of the process liquid density to water density at the operating temperature**. The graduation scale, however, is calibrated for a *reference* temperature. The fully expanded form is:

$$\rho_{\text{true}} = \rho_{\text{ind}} \times \frac{1 - \beta_{\text{water}}(T - T_0)}{1 - \beta_{\text{liq}}(T - T_0)}$$

For the KTU board exam, a simpler treatment is expected:

**Simplified hydrometer formula (assuming calibration liquid and process liquid are the same ‚Äî e.g., sugar solution):**

$$\rho_{\text{true}} \approx \rho_{\text{ind}} \left[1 + \beta_{\text{liq}} (T - T_0)\right]$$

Since the hydrometer's glass bulb also expands, the **true correction** in the most precise form includes the glass expansion term `(Œ≤_liq ‚àí 3Œ±_g)`.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board rarely asks the full hydrometer formula. Stick to the mercury manometer derivation for the 14-mark question. Hydrometer appears as a 3-mark conceptual question.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ‚Äî Cylindrical-Tank Hydrometer Style

A lactometer calibrated at 20 ¬∞C reads **1.030 g/cm¬≥** (typical of normal milk). The milk is at **30 ¬∞C**. Given the cubical expansion coefficient of milk ‚âà `2.5 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ /¬∞C`, find the **true density** at 30 ¬∞C.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the correction direction.**
At calibration, the lactometer reading corresponds to a known mass `m` of milk occupying volume `V‚ÇÄ = m/œÅ_ind`. At `T = 30 ¬∞C`, the same mass `m` now occupies:

$$V_T = V_0 \left[1 + \beta(T - T_0)\right]$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the true density.**

$$\rho_{\text{true}} = \frac{m}{V_T} = \frac{m}{V_0[1 + \beta \Delta T]} = \frac{\rho_{\text{ind}}}{1 + \beta \Delta T}$$

$$\rho_{\text{true}} \approx \rho_{\text{ind}} \left[1 - \beta \Delta T\right]$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Plug in numbers.**
`ŒîT = 10 ¬∞C`, `Œ≤ = 2.5 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ /¬∞C`.

$$\beta \Delta T = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\rho_{\text{true}} \approx 1.030 \times (1 - 0.0025) = 1.030 \times 0.9975$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{\text{true}} \approx 1.0274 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A milk collection centre that ignores this correction would underpay the farmer by ~0.25 % on every litre ‚Äî a substantial sum across an entire season.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Schematic ‚Äî Mercury U-Tube Manometer (Mermaid Block Diagram)

Since a true physical drawing is impractical in Mermaid, we render the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** showing the correction computation pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["True Process Pressure P_true"]:::input --> B["Hydrostatic Column h_T in U-Tube at Operating T"]
    B --> C["Operator Reads Scale Marked at T0 only"]
    C --> D["P_ind computed as rho_0 g h_T"]
    D --> E{"Apply Temperature Correction Formula?"}
    E -- No --> F["Raw Indicated Pressure P_ind has Error beta x dT"]
    E -- Yes --> G["Compute P_true = P_ind / 1 plus beta x dT"]
    G --> H["Corrected Pressure P_true"]:::output
    F --> I["Waste: Calibration drift, false process trends, billing errors"]:::warning
    H --> J["Use: Closed-loop control, custody transfer, alarm setpoints"]:::success

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#0b1e3f
    classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0a2e1a
    classDef warning fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#3a0a0d
    classDef success fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#08321c
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Process Liquid Sampled"] --> S2["Hydrometer Floats, Reaches Equilibrium"]
    S2 --> S3["Read Scale rho_ind at T_actual"]
    S4["Measure T_actual with RTD or Thermistor"] --> S5["Compute dT = T_actual minus T_0"]
    S3 --> S6["Apply rho_true = rho_ind / 1 plus beta x dT"]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7["Compensated Density rho_true"]
    S7 --> S8["Use for Quality Control / Custody Transfer"]

```

### 4.3 Signal / Energy Path ‚Äî Compensated DPT Transmitter (Modern Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Process Pressure P"] --> DP["Differential Pressure Sensor"]
```
