# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 Model Paper]
**"Define a Multivariable Flowmeter. List any four advantages it offers over a conventional differential pressure flowmeter."**

**Model Answer:**

A **Multivariable Flowmeter (MVF)** is a single, integrated flow-measurement device that simultaneously measures the **differential pressure (ŒîP)**, **static line pressure (P)**, and **process temperature (T)** of a fluid and uses an on-board microprocessor to compute the **fully compensated mass flow rate (·πÅ)**, **volumetric flow rate (Q)**, and **density (œÅ)** in real time.

**Advantages over conventional DP flowmeters:**

1. **Real-time density compensation** eliminates errors caused by pressure and temperature variations.
2. **Reduced installation cost** ‚Äî one device replaces three separate transmitters (DP, P, T), saving on piping, cabling, and instrument taps.
3. **Improved accuracy** (¬±0.5 % to ¬±1.0 % of reading vs. ¬±2 % to ¬±5 % for uncorrected DP).
4. **Single HART/Fieldbus communication channel** transmits all three variables and diagnostics.
5. **Built-in custody-transfer and steam-table libraries** for direct mass / energy computation.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**"Explain the necessity of temperature and pressure compensation in flow measurement with a suitable example."**

**Model Answer:**

In differential pressure flowmeters, the measured ŒîP is related to flow by $\Delta P \propto \rho v^{2}$. Since **œÅ changes with both P and T**, any uncorrected measurement will drift whenever process conditions deviate from the calibration point.

**Example:** A natural gas pipeline is calibrated at 4 barg and 25 ¬∞C. If the actual operating condition is 6 barg and 50 ¬∞C, the density rises by ~40 %, but the DP-only meter will read only the ŒîP change, missing the density effect and producing a **40 % over-estimation** in mass flow ‚Äî leading to huge financial loss in custody-transfer billing.

Compensation using **œÅ = P¬∑M/(Z¬∑R¬∑T)** inside the MVF corrects this in real time, ensuring the reported mass flow is accurate regardless of line-condition swings.

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE 14-mark questions in the **Instrumentation** discipline follow a **two-sub-part pattern (7 + 7)**. Below are two completely independent question options, exactly as the question paper would present them.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ‚Äî July 2024 Model]

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Multivariable Flowmeter. Discuss the role of each sensor and the on-board microprocessor in computing the compensated mass flow rate. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A natural gas pipeline uses an orifice-based multivariable flowmeter. The line conditions are: $P_1 = 8$ barg, $T = 35\ ¬∞C$, $\Delta P = 25$ kPa, orifice diameter $d = 50$ mm, pipe diameter $D = 150$ mm, $\beta = d/D$. Given: $C_d = 0.62$, $\varepsilon = 0.98$, $M_{gas} = 20$ kg/kmol, $Z = 0.95$. Calculate: **(i)** the gas density at line conditions, **(ii)** the mass flow rate in kg/s, and **(iii)** the standard volumetric flow in Nm¬≥/h, taking $P_n = 101.325$ kPa, $T_n = 288.15$ K. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Question A

**Part (a) ‚Äî Block Diagram and Principle:**

[Draw a block diagram similar to Section 4.1 of these notes showing: Pipe ‚Üí Orifice ‚Üí DP sensor + Pressure sensor + RTD ‚Üí Microprocessor ‚Üí Density calc ‚Üí Mass/Volume output ‚Üí Display / HART.]

**Explanation:**

The MVF integrates three sensors onto a single flow body:

1. **Differential Pressure (ŒîP) Sensor:** Mounted across the orifice taps; gives a signal proportional to $v^2 \rho$ ‚Äî the raw flow indicator.
2. **Static Pressure (P) Sensor:** Measures upstream line pressure ‚Äî needed for compressibility / density correction.
3. **Temperature (T) Sensor (RTD Pt100):** Measures process temperature ‚Äî second key input to density.

The **microprocessor** executes the following sequence every 100 ms:

- Reads ŒîP, P, T.
- Selects the appropriate fluid model (gas, liquid, steam).
- Computes density using $\rho = P M / (Z R T)$ (for gases) or $\rho = \rho_0[1 - \beta(T - T_0)]$ (for liquids).
- Applies the ISO 5167 mass-flow equation $\dot{m} = C_d \varepsilon A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$.
- Outputs the compensated ·πÅ, Q, and œÅ on 4‚Äì20 mA + HART.

The result is a **highly accurate, real-time, fully compensated mass flow** suitable for custody transfer and process control.

**Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert line pressure to absolute.**

$$P_1 = 8\ \text{barg} + 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 901.325\ \text{kPa} = 9.01325 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2: Convert temperature to Kelvin.**

$$T = 35\ ¬∞C + 273.15 = 308.15\ \text{K}$$

**Step 3: Compute the gas density at line conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \dfrac{P \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T} \\
    &= \dfrac{(9.01325 \times 10^{5}) \times 20}{(0.95)(8314)(308.15)} \\
    &= \dfrac{1.80265 \times 10^{7}}{2.4347 \times 10^{6}} \\
    &= 7.40\ \text{kg/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the throat area.**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^{2} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the mass flow rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= C_d \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P} \\
       &= (0.62)(0.98)(1.963 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{2 \times 7.40 \times 25\,000} \\
       &= (1.1909 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{370\,000} \\
       &= (1.1909 \times 10^{-3})(608.28) \\
       &= 0.7244\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the standard-condition density.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_n &= \dfrac{P_n \cdot M}{Z_n \cdot R \cdot T_n} \quad (\text{assume } Z_n = 1) \\
      &= \dfrac{(101.325 \times 10^{3})(20)}{(1)(8314)(288.15)} \\
      &= \dfrac{2.0265 \times 10^{6}}{2.3954 \times 10^{6}} \\
      &= 0.846\ \text{kg/Nm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the standard volumetric flow.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_n &= \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_n} = \dfrac{0.7244}{0.846} = 0.8563\ \text{Nm}^{3}/\text{s} \\
Q_n &= 0.8563 \times 3600 = 3083\ \text{Nm}^{3}/\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) œÅ = **7.40 kg/m¬≥**, (ii) ·πÅ = **0.724 kg/s**, (iii) Q‚Çô = **3083 Nm¬≥/h**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023 Model]

**a)** Explain the concept of density compensation in Multivariable Flowmeters. Derive the expression for gas density using the ideal gas law and discuss how the compressibility factor Z is incorporated for real gases. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A saturated steam line operates at $P = 10$ barg. The MVF measures $\Delta P = 30$ kPa across an orifice of $d = 80$ mm in a $D = 200$ mm pipe, with $C_d = 0.61$ and $\varepsilon = 0.97$. From the IAPWS-IF97 steam tables, saturated steam density at 10 barg is $\rho_{steam} = 5.147$ kg/m¬≥. Calculate the **mass flow rate** and the **equivalent heat-transfer rate** if the steam enthalpy (saturated) is $h_g = 2778$ kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Question B

**Part (a) ‚Äî Density Compensation Concept and Derivation:**

**Concept:** In a differential-pressure flowmeter, the DP signal alone is insufficient to determine mass flow because fluid density (œÅ) varies with **P** and **T**. A multivariable flowmeter compensates for this by **measuring P and T in real time** and computing œÅ using the appropriate **equation of state**.

**Derivation of gas density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= nRT \\
n &= \dfrac{m}{M} \quad\Rightarrow\quad PV = \dfrac{m}{M}RT \\
P &= \dfrac{m}{V}\cdot \dfrac{RT}{M} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{RT}{M} \\
\Rightarrow \rho &= \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Real gases (incorporating Z):** For non-ideal gases, the compressibility factor Z is introduced:

$$
PV = ZnRT \quad\Rightarrow\quad \rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}
$$

Z accounts for **intermolecular forces and finite molecular volume**, both of which the ideal gas model neglects. Z is a function of **reduced pressure** $P_r = P/P_{crit}$ and **reduced temperature** $T_r = T/T_{crit}$ (via the **Law of Corresponding States**). For accurate custody-transfer work, Z is read from a multi-variable lookup table or computed from the **Peng-Robinson** or **Redlich-Kwong** equation of state embedded in the MVF firmware.

**Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution for Saturated Steam:**

**Step 1: Throat area of the orifice.**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^{2} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the mass-flow equation with steam density.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= C_d \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P} \\
       &= (0.61)(0.97)(5.027 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{2 \times 5.147 \times 30\,000} \\
       &= (2.972 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{308\,820} \\
       &= (2.972 \times 10^{-3})(555.72) \\
       &= 1.652\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the heat-transfer rate.**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \times h_g = 1.652 \times 2778 = 4589\ \text{kW} \approx 4.59\ \text{MW}
$$

**Final Answers:** ·πÅ = **1.65 kg/s**, Heat-transfer rate = **4.59 MW**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ‚Äî Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge pressure to absolute** before using the ideal-gas density formula. This single mistake loses **3 marks** instantly. Always write $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + 101.325$ kPa.
> 2. **Using temperature in ¬∞C** instead of Kelvin in $\rho = PM/(ZRT)$. This loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting the compressibility factor Z** for real gases ‚Äî a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing mass flow ·πÅ with volumetric flow Q** when the question explicitly asks for "mass flow." Show the formula $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ clearly.
> 5. **Not stating the units** of the final answer (e.g., "kg/s" or "Nm¬≥/h"). Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme **deduct 1 mark** for missing units.
> 6. **Skipping the discharge coefficient and expansibility factor** in the final numerical ‚Äî full 1 mark lost per missing term.
> 7. **Using throat area $A$ without computing it first** ‚Äî write $A = \pi d^2/4$ explicitly, even if the numbers are obvious.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Multivariable Flowmeters (KTU PCICT503 - Module 1)**

- **Definition:** MVF = single device measuring **ŒîP + P + T** and computing **·πÅ, Q, œÅ** in real time.
- **Three Sensors, One Microprocessor:** DP cell + Static P sensor + RTD + DSP.
- **Core Mass-Flow Equation:** $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ ‚Äî memorize this!
- **Density Formulas You Must Know Cold:**
  - Gas: $\rho = PM/(ZRT)$
  - Liquid: $\rho = \rho_0[1 - \beta(T - T_0)]$
  - Steam: use **IAPWS-IF97** tables / equations
- **Standard-Condition Volume (Gas Billing):** $Q_n = Q \cdot (P/P_n)(T_n/T)(Z_n/Z)$
- **Universal Gas Constant R = 8314 J/(kmol¬∑K)** ‚Äî must use this exact value for kmol units.
- **Always convert P ‚Üí absolute** and **T ‚Üí Kelvin** before any density calculation.
- **Expansibility factor Œµ** < 1 for compressible flow, = 1 for incompressible liquids.
- **Discharge coefficient $C_d$** is empirically derived from ISO 5167 / ASME MFC; depends on Œ≤ and Re.
- **Accuracy:** MVF gives **¬±0.5 % to ¬±1.0 %** of reading vs **¬±2 % to ¬±5 %** for DP-only.
- **Standards Governing MVF Design:** ISO 5167, ASME MFC-3M, AGA-3, AGA-7, IAPWS-IF97.
- **Output Protocols:** 4‚Äì20 mA + HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus-PA.
- **Applications:** Custody transfer (gas, oil), boiler steam, combustion air, chemical dosing, custody steam.
- **Sampling rate** of typical MVF: 5‚Äì10 Hz.
- **Saturated vs. Superheated Steam:** both handled by IAPWS-IF97 ‚Äî saturated needs only P (or only T); superheated needs both P and T.
- **Velocity-of-approach factor $E_v$** appears only when orifice Œ≤ is large (typically when Œ≤ > 0.5).
- **In exam answers:** draw the **block diagram** even for 7-mark sub-parts ‚Äî easy 2 marks.
- **Number rounding rule for KTU:** show **3 significant figures** in the final answer; keep 4‚Äì5 in intermediate steps.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes ‚Äî Multivariable Flowmeters (KTU PCICT503, 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Pressure Flow Meter - Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential Pressure Flow Meter - Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Pressure (DP) Flow Meter:** A flow measurement device that operates on the principle of creating a known constriction in a pipe to produce a pressure difference (head loss) between the upstream and downstream sections. According to KTU 2024 Scheme, it is classified as a **"head-type" or "obstruction-type" flow meter**, where the flow rate is inferred by measuring the differential pressure generated across a primary element inserted into the flow stream.

The differential pressure flow meter consists of two major sub-systems:
1. **Primary Element (Obstruction Device):** The physical hardware placed in the flow path (e.g., orifice plate, venturi tube, flow nozzle, pitot tube) that creates a measurable pressure drop.
2. **Secondary Element (DP Transmitter):** A differential pressure sensor (typically a strain-gauge or capacitive cell) that converts the pressure difference into a standard signal (4‚Äì20 mA or digital HART).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Garden Hose Thumb Trick:**
> Imagine pinching the open end of a flowing garden hose with your thumb. The water squirts out faster and you can *feel* an increase in pressure on the upstream side of your thumb. The harder you pinch (smaller the opening), the higher the pressure builds up. The flow rate of water is directly tied to how much higher the upstream pressure becomes compared to the downstream side.
>
> A DP flow meter works on the **exact same principle** ‚Äî it artificially narrows the pipe using an obstruction, the flow speeds up to conserve mass, and the pressure difference between the two sides is mathematically related to the volumetric flow rate.

### 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics

The following are the **physical constants and standard reference values** that must be remembered for KTU examinations:

| Symbol / Term | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Reynolds Number (Re)** | Dimensionless number $\geq 10{,}000$ for turbulent flow (mandatory for valid DP flow measurement) |
| **Gas Constant (R)** | **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)** |
| **Specific Heat Ratio (Œ≥)** | **1.4 for air**, **1.33 for diatomic gases** |
| **Density of Water (œÅ)** | **1000 kg/m¬≥** at **4 ¬∞C** standard |
| **Beta Ratio (Œ≤)** | $\beta = d/D$, must be between **0.2 and 0.75** for orifice plates |
| **g (acceleration due to gravity)** | **9.81 m/s¬≤** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must understand that the DP flow meter is an **inferentially calibrated** device ‚Äî it does *not* measure flow directly, it measures the *consequence* of flow (pressure drop) and computes the flow rate via the Bernoulli-Darcy equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The differential pressure flow meter is governed by **two fundamental laws of physics** combined together:

> [!NOTE]
> **Law 1 ‚Äî Conservation of Energy (Bernoulli's Equation):** For an ideal, incompressible, frictionless fluid, the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy remains constant along a streamline.
>
> **Law 2 ‚Äî Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation):** The mass flow rate entering a section must equal the mass flow rate leaving the section, which forces velocity to increase when cross-sectional area decreases.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic

1. **Insertion of Primary Element:** An obstruction (orifice, nozzle, venturi) is placed in the pipe. This reduces the flow area from $A_1$ to $A_2$.
2. **Velocity Conversion:** To conserve mass, the fluid velocity increases from $V_1$ to $V_2$ (where $V_2 > V_1$) as it passes through the constriction.
3. **Pressure Drop Creation:** The increase in kinetic energy is accompanied by a **conversion of pressure energy into kinetic energy**, producing a measurable pressure drop $P_1 - P_2$.
4. **Tap Sensing:** Pressure taps (flange taps, corner taps, or D‚ÄìD/2 taps) capture the high-pressure side $P_1$ and the low-pressure side $P_2$ and route them to a DP cell.
5. **Mathematical Computation:** The DP transmitter applies the **square-root extraction** of the differential pressure ($\Delta P$) to compute the volumetric flow rate $Q$.

### 2.3 Types of Primary Elements (KTU High-Yield)

| Primary Element | Construction | Pressure Recovery | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concentric Orifice Plate** | Sharp-edged flat disc with concentric circular bore | **Low (~55‚Äì65%)** | Clean liquids, gases, steam |
| **Eccentric Orifice Plate** | Bore offset to one edge | Low | Dirty/slurry fluids |
| **Segmental Orifice Plate** | Circular segment cut-out | Low | Two-phase flow |
| **Venturi Tube** | Convergent cone + throat + divergent cone | **High (~95%)** | Large pipes, low pressure loss desired |
| **Flow Nozzle** | Convergent smooth bell-shaped nozzle | Medium (~80‚Äì90%) | High-velocity steam, water |
| **Pitot Tube** | Two concentric tubes sensing stagnation + static | High (point velocity) | Spot velocity measurement, ducts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point ‚Äî Pressure Recovery:** The **Venturi tube** has the highest pressure recovery, meaning it loses the least energy. The **orifice plate** has the lowest recovery and is the most widely used due to its **simplicity, low cost, and easy replaceability**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$ | **General volumetric flow rate** | m¬≥/s |
| 2 | $m = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho(P_1 - P_2)}{1-\beta^4}}$ | **Mass flow rate** | kg/s |
| 3 | $\beta = d/D$ | **Beta ratio** (d = throat dia, D = pipe dia) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Y = 1 - (0.41 + 0.35\beta^4) \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\kappa P_1}$ | **Expansion factor (gas)** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_{actual} = K \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | **Linearized flow equation** | m¬≥/s |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D \mu / \rho}$ | **Reynolds Number** | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1 - P_2} = 1 - \dfrac{P_3}{P_1}$ | **Pressure recovery factor** | dimensionless |
| 8 | $N_{Re,D} = \dfrac{4Q \rho}{\pi D \mu}$ | **Pipe Reynolds Number** | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** The **discharge coefficient $C_d$** is *not* a constant ‚Äî it is a function of the Reynolds number $Re_D$ and the beta ratio $\beta$. Many students mistakenly use $C_d = 0.6$ or $C_d = 0.98$ as fixed values. Always refer to the **ISO 5167 Stolz equation** or empirical charts for $C_d$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** Custody transfer of crude oil and natural gas uses orifice plates calibrated to AGA-3 / ISO 5167 standards.
- **Steam Boilers:** Boiler feed-water and steam mass flow metering via flow nozzles and orifice plates.
- **HVAC Systems:** Building air-handling units (AHUs) use orifice plates for low-cost flow measurement.
- **Chemical Plants:** Corrosive fluid flow measurement where the orifice can be made of exotic alloys (Hastelloy, Monel).
- **Power Plants:** Cooling water flow and HP/LP heater drain flow.

> [!NOTE]
> The **square-root relationship** ($\sqrt{\Delta P}$) is critical in control engineering ‚Äî modern DCS systems apply **square-root extraction** either inside the DP transmitter or in the analog/digital input card to linearize the flow output for PID control loops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Flow Equation (Orifice Meter)

This is the **most important derivation** in the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus. The flow equation is derived by combining **Bernoulli's equation** and the **Continuity equation**, then incorporating a **discharge coefficient** to account for real-world deviations.

---

#### **Step 1 ‚Äî Write the Continuity (Mass Conservation) Equation**

For an incompressible fluid, mass is conserved between the upstream section (1) and the throat section (2):

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

**Explanation:** The mass flow must be identical at any cross-section of the pipe (steady-state). Since $A_1 > A_2$ (pipe area > throat area), we conclude $V_2 > V_1$ (velocity increases through the constriction).

---

#### **Step 2 ‚Äî Express $V_1$ in Terms of $V_2$**

Rearranging the continuity equation to express the upstream velocity:

$$
V_1 = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} V_2
$$

**Explanation:** This substitution is necessary because Bernoulli's equation contains both $V_1$ and $V_2$, and we want a single-variable expression.

---

#### **Step 3 ‚Äî Write Bernoulli's Equation (Steady, Incompressible, Frictionless)**

Assuming the pipe is horizontal ($z_1 = z_2$) and the fluid is ideal:

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2
$$

**Explanation:** The horizontal assumption eliminates the gravitational potential term, simplifying the equation. $P_1$ is the upstream stagnation pressure; $P_2$ is the pressure at the vena contracta.

---

#### **Step 4 ‚Äî Rearrange Bernoulli's Equation for the Pressure Drop**

Subtracting the right-hand terms from the left:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)
$$

**Explanation:** The differential pressure $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ is the measurable quantity, and we are expressing it in terms of velocities.

---

#### **Step 5 ‚Äî Substitute $V_1 = (A_2/A_1) V_2$ into the Pressure Drop Equation**

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \left[ V_2^2 - \left( \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Explanation:** Now both velocity terms are expressed via $V_2$, allowing algebraic simplification.

---

#### **Step 6 ‚Äî Simplify the Bracketed Term**

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Explanation:** Factoring out $V_2^2$ gives a clean expression. Since $A = \pi d^2 / 4$, the ratio $(A_2/A_1)^2 = (d_2/d_1)^4 = \beta^4$, where $\beta = d/D$ is the diameter ratio.

---

#### **Step 7 ‚Äî Solve for $V_2$ (Theoretical Velocity at the Throat)**

$$
V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **theoretical (ideal) velocity** at the throat assuming no energy losses. The denominator $1-\beta^4$ is called the **velocity-of-approach factor** because it accounts for the fact that the upstream fluid already has some velocity.

---

#### **Step 8 ‚Äî Introduce the Discharge Coefficient $C_d$**

In reality, the fluid experiences viscous losses, turbulence, and the **vena contracta** effect (the actual stream contraction is smaller than the orifice bore). We therefore multiply by an empirical factor $C_d < 1$:

$$
V_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}
$$

**Explanation:** $C_d$ (discharge coefficient) accounts for all real-world losses. Typical values are $C_d \approx 0.6$ for orifice plates and $C_d \approx 0.98$ for venturi tubes.

---

#### **Step 9 ‚Äî Compute the Volumetric Flow Rate**

The actual volumetric flow rate is the actual velocity times the actual throat area (which is the orifice bore area $A_2$):

$$
Q = A_2 \cdot V_{actual}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **final general volumetric flow rate equation** for any obstruction-type DP flow meter. For orifice plates, $A_2 = \pi d^2 / 4$.

---

#### **Step 10 ‚Äî Mass Flow Rate Form**

Multiplying by density $\rho$ gives the mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho (P_1 - P_2)}{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

**Explanation:** This form is preferred in industrial applications because mass flow is independent of temperature and pressure variations (ideal for custody transfer).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Square-Root Relationship (Linearized Form)

The flow equation shows $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$. Let us derive this explicitly:

Let the constant term be $K = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$, then:

$$
Q = K \sqrt{\Delta P}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
Q^2 = K^2 \Delta P \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P = \dfrac{Q^2}{K^2}
$$

**Explanation:** A **doubling of flow rate quadruples the differential pressure**. This is the fundamental reason why DP flow meters exhibit **non-linear (square-root) characteristics**, and why **square-root extractors** are mandatory in industrial signal conditioning.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Expansion Factor $Y$ (for Compressible Fluids)

For gases, Bernoulli's equation must include a compressibility correction. The expansion factor $Y$ accounts for the density change across the orifice:

$$
Y = 1 - (0.41 + 0.35\beta^4) \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\kappa P_1}
$$

where $\kappa = c_p/c_v$ is the specific heat ratio.

The corrected mass flow rate becomes:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot Y \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho (P_1 - P_2)}{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

**Explanation:** For liquids (incompressible), $Y = 1$ and the equation collapses to the standard form. For gases, $Y < 1$ and the correction can be 2‚Äì5% even at moderate pressures.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Tap Locations and Their Significance

| Tap Type | Location | Use Case | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flange Taps** | 1 inch upstream and downstream of orifice face | Standard for pipes 2"‚Äì18" | ¬±1‚Äì2% |
| **Corner Taps** | Exactly at the orifice face (upstream and downstream) | Small pipes, ISO 5167 | ¬±1% |
| **D and D/2 Taps** | Upstream at 1D, downstream at D/2 | High accuracy, large pipes | ¬±0.5‚Äì1% |
| **Pipe Taps** | 2.5D upstream and 8D downstream | Older systems, less accurate | ¬±2‚Äì5% |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Important Note:** ISO 5167 currently recommends **D and D/2 taps** as the preferred configuration for new installations due to their high accuracy and insensitivity to tap location errors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DP Flow Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Pipe Carrying Fluid] --> B[Primary Element Orifice or Venturi]
    B --> C[High Pressure Tap P1]
    B --> D[Low Pressure Tap P2]
    C --> E[DP Transmitter HP Inlet]
    D --> F[DP Transmitter LP Inlet]
    E --> G[Differential Pressure Sensor Capacitive or Strain Gauge]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Signal Conditioning and Square Root Extractor]
    H --> I[Microprocessor Linearization and Compensation]
    I --> J[Output 4 to 20 mA or HART]
    J --> K[DCS or PLC Control System]
    K --> L[Flow Indicator Recorder and Controller]
```

**Description:** The fluid flows through the primary element, where the constriction creates a pressure drop. Two pressure taps route the high-side and low-side pressures to the DP transmitter. The transmitter's internal sensor measures the differential, applies square-root extraction, temperature compensation, and outputs a standard 4‚Äì20 mA signal to the control system for display and control.

### 4.2 Flow Logic of Mass Flow Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Begin Computation] --> B[Measure Pipe Diameter D and Orifice Diameter d]
    B --> C[Compute Beta Ratio beta = d slash D]
    C --> D[Measure DP Sensor Output deltaP]
    D --> E[Measure Process Temperature T and Pressure P1]
    E --> F[Compute Fluid Density rho at T and P1]
    F --> G[Look up Discharge Coefficient Cd from Re and beta]
    G --> H[Compute Expansion Factor Y for gases only]
    H --> I[Apply General Mass Flow Equation]
    I --> J[Output mass flow rate m dot in kg per s]
    J --> K[Send to DCS for Display and Control]
    K --> L[End]
```

**Description:** This flowchart shows the full computational chain in a modern **multi-variable DP transmitter** (e.g., Rosemount 3051S with HART or FOUNDATION Fieldbus). The transmitter simultaneously senses DP, static pressure, and process temperature to compute density and $C_d$ in real-time.

### 4.3 Tap Location Diagram (Textual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fluid Entry Upstream] --> B[Straight Pipe Run min 10D]
    B --> C[Orifice Plate with Orifice]
    C --> D[Vena Contracta Region]
    D --> E[Straight Pipe Run min 5D]
    E --> F[Fluid Exit Downstream]

    G[Upstream Tap P1 at 1D] -.-> C
    H[Downstream Tap P2 at D2] -.-> D
    I[Corner Taps] -.-> C
    J[Flange Taps at 1 inch] -.-> C
```

**Description:** This shows the standard **D and D/2 tap configuration** on an orifice plate installation. The **vena contracta** is the point where the flow stream is narrowest (and pressure is lowest) just downstream of the orifice ‚Äî this is a critical region for pressure tap placement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024] Define Differential Pressure Flow Meter and list any two primary elements used in it.**

**Model Answer:**
A Differential Pressure (DP) flow meter is an inferential flow measurement device that infers the flow rate of a fluid by measuring the pressure difference created across a known constriction (primary element) inserted in the flow path.

Two primary elements:
1. **Orifice Plate** ‚Äî A sharp-edged disc with a concentric bore; widely used due to low cost and simple construction.
2. **Venturi Tube** ‚Äî A converging-throat-diverging section providing high pressure recovery with minimal permanent pressure loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Two examples (1 mark) + Working principle (0.5 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023] State the value of the discharge coefficient for an orifice plate and a venturi tube. Why is it less than unity?**

**Model Answer:**
| Primary Element | Typical $C_d$ Value |
|---|---|
| Orifice Plate | **0.6 to 0.65** |
| Venturi Tube | **0.95 to 0.98** |

The discharge coefficient $C_d$ is **less than unity** because:
1. The **vena contracta** is smaller than the physical orifice bore, reducing the effective flow area.
2. **Viscous friction** and **turbulent eddies** dissipate kinetic energy, reducing the actual velocity below the theoretical value.
3. **Flow separation** and **boundary layer effects** at the orifice edges further reduce the effective flow throughput.

---

### 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 Model Paper]**

### **Q.A (a)** Derive the general volumetric flow rate equation for a DP flow meter, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

### **Q.A (b)** An orifice plate with diameter $d = 50$ mm is installed in a pipe of diameter $D = 100$ mm. Water flows at a rate such that the differential pressure is $\Delta P = 10$ kPa. Calculate the volumetric flow rate $Q$ if $C_d = 0.62$. Take $\rho = 1000$ kg/m¬≥. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a) ‚Äî Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions:**
1. Fluid is incompressible (constant $\rho$).
2. Steady-state flow.
3. Pipe is horizontal (no elevation change).
4. No friction or viscous losses (ideal flow ‚Äî corrected later by $C_d$).
5. Velocity is uniform across the cross-section.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 ‚Äî Continuity Equation:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

Step 2 ‚Äî Bernoulli's Equation (horizontal pipe):

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2
$$

Step 3 ‚Äî Rearrange for Pressure Drop:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)
$$

Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute $V_1 = (A_2/A_1) V_2$ and simplify:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2 (1 - \beta^4)
$$

where $\beta = d/D$ and $\beta^4 = (A_2/A_1)^2$.

Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for $V_2$ (theoretical throat velocity):

$$
V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}
$$

Step 6 ‚Äî Apply Discharge Coefficient:

$$
V_{actual} = C_d \cdot V_2
$$

Step 7 ‚Äî Volumetric Flow Rate:

$$
Q = A_2 \cdot V_{actual} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}
$$

**Final Equation:**

$$
\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b) ‚Äî Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Orifice diameter $d = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- Pipe diameter $D = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- Differential pressure $\Delta P = 10$ kPa $= 10{,}000$ Pa
- $C_d = 0.62$
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m¬≥

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the Beta Ratio:**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{d}{D} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.10} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute $\beta^4$:**

$$
\beta^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the Orifice Area $A_2$:**

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the Velocity-of-Approach Factor $1 - \beta^4$:**

$$
1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Apply the General Flow Equation:**

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}
$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Substitute the Numerical Values:**

$$
Q = 0.62 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10{,}000}{1000 \times 0.9375}}
$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Simplify the Denominator Inside the Square Root:**

$$
\dfrac{2 \times 10{,}000}{1000 \times 0.9375} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{937.5} = 21.333 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Take the Square Root:**

$$
\sqrt{21.333} = 4.619 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 9 ‚Äî Compute Final Volumetric Flow Rate:**

$$
Q = 0.62 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.619
$$

$$
Q = 5.625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 5.625 \text{ L/s} = 20.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}
$$

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]**

### **Q.B (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Venturi meter. Compare it with an orifice meter in terms of pressure recovery and discharge coefficient. **(7 marks)**

### **Q.B (b)** A venturi meter is installed in a pipe of diameter $D = 200$ mm. The throat diameter is $d = 100$ mm. The pressure difference is $\Delta P = 50$ kPa. If $C_d = 0.98$ and the fluid is water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m¬≥), find the mass flow rate in kg/s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a) ‚Äî Venturi Meter Description (7 marks)**

**Construction:**
A Venturi meter consists of three sections:
1. **Converging Section (Inlet Cone):** Half-angle of approximately **21¬∞**, smoothly accelerates the flow.
2. **Throat (Parallel Section):** A short cylindrical section of constant diameter $d$ where the velocity is maximum and pressure is minimum.
3. **Diverging Section (Outlet Cone):** A long diffuser cone with half-angle of **5¬∞ to 7¬∞** that gradually expands the flow back to the full pipe diameter, recovering most of the pressure.

**Working Principle:**
- Fluid enters the converging cone, smoothly accelerating.
- At the throat, velocity reaches maximum and pressure drops to its minimum.
- In the diverging section, velocity decreases and most of the kinetic energy is **recovered as pressure energy** (unlike an orifice, which loses this energy as turbulence).
- Pressure taps are placed at the upstream section (1) and the throat (2) to measure $\Delta P$.

**Comparison with Orifice Meter:**

| Parameter | Orifice Plate | Venturi Tube |
|---|---|---|
| **Discharge Coefficient $C_d$** | 0.6 to 0.65 | 0.95 to 0.98 |
| **Pressure Recovery** | 55‚Äì65% (low) | 90‚Äì95% (high) |
| **Permanent Pressure Loss** | High (35‚Äì45% of $\Delta P$) | Low (5‚Äì10% of $\Delta P$) |
| **Cost** | Low | High (3 to 5 times) |
| **Maintenance** | Easy to replace | Difficult to clean/repair |
| **Accuracy** | ¬±1‚Äì2% | ¬±0.5‚Äì1% |
| **Best Use** | Clean fluids, low budget | Large pipes, low energy loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always draw a **labelled sketch** in the exam showing the converging cone, throat, and diverging diffuser with the pressure tap locations ‚Äî this alone can earn **1 to 1.5 marks**.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b) ‚Äî Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 200$ mm $= 0.20$ m
- $d = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m
- $\Delta P = 50$ kPa $= 50{,}000$ Pa
- $C_d = 0.98$
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m¬≥

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute Beta Ratio:**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{d}{D} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.20} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute $\beta^4$:**

$$
\beta^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute Throat Area $A_2$:**

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Apply the Mass Flow Equation:**

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Substitute the Values:**

$$
\dot{m} = 0.98 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1000 \times 50{,}000}{1 - 0.0625}}
$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Simplify the Denominator:**

$$
1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375
$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Compute the Numerator Inside the Square Root:**

$$
2 \times 1000 \times 50{,}000 = 1 \times 10^8
$$

**Step 8 ‚Äî Divide by Denominator:**

$$
\dfrac{1 \times 10^8}{0.9375} = 1.0667 \times 10^8
$$

**Step 9 ‚Äî Take the Square Root:**

$$
\sqrt{1.0667 \times 10^8} = 10{,}328.2
$$

**Step 10 ‚Äî Compute Final Mass Flow Rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = 0.98 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 10{,}328.2
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 79.49 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 79.5 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity-of-approach factor** $(1 - \beta^4)$ in the denominator ‚Äî costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using $C_d = 0.62$ for a Venturi** (it is ~0.98) ‚Äî costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing up mass and volumetric flow equations** ‚Äî for gases, always use mass flow; for liquids, both are acceptable.
> 4. **Not converting units** (e.g., kPa to Pa, mm to m) ‚Äî costs **1‚Äì2 marks** silently.
> 5. **Writing $A_1$ instead of $A_2$** for the flow area ‚Äî use the throat/orifice area, not the pipe area.
> 6. **Skipping the square-root step** in numerical problems ‚Äî partial marks will be deducted.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **DP flow meters are inferential** ‚Äî they measure $\Delta P$, not flow directly.
- **Primary elements:** Orifice plate (most common), Venturi tube (best pressure recovery), Flow nozzle, Pitot tube.
- **Core equation:** $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}}$
- **Mass flow:** $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}$
- **Beta ratio:** $\beta = d/D$ must be in range **0.2 to 0.75**.
- **Discharge Coefficient $C_d$:** $\approx 0.6$ for orifice, $\approx 0.98$ for venturi; it is **NOT a constant** ‚Äî it depends on $Re$ and $\beta$.
- **Square-root relationship:** $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$ ‚Äî hence square-root extractors are mandatory in signal conditioning.
- **Tap locations:** Flange taps (most common), Corner taps (highest accuracy at the orifice face), D and D/2 taps (ISO 5167 standard).
- **Expansion factor $Y$** applies only to gases/compressible fluids; $Y = 1$ for liquids.
- **Reynolds number check:** $Re_D \geq 10{,}000$ for turbulent flow assumption to be valid.
- **Venturi > Flow Nozzle > Orifice** in terms of pressure recovery (and hence energy efficiency).
- **Orifice plate is the cheapest and most replaceable** ‚Äî preferred in custody transfer and steam flow.
- **Vena contracta** is the point of minimum cross-section (and minimum pressure) just downstream of an orifice ‚Äî it is **smaller** than the physical bore, hence the need for $C_d$.
- **Pressure recovery factor $E$:** $E = 1 - P_3/P_1$ where $P_3$ is the recovered downstream pressure.
- **Units to remember:** $\Delta P$ in **Pa**, $\rho$ in **kg/m¬≥**, $A$ in **m¬≤**, $g = 9.81$ m/s¬≤.
- **Two fundamental laws:** Bernoulli (energy conservation) + Continuity (mass conservation) = core of the derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 ‚Äî DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE TYPE FLOW METERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Differential Pressure (DP) Flow Meters ‚Äî The Big Picture

A **Differential Pressure (DP) Type Flow Meter** is a primary flow measurement device that determines the volumetric or mass flow rate of a fluid by creating a known constriction (or obstruction) in the flow path and measuring the resulting **pressure drop** (differential pressure) across it. The relationship between this pressure drop and the flow rate is established by the **Bernoulli principle** combined with the **continuity equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** All flow meters covered in this module ‚Äî **Venturi, Orifice, Pitot Tube, Dall Tube, Flow Nozzle, and Target Flow Meter** ‚Äî belong to the family of **Head Flow Meters / Obstruction-Type Flow Meters** because they convert kinetic energy of flow into a measurable pressure differential (head).

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**

Imagine you are riding a bicycle and squeeze the rubber handle of the pump with your thumb to restrict the airflow ‚Äî the air speeds up at the constriction, and you feel a slight pressure drop. Now imagine placing your hand behind a partially closed gate during a flood ‚Äî water jets through faster but at lower pressure. The same physics governs all DP meters: **narrow the pipe ‚Üí velocity rises ‚Üí pressure falls ‚Üí measure that pressure fall to compute the flow rate.**

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Differential Pressure | $\Delta P$ or $h$ | **Pa (N/m¬≤)** or **m of fluid** |
| Discharge Coefficient | $C_d$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Diameter Ratio | $\beta = d_2 / d_1$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | **m¬≥/s** |
| Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | **kg/s** |
| Fluid Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m¬≥** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s¬≤** |

---

### 1.2 Venturi Meter ‚Äî Definition, Construction & Applications

A **Venturi Meter** is a converging‚Äìdiverging pipe section that produces a measurable pressure difference between the upstream (inlet) and the throat (narrowest section). It was invented by **Giovanni Battista Venturi (1797)** and is the **most accurate, efficient, and low-pressure-loss** obstruction-type flow meter.

**Constructional Components:**

1. **Inlet Section (Converging Cone):** A gently tapered cone (angle $\approx 21¬∞$) that gradually accelerates the flow.
2. **Throat:** A short cylindrical parallel section of minimum cross-section (diameter $d_2$).
3. **Outlet Section (Diverging Cone):** A longer tapered cone (angle $\approx 5¬∞$ to $7¬∞$) that gradually decelerates the flow and recovers most of the pressure.
4. **Two Pressure Tappings:** One at the inlet, one at the throat ‚Äî connected to a manometer or DP transmitter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The Venturi is essentially a "polite speed bump" for the fluid ‚Äî it gently speeds the flow up at the throat (measurable pressure drop) and then **almost completely recovers** the pressure downstream. This is why it is preferred for permanent installations in large pipelines, water-supply mains, and steam systems.

**Applications of Venturi Meter:**

- Measurement of flow rate in **water supply distribution networks**.
- **Steam and gas flow** measurement in power plants and chemical industries.
- **High-pressure oil pipelines** (refineries, petrochemical).
- **Aerodynamic testing** of aircraft and rockets (large-scale test sections).
- **Irrigation canals and dam outlets.**
- Anywhere **low permanent pressure loss** is critical.

---

### 1.3 Types of Venturi Meter

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Classical / Long Form Venturi** | Inlet cone $21¬∞$, outlet cone $5¬∞$‚Äì$7¬∞$, throat length = throat diameter | Highest accuracy, low $\Delta P$ loss (10‚Äì15%) |
| **Short Form Venturi (Dall Type)** | Replaces outlet cone with a sudden expansion; throat length $< d_2$ | Compact installation, slightly higher loss |
| **Eccentric Venturi** | Throat offset from centreline ‚Äî used for dirty fluids / slurries where solids could settle at the bottom | Slurries, sewage, pulp |
| **Conical Venturi** | Sharp conical entrance and exit; not as smooth as classical | Less common |
| **Insert / "Venture" type** | A machined insert fitted inside an existing pipe | Retrofit installations |

---

### 1.4 Limitations of Venturi Meter

1. **High initial cost** due to precise machining and long length.
2. **Large space requirement** ‚Äî needs about $5 D_1$ to $10 D_1$ pipe lengths.
3. **Limited $\beta$ range** ‚Äî typically $0.4 \le \beta \le 0.75$.
4. **Not suitable for very dirty fluids** (orifice is preferred).
5. **Stricter fabrication tolerances** ‚Äî sensitive to wear and deposits.
6. **Permanent pressure loss** is small but not zero (~10‚Äì15% of measured $\Delta P$).

---

### 1.5 Pressure Tapping in Venturi Meter

Pressure tappings are small, smooth-bore holes that sense the static pressure at the **inlet** and the **throat**. There are four standard tapping arrangements (per **ISO 5167**):

| Type | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Corner Tappings** | At the plane of the inlet face & throat face | Rarely used now |
| **D and D/2 Tappings** | One at $D_1$ from inlet face, other at $D_2/2$ from throat | For standard long-form Venturi |
| **Flange Tappings** | 25.4 mm (1") upstream of inlet face & 25.4 mm downstream of throat face | Most common in industry |
| **Piezo / Radius Tappings** | At the curved upstream surface of the inlet cone and the curved downstream surface of the throat | Used in research-grade meters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pressure taps **must be flush with the inner wall, burr-free, and free of chips** ‚Äî any protrusion disturbs the streamline and ruins the discharge coefficient.

---

### 1.6 Orifice Meter ‚Äî Definition, Construction & Applications

An **Orifice Meter** is the simplest, cheapest, and most widely used DP flow meter. It consists of a thin, flat, circular plate with a **concentric (or eccentric/segmental) hole** mounted between two pipe flanges. The flow contracts to a **vena contracta** just downstream of the plate, where velocity is maximum and pressure is minimum.

**Constructional Features:**

1. **Orifice Plate** ‚Äî thin (usually **3 to 6 mm**), flat, stainless steel, with sharp upstream edge (radius $\le 0.0004 d$).
2. **Plate Holder / Flanges** ‚Äî supports the plate between pipe flanges.
3. **Pressure Taps** ‚Äî upstream and downstream of the plate.
4. **Differential Pressure Transducer / Manometer.**

**Applications of Orifice Meter:**

- **Steam, water, air, gas, oil** ‚Äî virtually every process industry.
- Custody transfer of natural gas (with proper AGA/API compliance).
- Chemical dosing, refinery charge stocks, HVAC ducts.
- Clean to moderately dirty fluids.

---

### 1.7 Types of Orifice Meter (Plate Types)

| Type | Shape & Feature | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Concentric (Sharp-Edged) Orifice** | Hole is concentric, sharp edge | Clean liquids & gases; most common |
| **Eccentric Orifice** | Hole shifted to bottom of plate | Fluids with suspended solids (avoids sediment accumulation) |
| **Segmental Orifice** | A segment of circle removed from bottom | Slurries & dirty liquids with high solid content |
| **Quadrant-Edged Orifice** | Quarter-circle rounded edge on inlet | Viscous fluids (Reynolds < 10‚Å¥) |
| **Conical (Quarter-Circle) Entrance Orifice** | Conical entrance profile | Very low Reynolds number flow |
| **Multi-Hole Orifice (Rosemount 405 etc.)** | Multiple small holes across the plate | Improved accuracy, reduced straight-pipe run needs |

---

### 1.8 Pressure Taps in Orifice Meter (ISO 5167)

| Tap Type | Location |
|---|---|
| **Corner Taps** | Exactly at the faces of the orifice plate (upstream & downstream) |
| **Flange Taps** | 25.4 mm (1") upstream and 25.4 mm downstream of the plate faces |
| **D and D/2 Taps** | 1 pipe diameter upstream and $D_2/2$ downstream |
| **Pipe Taps (2¬ΩD, 8D)** | $2.5 D_1$ upstream and $8 D_1$ downstream ‚Äî common for large pipes |

---

### 1.9 Limitations of Orifice Meter

1. **High permanent pressure loss** ‚Äî up to **40%‚Äì60%** of measured $\Delta P$ (much greater than Venturi).
2. **Lower accuracy** than Venturi, especially at low Reynolds numbers.
3. **Sharp edge of plate degrades** over time with abrasive fluids ‚Üí must be recalibrated/replaced.
4. **Susceptible to wear, corrosion, fouling** in dirty service.
5. **High $\beta$ (low flow) is inaccurate**; $\beta < 0.2$ or $\beta > 0.75$ is not recommended.
6. **Requires long upstream and downstream straight pipe lengths** (typically $10 D_1$ to $50 D_1$).
7. **Not suitable for slurries with large particles** (concentric type) or high-viscosity fluids.

---

### 1.10 Pitot Tube ‚Äî Definition & Working

A **Pitot Tube** is a **point-velocity measurement** device, not an averaging flow meter. It measures the **stagnation (total) pressure** of the flow at a point and compares it with the **static pressure** of the moving stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Henri Pitot (1732)** invented it to measure river flow velocity. **Modified by Darcy (1858)** to the modern form. It is the **principal instrument on aircraft** (airspeed indicator) and in HVAC balancing.

**Two Concentric Tubes:**

1. **Inner (Stagnation) Tube** ‚Äî open end faces directly into the flow ‚Üí measures **Total (Stagnation) Pressure** $P_t$.
2. **Outer (Static) Tube** ‚Äî has small holes on the side ‚Üí measures **Static Pressure** $P_s$.
3. Difference $P_t - P_s = P_d$ = **Dynamic (Velocity) Pressure**.

---

### 1.11 Limitations of Pitot Tube

1. Measures only **point velocity** ‚Äî must traverse the duct to obtain average velocity.
2. **Highly sensitive to misalignment** (yaw/pitch) ‚Äî errors of $1¬∞$ can cause ~1% error.
3. **Plug prone** in dirty, sticky, or particulate-laden service.
4. **Low differential pressure** at low velocities ‚Üí requires sensitive manometer.
5. **Not suitable for highly turbulent or pulsating flow** without averaging.
6. **Damaged or bent tubes** give false readings.

---

### 1.12 Pitot Tube ‚Äî Method of Use in Circular Duct

1. Drill/insert two pressure tap holes in the duct wall **90¬∞ apart** at the same cross-section.
2. Insert the **static tube** to sample the wall static pressure.
3. Insert the **stagnation (impact) tube** and traverse across the duct diameter in **at least 10 equal areas** (per ISO 3966).
4. For each point, record $\Delta h$ and compute point velocity $V = \sqrt{2 g h}$.
5. **Average the readings** ‚Äî this gives mean velocity $\bar{V}$ in the duct.
6. Compute flow: $Q = \bar{V} \times A_{duct}$.

**Traverse positions for circular duct (per IS 3633 / ISO 3966):**

> Equal-area logarithmic positions (10-point): **0.026 R, 0.082 R, 0.146 R, 0.226 R, 0.342 R, 0.658 R, 0.774 R, 0.854 R, 0.918 R, 0.974 R** from one wall (and a centre reading for 10-point ISO method).

---

### 1.13 Pitot Tube ‚Äî Method of Use in Rectangular Duct

1. Divide the duct cross-section into **a grid of equal rectangles** (e.g., $4 \times 4$, $5 \times 5$ ‚Äî minimum 16 points per ISO 5801).
2. Insert Pitot probe at the centre of each rectangle.
3. Record $\Delta h$ at each grid point.
4. Compute local velocity $V_i$ and take the **arithmetic mean** as the average velocity.
5. Flow: $Q = \bar{V} \times A_{duct}$ where $A_{duct} = w \times h$.

---

### 1.14 Precautions During Installation of Pitot Tube

- The **head must point directly into the flow** ‚Äî misalignment > $\pm 5¬∞$ causes significant error.
- **Nozzle bore must be clean** and burr-free.
- The **static pressure holes** must be on a true cylindrical section, not on a bend or valve.
- Maintain at least **10 duct diameters of straight run** upstream and **5 diameters** downstream.
- **Purge air bubbles** from the manometer lines.
- For hot gases, use **water-cooled Pitot tubes** to protect the probe.
- Do not use in flows with **Reynolds number < 200** or > $10^6$ without calibration.
- Ensure **thermal expansion** does not cause misalignment.

---

### 1.15 Dall Tube

A **Dall Tube** is a short-form Venturi with a unique annular slot at the throat that creates two converging jets, which then impinge on a central cone. Invented by **William Dall**, it has:

- **Very low permanent pressure loss** (~3%‚Äì5%).
- **Short axial length** ‚Äî typically only **1 to 2 pipe diameters**.
- Excellent for **large diameter** water and gas pipes.

**Installation:**

- Mounted between flanges like an orifice plate.
- Requires **5‚Äì10 $D_1$** straight pipe upstream and **2‚Äì3 $D_1$** downstream.
- The **larger end of the cone faces the flow** (inlet).
- Pressure tappings are at the inlet (between flange and entry to slot) and at the throat.

---

### 1.16 Flow Nozzle ‚Äî Definition, Types & Applications

A **Flow Nozzle** is a smooth, contoured obstruction (resembling the converging section of a Venturi) with **no divergent outlet**. It is essentially **half a Venturi meter** ‚Äî converging section only. It is used where the high velocity at the exit would otherwise erode a divergent cone (e.g., **high-pressure steam**).

**Types of Flow Nozzle:**

1. **Long Radius Nozzle (ASME / DIN)** ‚Äî entry profile = elliptical or toroidal, exits with cylindrical throat.
2. **ISA 1932 Nozzle** ‚Äî a standardised nozzle with a flat face and specific radius of curvature at the inlet; widely used in **Europe** for steam and gas.
3. **Venturi Nozzle** ‚Äî combines nozzle inlet with divergent outlet.

---

### 1.17 Long Radius Nozzle (ASME Long Radius Nozzle)

- Throat is a true cylinder of length $0.5 d_2$.
- Inlet profile is a quarter-ellipse with major axis $D_1$ and minor axis $0.5 d_2$.
- $\beta$ range: $0.2 \le \beta \le 0.8$.
- **$C_d$ ‚âà 0.95‚Äì0.99** (very high).
- Suitable for **high-Reynolds turbine flow measurement**.

---

### 1.18 ISA 1932 Nozzle

- Flat face at the inlet; circular arc of radius $r = 0.171 d_2$ joining inlet face to throat.
- Throat is cylindrical with length $\approx 0.3 d_2$.
- $\beta$ range: $0.3 \le \beta \le 0.8$.
- **$C_d$ ‚âà 0.95‚Äì0.99**.
- Most common **European standard** for high-pressure steam.
- Reference: **ISO 5167-3**.

---

### 1.19 Generalised Features of Differential Pressure Type Flow Meters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Common Features:**

1. **Bernoulli + Continuity based** ‚Äî flow rate $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$.
2. **Square-root relationship** ‚Äî must use **square-root extractor** in transmitter.
3. **Rangeability** ‚Äî limited to **4 : 1** (for 1% accuracy) due to square-root law; transmitter can show 10:1 with relaxed accuracy.
4. **Standardised by ISO 5167** ‚Äî discharge coefficients and expansivity factors are tabulated.
5. **Output is $\Delta P$**, not direct flow ‚Äî requires DP cell + flow computer / square root extractor.
6. **All are intrusive** ‚Äî introduce a small permanent pressure loss.
7. **Affected by density and viscosity** changes.
8. **Require straight pipe runs** upstream and downstream.
9. **Turndown limited** ‚Äî the practical minimum measurable flow is ~30% of full scale.
10. **Compressible-fluid correction** is required for gases (expansivity factor $\epsilon$).

---

### 1.20 Installation Requirements of DP Flow Meters

| Requirement | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Minimum straight run upstream** (10‚Äì50 $D_1$) | Distorted velocity profile causes measurement error |
| **Minimum straight run downstream** (2‚Äì5 $D_1$) | Allows re-development of flow |
| **Pulsation dampeners** upstream | Pulsating flow gives false $\Delta P$ |
| **Air bleed valves** at top of manometer lines | Removes trapped gas bubbles |
| **Drain valves** at bottom for liquid service | Removes sediment, ensures liquid-filled lines |
| **Pressure taps at the same elevation** | Avoids hydrostatic head error |
| **Use of seal pots / diaphragm seals** for corrosive fluids | Protects the transmitter |
| **Thermal insulation** for hot/cold fluids | Prevents density change in impulse lines |
| **Bypass loop & isolation valves** | Allows zero and maintenance checks |
| **Avoid installing near pumps, valves, fittings** | Disturbs flow profile |

---

### 1.21 Target Flow Meter

A **Target Flow Meter** (also called **drag-body flow meter**) uses a **flat circular disc (target)** placed in the flow path. The flowing fluid exerts a **drag (impact) force** $F$ on the disc, and this force is measured by a **force balance** (load cell or strain gauge bridge). The drag force is proportional to the square of the flow velocity.

**Key Features:**

- **No pressure taps, no manometer lines, no impulse lines** ‚Äî clean signal.
- Suitable for **dirty, corrosive, slurry, or high-temperature fluids** (e.g., pulp, paper stock, geothermal steam).
- Ideal where **conventional DP meters plug or corrode**.
- Flow equation: $F = C_d \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A_T$, where $A_T$ is the disc area.
- $V = \sqrt{2 F / (C_d \rho A_T)}$ and $Q = V \cdot A_{pipe}$.
- **Square-root output** relationship to flow.
- **Rangeability ‚âà 4:1.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle ‚Äî Bernoulli & Continuity

The fundamental theory behind **all obstruction-type flow meters** rests on two equations:

**Bernoulli's Equation (ideal, incompressible, steady flow):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Continuity Equation (conservation of mass):**

$$A_1 V_1 \;=\; A_2 V_2$$

Combining these for a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$) and eliminating $V_1$:

$$V_2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$

The **theoretical flow rate** becomes:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

The **actual flow rate** introduces a **discharge coefficient $C_d$** to account for friction, vena-contracta, and turbulence:

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{C_d \, A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} \;=\; C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1-\beta^4}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a square-root?** Because $\Delta P \propto V^2$, so $V \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$. The transmitter must apply **square-root extraction** to obtain a linear flow signal.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise these ‚Äî they appear in every KTU numerical on this topic. **Do not use the `|` character inside any table row.**

| # | Quantity / Term | Formula | Definition of Symbols |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Flow Rate (DP meter)** | $Q = \dfrac{C_d \, A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $C_d$ discharge coeff., $A_2$ throat area, $\beta = d_2/d_1$ |
| 2 | **Flow in terms of head $h$** | $Q = C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1-\beta^4}}$ | $h = \Delta P / (\rho g)$ in m of fluid |
| 3 | **Mass flow rate** | $\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$ | kg/s |
| 4 | **Diameter ratio** | $\beta = d_2 / d_1$ | $d_2$ throat/orifice bore, $d_1$ pipe ID |
| 5 | **Area ratio** | $m = A_2 / A_1 = \beta^2$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Velocity of approach factor** | $K = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}}$ | corrects for upstream velocity |
| 7 | **Orifice plate bore** | $d_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \, Q \, \sqrt{1-\beta^4}}{\pi \, C_d \, \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P / \rho}}}$ | design equation |
| 8 | **Venturi / Orifice volume flow** | $Q = C_d \, A_2 \, K \, \sqrt{2 g h}$ | combining (2) and (6) |
| 9 | **Mass flow (compressible)** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{C_d \, A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{2 \, \rho_1 \, \Delta P}$ | $\rho_1$ upstream density |
| 10 | **Compressible flow correction** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{C_d \, A_2 \, \varepsilon}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{2 \, \rho_1 \, \Delta P}$ | $\varepsilon$ = expansivity factor (< 1) |
| 11 | **Pitot tube point velocity** | $V = \sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P / \rho}$ | from $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ |
| 12 | **Reynolds number (pipe)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, V \, D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V \, D}{\nu}$ | $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity |
| 13 | **Target meter drag force** | $F = C_d \, \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A_T$ | $A_T$ = target disc area |
| 14 | **Target meter velocity** | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 F}{C_d \rho A_T}}$ | rearranged |
| 15 | **Manometer equation (U-tube)** | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{(\rho_m - \rho_f) \, h_m}{\rho_f}$ | $\rho_m$ manometric fluid, $h_m$ deflection |

---

## 2.3 Expansion Factor $\varepsilon$ (Compressible Fluids)

For gases and steam, density changes between taps. The **expansivity factor** is:

$$\varepsilon \;=\; 1 - \left(0.41 + 0.35 \beta^4\right) \frac{\Delta P}{\kappa \, P_1}$$

where $\kappa$ = ratio of specific heats $C_p / C_v$.

> When $\Delta P / P_1 < 0.05$, $\varepsilon \approx 1$ (incompressible approximation holds).

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of DP Flow Meters

| Industry / Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Oil & Gas custody transfer** | Orifice + flow computer for gas pipelines (AGA 3 / ISO 5167) |
| **Power plants** | Venturi / Flow Nozzle in feedwater, condensate, main steam lines |
| **Water utilities** | Venturi in raw water mains (low loss) |
| **Chemicals** | Orifice / Dall tube in reactor feed lines |
| **HVAC** | Pitot-static probe for duct balancing, building air measurement |
| **Pulp & Paper** | Target meter for stock (fibrous slurry) |
| **Aerospace** | Pitot tube as primary airspeed sensor |
| **Steel & Cement** | Target meter for high-temp gas flow in kilns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world rule of thumb:** Use **Venturi** for low-loss, large-pipe, clean service; use **Orifice** for cost-sensitive, small-to-medium pipe, clean service; use **Flow Nozzle** for high-temperature steam; use **Dall Tube** for very large-diameter short-run installations; use **Target** for dirty, slurry, or corrosive service; use **Pitot** for duct traverse and airspeed.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General DP Flow Meter Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU exam ‚Äî it is the foundation for Venturi, Orifice, and Flow Nozzle problems.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Start with Bernoulli's Equation (between section 1 = upstream, section 2 = throat)

For a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$), incompressible, steady, frictionless flow:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$

Rearrange to isolate the velocity term:

$$\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho}$$

$$\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{\rho}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the Continuity Equation

$$A_1 V_1 \;=\; A_2 V_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V_1 \;=\; \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute $V_1$ in the Bernoulli Result

$$V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2 \;=\; \frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}$$

Factor out $V_2^2$:

$$V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right] \;=\; \frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Solve for the Throat Velocity $V_2$

$$V_2^2 \;=\; \frac{2 \Delta P / \rho}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$$

$$V_2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

Recognise that $A_2 / A_1 = (d_2/d_1)^2 = \beta^2$, so $(A_2/A_1)^2 = \beta^4$:

$$\boxed{\,V_2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}\,}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Multiply by Throat Area to get Theoretical Volumetric Flow

$$Q_{th} \;=\; A_2 \, V_2 \;=\; \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Introduce the Discharge Coefficient $C_d$

The actual flow is **less** than theoretical because of **friction losses, vena-contracta formation, and turbulence**. We introduce a correction factor $C_d$ (between 0.6 and 1.0):

$$\boxed{\,Q_{actual} \;=\; C_d \, \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical $C_d$ values for KTU problems:**
> - Venturi: $C_d = 0.95$‚Äì$0.99$
> - Orifice (sharp-edged): $C_d = 0.60$‚Äì$0.65$
> - Flow Nozzle: $C_d = 0.95$‚Äì$0.99$
> - Dall Tube: $C_d = 0.65$‚Äì$0.75$

### Step 7 ‚Äî Rewrite Using Head $h$

Since $\Delta P = \rho g h$:

$$Q \;=\; C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1 - \beta^4}}$$

> This is the **most-tested KTU form** ‚Äî commit it to memory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Pitot Tube Velocity Equation

### Step 1 ‚Äî Apply Bernoulli at a point in the flow (1) and at the stagnation point (2)

At the stagnation point, $V_2 = 0$ and $P_2 = P_t$ (total pressure):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho} + 0$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Recognise $P_1 = P_s$ (static pressure), $P_2 = P_t$

$$\frac{P_t - P_s}{\rho} \;=\; \frac{V_1^2}{2}$$

$$P_t - P_s \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \rho V_1^2$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for $V_1$

$$\boxed{\,V_1 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_t - P_s)}{\rho}} \;=\; \sqrt{2 g h}\,}$$

> **Pitot tube does not require $C_d$ or $\beta$** ‚Äî it measures the point velocity directly. This is a KTU favourite trick question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Manometer Head Equation (U-Tube)

For a differential manometer connected to a DP flow meter:

$$\Delta P \;=\; P_1 - P_2 \;=\; (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h_m$$

where:
- $\rho_m$ = density of manometric fluid (mercury, water, oil)
- $\rho_f$ = density of flowing fluid
- $h_m$ = manometer deflection (in metres)

For **mercury‚Äìwater** combination ($\rho_m \approx 13600 \,\text{kg/m}^3$):

$$h \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_f g} \;=\; \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1\right) h_m \;=\; \left(\frac{13600}{1000} - 1\right) h_m \;=\; 12.6 \, h_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mercury manometer trick:** $h = 12.6 \, h_m$ for water, and $h \approx 0.12 \, h_m$ for air at STP.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Target Flow Meter Drag Equation

A flat disc of area $A_T$ placed perpendicular to flow. The drag force is given by the standard fluid mechanics formula:

$$F_D \;=\; C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A_T$$

Solving for velocity:

$$V \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 F_D}{C_D \, \rho \, A_T}}$$

Volumetric flow:

$$Q \;=\; V \cdot A_{pipe} \;=\; A_{pipe} \sqrt{\frac{2 F_D}{C_D \, \rho \, A_T}}$$

**Disc (target) diameter $d_T$ is chosen smaller than pipe** ‚Äî typically $d_T = 0.7 \, d_1$ to give a measurable drag.

---

## 3.5 KTU Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A Venturi meter has an inlet diameter of $200$ mm and throat diameter of $100$ mm. The differential pressure across the meter is **$60$ kPa** when the flowing fluid is water ($\rho = 1000 \,\text{kg/m}^3$). Take $C_d = 0.98$. Calculate the volumetric flow rate.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $d_1 = 0.200$ m, $d_2 = 0.100$ m
- $\Delta P = 60{,}000$ Pa, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m¬≥
- $C_d = 0.98$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute $\beta$**

$$\beta = \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{0.100}{0.200} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute $\beta^4$**

$$\beta^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute throat area $A_2$**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute velocity-of-approach factor**

$$\sqrt{1-\beta^4} = \sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9683$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Apply the flow equation**

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 60{,}000}{1000}} = \sqrt{120} = 10.954 \,\text{m/s}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9683} \times 10.954$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times 8.110 \times 10^{-3} \times 10.954$$

$$\boxed{\,Q \approx 0.0870 \,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \;=\; 87 \,\text{L/s}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Tip:** A full-marks derivation must include the Bernoulli step, continuity substitution, solving for $V_2$, multiplying by $A_2$, and introducing $C_d$ ‚Äî **all five steps are necessary**.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Performance Matrix of DP Meters

| Property | Venturi | Orifice | Flow Nozzle | Dall Tube | Pitot | Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_d$ | 0.95‚Äì0.99 | 0.60‚Äì0.65 | 0.95‚Äì0.99 | 0.65‚Äì0.75 | ‚âà 1.0 | ~0.8 |
| Permanent $\Delta P$ loss | 10‚Äì15% | 40‚Äì60% | 30‚Äì50% | 3‚Äì5% | Very low | Moderate |
| Cost | High | Low | Medium | High | Low | Medium |
| Accuracy | ¬±1% | ¬±2‚Äì3% | ¬±1.5% | ¬±1.5% | ¬±2‚Äì3% | ¬±2‚Äì3% |
| Rangeability | 4:1 | 3‚Äì4:1 | 4:1 | 4:1 | Wide (point) | 4:1 |
| Dirty fluids | Poor | Limited (eccentric OK) | Limited | Limited | Poor | **Excellent** |

| Slurry service | No | Eccentric only | No | No | No | **Best** |
| Length required | $5 D_1$ to $8 D_1$ | $2 D_1$ to $3 D_1$ | $2 D_1$ to $3 D_1$ | $1 D_1$ to $2 D_1$ | Point | $3 D_1$ to $5 D_1$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Venturi Meter ‚Äî Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Pipe Section 1<br/>Diameter D1, Pressure P1, Velocity V1] --> B[Converging Cone<br/>Angle 21 degrees]
    B --> C[Throat Section<br/>Diameter D2, Pressure P2, Velocity V2<br/>D2 less than D1]
    C --> D[Diverging Cone<br/>Angle 5 to 7 degrees]
    D --> E[Outlet Pipe<br/>Pressure P3 approximately P1]
    F[Upstream Pressure Tap<br/>at Inlet] --> G[Differential Manometer<br/>or DP Transmitter]
    H[Throat Pressure Tap<br/>at Throat] --> G
    G --> I[Flow Rate Q Display<br/>or Control Output]
```

## 4.2 Orifice Meter ‚Äî Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Pipe<br/>Diameter D1, Velocity V1] --> B[Sharp Edged Orifice Plate<br/>Concentric hole of diameter d2<br/>Thickness 3 to 6 mm]
    B --> C[Vena Contracta<br/>Minimum cross section<br/>Diameter d2 actual less than d2 plate]
    C --> D[Recovered Flow<br/>Downstream Pipe]
    E[Upstream Pressure Tap<br/>Flange or D and D half tap] --> F[Manometer Lines<br/>filled with same fluid]
    G[Downstream Pressure Tap<br/>Flange or D and D half tap] --> F
    F --> H[DP Cell with Square Root Extractor]
    H --> I[Flow Indicator or Recorder]
```

## 4.3 Pitot Tube ‚Äî Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flowing Fluid Stream<br/>Static Pressure Ps, Total Pressure Pt] --> B[Stagnation Tube<br/>Open end facing flow<br/>Measures Pt]
    A --> C[Static Tube<br/>Side holes on cylindrical surface<br/>Measures Ps]
    B --> D[Differential Manometer<br/>Reads Pt minus Ps equals Pd]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Compute Point Velocity<br/>V equals square root of 2 g h]
    E --> F[Traverse across Duct<br/>10 point log Chebyshev method]
    F --> G[Average Velocity Vbar]
    G --> H[Flow Q equals Vbar times A duct]
```

## 4.4 Flow Nozzle and Target Meter ‚Äî Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Flow<br/>Pressure P1, Velocity V1] --> B[Long Radius Nozzle<br/>ISA 1932 Nozzle<br/>Smooth contoured entry]
    B --> C[Throat Cylinder<br/>Diameter d2, Pressure P2]
    C --> D[Exit to Pipe<br/>No divergent cone]
    A2[Target Meter Disc<br/>Area AT, Diameter 0.7 D1] --> A3[Force Sensor<br/>Load Cell or Strain Gauge]
    A3 --> A4[Force F equals Cd half rho V squared AT]
    A4 --> A5[Square Root Extractor]
    A5 --> A6[Flow Display Q]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    A --> E[Pipe Taps<br/>2.5 D1 upstream and 8 D1 downstream]

```
