## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Diaphragm Stem Position vs. Signal Pressure

**Given:**
- Effective diaphragm area $A_d = 300 \; \text{cm}^2 = 0.03 \; \text{m}^2$
- Spring constant $k = 30{,}000 \; \text{N/m}$
- Input signal pressure $\Delta P = 0.6 \; \text{bar} = 60{,}000 \; \text{Pa}$

**Step 1: Compute the thrust force generated by the signal pressure on the diaphragm.**

$$F_{thrust} = \Delta P \cdot A_d$$

$$F_{thrust} = 60{,}000 \;\text{Pa} \times 0.03 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$F_{thrust} = 1{,}800 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law for the spring.**

$$F_{spring} = k \cdot x$$

**Step 3: Apply force-balance equilibrium.**

The diaphragm pushes the stem against the spring. At equilibrium, the two forces are equal:

$$\Delta P \cdot A_d = k \cdot x$$

**Step 4: Solve for stem displacement $x$.**

$$x = \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_d}{k}$$

$$x = \frac{1{,}800 \;\text{N}}{30{,}000 \;\text{N/m}}$$

$$x = 0.06 \;\text{m} = 60 \;\text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A signal of 0.6 bar drives the stem 60 mm. If the maximum signal is 1.0 bar and the spring is sized so that 0.2 bar = 0 mm and 1.0 bar = full stroke, the same calculation gives the full-stroke thrust and validates the spring's design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Hydraulic Cylinder Force

**Given:**
- Hydraulic supply pressure $P_{hyd} = 100 \; \text{bar} = 10 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa}$
- Bore diameter $D = 80 \; \text{mm} = 0.08 \; \text{m}$
- Rod diameter $d = 45 \; \text{mm} = 0.045 \; \text{m}$
- Back pressure $P_{back} = 5 \; \text{bar} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa}$

**Step 1: Calculate the bore cross-sectional area.**

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{bore} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the rod cross-sectional area.**

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi (0.045)^2}{4}$$

$$A_{rod} = 1.590 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate the net (annulus) area on the retraction side.**

$$A_{annulus} = A_{bore} - A_{rod}$$

$$A_{annulus} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} - 1.590 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$A_{annulus} = 3.437 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Force during extension (pressure on full bore, back on annulus).**

$$F_{extend} = P_{hyd} \cdot A_{bore} - P_{back} \cdot A_{annulus}$$

$$F_{extend} = (10 \times 10^6)(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.5 \times 10^6)(3.437 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{extend} = 50{,}270 - 1{,}718.5$$

$$F_{extend} = 48{,}551.5 \;\text{N} \approx 48.55 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 5: Force during retraction (pressure on annulus, back on bore side).**

$$F_{retract} = P_{hyd} \cdot A_{annulus} - P_{back} \cdot A_{bore}$$

$$F_{retract} = (10 \times 10^6)(3.437 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.5 \times 10^6)(5.027 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{retract} = 34{,}370 - 2{,}513.5$$

$$F_{retract} = 31{,}856.5 \;\text{N} \approx 31.86 \;\text{kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** $F_{extend} \neq F_{retract}$ because the rod occupies area on one side. This asymmetry is critical in sizing hydraulic actuators for control valves ‚Äî always compute both directions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Solenoid Pull Force

**Given:**
- Air-gap flux density $B = 1.2 \; \text{T}$
- Air-gap cross-sectional area $A_g = 2 \; \text{cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2$
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \text{H/m}$

**Step 1: Apply the magnetic force equation for a solenoid.**

$$F_{mag} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A_g}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values.**

$$F_{mag} = \frac{(1.2)^2 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$F_{mag} = \frac{1.44 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{8\pi \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$F_{mag} = \frac{2.88 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F_{mag} \approx 114.6 \;\text{N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A modest flux density of 1.2 T in a 2 cm¬≤ gap produces 114.6 N of pull ‚Äî enough for small process solenoid valves. Increasing $B$ to 1.5 T (better steel, more coil turns) raises the force to ~179 N, demonstrating the **quadratic sensitivity** to flux density.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Stroking Time & Air Consumption

**Given:**
- Diaphragm diameter $D_d = 200 \; \text{mm} = 0.2 \; \text{m}$
- Stroke $S = 30 \; \text{mm} = 0.03 \; \text{m}$
- Supply pressure $P_{supply} = 1.4 \; \text{bar (gauge)}$

**Step 1: Volume displaced per stroke (chamber volume).**

$$V_{stroke} = \frac{\pi D_d^2}{4} \cdot S$$

$$V_{stroke} = \frac{\pi (0.2)^2}{4} \times 0.03$$

$$V_{stroke} = 0.03142 \times 0.03$$

$$V_{stroke} = 9.425 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3 \approx 0.943 \;\text{L}$$

**Step 2: Mass of air consumed (using ideal gas at supply conditions).**

Assuming air at 1.4 bar (gauge) = 2.4 bar absolute, temperature $T = 293 \; \text{K}$, $R_{air} = 287 \; \text{J/(kg¬∑K)}$:

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$$

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^5}{287 \times 293}$$

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{240{,}000}{84{,}091}$$

$$\rho_{air} = 2.854 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Mass per stroke.**

$$m_{stroke} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_{stroke}$$

$$m_{stroke} = 2.854 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{stroke} = 2.69 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg} = 2.69 \;\text{g per stroke}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** For 60 strokes per minute, hourly air consumption is $2.69 \times 60 \times 60 = 9.68 \; \text{kg/hr}$, which the air-compressor sizing calculations must account for.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚Äî Actuator Selection

| Selection Criterion | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Electric (Solenoid) | Electric (Motor) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thrust Available** | Medium (‚â§ 25 kN) | **Very High (‚â§ 1000 kN)** | Low (‚â§ 500 N) | Medium‚ÄìHigh |
| **Stroke Speed** | Fast (0.5‚Äì5 s) | Medium (1‚Äì10 s) | **Very Fast (< 0.1 s)** | Slow‚ÄìMedium |
| **Positioning Accuracy** | ¬±1% | ¬±0.5% | ON/OFF only | **¬±0.1% (servo)** |
| **Intrinsic Safety** | **Yes (air = safe)** | Yes (oil) | No (sparks) | No |
| **Fail-Safe on Power Loss** | **Yes (spring)** | Yes (spring) | No (needs UPS) | No |
| **Cost (Low Power)** | Low | High | **Lowest** | Medium |
| **Maintenance** | Low (air leaks) | High (oil leaks) | **Very Low** | Medium |
| **Hazardous Area Use** | **Preferred** | Preferred | Restricted | Restricted |
| **Modulating Control** | Yes (with I/P) | Yes | No | **Yes (with servo)** |
| **Communication** | 3‚Äì15 psi only | 4‚Äì20 mA possible | None (binary) | **HART / Fieldbus** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trend:** In recent papers, examiners have asked students to *justify* the actuator choice for a given process. Use the matrix above to construct a clear, criterion-by-criterion justification.

---

## 3.6 Failure Mode & Fail-Safe Action

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *action* with *failure mode*.

| Valve Action | Signal at 0.2 bar | Signal at 1.0 bar | Signal Lost (Air/Pressure) | Safe Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Air-to-Close (ATC)** | Stem at top ‚Üí Valve **Open** | Stem at bottom ‚Üí Valve **Closed** | Spring pushes stem to **Open** | **Fail-Open** |
| **Air-to-Open (ATO)** | Stem at bottom ‚Üí Valve **Closed** | Stem at top ‚Üí Valve **Open** | Spring pushes stem to **Closed** | **Fail-Closed** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** For cooling water valves, choose **fail-open** (ATC) ‚Äî if air fails, the cooling water continues to flow. For fuel valves, choose **fail-closed** (ATO) ‚Äî if air fails, fuel is shut off to prevent over-heating/explosion.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Position of Actuator in Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Process Variable] --> procB[Sensor / Transmitter]
    procB --> procC[Controller / PLC]
    procC -- 4-20 mA / 3-15 psi --> procD[I-P or V-P Converter]
    procD -- 0.2-1.0 bar --> procE[Actuator]
    procE -- Mechanical Motion --> procF[Final Control Element Valve / Damper]
    procF --> procG[Process Plant]
    procG --> procA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The actuator (procE) is the *only* element that converts a *signal* into *mechanical motion*. Every element before it deals in signals; every element after it deals in motion and process fluids.

---

## 4.2 Schematic ‚Äî Spring-and-Diaphragm Pneumatic Actuator (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    chamber1[Upper Diaphragm Chamber] --> diag1[Elastomer Diaphragm]
    diag1 -- Bonded --> stem1[Actuator Stem]
    spring1[Range Spring] -- Opposes --> stem1
    stem1 --> out1[To Valve Plug]
    air1[Signal 0.2 to 1.0 bar] --> chamber1
    supp1[Supply 1.4 bar] --> chamber1
    vent1[Exhaust Vent] --> chamber1
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic ‚Äî Solenoid Actuator (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    coilA[Coil Winding] -- energised --> fluxA[Magnetic Flux Lines]
    fluxA --> gapA[Air Gap]
    gapA -- Pull Force --> plungerA[Plunger / Armature]
    plungerA --> motionA[Linear Motion]
    coilA -- de-energised --> springA[Return Spring]
    springA --> motionA
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic ‚Äî Motorized Valve Actuator (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    motorA[AC or DC Motor] -- Shaft --> gearA[Gear Reducer]
    gearA -- Torque x eta --> outA[Output Shaft]
    outA --> stemA[Valve Stem]
    fbA[Position Feedback Potentiometer / Encoder] --> fbB[Feedback Signal]
    fbB --> ctrlA[Local Controller / Smart Positioner]
    ctrlA -- 4-20 mA / HART --> ctrlA
    ctrlA -- Commands --> motorA
```

---

## 4.5 Functional Block Diagram ‚Äî Hydraulic Actuator System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pumpA[Hydraulic Pump] -- High-Pressure Oil --> valveA[Directional Control Valve]
    valveA -- Forward / Reverse --> cylA[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    cylA -- Piston Rod --> loadA[Valve Plug / Load]
    tankA[Reservoir / Tank] -- Return Oil --> pumpA
    cylA -- Return Oil --> tankA
    ctrlA[Solenoid / Proportional Valve Driver] --> valveA
    ctrlA -- Commands --> ctrlA
```

---

## 4.6 Decision Tree ‚Äî Actuator Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA{What is the<br/>primary energy<br/>available?}
    startA -- Compressed Air --> pneumA[Pneumatic Actuator]
    startA -- High-Pressure Oil --> hydA[Hydraulic Actuator]
    startA -- Electric Power --> elecA{Modulating<br/>or ON/OFF?}
    elecA -- ON/OFF --> solA[Solenoid Actuator]
    elecA -- Modulating --> motA{Motorised or<br/>Servo?}
    motA -- Standard --> motB[Motor + Gearbox]
    motA -- Precision --> motC[Servo or Stepper]
    pneumA --> chkA{Required Thrust?}
    chkA -- Low to Medium --> diaA[Diaphragm Type]
    chkA -- High --> pisA[Piston Type]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** When the KTU question asks *"Select an actuator for an oil refinery control valve handling high-pressure steam"*, walk through this decision tree step-by-step. Examiners award process marks for the *reasoning*, not just the final answer.

---

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]

**Define an actuator. List any four types of actuators based on the energy source used.**

**Model Answer:**

An **actuator** is the final control element in a process control loop that converts a control signal (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, or digital) from the controller into a proportional mechanical motion or force, which in turn manipulates the process variable by moving a valve plug, damper, or similar element.

**Four types based on energy source:**

1. **Pneumatic actuator** ‚Äî uses compressed air (signal 3‚Äì15 psi or 0.2‚Äì1.0 bar).
2. **Hydraulic actuator** ‚Äî uses pressurised hydraulic oil (35‚Äì210 bar).
3. **Electrical actuator (solenoid)** ‚Äî uses electromagnetism to produce linear motion.
4. **Motorised (electromechanical) actuator** ‚Äî uses AC/DC/stepper/servo motor with gear train.

*(Any four accepted. Add a fifth example for 1 extra mark.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]

**Explain the working principle of a spring-and-diaphragm pneumatic actuator with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

A spring-and-diaphragm pneumatic actuator operates on the **force-balance principle**.

1. A control signal (0.2‚Äì1.0 bar / 3‚Äì15 psi) from an I/P converter is admitted into the **upper diaphragm chamber**.
2. The signal pressure acts on the **effective diaphragm area** $A_d$, generating a thrust $F = \Delta P \cdot A_d$.
3. This thrust pushes the **diaphragm-stem assembly** downward against a calibrated **range spring**.
4. The spring compresses, generating an opposing force $F_{spring} = k \cdot x$.
5. At **equilibrium**, $\Delta P \cdot A_d = k \cdot x$, giving a linear relationship between signal pressure and stem position.

**Features:** Simple, intrinsically safe, fail-safe (spring returns stem on air loss), and widely used in process industries for globe and butterfly valves.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the force-balance equation for a spring-and-diaphragm pneumatic actuator. Show that the stem displacement is linearly proportional to the input signal pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pneumatic diaphragm actuator has an effective diaphragm diameter of $250 \; \text{mm}$ and a spring constant of $50{,}000 \; \text{N/m}$. If the signal pressure varies from 0.2 bar to 1.0 bar, calculate: (i) the thrust at full signal, (ii) the total stem stroke, and (iii) the thrust at 0.6 bar signal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a)

The signal pressure $\Delta P$ acts on the diaphragm of effective area $A_d$, producing a thrust:

$$F_{thrust} = \Delta P \cdot A_d \quad \text{...(1)}$$

This thrust pushes the stem against the range spring. The spring force opposing motion is given by Hooke's Law:

$$F_{spring} = k \cdot x \quad \text{...(2)}$$

At steady-state equilibrium, the net force on the diaphragm-stem assembly is zero:

$$\Delta P \cdot A_d = k \cdot x \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Solving for stem displacement $x$:

$$x = \frac{A_d}{k} \cdot \Delta P \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Since $A_d$ and $k$ are constants for a given actuator, $x$ is **linearly proportional** to $\Delta P$. This linear relationship is the foundation of predictable modulating control using pneumatic actuators.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b)

**Given:**
- $D_d = 250 \; \text{mm} = 0.25 \; \text{m}$
- $k = 50{,}000 \; \text{N/m}$
- $\Delta P_{min} = 0.2 \; \text{bar} = 20{,}000 \; \text{Pa}$
- $\Delta P_{max} = 1.0 \; \text{bar} = 100{,}000 \; \text{Pa}$

**Step 1: Diaphragm area.**

$$A_d = \frac{\pi D_d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.25)^2}{4} = 0.04909 \; \text{m}^2$$

**(i) Thrust at full signal (1.0 bar):**

$$F_{max} = \Delta P_{max} \cdot A_d = 100{,}000 \times 0.04909$$

$$F_{max} = 4{,}909 \; \text{N} \approx 4.91 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 2: Total stem stroke (from 0.2 bar to 1.0 bar).**

The *change* in thrust over the signal range:

$$\Delta F = (\Delta P_{max} - \Delta P_{min}) \cdot A_d$$

$$\Delta F = (100{,}000 - 20{,}000) \times 0.04909$$

$$\Delta F = 80{,}000 \times 0.04909 = 3{,}927.2 \; \text{N}$$

Corresponding stem stroke:

$$S = \frac{\Delta F}{k} = \frac{3{,}927.2}{50{,}000}$$

$$S = 0.0785 \; \text{m} \approx 78.5 \; \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Thrust at 0.6 bar signal:**

$$F_{0.6} = \Delta P \cdot A_d = 60{,}000 \times 0.04909$$

$$F_{0.6} = 2{,}945.4 \; \text{N} \approx 2.95 \; \text{kN}$$

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Thrust at 1.0 bar | 4.91 kN |
| Total Stroke | 78.5 mm |
| Thrust at 0.6 bar | 2.95 kN |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) ‚Äî [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a solenoid actuator. Derive the expression for the magnetic pull force. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare pneumatic, hydraulic, and electrical actuators based on thrust, speed, accuracy, fail-safe action, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a)

**Construction:**

A solenoid actuator consists of:
1. **Coil winding** ‚Äî many turns of insulated copper wire wound on a bobbin.
2. **Iron core / yoke** ‚Äî provides a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux.
3. **Plunger (armature)** ‚Äî movable soft-iron piece inside the coil.
4. **Air gap** ‚Äî small space between the plunger and the core face.
5. **Return spring** ‚Äî pushes the plunger back when the coil is de-energised.

**Working:**

- When the coil is **energised** (current flows), it generates a magnetic flux $\Phi$ that travels through the core, across the air gap, and back. The flux density $B$ in the gap exerts an attractive force on the plunger.
- The plunger is pulled into the coil, performing useful work (e.g., opening a valve).
- When the coil is **de-energised**, the magnetic force vanishes, and the return spring restores the plunger to its rest position.

**Derivation of Magnetic Pull Force:**

The energy stored in the air gap of a magnetic circuit is:

$$W = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot A_g \cdot \ell$$

The force is the rate of change of energy with respect to the air-gap length (when flux is held constant, virtual work principle):

$$F = \frac{dW}{d\ell} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A_g}{2 \mu_0}$$

Since $B = \mu_0 \cdot H$ and $H \propto I$ (ampere-turns), we have:

$$\boxed{F_{mag} = \frac{B^2 A_g}{2\mu_0} \propto I^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Because force is proportional to the square of current, a solenoid provides a *non-linear* force‚Äìcurrent relationship. This makes solenoids ideal for **ON/OFF** applications (snap action) but unsuitable for **proportional** control.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b)

| Parameter | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Electrical (Motorized) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thrust** | Medium (‚â§ 25 kN) | Very High (‚â§ 1000 kN) | High (varies with motor) |
| **Speed** | Fast (0.5‚Äì5 s) | Medium (1‚Äì10 s) | Variable (depends on gearing) |
| **Accuracy** | ¬±1% | ¬±0.5% | ¬±0.1% (servo) |
| **Fail-Safe** | **Yes** (spring returns stem) | Yes (spring / accumulator) | No (requires UPS / brake) |
| **Intrinsic Safety** | **Yes** (air is non-flammable) | Yes (oil) | No (electrical sparks) |
| **Modulating Control** | Yes (with I/P + positioner) | Yes | Yes (with positioner) |
| **Typical Applications** | Refinery control valves, dampers | High-$\Delta P$ valves, forging presses | Power plant, water treatment, HVAC |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚Äî Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "fail-safe" with "action":** Many students write "fail-open" but forget to specify the *signal direction* (air-to-close vs. air-to-open). Always state both.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the force-balance derivation:** Examiners specifically look for "$\text{Pa} \times \text{m}^2 = \text{N}$" dimensional consistency.
> 3. **Using $D$ instead of $A_d$:** The formula $F = \Delta P \cdot A_d$ requires the *effective* area, not the diameter. A common 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Neglecting rod area in hydraulic force:** The retraction force is *not* the same as the extension force ‚Äî students who write a single force equation lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Writing "solenoid is proportional":** It is NOT. It is a square-law device. This is a 2-mark conceptual error in many papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî KTU Module 4 (Actuator)**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] Actuator = final control element converting signal ‚Üí mechanical motion.
- [ ] Pneumatic actuator = air-driven (3‚Äì15 psi / 0.2‚Äì1.0 bar).
- [ ] Hydraulic actuator = oil-driven (35‚Äì210 bar).
- [ ] Solenoid = ON/OFF electromagnetic device; force ‚àù $I^2$.
- [ ] Motorized actuator = AC/DC/stepper/servo + gear train + positioner.

### Must-Know Equations
- [ ] $F_{diaphragm} = \Delta P \cdot A_d$ ‚Äî most-tested formula.
- [ ] $x = \dfrac{A_d}{k} \cdot \Delta P$ ‚Äî stem position linearity.
- [ ] $F_{hyd} = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ ‚Äî hydraulic extension.
- [ ] $F_{retract} = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ ‚Äî hydraulic retraction.
- [ ] $F_{mag} = \dfrac{B^2 A_g}{2 \mu_0}$ ‚Äî solenoid force.
- [ ] $\tau_{out} = G \cdot \tau_m \cdot \eta$ ‚Äî gear torque.

### Critical Concepts
- [ ] **Force-balance** principle = foundation of every diaphragm analysis.
- [ ] **Spring-return** = default fail-safe for pneumatic/hydraulic.
- [ ] **Air-to-Close vs. Air-to-Open** = determines fail-safe direction.
- [ ] **Stepper motor** = open-loop positioning; **servo motor** = closed-loop.
- [ ] **I/P converter** = 4‚Äì20 mA ‚Üí 3‚Äì15 psi (needed before pneumatic actuator in electronic loops).
- [ ] **Positioner** = local feedback loop that ensures stem position matches controller signal accurately.

### Real-World Links
- [ ] Refineries ‚Üí pneumatic diaphragm (most common).
- [ ] Power plants ‚Üí hydraulic (high-$\Delta P$ feedwater valves).
- [ ] Domestic appliances ‚Üí solenoid (washing machine, dishwasher).
- [ ] CNC / robotics ‚Üí servo motor + encoder.
- [ ] HVAC dampers ‚Üí electric motorized (BACnet / Modbus).

### Examiner's "Watch Out" List
- [ ] Always state units in the final answer.
- [ ] Always use $A_d$ (effective area), not $D_d$ (diameter), in the force formula.
- [ ] Never confuse "action" with "failure mode."
- [ ] For solenoid: $F \propto I^2$, *not* $F \propto I$.
- [ ] For hydraulic: extension force ‚âÝ retraction force (rod area matters).
- [ ] Stroke time = $\frac{V}{Q}$; show the volume calculation explicitly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** In KTU viva voce, expect a follow-up: *"Why is pneumatic preferred in oil refineries despite its lower thrust?"* ‚Äî Answer: *Intrinsic safety* (no spark risk), *fail-safe by spring*, *low cost*, and *ease of maintenance*. The single word **"intrinsic safety"** is often the difference between full marks and 0.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Packing (Control Valve Packing)

In the domain of Industrial Instrumentation and Final Control Elements, **Packing** refers to the engineered sealing assembly installed in the **bonnet (gland) region** of a control valve to provide a pressure-tight seal around the **valve stem (or shaft)** as it reciprocates to modulate fluid flow. The packing prevents the leakage of the process medium to the atmosphere while allowing smooth axial motion of the stem with minimal friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Packing is a critical interface element located between the bonnet and the valve body, designed to control fugitive emissions, maintain process integrity, and ensure operator safety in a pneumatically/hydraulically actuated control valve assembly.

Mathematically, the packing region can be described by its **location coordinate `y`**, extending from the bonnet flange (`y = 0`) to the bottom of the packing box (`y = L_p`), where the **stem diameter `d_s`** and the **packing inner diameter `D_i`** form the annular sealing gap.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ‚Äî The "Straw in a Cup" Concept

Imagine inserting a **straw (stem)** through the **lid (bonnet/packing box)** of a **sealed cup of water (process fluid inside the valve body)**.

- If the hole in the lid is too loose, water sprays out every time you push the straw down ‚Äî this is **fugitive emission** (process fluid leakage).
- If the hole is too tight, you cannot move the straw ‚Äî this is **stem binding** (excessive friction, valve becomes inoperable).

**Packing is the "smart gasket" wrapped around the straw** ‚Äî it squeezes gently to seal the water, yet slips smoothly when the straw moves. The right balance between **sealing** and **sliding friction** is the entire engineering problem of valve packing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Packing Matters in Industry:**
> - Prevents loss of expensive/hazardous process fluids.
> - Reduces environmental emissions (compliance with **EPA Leak Detection and Repair / LDAR** norms).
> - Protects operators from toxic or flammable leaks.
> - Maintains stem positioner accuracy by keeping friction low and predictable.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Memorize These for KTU)

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Packing Pressure (vs. Line Pressure) | **3 √ó to 5 √ó** line pressure | bar / psi |
| Stem Friction Coefficient (Œº) | **0.05 to 0.20** (PTFE) ; **0.10 to 0.30** (graphite) | dimensionless |
| Standard Packing Height `L_p` | **25 to 75** (typical) | mm |
| Maximum Allowable Fugitive Emissions | **< 100 ppm** (modern standards) | ppm |
| Operating Temperature Range (PTFE) | **-200 ¬∞C to +260 ¬∞C** | ¬∞C |
| Operating Temperature Range (Graphite) | **-200 ¬∞C to +1000 ¬∞C** (inert atm.) | ¬∞C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Functions of a Packing Assembly

1. **Primary Sealing:** Restrict the leakage of the process fluid along the stem to an acceptable limit (typically a few bubbles per minute in critical services, near-zero in modern low-emission designs).
2. **Allowing Stem Motion:** Permit reciprocating (and in some valves, rotary) motion of the stem with **minimum and stable friction**.
3. **Chemical Compatibility:** Resist the corrosive/erosive action of the process fluid.
4. **Thermal Endurance:** Withstand the process temperature without degradation.
5. **Mechanical Stability:** Resist extrusion, blow-out, and extrusion into the stem-bore gap under thermal cycling.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Packing Assembly

The complete packing stack (from atmosphere side to process side) consists of:

- **Packing Follower / Gland Bushing:** The thrust element that transmits the load from the packing nut to the packing rings.
- **Packing Rings (the actual seal):** Typically 3 to 6 rings stacked axially. Each ring is a preformed shape.
- **Lantern Ring / Tell-Tale Ring:** A spacer ring with a side-port connection, used to:
  - Inject a **barrier fluid** (purge) to prevent leakage of hazardous service fluids.
  - Detect leakage via a vent port.
- **Bottom Wiper Ring / Anti-extrusion Ring:** Protects the packing from being extruded into the stem-bore gap under high pressure.
- **Packing Nut / Gland Flange:** The threaded member that pre-loads (compresses) the packing stack.

## 2.3 Types of Packing ‚Äî KTU High-Yield Classification

### A. By Configuration / Geometry

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression Packing (Braided / Die-Formed)** | Rings of square/rectangular cross-section squeezed axially | General purpose, slurries, steam |
| **V-Ring Packing (Chevron / Vee-Set)** | Stacked V-shaped elastomeric rings, energized by a spring | High-pressure hydraulic service, low-friction |
| **Lip Packing (U-Cup)** | U-shaped elastomeric ring with a flexible lip | Unidirectional sealing, pneumatic actuators |
| **O-Ring (single elastomer ring)** | Toroidal elastomer seated in a groove | Light-duty, low-friction (used in piston actuators) |
| **Live-Loaded Packing** | A Belleville spring or disc-spring stack maintains packing load despite wear/thermal cycling | Toxic / hazardous / low-emission service |

### B. By Material

| Material | Temperature | Chemical Resistance | Friction | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PTFE (Teflon)** | -200 to +260 ¬∞C | Excellent (almost universal) | Very Low | Most common, soft, susceptible to cold-flow |
| **Graphite (flexible graphite)** | -200 to +1000 ¬∞C (inert) | Excellent (except strong oxidizers) | Moderate | High-T, fire-safe |
| **Asbestos** | Up to +500 ¬∞C | Good | Moderate | Largely phased out (health hazard) |
| **Carbon-Fiber Reinforced** | High | Good | Moderate | High-pressure service |
| **Metal (Lead / Copper / Soft Iron)** | Very High | Variable | High | Forged-body valves, high-T gate valves |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Live-Loaded Packing** is the **KTU 2024 high-weightage topic**. Unlike conventional packing whose load decays as the rings wear, live-loading uses a **disc spring (Belleville washer) stack** to maintain a near-constant seating force throughout the life of the packing. This is mandatory in **API 622 / API 624** and **ISO 15848** fugitive-emission services.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Packing Pressure

The packing is intentionally loaded to a pressure **3√ó to 5√ó the maximum line pressure**. The reasoning chain:

- The process pressure acts on the stem tip and tends to push fluid *upward* along the stem.
- A radial sealing force on the packing creates a **radial stress `œÉ_r`** on the stem, which generates the **friction force `F_f`**.
- Higher line pressure ‚Üí requires higher `œÉ_r` ‚Üí more friction ‚Üí the actuator must overcome this friction.
- The trade-off: **Seal Tightness ‚Üî Stem Friction**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_{axial}$$ | Basic friction equation for sliding contact | N |
| 2 | $$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot P_{packing} \cdot A_{contact}$$ | Packing-specific friction (P is radial pressure, A is stem-packing contact area) | N |
| 3 | $$A_{contact} = \pi \cdot d_s \cdot L_p$$ | Lateral contact area between stem and packing | m¬≤ |
| 4 | $$P_{packing} = k \cdot P_{line}$$ | Packing pressure is a multiplier `k` (3‚Äì5) of line pressure | Pa |
| 5 | $$F_{gland\ load} = P_{packing} \cdot A_{eff}$$ | Where `A_eff` is the effective gland cross-section | N |
| 6 | $$C_{v,corrected} = C_{v,nominal} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_{line} - F_{friction}/A_{plug}}{P_{line}}}$$ | Effective valve authority accounting for packing friction | ‚Äî |
| 7 | $$\Delta T_{allowed} = \frac{F_{spring}}{k_{spring} \cdot n}$$ | Belleville spring deflection in live-loaded packing | mm |
| 8 | $$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P_i \cdot D_i}{2 \cdot t}$$ | Hoop stress on packing containment (vessel-type) | Pa |

> **Convention Used Above:** All pressures in **Pa (N/m¬≤)**, forces in **N**, areas in **m¬≤**, lengths in **m**. In KTU problems, you will often see **bar**, **kg/cm¬≤**, or **psi** ‚Äî convert using **1 bar = 10‚Åµ Pa**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical & Refining:** Live-loaded graphite packing on hydrocarbon valves to comply with **EPA Quad-O / 40 CFR Part 60 Subpart VV**.
- **Power Plants:** High-temperature graphite packing for steam turbine bypass and boiler feedwater control valves.
- **Pharmaceutical & Food:** PTFE packing for clean-in-place (CIP) compatibility.
- **Offshore & Subsea:** All-metal packing (Inconel) for sour service (H‚ÇÇS / NACE MR0175).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Packing Friction Force on the Valve Stem

### Given
- Stem diameter `d_s = 25 mm = 0.025 m`
- Packing height (length of seal) `L_p = 40 mm = 0.040 m`
- Line (process) pressure `P_line = 20 bar = 20 √ó 10‚Åµ Pa`
- Packing pressure multiplier `k = 4`
- Friction coefficient between stem and packing `Œº = 0.10`

### To Find
The friction force `F_f` acting on the stem due to packing.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the basic friction law.**

The friction force opposing stem motion equals the coefficient of friction times the normal (radial) load from the packing.

$$F_{f} = \mu \cdot F_{N}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Express the normal (radial) load `F_N` in terms of packing pressure.**

The packing exerts a uniform radial pressure `P_packing` on the cylindrical surface of the stem. The normal force on the stem equals this pressure times the cylindrical contact area.

$$F_{N} = P_{packing} \cdot A_{contact}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the cylindrical contact area.**

For a stem of diameter `d_s` and packing length `L_p`, the area is the circumference times the length.

$$A_{contact} = \pi \cdot d_s \cdot L_p$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$A_{contact} = \pi \cdot 0.025 \cdot 0.040$$

$$A_{contact} = \pi \cdot 0.00100$$

$$A_{contact} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Determine the packing pressure.**

The packing is pre-loaded to a multiple `k` of the line pressure to ensure sealing under all operating transients.

$$P_{packing} = k \cdot P_{line}$$

$$P_{packing} = 4 \cdot 20 \times 10^{5}$$

$$P_{packing} = 80 \times 10^{5} \ \text{Pa} = 8.0 \ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Compute the normal force.**

$$F_{N} = P_{packing} \cdot A_{contact}$$

$$F_{N} = (80 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (3.1416 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{N} = 8.0 \times 10^{6} \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{N} = 25{,}133 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 6 ‚Äî Compute the friction force.**

$$F_{f} = \mu \cdot F_{N}$$

$$F_{f} = 0.10 \cdot 25{,}133$$

$$\boxed{F_{f} = 2{,}513.3 \ \text{N} \ \approx \ 2.51 \ \text{kN}}$$

**Step 7 ‚Äî Interpret the result.**

The actuator must generate an additional **~2.51 kN** of thrust *purely to overcome packing friction* in addition to the hydrodynamic unbalance force. If the actuator sizing neglects this, the valve will suffer **hysteresis** and **dead-band** in tight flow control loops.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gland Follower Load on a Single Packing Ring

### Given
- Effective gland (piston) area `A_g = 1200 \ \text{mm}^2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2`
- Packing pressure `P_packing = 8.0 MPa` (from previous derivation)
- Number of active packing rings `n = 4`

### To Find
The axial load transmitted to the stem side and the load per ring.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ‚Äî Total gland load (Pascal's Law on the gland piston).**

$$F_{gland} = P_{packing} \cdot A_{g}$$

$$F_{gland} = (8.0 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (1.2 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{gland} = 9{,}600 \ \text{N} = 9.6 \ \text{kN}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Load per packing ring (assuming equal sharing).**

$$F_{ring} = \frac{F_{gland}}{n}$$

$$F_{ring} = \frac{9{,}600}{4}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ring} = 2{,}400 \ \text{N} \ \text{per ring}}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Pressure distribution ‚Äî Why use a lantern ring?**

A **lantern ring** (with a side port) is placed in the middle of the stack. It serves three purposes:

- Distributes packing pressure evenly across rings.
- Allows **purge / barrier fluid injection** (for toxic service).
- Acts as a **leak detector** (vents to atmosphere, alarming on emission).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Live-Loaded Belleville Spring Stack Sizing

### Given
- Required packing load to maintain `P_packing = 8.0 MPa`
- Gland area `A_g = 1.2 √ó 10‚Åª¬≥ m¬≤`
- Permissible load loss over 5 years of wear: **15 %**
- Single Belleville spring: load `F_1 = 1500 N` at design deflection; stiffness `k_1 = 200 N/mm`

### To Find
Number of springs in series `n_s` and number in parallel `n_p`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ‚Äî Total required spring force `F_total`.**

$$F_{total} = P_{packing} \cdot A_{g} = 9{,}600 \ \text{N (from Section 3.2)}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Choose springs in parallel to meet the load.**

$$n_p = \left\lceil \frac{F_{total}}{F_1} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{9{,}600}{1500} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.4 \rceil = 7$$

So 7 springs in **parallel** give:

$$F_{parallel} = 7 \cdot 1500 = 10{,}500 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Choose springs in series to absorb wear (deflection travel).**

Wear depth over 5 years: `Œîw = 1.5 mm`. Per-spring deflection capacity `Œ¥_1 = 2.0 mm`.

$$n_s = \left\lceil \frac{\Delta w}{\delta_1} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1.5}{2.0} \right\rceil = 1$$

So **1 spring in series** is sufficient, but to be safe and ensure redundancy against thermal expansion, designers typically use **2 springs in series**.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Final design.**

$$\boxed{n_p = 7 \ \text{(parallel)}, \quad n_s = 2 \ \text{(series)}}$$

Total pack height of spring stack = `2 √ó 2.0 = 4.0 mm`. This is the live-loaded travel that compensates for packing wear.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚Äî Packing Material Trade-Off

| Property | PTFE | Flexible Graphite | Carbon Fiber | Metal (Soft Iron) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Temperature (¬∞C) | **260** | **1000 (inert)** | **650** | **> 600** |
| Chemical Resistance | Excellent | Excellent (ex. strong oxidizers) | Good | Poor (corrodes) |
| Coefficient of Friction | **0.04 ‚Äì 0.10** | **0.10 ‚Äì 0.20** | **0.10 ‚Äì 0.18** | **0.20 ‚Äì 0.40** |
| Cold Flow / Creep | High | Low | Low | None |
| Cost (relative) | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| Fire-Safe | No | Yes (with Inconel wire) | Yes | Yes |
| Typical Application | Chemical, food | Steam, hydrocarbons | Power, refining | High-T, gate valves |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. What is the function of packing in a control valve? List any four desirable properties of a good packing material.**

**Model Answer:**
The packing provides a pressure-tight seal around the valve stem to prevent leakage of the process fluid to the atmosphere, while permitting smooth reciprocating motion of the stem under actuator thrust.

Four desirable properties of a good packing material:
1. **Chemical compatibility** with the process fluid.
2. **Low and stable coefficient of friction** against the stem.
3. **Resistance to extrusion and blow-out** under high pressure.
4. **Thermal endurance** across the full operating temperature range.

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between compression packing and live-loaded packing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Compression Packing | Live-Loaded Packing |
|---|---|---|
| Load Source | Packing nut tightened once at installation | Belleville spring stack maintains constant load |
| Load Decay | Loses sealing force as rings wear | Maintains sealing force over time |
| Maintenance | Frequent re-torquing required | Long maintenance intervals |
| Application | General service | Toxic / hazardous / fugitive-emission services |
| Standards | ‚Äî | API 622, API 624, ISO 15848 |

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚Äî *[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022 Style]*

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **live-loaded packing assembly** used in modern control valves with the help of a neat block diagram. Why is live loading essential for fugitive-emission service? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A control valve has a stem diameter of `30 mm` and a packing height of `50 mm`. The line pressure is `25 bar` and the packing pressure multiplier is `4`. If the coefficient of friction between the stem and packing is `0.12`, calculate the friction force on the stem. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

A live-loaded packing assembly is designed to **maintain a near-constant seating force** on the packing rings throughout their service life, irrespective of wear, thermal cycling, or relaxation. The key components are:

- **Packing rings (typically graphite or PTFE):** The primary sealing elements stacked axially around the stem.
- **Lantern ring (purge ring):** A spacer ring placed in the middle of the stack, with a side port to inject a barrier fluid (or detect leakage).
- **Belleville spring stack (disc springs):** A series-parallel arrangement of conical washers loaded in the **gland follower**. These springs are pre-compressed to a force exceeding the required packing load.
- **Packing nut / gland flange:** The structural member that holds the spring stack and transfers its load to the packing.

**Working Principle:**
As the packing rings wear or undergo thermal relaxation, the spring stack **expands slightly** (within its elastic travel range), keeping the seating pressure on the rings nearly constant. Conventional packing would lose 30‚Äì50 % of its load in the first 6 months, requiring frequent re-torque. Live-loaded packing maintains > 85 % load for **5+ years** without adjustment.

**Why essential for fugitive-emission service:**
Standards such as **API 624 (Type I)** and **ISO 15848-1 Class A** mandate leak rates below **100 ppm** of the stem diameter in VOC service. Without live loading, the packing cannot maintain the radial stress required to keep leakage below the threshold. Live loading is therefore **mandatory** in valves handling hydrocarbons, HF, HCl, and other hazardous fluids.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute the cylindrical contact area between stem and packing.**

$$A_{contact} = \pi \cdot d_s \cdot L_p$$

$$A_{contact} = \pi \cdot 0.030 \cdot 0.050$$

$$A_{contact} = 4.712 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute the packing pressure.**

$$P_{packing} = k \cdot P_{line} = 4 \cdot 25 \ \text{bar}$$

$$P_{packing} = 100 \ \text{bar} = 100 \times 10^{5} \ \text{Pa} = 1.0 \times 10^{7} \ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute the normal (radial) load on the stem.**

$$F_{N} = P_{packing} \cdot A_{contact}$$

$$F_{N} = (1.0 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (4.712 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{N} = 47{,}124 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute the friction force.**

$$F_{f} = \mu \cdot F_{N}$$

$$F_{f} = 0.12 \cdot 47{,}124$$

$$\boxed{F_{f} = 5{,}654.9 \ \text{N} \ \approx \ 5.65 \ \text{kN}}$$

**Verification of Units:** Pressure (Pa = N/m¬≤) √ó Area (m¬≤) = N. Force √ó dimensionless = N. ‚úì

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚Äî Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare **PTFE packing, graphite packing, and metal packing** based on temperature range, friction coefficient, chemical resistance, and typical application. State the role of the **lantern ring** in a packing assembly. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A control valve has a gland area of `1500 mm¬≤`. The packing pressure required is `10 MPa`. If the gland nut is tightened to develop this pressure, calculate (i) the gland load, and (ii) the load per ring if there are **5 packing rings**. If wear causes a **1.2 mm** axial compression over 3 years, design a **Belleville spring stack** with single-spring deflection of `2.0 mm` and single-spring load of `2000 N` at design deflection. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Comparison Table (must be drawn in the answer book):**

| Property | PTFE | Flexible Graphite | Metal (Soft Iron) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Temperature | **260 ¬∞C** | **1000 ¬∞C (inert)** | **> 600 ¬∞C** |
| Friction Coefficient | **0.04 ‚Äì 0.10** | **0.10 ‚Äì 0.20** | **0.20 ‚Äì 0.40** |
| Chemical Resistance | Excellent (universal) | Excellent (ex. strong oxidizers) | Poor (corrodes in acids) |
| Typical Application | Chemical, food, pharmaceutical | Steam, hydrocarbon, refining | High-pressure gate, high-T |

**Role of the Lantern Ring:**

- **Pressure equalization:** Distributes the gland load evenly across all packing rings.
- **Purge / barrier injection:** Allows injection of an inert barrier fluid between the upper and lower packing rings to prevent leakage of hazardous process fluids outward and to prevent ingress of air/moisture inward.
- **Leak detection:** Acts as a tell-tale ‚Äî any fluid appearing at the lantern-ring vent port indicates packing failure, enabling early warning.
- **Lubrication channel:** Distributes lubricant evenly around the stem circumference.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Part (i) ‚Äî Gland load:**

$$F_{gland} = P_{packing} \cdot A_{g}$$

$$F_{gland} = (10 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (1500 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$F_{gland} = 15{,}000 \ \text{N} = 15 \ \text{kN}$$

**Part (ii) ‚Äî Load per ring:**

$$F_{ring} = \frac{F_{gland}}{n} = \frac{15{,}000}{5} = 3{,}000 \ \text{N per ring}$$

**Part (iii) ‚Äî Spring stack design:**

- Required total spring force: `F_total = 15,000 N`.
- Single spring load: `F_1 = 2000 N`.
- Springs in parallel: `n_p = 15000 / 2000 = 7.5 ‚Üí 8 springs`.
- Springs in series to absorb 1.2 mm wear: `n_s = 1.2 / 2.0 = 0.6 ‚Üí 1 spring` (round up to **2 in series** for safety).

$$\boxed{n_p = 8 \ \text{parallel}, \quad n_s = 2 \ \text{series}, \quad F_{stack} = 16{,}000 \ \text{N (margin)}}$$

Total spring stack height: `2 √ó (spring thickness at full deflection) ‚âà 4 to 6 mm`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Packing Questions:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** The most common mistake. KTU problems give `mm` for length and `bar` for pressure ‚Äî converting correctly to SI (**m** and **Pa**) is worth 1 mark by itself.
> 2. **Forgetting the packing pressure multiplier `k`:** Students often use `P_line` directly as `P_packing`. The multiplier (3‚Äì5) is the *whole point* of packing design.
> 3. **Confusing "Packing pressure" with "Gland pressure":** Gland load is `P_packing √ó A_gland`; packing-induced stem friction is `P_packing √ó A_stem-contact`. They are different areas.
> 4. **No diagram:** In 7-mark questions, the **neat block diagram of the packing assembly is mandatory**. Drawing it (with labels) fetches 2 marks minimum. Skipping it loses easy marks.
> 5. **Live-loading explanation:** A vague answer like "springs are used" gets 1 mark. A *correct* explanation of *why* (to compensate for wear and thermal relaxation and meet fugitive-emission norms) gets 3 marks.
> 6. **Rounding errors:** In numerical problems, do not round intermediate values. Carry at least 4 significant digits until the final step.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Definition:** Packing = sealing element around the valve stem in a control valve.
- **Location:** Inside the **bonnet**, between the gland nut and the bonnet flange seat.
- **Primary functions:** Seal process fluid; allow stem motion; protect operator/environment.
- **Packing pressure:** Typically **3√ó to 5√ó the line pressure**.
- **Friction force on stem:** `F_f = Œº ¬∑ P_packing ¬∑ (œÄ ¬∑ d_s ¬∑ L_p)`.
- **Lantern ring roles:** Purge injection + leak detection + pressure equalization.
- **Live-loaded packing:** Uses **Belleville (disc) springs** to keep load constant over time. **Mandatory for fugitive-emission service** (API 624 / ISO 15848).
- **PTFE packing:** Up to 260 ¬∞C, ultra-low friction (Œº ‚âà 0.05), chemically inert.
- **Graphite packing:** Up to 1000 ¬∞C, fire-safe, used for steam and hydrocarbons.
- **Metal packing:** For very high temperatures and pressure, but high friction.
- **Standards to remember:** **API 622** (fugitive emission test), **API 624** (Type I), **ISO 15848** (Class A/B), **EPA Quad-O** (US regulation).
- **Trade-off:** Higher packing pressure ‚Üí better sealing ‚Üí higher stem friction ‚Üí bigger actuator needed.
- **Always include a diagram** in 7-mark answers ‚Äî packing stack with named components.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Definition of a Valve Seat

In **Industrial Instrumentation II (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 ‚Äì Final Control Elements)**, the term **"Seat"** refers to the **Valve Seat**, which is the stationary internal sealing surface inside a control valve body against which the **plug, disc, or closure member** mates to stop or regulate fluid flow. The seat is one of the most critical **wetted trim components** of a control valve because it directly determines the valve's **shut-off class (leakage rate), flow characteristic, and service life**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> A *valve seat* is the renewable or non-renewable annular sealing surface within a valve body that provides the leak-tight closure when the plug is in the closed position, and forms a part of the flow restriction (trim) when the valve is in the open position.

### Constructional Location

The seat is typically located:

- **At the valve inlet** (single-ported globe valves) ‚Äî fluid enters and impinges directly on the plug-seat assembly.
- **At the valve outlet** (reverse-ported valves) ‚Äî protects the plug from direct erosive flow.
- **In cage-guided valves** ‚Äî the seat is integrated into the cage itself (cage-style trim).
- **On the disc edge** (rotary valves like ball, butterfly, plug valves) ‚Äî the seat is the sealing ring pressed against the disc periphery.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Kitchen Tap:**
> Imagine a household water tap. When you screw the tap down, a rubber washer at the bottom presses against a metal rim and stops the water. That metal rim is the **seat**, and the washer is the **plug/disc**. The "tightness" of the shut-off depends on how smooth, hard, and concentric that rim is. Industrial valve seats work on exactly this principle, but they handle **high pressures (up to 2500 lb/in¬≤), extreme temperatures (‚àí196 ¬∞C to 800 ¬∞C), corrosive chemicals, and abrasive slurries**.

### Classification by Renewability

| Seat Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Renewable Seat** | Threaded or clamped into the body; can be re-machined or replaced without removing the valve body from the line. | General process control, maintenance-friendly services. |
| **Non-Renewable (Integral) Seat** | Machined directly into the valve body; replacement requires valve removal. | Low-cost, low-pressure, non-critical services. |
| **Cage Seat** | Integrated into the cage trim; seat is part of a removable cage assembly. | Modern globe valves, noise reduction, anti-cavitation. |
| **Soft Seat** | Uses PTFE, elastomer, or graphite as the seating material for bubble-tight shut-off. | Low-temperature, clean fluid services. |
| **Metal Seat** | Hard alloys (Stellite, Inconel) for high temperature and erosive service. | High-temperature steam, slurry, hydrocarbon services. |

### Standard Metrics in Valve Seating

- **Seat Leakage Class** is defined by standards like **ANSI/FCI 70-2** and **API 598**, ranging from **Class I (bubbles)** to **Class VI (zero leakage)**.
- **Seat Bore Diameter** ($D_s$) typically equals the **pipe nominal size** for full-bore valves.
- **Seat Surface Finish** is specified in **micro-inches (Œºin)** or **micro-metres (Œºm)** ‚Äî typically **32 Œºin Ra** for general service and **8 Œºin Ra** for Class V/VI shut-off.
- **Hardness Differential**: The seat material must be at least **50 BHN (Brinell Hardness Number) harder** than the plug to prevent galling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Seat Matters (Engineering Perspective):**
> 1. Determines **shut-off tightness** (no leakage vs. drip-tight).
> 2. Controls **flow coefficient ($C_v$)** because the seat bore is a flow restriction.
> 3. Resists **erosion, corrosion, and cavitation damage**.
> 4. Affects **valve noise and vibration** characteristics.
> 5. Influences the **inherent flow characteristic** (linear, equal %, etc.).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Working Principle of the Valve Seat

The seat performs **two simultaneous functions** in a control valve:

1. **Static Function (Shut-off):** Provides a leak-tight seal when the plug is fully closed.
2. **Dynamic Function (Flow Regulation):** Acts as the primary flow restriction that, combined with the plug travel, defines the valve's $C_v$ curve.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Fluid Entry:** The process fluid enters the valve body through the inlet port.
2. **Plug Approach:** The actuator (pneumatic diaphragm, piston, or electric) drives the plug stem downward (or rotates the shaft for rotary valves).
3. **Sealing Engagement:** The plug tip contacts the seat surface. The **seating load** (force exerted at contact) must exceed the line pressure √ó seat area to maintain closure.
4. **Flow Throttling:** As the plug lifts off the seat, the **annular area** between the plug and seat bore controls the flow rate.
5. **Characterization:** The shape of the plug (contoured, V-port, parabolic) interacting with the seat defines the **inherent flow characteristic**.

### Seating Mechanics ‚Äî Force Balance

The actuator must generate sufficient **stem thrust** to overcome both the **hydrostatic unbalance force** and provide a **sealing load**. This is quantified as:

$$F_{thrust} = P_1 \cdot A_{plug} + F_{seating}$$

Where:
- $F_{thrust}$ = Required stem thrust from actuator (N or lbf)
- $P_1$ = Upstream pressure (Pa or psi)
- $A_{plug}$ = Effective plug area exposed to upstream pressure (m¬≤ or in¬≤)
- $F_{seating}$ = Minimum force needed for leak-tight shut-off (depends on seat material & class)

### Cavitation and Seat Damage

When the fluid pressure drops below the **vapor pressure** at the vena contracta (narrowest point between plug and seat), vapor bubbles form. These collapse downstream, producing micro-jets that erode the seat. The **cavitation index** $\sigma$ is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P_2 - P_v}{P_1 - P_v}$$

Where:
- $P_2$ = Downstream pressure
- $P_v$ = Vapor pressure of the fluid
- $P_1$ = Upstream pressure

> **Cavitation occurs when $\sigma < \sigma_i$ (incipient cavitation index, typically 1.0‚Äì2.5 for globe valves).** This is a critical design parameter in seat selection.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{thrust} = P_1 \cdot A_{plug} + F_{seat}$ | Actuator stem thrust requirement | Sizing pneumatic/hydraulic actuators |
| $C_v = \frac{Q}{N_1 \sqrt{\Delta P / (G \cdot T \cdot Z)}}$ | Flow coefficient through valve | Sizing valve and seat bore |
| $\sigma = \frac{P_2 - P_v}{P_1 - P_v}$ | Cavitation index | Seat erosion prediction |
| $Q = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | Volumetric flow rate (orifice model) | Seat as flow restriction |
| $L_{seat} = \pi D_s \cdot \mu \cdot N$ | Friction torque on seat (rotary valves) | Ball/butterfly actuator torque |
| $\tau_{seat} = \frac{F_{contact}}{\pi D_s \cdot h}$ | Contact pressure on seat (Pa) | Seat wear/life calculation |
| $P_{rat} \cdot A_{seat} = F_{hold}$ | Hold-tight pressure relationship | Seat leakage class verification |

### Seat Leakage Classes (ANSI/FCI 70-2)

| Class | Leakage Rate | Test Medium |
|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | No test required | ‚Äî |
| **Class II** | 0.5% of rated $C_v$ | Water/Air |
| **Class III** | 0.1% of rated $C_v$ | Water/Air |
| **Class IV** | 0.01% of rated $C_v$ | Water/Air |
| **Class V** | $5 \times 10^{-6} \times \Delta P \cdot D$ mL/min | Water |
| **Class VI** | Bubble-tight (zero visible leakage) | Air |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Seat selection directly determines whether a refinery column can be isolated for maintenance without hydrocarbon release, whether a pharmaceutical reactor can achieve sterile isolation, and whether a power plant's boiler feedwater stop valve passes the **OSHA/ASME leak-tightness audit**.

### Material Selection Criteria for Seats

- **Hardness ‚â• 50 BHN above plug material** ‚Üí prevents galling.
- **Corrosion resistance** ‚Üí matches fluid chemistry (Hastelloy for HCl, Monel for HF, 316 SS for general hydrocarbons).
- **Thermal expansion compatibility** ‚Üí seat and body must expand similarly to avoid gap formation at high T.
- **Erosion resistance** ‚Üí Stellite overlay for slurries, ceramic inserts for highly abrasive services.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Seating Force Required for Leak-Tight Shut-Off

### Problem Statement
A globe valve with a plug diameter of 50 mm is used to shut off water at an upstream pressure of 20 bar. The seat leakage class required is **Class IV (0.01% of $C_v$)**. Determine the minimum seating force if the seat material is Stellite against 316 SS plug.

### Given Data
- Upstream pressure: $P_1 = 20 \text{ bar} = 20 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Plug diameter: $D_p = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Seat material: Stellite (hardness ~ 450 BHN)
- Plug material: 316 SS (hardness ~ 180 BHN)
- Required: Class IV shut-off

### Step 1: Calculate the Effective Plug Area

The effective area exposed to upstream pressure is the projected area of the plug:

$$A_{plug} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_p^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2$$

$$A_{plug} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0025 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Hydrostatic Unbalance Force

This is the force the actuator must overcome just to hold the plug in the closed position against line pressure:

$$F_{unbalance} = P_1 \cdot A_{plug}$$

$$F_{unbalance} = (20 \times 10^5) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{unbalance} = 3927 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3: Determine the Seating Force ($F_{seating}$)

For Class IV leakage, empirical guidelines (ISA 75.01 / Masoneilan) suggest a **seating stress** of approximately **3.45 MPa (500 psi)** for metal-to-metal seats.

$$F_{seating} = \sigma_{seat} \cdot A_{seat\ contact}$$

Assuming the contact is on the seating surface, the contact area is approximately equal to the projected area for a flat-faced seat:

$$F_{seating} = 3.45 \times 10^6 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{seating} = 6774 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4: Total Stem Thrust Required

$$F_{thrust} = F_{unbalance} + F_{seating}$$

$$F_{thrust} = 3927 + 6774$$

$$F_{thrust} = 10701 \text{ N} \approx 10.7 \text{ kN}$$

### Conclusion
The pneumatic actuator must develop at least **10.7 kN** of stem thrust to achieve Class IV shut-off on this valve. This informs the diaphragm area and spring range selection.

---

## Derivation 2: Cavitation Susceptibility of a Seat

### Problem Statement
A control valve handles liquid ammonia at $P_1 = 8 \text{ bar}$ (upstream) and $P_2 = 2.5 \text{ bar}$ (downstream). The vapor pressure of ammonia at operating temperature is $P_v = 1.2 \text{ bar}$. Determine if cavitation will occur at the seat (incipient $\sigma_i = 1.8$).

### Step 1: Apply the Cavitation Index Formula

$$\sigma = \frac{P_2 - P_v}{P_1 - P_v}$$

Substituting values:

$$\sigma = \frac{2.5 - 1.2}{8.0 - 1.2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{1.3}{6.8} = 0.191$$

### Step 2: Compare with Incipient Cavitation Index

Since calculated $\sigma = 0.191$ is **much less** than the incipient value $\sigma_i = 1.8$:

$$\sigma < \sigma_i \implies \text{Severe cavitation will occur}$$

### Step 3: Engineering Recommendation

To prevent seat damage, the following seat modifications are recommended:
1. Use a **cage-style trim with multi-stage pressure drop** to keep local $P$ above $P_v$.
2. Specify **hard-faced Stellite seat** with erosion-resistant profile.
3. Limit pressure drop across the seat to $\Delta P_{allow} < (P_1 - P_v) \cdot \sigma_i$.

Calculate allowable pressure drop:

$$\Delta P_{allow} = (8.0 - 1.2) \times 1.8 = 12.24 \text{ bar}$$

But since actual $\Delta P = 8 - 2.5 = 5.5 \text{ bar} < 12.24 \text{ bar}$ by simple criterion, cavitation is controlled by **vena contracta pressure** dropping below $P_v$. Multi-stage trim is essential.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]
**Define the term "valve seat" and explain its two primary functions in a control valve.**

**Model Answer:**

A valve seat is the stationary annular sealing surface inside a control valve body against which the plug mates to stop flow. It performs two primary functions:

1. **Shut-off Function:** Provides a leak-tight seal when the plug is fully closed, achieving the specified leakage class (ANSI/FCI 70-2 Class I through VI).

2. **Flow Regulation Function:** Acts as the primary flow restriction. The annular area between the plug and seat bore, which varies with plug travel, determines the flow coefficient ($C_v$) and defines the inherent flow characteristic of the valve.

[3 Marks ‚Äî 1 for definition, 1 each for both functions]

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]
**What is cavitation index? How does it relate to seat damage?**

**Model Answer:**

The cavitation index $\sigma$ is a dimensionless number that quantifies the tendency of a fluid to vaporize at the vena contracta between the plug and seat. It is defined as:

$$\sigma = \frac{P_2 - P_v}{P_1 - P_v}$$

When $\sigma$ falls below the incipient cavitation threshold $\sigma_i$ (typically 1.0‚Äì2.5 for globe valves), vapor bubbles form in the low-pressure zone near the seat. As these bubbles collapse downstream of the seat, they generate micro-jets that cause **erosion, pitting, and eventual destruction** of the seat surface, drastically reducing service life.

[3 Marks ‚Äî 1 for formula, 1 for interpretation, 1 for damage mechanism]

---

## PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the classification of valve seats based on **renewability** and **material** with suitable diagrams. Discuss the relative merits and limitations of each type for industrial applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A globe valve with a plug diameter of 80 mm is used to control water flow at $P_1 = 25 \text{ bar}$. The seat is renewable Stellite against a 316 SS plug. For Class IV leakage, the required seating stress is 3.5 MPa. Calculate:
   (i) Effective plug area
   (ii) Hydrostatic unbalance force
   (iii) Minimum seating force
   (iv) Total actuator thrust required

**Take safety factor of 1.25.** **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Seat Classification

**1. Based on Renewability:**

- **Renewable Seat:** Threaded or clamped into the body; can be re-machined or replaced in-place. *Advantages:* Maintenance-friendly, no need to remove valve from line, suitable for frequent shut-off service. *Disadvantages:* Higher initial cost, potential for seat loosening under thermal cycling.

- **Non-Renewable (Integral) Seat:** Machined directly into the valve body; replacement requires valve removal. *Advantages:* Low cost, compact, no seat-retaining parts. *Disadvantages:* Cannot be re-machined, full valve replacement needed upon wear.

**2. Based on Material:**

- **Metal Seat (Stellite, Inconel, 17-4 PH):** Suitable for high-temperature (> 200 ¬∞C), high-pressure, and erosive service. Provides Class I‚ÄìIV shut-off. Limitation: requires high seating force.

- **Soft Seat (PTFE, PFA, Viton, EPDM):** Provides bubble-tight Class VI shut-off at low temperatures (< 200 ¬∞C). Not suitable for abrasive or high-T services.

- **Cage Seat:** Integrated into a removable cage; enables multi-stage pressure drop for cavitation control. Common in noise reduction trims.

*Engineering Selection Criteria:* Match seat material to fluid chemistry, temperature, pressure, required shut-off class, and frequency of operation.

**[7 Marks: 2 for renewability, 3 for materials with merit/demerit, 2 for selection criteria]**

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $D_p = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- $P_1 = 25 \text{ bar} = 25 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\sigma_{seat} = 3.5 \text{ MPa} = 3.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Safety factor (SF) = 1.25

**(i) Effective Plug Area:**

$$A_{plug} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_p^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2$$

$$A_{plug} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Hydrostatic Unbalance Force:**

$$F_{unbal} = P_1 \cdot A_{plug} = (25 \times 10^5) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{unbal} = 12566.4 \text{ N} \approx 12.57 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Minimum Seating Force:**

Assuming contact area equals projected area for flat-faced seat:

$$F_{seat} = \sigma_{seat} \cdot A_{plug} = (3.5 \times 10^6) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F_{seat} = 17592.7 \text{ N} \approx 17.59 \text{ kN}$$

**(iv) Total Actuator Thrust (with safety factor):**

$$F_{thrust} = SF \times (F_{unbal} + F_{seat})$$

$$F_{thrust} = 1.25 \times (12566.4 + 17592.7)$$

$$F_{thrust} = 1.25 \times 30159.1$$

$$F_{thrust} = 37698.9 \text{ N} \approx 37.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** The actuator must deliver a minimum thrust of **37.7 kN** to achieve Class IV shut-off under these conditions.

**[7 Marks: 1 each for steps (i)-(iii), 2 for (iv) with safety factor and final answer]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ‚Äî Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction of a **globe valve** and identify the location and function of its seat. Explain how the seat contributes to **flow characterization** in equal percentage and linear trim designs. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A valve handles saturated liquid propane at $P_1 = 12 \text{ bar}$, $P_2 = 3 \text{ bar}$, with vapor pressure $P_v = 6 \text{ bar}$. The incipient cavitation index for the seat geometry is $\sigma_i = 2.2$. Determine whether cavitation will occur. If yes, recommend **two seat-related solutions** to mitigate it. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) ‚Äî Globe Valve Construction and Seat Function

A globe valve consists of:
- **Body** with horizontal inlet and vertical outlet ports.
- **Bonnet** mounted on top, housing the packing and stem.
- **Stem** connected to the plug via the actuator.
- **Plug** (movable) ‚Äî contoured for specific flow characteristics.
- **Seat ring** (stationary, renewable) ‚Äî located at the inlet port, providing the sealing surface.

**Seat Contribution to Flow Characterization:**

- **Linear Trim:** The plug is shaped so that the **annular area between plug and seat varies linearly** with stem travel. Equal increments in travel produce equal increments in flow. Suitable for systems where the pressure drop across the valve is approximately constant.

- **Equal Percentage Trim:** The plug contour is designed so that equal increments in stem travel produce **equal percentage changes** in the existing flow. Achieved by shaping the plug such that the seat-plug geometry gives a logarithmic area-vs-travel relationship. Suitable for systems with large pressure drop variations.

The seat bore and its interaction with the plug contour are the **physical basis** of these inherent characteristics.

**[7 Marks: 3 for construction/sketches, 2 for linear, 2 for equal percentage]**

#### Part (b) ‚Äî Cavitation Analysis

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 12 \text{ bar}$, $P_2 = 3 \text{ bar}$, $P_v = 6 \text{ bar}$, $\sigma_i = 2.2$

**Step 1: Calculate Cavitation Index**

$$\sigma = \frac{P_2 - P_v}{P_1 - P_v} = \frac{3 - 6}{12 - 6} = \frac{-3}{6} = -0.5$$

**Step 2: Interpretation**

Since $\sigma = -0.5$ is **negative**, it means $P_2 < P_v$, i.e., the downstream pressure is already below the vapor pressure of propane. This indicates **flashing cavitation** ‚Äî the fluid vaporizes before reaching the seat, and two-phase flow destroys the seat surface violently.

**Conclusion:** **Severe cavitation will occur.** The seat will suffer rapid erosion.

**Recommended Seat-Related Solutions:**

1. **Multi-Stage Anti-Cavitation Cage Trim:** Replace the single seat with a cage having multiple small orifices that divide the pressure drop into stages, keeping the local pressure at every stage above $P_v = 6 \text{ bar}$.

2. **Hard-Faced Seat with Stellite Overlay and Increased Seat Bore:** Use a Stellite-grade 12 seat (hardness ~ 50 HRC) with a larger bore to reduce velocity and provide erosion resistance. Additionally, install a **diffuser downstream** of the seat to recover pressure gradually.

3. **Alternative (Bonus):** Operate the valve at a lower pressure drop by selecting a larger valve size, so that $\Delta P$ across the seat is reduced.

**[7 Marks: 2 for calculation, 2 for interpretation, 3 for two well-explained solutions]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚Äî Seat Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing seat with gasket:** The seat is *inside* the valve, sealing the plug; the gasket seals the body-bonnet joint. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Wrong unit in F = P¬∑A:** Pressure must be in **Pa (N/m¬≤)** and area in **m¬≤**; do not mix bar with mm¬≤. Always convert.
> 3. **Forgetting safety factor:** In Part B numericals, examiners explicitly look for the SF multiplier in actuator sizing. Skipping it = lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Cavitation index sign:** A *negative* $\sigma$ means flashing has begun; many students write "no cavitation" ‚Äî this is a critical error.
> 5. **Class VI vs Class IV confusion:** Soft seats (PTFE) give Class VI; metal seats typically achieve Class IV maximum. Do not claim metal seats give zero leakage.
> 6. **Not specifying units** in formula sheet or numerical answers is a recurring 0.5‚Äì1 mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ‚úÖ A **valve seat** is the stationary sealing surface inside a control valve that mates with the plug for shut-off and acts as a flow restriction.
- ‚úÖ Seats are classified as **renewable** vs. **non-renewable**, and **metal** vs. **soft** vs. **cage**.
- ‚úÖ The seat bore diameter, surface finish (in Œºin Ra), and material hardness (‚â• 50 BHN above plug) determine service life.
- ‚úÖ **ANSI/FCI 70-2** defines six leakage classes (I to VI); Class VI requires soft seats.
- ‚úÖ Actuator thrust must overcome **unbalance force** + **seating load** + apply a **safety factor (typically 1.25)**.
- ‚úÖ **Cavitation index** $\sigma = (P_2 - P_v)/(P_1 - P_v)$; cavitation occurs when $\sigma < \sigma_i$, damaging the seat by bubble-collapse erosion.
- ‚úÖ **Flashing** occurs when $P_2 < P_v$, leading to two-phase flow and rapid seat destruction.
- ‚úÖ **Linear trim** seats give constant flow change per travel; **equal-percentage trim** seats give constant percentage change per travel.
- ‚úÖ Seat materials: **Stellite** for high-T/erosion, **PTFE** for bubble-tight low-T, **Hastelloy/Monel** for corrosive service, **ceramics** for slurries.
- ‚úÖ Always use **Pa and m¬≤** in force calculations; convert all pressures and dimensions to SI before substituting.
- ‚úÖ KTU frequently asks: definition (3 marks), seat types (7 marks), thrust calculation (7 marks), and cavitation analysis (7 marks) ‚Äî master these patterns.
- ‚úÖ Renewable seats reduce **downtime**; integral seats reduce **cost**; cage seats enable **anti-cavitation** ‚Äî match to application.
- ‚úÖ **Seating stress** for metal Class IV ‚âà **3.5 MPa (500 psi)**; for soft Class VI ‚âà **0.5‚Äì1.0 MPa**.
- ‚úÖ Always verify **hardness differential** between seat and plug to prevent galling ‚Äî a common viva question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disk (in Industrial Instrumentation Context):**
> A **disk** is a rotating, flat, circular storage medium‚Äîeither **magnetic** or **optical**‚Äîused as the primary or secondary data-storage element in industrial data recorders, data loggers, digital chart recorders, and process-data acquisition systems. It stores process variables (temperature, pressure, flow, level, etc.) in the form of **digitally encoded binary data** on concentric tracks distributed over one or more platters.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Industrial Instrumentation II (PCICT503)**, under **Module 4 ‚Äì Data Recorders, Data Loggers, and Storage Devices**, the term "Disk" refers to the **magnetic-disk-based digital storage sub-system** that forms the back-end of modern paperless recorders and process-data historians. The three principal disk technologies covered are:

1. **Hard Disk Drive (HDD)** ‚Äì rigid magnetic platters with flying read/write heads.
2. **Floppy Disk (legacy)** ‚Äì flexible magnetic disk, historically used in early strip-chart and X‚ÄìY recorders.
3. **Optical Disk (CD / DVD / Blu-ray)** ‚Äì laser-read storage used in archival and portable industrial recorders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ‚Äì PCICT503 Module 4):**
> *Students are expected to explain the construction, working, and recording formats of magnetic and optical disks, derive storage capacity and data transfer rate, and compare their use in industrial data recorders.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an industrial disk the way you think of a **black-box flight recorder in an aeroplane**:

- The aeroplane records every instrument reading (altitude, speed, heading) onto a **spinning tape or disk** inside the box.
- Similarly, a process plant records every sensor reading (reactor temperature, feed flow, pH) onto a **spinning magnetic disk** inside a data logger.
- Just as the flight recorder's disk lets investigators **rewind and replay** the last few hours of the flight, the industrial disk lets the process engineer **rewind and analyse** the last shift, the last batch, or the last year of plant operation.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** A disk is simply a *very fast, very dense, very reliable notebook* that the instrumentation system uses to *write down what is happening in the plant* ‚Äî except instead of writing on paper with ink, it writes **magnetic patterns** (or **microscopic pits**) on a spinning metal (or plastic) disc, and instead of being read by eyes, it is read by a **magnetic head** (or a **laser**).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

The following physical constants and standard units are essential for any disk-related KTU question:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Areal density** | $D_A$ | $\text{GB/in}^2$ or $\text{bits/mm}^2$ |
| **Track density** | $D_T$ | **tracks per inch (TPI)** or tracks/mm |
| **Linear (bit) density** | $D_L$ | **bits per inch (BPI)** or bits/mm |
| **Platter diameter (HDD)** | $d$ | **3.5 inch** (standard industrial), 2.5 inch, 1.8 inch |
| **Spindle speed** | $N$ | **rpm** (e.g., 5400, 7200, 15000) |
| **Standard CD capacity** | $C_{CD}$ | **700 MB** (80 min audio) |
| **Standard DVD capacity** | $C_{DVD}$ | **4.7 GB** (single-side, single-layer) |
| **Standard Blu-ray capacity** | $C_{BD}$ | **25 GB** (single-layer) |
| **Floppy disk capacity** | $C_{F}$ | **1.44 MB** (3.5" HD) |
| **Wavelength of CD laser** | $\lambda_{CD}$ | **780 nm** (infrared) |
| **Wavelength of DVD laser** | $\lambda_{DVD}$ | **650 nm** (red) |
| **Wavelength of Blu-ray laser** | $\lambda_{BD}$ | **405 nm** (blue-violet) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The *shorter the laser wavelength*, the **smaller the pit** that can be resolved, and hence the **higher the storage capacity**. This is why Blu-ray (405 nm) stores ~5√ó more data than DVD (650 nm) on the same 12 cm disc.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles ‚Äî Broken Down Step by Step

### A. Magnetic Hard Disk (HDD)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Mechanical Layout:**
- A rigid **aluminium or glass platter** is coated with a thin ferromagnetic film (typically a cobalt-based alloy).
- One or more platters are mounted on a common **spindle** rotated at constant angular velocity (CAV) or, in modern drives, **zoned constant angular velocity (Z-CAV)**.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Data Organisation:**
- The surface is divided into **concentric circles called tracks**.
- Each track is divided into **sectors** (commonly **512 bytes** historically, **4096 bytes** in Advanced Format drives).
- A **stack of tracks at the same radius on every surface** is called a **cylinder**.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Read/Write Mechanism:**
- A **floating magnetic head** rides on a microscopic **air bearing** (‚âà 5‚Äì10 nm above the surface).
- During *write*, the head's coil magnetises tiny regions of the ferromagnetic film ‚Äî one polarity = binary **1**, opposite polarity = binary **0** (NRZ encoding).
- During *read*, the magnetised regions induce a voltage in the head's coil (**electromagnetic induction**, Faraday's law).

**Step 4 ‚Äî Head Actuation:**
- A **voice-coil actuator (VCA)** moves the head arm radially to the desired track in a **seek operation**.
- After arriving, the drive must wait for the right sector to rotate under the head ‚Äî this is **rotational latency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the head "fly"?** The spinning platter drags air with it (viscous entrainment), creating a wedge of pressurised air that lifts the head. This *avoids mechanical contact*, which would otherwise cause wear and tear.

### B. Floppy Disk (Legacy)

- Flexible **Mylar** substrate coated with iron-oxide.
- Spins at **300 rpm** (HD 3.5") ‚Äî much slower than HDD.
- Uses **contact recording** (head touches the surface) ‚Äî hence the audible "click-click" of old recorders.
- Capacity is only **1.44 MB** (HD) or **720 KB** (DD).

### C. Optical Disk (CD / DVD / Blu-ray)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Mastering:** A high-power laser burns (or presses) **microscopic pits** and **lands** (flat areas) along a single **spiral track** running from inner to outer radius.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Read-Back:** A low-power laser of the appropriate wavelength illuminates the track.
- The pit depth is set to **Œª / 4** in the disc material, so that light reflected from a pit is **Œª / 2** out of phase with light reflected from a land ‚Üí destructive interference ‚Üí low intensity = binary **0**.
- A land (no pit) reflects strongly ‚Üí high intensity = binary **1**.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Tracking & Focus:**
- A **quadrant photodetector** generates an error signal to keep the laser on-track (radial) and in-focus (axial) using a closed-loop servo.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Rotation:**
- **Constant Linear Velocity (CLV)** is used in CD/DVD ‚Äî the disc spins faster when reading inner tracks and slower at outer tracks, so that the **linear data rate** stays constant.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations ‚Äî They Appear in Nearly Every KTU Question on Disks.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Storage Capacity (HDD)** | $C = n_{\text{tracks}} \times n_{\text{sect/track}} \times n_{\text{bytes/sect}} \times n_{\text{surfaces}}$ | bytes | Multiply by 2 for double-sided |
| 2 | **Areal Density** | $D_A = D_T \times D_L$ | $\text{bits/in}^2$ | Tracks/in √ó Bits/in |
| 3 | **Total Capacity from Areal Density** | $C = D_A \times A_{\text{usable}}$ | bits | $A_{\text{usable}} = \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ |
| 4 | **Linear Data Rate (Magnetic)** | $R_L = D_L \times v_{\text{linear}}$ | $\text{bits/s}$ | $v_{\text{linear}} = \pi d N / 60$ for diameter $d$ (m) and $N$ (rpm) |
| 5 | **Average Seek Time** | $T_{\text{seek}} = \dfrac{1}{3} T_{\text{stroke,max}}$ | s | Stroke = full-track movement |
| 6 | **Average Rotational Latency** | $T_{\text{lat}} = \dfrac{1}{2} T_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{30}{N}$ | s | $N$ in rpm |
| 7 | **Average Access Time (HDD)** | $T_a = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{lat}} + T_{\text{xfer}}$ | s | Sum of three components |
| 8 | **Transfer Time** | $T_{\text{xfer}} = \dfrac{\text{bytes to read}}{R_L / 8}$ | s | Bytes √∑ (Bytes/s) |
| 9 | **Optical Pit Length** | $L_{\text{pit}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{linear}}}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ | m | Where $f_{\text{clock}}$ is the channel-bit rate |
| 10 | **Optical Resolution (Rayleigh)** | $d_{\text{spot}} = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | m | $NA$ = numerical aperture of objective lens |
| 11 | **CLV Rotation Speed** | $N(r) = \dfrac{v_{\text{lin}}}{\pi \, d(r)} \times 60$ | rpm | Speed varies with track radius $r$ |
| 12 | **Capacity (Optical CD)** | $C_{CD} = \dfrac{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}{A_{\text{bit}}}$ | bytes | $A_{\text{bit}} = L_{\text{pit}} \times P_{\text{track}}$ |
| 13 | **Track Pitch** | $P_{\text{track}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | m | Minimum spacing between adjacent tracks |
| 14 | **Encoding Efficiency (EFM/EFMPlus)** | $\eta_{\text{enc}} = \dfrac{m}{n}$ | ‚Äî | EFM = 8/17, EFMPlus = 8/16 |

> [!TIP]
> **Why track pitch scales with Œª/NA:** To keep crosstalk between adjacent tracks low, the focused spot diameter (which itself scales as Œª/NA by Rayleigh's criterion) must be smaller than the track pitch. So **shorter wavelength + higher NA ‚Üí tighter tracks ‚Üí more capacity**.

---
