## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Hard Disks** ‚Üí back-end of **paperless recorders** (Eurotherm 6100A, Yokogawa DX1000), **DCS historians** (PI Server, Wonderware), **data-acquisition PCs** in plant control rooms.
- **Optical Disks** ‚Üí **archival storage** of **batch records** for FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance in pharmaceutical plants; portable handover of calibration data to field engineers.
- **Floppy Disks (legacy)** ‚Üí older **strip-chart replacements** and **PC-based chart recorders** of the 1990s; still referenced in older plant documentation.
- **Industrial-grade SSDs** (Solid-State Disks) are increasingly replacing HDDs in **vibration-prone** plant environments (compressors, mills).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Storage Capacity of a Single Magnetic Platter (Tracks √ó Sectors √ó Bytes)

**Given:** A 3.5" HDD platter has **80 000 tracks per surface**, **500 sectors per track**, and **512 bytes per sector**. The drive contains **2 platters** (i.e., 4 surfaces). Find the **total formatted capacity** in MB and GB.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Capacity per surface:**
$$C_{\text{surface}} = n_{\text{tracks}} \times n_{\text{sect/track}} \times n_{\text{bytes/sect}}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute the numbers:**
$$C_{\text{surface}} = 80\,000 \times 500 \times 512 \;\text{bytes}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Multiply out:**
$$C_{\text{surface}} = 80\,000 \times 256\,000 \;\text{bytes} = 2.048 \times 10^{10}\;\text{bytes}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Capacity of 4 surfaces:**
$$C_{\text{total}} = 4 \times 2.048 \times 10^{10} = 8.192 \times 10^{10}\;\text{bytes}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Convert to MB (1 MB = 10‚Å∂ bytes) and GB (1 GB = 10‚Åπ bytes):**
$$C_{\text{total}} = 81.92\;\text{MB} \quad\longleftarrow \text{[Common mistake: use 2¬≤‚Å∞ for MiB]}$$
$$C_{\text{total}} = 0.08192\;\text{GB}$$

> **Re-doing with binary (MiB) ‚Äî the KTU-exam safe convention:**
$$C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{8.192 \times 10^{10}}{2^{20}} = 78\,125\;\text{MiB} \approx 76.3\;\text{MB (binary)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Always state which convention you are using ‚Äî decimal (10‚Å∂) or binary (2¬≤‚Å∞) ‚Äî and **never mix** them. KTU examiners deduct ¬Ω mark for an ambiguous unit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Average Access Time of a Hard Disk

**Given:** A 7200-rpm HDD has a **maximum seek time of 10 ms**. Find the **average access time** for a 4-KB sector transfer, assuming the **transfer rate is 80 MB/s**.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Rotational period:**
$$T_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{60}{N} = \dfrac{60}{7200} = 8.333\;\text{ms}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Average rotational latency** (¬Ω revolution):
$$T_{\text{lat}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{rev}}}{2} = \dfrac{8.333}{2} = 4.167\;\text{ms}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Average seek time** (‚âà ‚Öì of full stroke for a uniformly distributed request):
$$T_{\text{seek}} = \dfrac{10}{3} = 3.333\;\text{ms}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Transfer time** for 4 KB at 80 MB/s:
$$T_{\text{xfer}} = \dfrac{4 \times 1024\;\text{bytes}}{80 \times 10^6\;\text{bytes/s}} = 5.12 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{s} = 0.0512\;\text{ms}$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Total average access time:**
$$T_a = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{lat}} + T_{\text{xfer}}$$
$$T_a = 3.333 + 4.167 + 0.0512 = 7.551\;\text{ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_a \approx 7.55\;\text{ms}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *transfer time* is **negligible** compared to *seek + latency*. This is why disk-I/O performance is dominated by *where the head is*, not by *how fast data spins past it*. This motivates **disk scheduling algorithms** (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN) taught in OS courses.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Linear Bit Density and Areal Density

**Given:** A platter of **outer radius 4.5 cm** and **inner radius 2.0 cm** has **30 000 tracks** with a **track density of 80 000 TPI**. The **linear density** at the inner radius is **500 000 BPI**. Compute the **total capacity in bits**.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Usable area:**
$$A_{\text{usable}} = \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2) = \pi (0.045^2 - 0.020^2)\;\text{m}^2$$
$$A_{\text{usable}} = \pi (2.025 \times 10^{-3} - 4.0 \times 10^{-4}) = \pi (1.625 \times 10^{-3})$$
$$A_{\text{usable}} = 5.105 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Areal density at inner radius:**
$$D_A = D_T \times D_L = 80\,000 \times 500\,000 = 4.0 \times 10^{10}\;\text{bits/in}^2$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Convert to bits/m¬≤** (1 in¬≤ = 6.4516 √ó 10‚Åª‚Å¥ m¬≤):
$$D_A = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{10}}{6.4516 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.20 \times 10^{13}\;\text{bits/m}^2$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Total capacity:**
$$C = D_A \times A_{\text{usable}} = 6.20 \times 10^{13} \times 5.105 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$C = 3.17 \times 10^{11}\;\text{bits} \approx 395\;\text{MB}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚Äî Why Blu-ray Holds More Data Than DVD

**Step 1 ‚Äî Spot diameter (Rayleigh criterion):**
$$d_{\text{spot}} = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî DVD:** $\lambda_{DVD} = 650\;\text{nm}$, $NA_{DVD} = 0.60$
$$d_{DVD} = 1.22 \times \dfrac{650 \times 10^{-9}}{0.60} = 1.32\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Blu-ray:** $\lambda_{BD} = 405\;\text{nm}$, $NA_{BD} = 0.85$
$$d_{BD} = 1.22 \times \dfrac{405 \times 10^{-9}}{0.85} = 0.581\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Ratio of areas** (assuming square bits):
$$\dfrac{A_{DVD}}{A_{BD}} = \left(\dfrac{1.32}{0.581}\right)^2 = 5.16$$

$$\boxed{\text{That is why Blu-ray holds } \sim 5\times \text{ more data than DVD.}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Magnetic vs Optical vs SSD)

| Property | HDD (Magnetic) | Optical (CD/DVD/BD) | SSD (Solid-State Disk) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access time** | 3 ‚Äì 15 ms | 80 ‚Äì 200 ms | 0.05 ‚Äì 0.2 ms |
| **Capacity** | 500 GB ‚Äì 20 TB | 700 MB ‚Äì 128 GB | 256 GB ‚Äì 100 TB |
| **Vibration tolerance** | **Poor** | **Excellent** | **Good** |
| **Shock tolerance** | 300 g (operating) | 50 g | 1500 g |
| **Recording type** | Magnetic flux | Pit/land | Floating-gate transistor |
| **Read mechanism** | Inductive head | Laser + photodiode | CMOS sense amplifier |
| **Write mechanism** | Inductive head | Laser + dye change (WORM) | Tunnel-Fowler-Nordheim |
| **Industrial use** | Plant historian, NAS | Archive, compliance | Paperless recorder, edge PC |
| **Cost per GB** | ‚Çπ2 ‚Äì 4 | ‚Çπ0.5 ‚Äì 1 (write once) | ‚Çπ4 ‚Äì 8 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** For a *paperless industrial recorder in a vibration-heavy area* (e.g., near a crusher), an **SSD** is preferred. For *archival of batch records for 7 years* (FDA), an **optical disk** is preferred. For *active historian with continuous read/write*, an **HDD in RAID** is preferred.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî HDD Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SpindleAssembly
        s1[MotorDriver] --> s2[BrushlessDCmotor]
        s2 --> s3[SpindleHub]
        s3 --> s4[Platter1top]
        s3 --> s5[Platter2top]
        s3 --> s6[Platter2bottom]
        s3 --> s7[Platter1bottom]
    end

    subgraph HeadStackAssembly
        h1[VoiceCoilActuator] --> h2[ActuatorArm1]
        h1 --> h3[ActuatorArm2]
        h2 --> h4[ReadWriteHead1]
        h2 --> h5[ReadWriteHead2]
        h3 --> h6[ReadWriteHead3]
        h3 --> h7[ReadWriteHead4]
    end

    s4 -.air bearing.-> h4
    s5 -.air bearing.-> h5
    s6 -.air bearing.-> h6
    s7 -.air bearing.-> h7

    subgraph ServoAndInterface
        c1[ControllerASIC] --> c2[ServoDemodulator]
        c2 --> c1
        c1 --> c3[SATAController]
        c3 --> c4[HostPC_or_Datalogger]
        c1 --> h1
    end

    h4 --> c1
    h5 --> c1
    h6 --> c1
    h7 --> c1
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Optical Disk Read Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CDDVDorBDDiscSpinningatCLV] --> B[LaserDiode780or650or405nm]
    B --> C[CollimatingLens]
    C --> D[Beamsplitter45deg]
    D --> E[ObjectiveLensNA065or085]
    E --> F[FocusedSpotonPitLand]
    F --> G[ReflectedLightbacktoBeamsplitter]
    G --> H[QuarterWavePlateandAstigmaticLens]
    H --> I[QuadrantPhotodetectorABCD]
    I --> J[RFAmplifierandEqualizer]
    I --> K[FocusErrorSignal]
    I --> L[TrackingErrorSignal]
    J --> M[EFMorEFMPlusDecoder]
    M --> N[ByteStreamtoHost]
    K --> O[FocusServoActuator]
    L --> P[TrackingServoActuator]
    O --> E
    P --> E
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Disk Role in a Paperless Industrial Recorder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Field
        T[TemperatureSensorPT100]
        P[PressureTransmitter4to20mA]
        F[FlowmeterHART]
    end

    subgraph SignalConditioning
        T --> SC1[RTDLinearizer]
        P --> SC2[ShuntResistor250ohm]
        F --> SC3[HARTModem]
    end

    subgraph RecorderCPU
        SC1 --> ADC[AnalogtoDigitalConverter24bit]
        SC2 --> ADC
        SC3 --> ADC
        ADC --> CPU[ARMorDSPProcessor]
        CPU --> RAM[VolatileMemoryDisplayBuffer]
        CPU --> DISK[(MagneticorSSDDiskNonVolatile)]
        CPU --> LCD[ColourLCDDisplay]
        CPU --> ETH[EthernetModbusTCP]
    end

    ETH --> HIS[PlantHistorianServer]
    DISK -.scheduled dump.-> USB[USBExporttoEngineerLaptop]
    DISK -.alarm snapshot.-> MAIL[EmailAlertwithCSVAttachment]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart ‚Äî Disk Scheduling Algorithm (SCAN / Elevator) for Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([NewIORequestArrives]) --> Sort[InsertRequestintoSortedRequestQueue]
    Sort --> Dir{CurrentHeadDirection}
    Dir -- Moving toward outer track --> ServiceOut[Serviceallrequestswithincreasingcylinder]
    ServiceOut --> ReachMax{Reachedoutermostcylinder}
    ReachMax -- No --> ServiceOut
    ReachMax -- Yes --> Reverse[Reversedirection]
    Reverse --> ServiceIn[Serviceallrequestswithdecreasingcylinder]
    ServiceIn --> ReachMin{Reachedinnermostcylinder}
    ReachMin -- No --> ServiceIn
    ReachMin -- Yes --> Idle[HeadIdlesuntilnextrequest]
    Idle --> Start
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023]
**Differentiate between magnetic and optical data storage disks. List any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚Äî 1 each):**

| # | Magnetic Disk (HDD) | Optical Disk (CD/DVD/BD) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses **ferromagnetic coating** on a rigid or flexible platter. | Uses **pits and lands** stamped on a polycarbonate substrate. |
| 2 | Read/write by **magnetic head** that flies (or contacts) the surface. | Read by **laser + photodiode**; write is *write-once* (WORM) for recordable types. |
| 3 | Data organised in **concentric tracks**; supports random read/write and re-write. | Data on a **single spiral track**; read-only or write-once in most industrial uses. |

*(1 mark for the differentiation, 1 mark for the storage medium, 1 mark for the read mechanism.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022]
**What is meant by "areal density" in a magnetic disk? State its typical value in a modern HDD.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Areal density is the number of **bits that can be stored per unit area** of the platter surface. It is the **product of track density (TPI)** and **linear bit density (BPI)**:
$$D_A = D_T \times D_L \quad [\text{bits/in}^2]$$
- **Typical value (1 mark):** In modern industrial HDDs, areal density ranges from **500 Gbit/in¬≤ to 1.5 Tbit/in¬≤**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Rule Reminder:** Answer **ONE full question** (a + b = 7 + 7 = **14 marks**). Either Question A or Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** ‚Äî *[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]*
With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a Hard Disk Drive (HDD)**. Explain the role of the **spindle motor, actuator assembly, read/write head, and platter**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Platters (1 mark):** Rigid aluminium or glass discs (typically 3.5" in industrial recorders) coated with a thin ferromagnetic film. Multiple platters are stacked on a common spindle.
2. **Spindle motor (¬Ω mark):** Brushless DC motor that spins the platters at a constant angular velocity (5400, 7200, or 15000 rpm).
3. **Read/Write heads (¬Ω mark):** Tiny electromagnetic coils mounted on slider arms; fly on an air cushion ‚âà 10 nm above the platter.
4. **Actuator assembly (¬Ω mark):** Voice-coil actuator that radially positions the head arm to the desired cylinder.
5. **Controller ASIC + SATA interface (¬Ω mark):** Manages servo control, data encoding, error correction, and host communication.

**(ii) Working ‚Äî Write Operation (2 marks):**
- The controller receives a write command from the host.
- The VCA moves the head to the correct track (seek) and waits for the right sector (latency).
- Current pulses in the head coil magnetise tiny regions of the platter ‚Äî one polarity = 1, the opposite = 0 (NRZ or PRML encoding).

**(iii) Working ‚Äî Read Operation (2 marks):**
- As magnetised regions pass under the head, the changing flux induces a voltage (Faraday's law: $\varepsilon = -N \, d\Phi/dt$).
- The induced EMF is amplified, equalised, and decoded back to binary data.
- A servo subsystem keeps the head centred on the track using a dedicated **embedded servo pattern** written at manufacture.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** ‚Äî *[KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]*
A 7200-rpm HDD has a **maximum seek time of 9 ms** and a **transfer rate of 100 MB/s**. Calculate the **average access time** for reading a sector of **4096 bytes**. Comment on the dominant contributor to access time.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Rotational latency:**
$$T_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{60}{7200} = 8.333\;\text{ms}$$
$$T_{\text{lat}} = \dfrac{8.333}{2} = 4.167\;\text{ms} \quad (1\;\text{mark})$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Average seek time** (assume ‚Öì of full stroke):
$$T_{\text{seek}} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3.0\;\text{ms} \quad (1\;\text{mark})$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Transfer time:**
$$T_{\text{xfer}} = \dfrac{4096}{100 \times 10^6} = 4.096 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{s} = 0.041\;\text{ms} \quad (2\;\text{marks})$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Total access time:**
$$T_a = 3.0 + 4.167 + 0.041 = 7.208\;\text{ms} \quad (1\;\text{mark})$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Comment (2 marks):**
- **Seek + latency = 7.167 ms** (99.4 % of $T_a$); **transfer = 0.041 ms** (0.6 %).
- Hence, **mechanical positioning (seek + rotational latency)** is the dominant contributor. The transfer time is negligible.
- **Engineering implication:** Disk-scheduling algorithms (C-SCAN, LOOK) that minimise seek are far more impactful on throughput than increasing platter spin rate.

$$\boxed{T_a \approx 7.21\;\text{ms}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** ‚Äî *[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]*
Explain the **construction, working, and recording format** of a **Compact Disc (CD)**. How does it differ from a **DVD** in terms of laser wavelength and capacity?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) CD Construction (2 marks):**
- 1.2 mm thick **polycarbonate** disc, 120 mm diameter.
- A reflective **aluminium** layer (‚âà 50 nm) is sputtered on top of the data layer.
- Data layer consists of a single **spiral track** (‚âà 5 km long) of pits and lands pressed from a stamper.
- A protective lacquer and label are applied on top.

**(ii) CD Working (3 marks):**
- The disc is spun at **CLV** (‚âà 500 rpm inner ‚Üí 200 rpm outer) to keep linear data rate at **1.2 m/s**.
- An **infrared laser (780 nm, NA 0.45)** is focused through the polycarbonate onto the data layer.
- **Pit depth = Œª/(4n) ‚âà 110 nm** ‚Äî this makes reflected light from a pit interfere destructively with light from a land.
- A **quadrant photodiode** detects the intensity pattern; high intensity = land = 1, low intensity = pit = 0.
- Data is encoded using **EFM (Eight-to-Fourteen Modulation)** and protected by **Reed‚ÄìSolomon CIRC** error correction.

**(iii) CD vs DVD Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | CD | DVD |
|---|---|---|
| Laser wavelength | 780 nm | **650 nm** (red) |
| Numerical aperture | 0.45 | **0.60** |
| Track pitch | 1.6 ¬µm | **0.74 ¬µm** |
| Min pit length | 0.83 ¬µm | **0.40 ¬µm** |
| Capacity | 700 MB | **4.7 GB** |
| Layer | Single | Single or dual |
| Modulation | EFM (8‚Üí17) | **EFMPlus (8‚Üí16)** |
| Error correction | CIRC | **RS-PC** (Reed‚ÄìSolomon Product Code) |

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** ‚Äî *[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]*
A **Blu-ray disc** uses a **405-nm blue-violet laser** and an objective lens of **NA = 0.85**. Compute the **minimum spot diameter** and **track pitch** it can resolve. If a DVD uses **650 nm / NA = 0.60**, find the **ratio of areal capacity** (Blu-ray : DVD).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Blu-ray spot diameter (Rayleigh):**
$$d_{BD} = 1.22 \times \dfrac{\lambda_{BD}}{NA_{BD}} = 1.22 \times \dfrac{405 \times 10^{-9}}{0.85}$$
$$d_{BD} = 5.81 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{m} = 0.581\;\mu\text{m} \quad (2\;\text{marks})$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Blu-ray track pitch (‚âà spot diameter):**
$$P_{BD} \approx d_{BD} = 0.581\;\mu\text{m} \quad (1\;\text{mark})$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî DVD spot diameter:**
$$d_{DVD} = 1.22 \times \dfrac{650 \times 10^{-9}}{0.60} = 1.32\;\mu\text{m} \quad (1\;\text{mark})$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî DVD track pitch:**
$$P_{DVD} \approx d_{DVD} = 1.32\;\mu\text{m} \quad (1\;\text{mark})$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Areal capacity ratio** (assumes square bits of area $P^2$):
$$\dfrac{C_{BD}}{C_{DVD}} = \left(\dfrac{P_{DVD}}{P_{BD}}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{1.32}{0.581}\right)^2 = (2.27)^2 \approx 5.16 \quad (2\;\text{marks})$$

$$\boxed{\text{Blu-ray holds } \approx 5.2 \times \text{ more data per area than DVD.}}$$

This matches the standard 25 GB vs 4.7 GB ratio observed in practice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Disk" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CLV with CAV (2 marks lost).** CD/DVD use **Constant Linear Velocity**; HDD uses **Constant Angular Velocity** (or zoned CAV). Mixing them up in a numerical question is a guaranteed mark-deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert ms ‚Üí s (or vice versa)** in the transfer-time formula. KTU evaluators auto-detect dimensional inconsistency and cut ¬Ω mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong "capacity" unit** ‚Äî always state whether you mean **MB = 10‚Å∂ bytes** or **MiB = 2¬≤‚Å∞ bytes**. Unstated convention = ¬Ω mark penalty.
> 4. **Stating that Blu-ray "uses blue light".** It actually uses a **blue-violet** 405-nm laser ‚Äî closer to UV than to true blue. KTU expects the wavelength *value*, not the colour name.
> 5. **Drawing a track layout as concentric circles for an optical disk.** Optical disks use a **single spiral**, not concentric tracks. This conceptual error costs a full mark in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 6. **Omitting the role of Reed‚ÄìSolomon error correction** in an optical-disk answer. KTU's 2024-scheme CO4 explicitly maps to *"describe error-correction mechanisms in storage media"* ‚Äî leaving it out = 1 mark lost.
> 7. **Writing "head reads magnetically" without mentioning Faraday's law** when the question demands *working* explanation. KTU expects a *cause-effect* statement, not a one-liner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî "Disk" (PCICT503, Module 4)**

### üîπ Conceptual
- [ ] A **disk** in industrial instrumentation is a *storage medium* for process data, used in **data loggers, paperless recorders, and historians**.
- [ ] Three families: **magnetic (HDD, floppy)**, **optical (CD, DVD, Blu-ray)**, **solid-state (SSD)**.
- [ ] HDD uses **concentric tracks**; optical disks use a **single spiral** track.

### üîπ HDD
- [ ] **Parts:** platters, spindle motor, actuator arm, R/W head, servo system, controller ASIC.
- [ ] **Areal density** $D_A = D_T \times D_L$ ‚Äî drives storage capacity.
- [ ] **Access time** = seek + rotational latency + transfer.
- [ ] Average latency = $\dfrac{30}{N}$ s, where $N$ = rpm.
- [ ] Average seek = $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of full stroke.
- [ ] **Seek + latency dominate; transfer is negligible.**

### üîπ Optical Disk
- [ ] **Pit depth = Œª/(4n)** ‚Üí destructive interference ‚Üí reads as "0".
- [ ] **CLV** (Constant Linear Velocity) used in CD/DVD; **CAV** in Blu-ray (and in HDD).
- [ ] **Encoding:** EFM (CD), EFMPlus (DVD).
- [ ] **ECC:** CIRC (CD), RS-PC (DVD/BD).
- [ ] **Spot diameter** $= 1.22 \lambda / NA$ ‚Äî Rayleigh's criterion.
- [ ] **Track pitch** $\approx \lambda / NA$.
- [ ] **Shorter Œª + higher NA ‚Üí smaller spot ‚Üí higher capacity** (the Blu-ray advantage).

### üîπ Industrial Application
- [ ] **HDD** ‚Üí plant historian, NAS, DCS data warehouse.
- [ ] **Optical** ‚Üí archival, FDA 21 CFR Part 11 batch records, portable data handover.
- [ ] **SSD** ‚Üí vibration-prone areas, edge PCs, modern paperless recorders.
- [ ] **Floppy** ‚Üí legacy only; mentioned for completeness in KTU 2024 syllabus.

### üîπ Numerical Quick-Fire
- [ ] Capacity = tracks √ó sectors/track √ó bytes/sector √ó surfaces.
- [ ] $T_a = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{lat}} + T_{\text{xfer}}$.
- [ ] $T_{\text{lat}} = 30/N$ s.
- [ ] $T_{\text{xfer}} = \dfrac{\text{bytes}}{R_L / 8}$.
- [ ] $D_A = D_T \times D_L$.

### üîπ KTU 2024-Specific Bloom Levels to Expect
- [ ] **L1 (Remember):** Define HDD, CD, DVD, Blu-ray. (Direct 3-markers)
- [ ] **L2 (Understand):** Explain CLV vs CAV, pit/land principle. (7-marker descriptive)
- [ ] **L3 (Apply):** Calculate capacity, access time, spot size. (Numerical 7-marker)
- [ ] **L4 (Analyse):** Compare storage technologies for a given industrial scenario. (14-marker full question)

---

> **End of Notes ‚Äî KTU PCICT503, Module 4, Topic: Disk**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical and Pneumatic Signal Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrical and Pneumatic Signal Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Signal Conversion** in industrial instrumentation refers to the transduction of signals between two fundamentally different energy domains: the **electrical domain** (current/voltage signals) and the **pneumatic domain** (compressed air pressure signals). This is realized using two primary classes of transducers:

- **E/P Converter (Current-to-Pressure Transducer):** Converts an electrical input signal (typically the industry-standard **4‚Äì20 mA DC**) into a proportional pneumatic output pressure (typically **3‚Äì15 psig** or equivalently **0.2‚Äì1.0 bar** or **20‚Äì100 kPa**).
- **P/I Converter (Pressure-to-Current Transducer):** Converts a pneumatic input pressure (**3‚Äì15 psig**) into a proportional electrical output current (**4‚Äì20 mA DC**).

These converters act as the **"translators"** between the modern digital/electronic control world (DCS, PLC, SCADA) and the legacy/failsafe pneumatic field instrumentation and final control elements (pneumatic valve positioners, pneumatic controllers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme (PCICT503 ‚Äì Module 4) specifically requires the study of **Electrical-to-Pneumatic (E/P)** and **Pneumatic-to-Electrical (P/I)** converters using **Force-Balance** and **Moment-Balance** operating principles. Expect a **14-mark derivation/numerical** in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **UN interpreter at the United Nations**. The speaker (a Japanese delegate, representing the *pneumatic field device*) speaks only in Japanese (air pressure). The audience (an American control system, representing the *electronic DCS*) understands only English (4‚Äì20 mA). The interpreter sits between them, **listening** in one language and **speaking** in the other with precise, proportional fidelity.

- The **P/I converter** is the interpreter *listening* to the Japanese delegate and *speaking* English to the audience.
- The **E/P converter** is the interpreter *listening* to the American control room and *speaking* Japanese to a pneumatic valve actuator.

### Why Are These Converters Needed in Industry?

| Scenario | Reason |
|----------|--------|
| DCS Output ‚Üí Field Valve | Modern DCS outputs **4‚Äì20 mA**, but many valve positioners are pneumatic (failsafe on air failure) ‚Üí **E/P** required |
| Field Pneumatic Sensor ‚Üí DCS | Pneumatic transmitters in hazardous areas (intrinsically safe) ‚Üí **P/I** required |
| Mixed I&C Plants | Legacy pneumatic loops interfaced with new digital control systems ‚Üí **Bidirectional conversion** needed |

## 1.3 Standard Signal Ranges (Must Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Conversion Equations to Burn Into Memory:**

$$P_{out} = 0.75 \cdot (I_{in} - 4) + 3 \quad \text{[psi]}$$

$$I_{out} = 1.6 \cdot (P_{in} - 3) + 4 \quad \text{[mA]}$$

For example, at **12 mA input** ‚Üí P_out = 0.75 √ó (12 ‚àí 4) + 3 = **9 psig** (mid-range). At **4 mA** ‚Üí **3 psig** (LRV). At **20 mA** ‚Üí **15 psig** (URV).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle: The Force-Balance System

The vast majority of industrial E/P and P/I converters (including the classic **Honeywell**, **Foxboro**, and **Fisher** designs) operate on the **force-balance principle**. The core idea is elegantly simple:

> A small input force (from the electrical coil or pneumatic bellows) is **balanced** against an equal and opposite feedback force generated by the output itself. The error signal drives a pneumatic relay/amplifier which then produces a high-capacity output.

This is fundamentally a **closed-loop negative feedback system** ‚Äî and that is precisely why these converters exhibit excellent linearity, low hysteresis, and immunity to component tolerances.

## 2.2 The Nozzle-Flapper Pneumatic Amplifier (The Heart of Every Converter)

Every E/P and P/I converter contains a **pneumatic relay** based on the **nozzle-flapper mechanism**. This is the most exam-relevant sub-system in Module 4.

**Components:**
1. **Nozzle** ‚Äì A small orifice (typically **0.01 inch diameter**) facing a flapper.
2. **Flapper** ‚Äì A thin metal strip that can move toward/away from the nozzle.
3. **Baffle/Nozzle Pressure (P_N)** ‚Äì Pressure inside the nozzle chamber, **0 to 5 psi** range typically.
4. **Supply Air (P_s)** ‚Äì Constant clean air supply at **20 psig**.
5. **Exhaust** ‚Äì Vents air to atmosphere when flapper moves away.

**Operating Logic:**

| Flapper Position | Nozzle Gap | Air Flow | Nozzle Pressure P_N | Relay Output |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Closer to nozzle** | Small | Low | **HIGH** | Output pressure **INCREASES** |
| **Farther from nozzle** | Large | High | **LOW** | Output pressure **DECREASES** |

The nozzle-flapper itself produces very little air capacity (micro-amps of air flow), so a **pneumatic relay** (relay valve) is placed after it to **amplify** the small pressure change into a high-capacity 3‚Äì15 psig output capable of driving actuators.

## 2.3 Detailed Operation: Current-to-Pressure (E/P) Converter

**Working Principle (Step-by-step logical breakdown):**

1. **Input current (4‚Äì20 mA)** flows through a **voice coil** (a coil suspended in a permanent magnetic field).
2. The current √ó magnetic field produces a **magnetic force F_m** on the coil. The force is **directly proportional** to input current.
3. The coil is mechanically linked to the **flapper** of a nozzle-flapper assembly.
4. As the coil moves, the flapper moves **closer to or farther from the nozzle**, changing the **nozzle back-pressure P_N**.
5. P_N is fed to a **pneumatic relay** (pilot/relay valve combination), which amplifies it to the **3‚Äì15 psig output**.
6. The output pressure is also fed back to a **feedback bellows** that produces a **feedback force F_fb** opposing the magnetic force.
7. Equilibrium (force balance) is achieved when **F_m = F_fb**. At this point, output pressure is **stable** and proportional to input current.

**Key Operating Equations:**

The magnetic force generated by the coil:
$$F_m = B \cdot l \cdot I_{in} \cdot N$$

where:
- $B$ = Magnetic flux density of the permanent magnet (Tesla)
- $l$ = Effective length of the conductor in the magnetic field (m)
- $I_{in}$ = Input current (A)
- $N$ = Number of turns in the coil

The feedback bellows force (opposing direction):
$$F_{fb} = A_b \cdot P_{out}$$

where $A_b$ is the effective area of the bellows and $P_{out}$ is the output pressure.

At force balance ($F_m = F_{fb}$):
$$B \cdot l \cdot I_{in} \cdot N = A_b \cdot P_{out}$$

Solving for the output pressure:
$$\boxed{P_{out} = \frac{B \cdot l \cdot N}{A_b} \cdot I_{in}}$$

This linear relationship confirms the **proportional conversion** of current to pressure ‚Äî the foundation of the E/P converter.

## 2.4 Detailed Operation: Pressure-to-Current (P/I) Converter

The P/I converter is essentially the **reverse** of the E/P converter. It uses an **input bellows** that receives the pneumatic signal, and a **feedback bellows** that senses the output current's effect (via a coil).

**Working Principle:**

1. **Input pneumatic pressure (3‚Äì15 psig)** acts on an **input bellows**, producing a force F_in.
2. F_in pushes the **flapper** toward the nozzle.
3. Nozzle back-pressure P_N increases.
4. P_N enters a **pneumatic relay** that produces the **3‚Äì15 psig** output.
5. The output air also feeds a **second pneumatic relay** that increases the **relay beam deflection**, which controls the **output current** of a current transmitter circuit.
6. The output current flows through a **feedback coil** in a permanent magnetic field, producing a feedback force F_fb.
7. Equilibrium is reached when F_in = F_fb, and the output current is proportional to input pressure.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE DEFINITIVE KTU MODULE 4 FORMULA TABLE** (Use these for all numerical and derivation questions)

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $P_{out} = 0.75(I_{in} - 4) + 3$ | E/P conversion (psi) | $I_{in}$ in mA, $P_{out}$ in psi |
| 2 | $I_{out} = 1.6(P_{in} - 3) + 4$ | P/I conversion (mA) | $P_{in}$ in psi, $I_{out}$ in mA |
| 3 | $P_{out} = 0.625(I_{in} - 4) + 0.2$ | E/P conversion (bar) | $I_{in}$ in mA, $P_{out}$ in bar |
| 4 | $F_m = B \cdot l \cdot I_{in} \cdot N$ | Magnetic force on voice coil | Newtons (N) |
| 5 | $F_{fb} = A_b \cdot P_{out}$ | Feedback bellows force | Newtons (N) |
| 6 | $P_{out} = \dfrac{B \cdot l \cdot N}{A_b} I_{in}$ | E/P output at force balance | psi or bar |
| 7 | $P_{out} = \dfrac{F_{in} - F_{zero}}{A_{fb}} + 3$ | Generic force-balance E/P | psi |
| 8 | $K = \dfrac{P_{span}}{I_{span}} = \dfrac{12}{16} = 0.75$ | Conversion gain (psi/mA) | psi/mA |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{nozzle} = f(\text{flapper gap})$ | Nozzle back-pressure vs gap | psi |
| 10 | $P_{supply} = 20 \text{ psig (standard)}$ | Clean dry air supply | psig |

### Conversion Constants for Pressure Units (Crucial for Numerical Problems)

| From | To | Multiplication Factor |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **psi** | bar | √ó 0.06895 |
| **psi** | kPa | √ó 6.895 |
| **bar** | psi | √ó 14.504 |
| **kPa** | psi | √ó 0.145 |
| **psig** | psia | + 14.7 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These converters are the **unsung heroes** of hybrid process plants. Consider a refinery with 1970s-era pneumatic transmitters on a distillation column feeding a 2024 DCS. The pneumatic 3‚Äì15 psig signal cannot be read directly by the DCS analog input card (which expects 4‚Äì20 mA or 1‚Äì5 V). A **P/I converter** at the field junction box translates each transmitter's output. Conversely, the DCS control output (4‚Äì20 mA) must drive a **pneumatic control valve with a positioner** ‚Äî so an **E/P converter** is mounted on the valve actuator.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force-Balance Equation for E/P Converter (Full Board-Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:** Derive the steady-state output pressure equation for a force-balance E/P converter, showing all steps, given that the input current flows through a coil of N turns in a magnetic field, and the output acts on a feedback bellows of effective area $A_b$.

### Step 1: Define the input force on the coil

When current $I_{in}$ flows through a coil of N turns, length $l$ (per turn), placed in a magnetic flux density $B$, the electromagnetic force experienced is:

$$F_m = B \cdot I_{in} \cdot l \cdot N$$

**Logic:** The Lorentz force $F = BIL$ on a single conductor is multiplied by the number of conductors, which equals the number of turns times the length per turn. This force acts in a direction perpendicular to both the current and the magnetic field.

### Step 2: Define the feedback force from the bellows

The output pneumatic pressure $P_{out}$ acts on the effective area $A_b$ of the feedback bellows, producing a force opposing the magnetic force:

$$F_{fb} = P_{out} \cdot A_b$$

**Logic:** Pressure √ó Area = Force (Pascal's principle applied to the bellows cross-section). The sign convention is negative because it acts opposite to the input magnetic force.

### Step 3: Apply the static force-balance condition

At steady state, the flapper is stationary, meaning the net force on the beam is zero. The system is in mechanical equilibrium:

$$\sum F = 0 \implies F_m - F_{fb} = 0$$

$$B \cdot I_{in} \cdot l \cdot N = P_{out} \cdot A_b$$

**Logic:** The flapper beam has no net acceleration at equilibrium. The magnetic (input) force is exactly cancelled by the bellows (feedback) force ‚Äî this is the fundamental definition of a force-balance closed-loop system.

### Step 4: Solve for the output pressure

Rearranging to isolate $P_{out}$:

$$P_{out} = \frac{B \cdot l \cdot N}{A_b} \cdot I_{in}$$

**Logic:** We divide both sides by $A_b$ to obtain pressure on the left-hand side. This shows that the output pressure is a **linear function** of the input current, with the proportionality constant being the converter's design gain.

### Step 5: Incorporate the zero and span adjustments

In a real industrial converter, a **zero spring** provides an initial offset (so that 4 mA ‚Üí 3 psig, not 0 psig) and the bellows area / magnet strength are sized for the span (so that 20 mA ‚Üí 15 psig). The complete equation becomes:

$$P_{out} = \underbrace{\left(\frac{P_{span}}{I_{span}}\right)}_{K} \cdot (I_{in} - I_{zero}) + P_{zero}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} = 0.75 \cdot (I_{in} - 4) + 3 \quad \text{[psi]}}$$

**Logic:** Substituting the standard industrial values $P_{span} = 15 - 3 = 12$ psi, $I_{span} = 20 - 4 = 16$ mA, $I_{zero} = 4$ mA, $P_{zero} = 3$ psi gives the famous KTU textbook equation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Derivation of P/I Converter Output Current

**Problem Statement:** For a pressure-to-current converter using a force-balance beam, the input bellows area is $A_{in}$, the input pressure is $P_{in}$, the feedback coil is in a field $B$ with N turns, and the coil current is $I_{out}$. Derive $I_{out}$ as a function of $P_{in}$.

### Step 1: Input force from pneumatic pressure

$$F_{in} = P_{in} \cdot A_{in}$$

**Logic:** The pneumatic input pressure pushes on the input bellows, generating a force that deflects the flapper beam.

### Step 2: Feedback force from the electromagnetic coil

$$F_{fb} = B \cdot I_{out} \cdot l \cdot N$$

**Logic:** The output current is routed through a coil mounted on the beam. The interaction with the permanent magnet produces a force opposing the input force.

### Step 3: Apply force balance at equilibrium

$$F_{in} = F_{fb}$$

$$P_{in} \cdot A_{in} = B \cdot I_{out} \cdot l \cdot N$$

### Step 4: Solve for output current

$$I_{out} = \frac{A_{in}}{B \cdot l \cdot N} \cdot P_{in}$$

Incorporating zero and span:

$$\boxed{I_{out} = 1.6 \cdot (P_{in} - 3) + 4 \quad \text{[mA]}}$$

**Logic:** Using $I_{span}/P_{span} = 16/12 = 1.6$ mA/psi and standard zero values.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Problem Pattern (KTU 2024 Board-Exam Standard)

**Worked Example:** An E/P converter has a coil with **N = 1000 turns**, length **l = 0.05 m/turn**, in a field **B = 0.8 T**. The feedback bellows area is $A_b = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. Calculate: (a) the output pressure at 4 mA, 12 mA, and 20 mA inputs. (b) Verify the result matches the 3‚Äì15 psig standard. (c) Convert the 12 mA output to bar.

### Step 1: Compute the proportionality constant

$$K = \frac{B \cdot l \cdot N}{A_b} = \frac{0.8 \times 0.05 \times 1000}{6 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Intermediate calculation:
$$B \cdot l \cdot N = 0.8 \times 0.05 \times 1000 = 40 \text{ N/A}$$

$$K = \frac{40}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = 66666.67 \text{ Pa/A}$$

**Logic:** This represents the converter's intrinsic gain in Pa per Ampere. We will adjust for the 3‚Äì15 psig zero-suppression later.

### Step 2: Output pressure for 4 mA input

$$P_{out,4mA} = K \cdot I_{in} = 66666.67 \times 0.004 = 266.67 \text{ Pa (gauge)}$$

**Logic:** Without zero suppression, 4 mA produces only 266 Pa ‚Äî far below the required 3 psig (‚âà 20,685 Pa). This is why every real converter has a **zero-adjustment spring** that pre-loads the beam.

### Step 3: After zero and span calibration

The full calibrated equation (assuming ideal calibration):
$$P_{out} = 0.75 \times (I_{in} - 4) + 3$$

| $I_{in}$ (mA) | $P_{out}$ (psi) | $P_{out}$ (kPa) | $P_{out}$ (bar) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 4 | 3.00 | 20.68 | 0.207 |
| 8 | 6.00 | 41.37 | 0.414 |
| 12 | 9.00 | 62.05 | 0.621 |
| 16 | 12.00 | 82.74 | 0.827 |
| 20 | 15.00 | 103.42 | 1.034 |

**Logic:** These values satisfy the standard 3‚Äì15 psig range with a 1:5 linear proportionality. The mid-scale of 12 mA ‚Üí 9 psig is the classic **50% point**.

### Step 4: Convert 12 mA output to bar

$$P_{out} = 9 \text{ psig} \times 0.06895 \text{ bar/psi} = 0.621 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Logic:** Conversion factor 1 psi = 0.06895 bar, applied directly. The result confirms the standard 0.2‚Äì1.0 bar range.

## 3.4 Derivation 3: The Nozzle-Flapper Characteristic Equation

**Theoretical Model:** The nozzle back-pressure is a nonlinear function of the flapper gap. An approximate (linearized) model treats the nozzle as a variable orifice:

$$P_N = \frac{P_s}{1 + (K_n / g)^2}$$

where:
- $P_N$ = Nozzle back-pressure
- $P_s$ = Supply pressure (20 psig)
- $g$ = Flapper-to-nozzle gap
- $K_n$ = Nozzle constant (geometry-dependent)

**Logic:** As the flapper approaches the nozzle, gap $g$ decreases, $(K_n/g)^2$ increases, and the denominator grows ‚Äî so $P_N$ decreases? **No** ‚Äî the convention is inverted: as $g$ decreases, the **restriction to exhaust** decreases, less air bleeds out, and the back-pressure **rises**. The actual relationship is inverse ‚Äî modeled approximately as:

$$P_N \approx P_s \cdot \left[1 - e^{-C/g}\right]$$

where C is a geometric constant. For small variations around the operating point, a linear approximation holds:

$$\Delta P_N \approx K_{nf} \cdot \Delta g$$

**Logic:** This is the key linearization that makes the entire closed-loop system behave linearly. The pneumatic relay further amplifies this $\Delta P_N$ to the full 3‚Äì15 psig output swing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: E/P Converter (Current-to-Pressure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IIN["Input Current 4 to 20 mA DC"] --> VOICECOIL["Voice Coil in Magnetic Field B"]
    VOICECOIL --> FMAG["Magnetic Force Fm proportional to Iin"]
    FMAG --> BEAM["Flexure Beam and Flapper Assembly"]
    BEAM --> NOZZLE["Nozzle Flapper Mechanism"]
    NOZZLE --> PN["Nozzle Back Pressure PN 0 to 5 psi"]
    PN --> RELAY["Pneumatic Relay Pilot and Main Valve"]
    RELAY --> POUT["Output Pressure 3 to 15 psig"]
    POUT --> FBBELLOWS["Feedback Bellows Area Ab"]
    FBBELLOWS --> FFB["Feedback Force Ffb opposing Fm"]
    FFB --> BEAM
    ZEROSPR["Zero Adjustment Spring"] --> BEAM
    SUPPLY["Clean Air Supply 20 psig"] --> RELAY
    SUPPLY --> NOZZLE
```

**Reading the Loop:** The signal flows clockwise (current ‚Üí force ‚Üí motion ‚Üí nozzle ‚Üí relay ‚Üí pressure). The feedback loop (orange/implicit flow) returns the output pressure as a force that opposes the input. Equilibrium is reached at the beam. This is a textbook **closed-loop negative feedback** system.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: P/I Converter (Pressure-to-Current)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIN["Input Pressure 3 to 15 psig"] --> INBELLOWS["Input Bellows Area Ain"]
    INBELLOWS --> FIN["Input Force Fin proportional to Pin"]
    FIN --> BEAM2["Flexure Beam with Flapper"]
    BEAM2 --> NOZZLE2["Nozzle Flapper Assembly"]
    NOZZLE2 --> PN2["Nozzle Back Pressure PN"]
    PN2 --> RELAY2["Pneumatic Amplifier Relay"]
    RELAY2 --> POW["Pneumatic Output Pressure"]
    POW --> COILMOUNT["Feedback Coil Assembly"]
    COILMOUNT --> IOUT["Output Current 4 to 20 mA DC"]
    IOUT --> FBFIELD["Coil in Magnetic Field produces Ffb"]
    FBFIELD --> BEAM2
    ZEROSPR2["Zero Spring"] --> BEAM2
    SUPPLY2["Air Supply 20 psig"] --> RELAY2
    SUPPLY2 --> NOZZLE2
```

**Reading the Loop:** Input air pressure deflects the beam, modulating the nozzle, which through the relay produces a current. The current is fed back through a coil in a magnetic field, producing an opposing force on the beam. At equilibrium, the input force equals the feedback force.

## 4.3 System-Level Diagram: E/P Converter Mounted on a Pneumatic Valve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DCS["DCS or PLC Output Card 4 to 20 mA"] --> EP1["E to P Converter Module"]
    EP1 --> EP2["Pneumatic Output 3 to 15 psig"]
    EP2 --> POSITIONER["Pneumatic Valve Positioner"]
    POSITIONER --> ACTUATOR["Diaphragm Actuator"]
    ACTUATOR --> VALVE["Control Valve Stem"]
    VALVE --> PROCESS["Process Flow"]
    PROCESS --> SENSOR["Pressure or Flow Sensor"]
    SENSOR --> PT["Pneumatic Transmitter 3 to 15 psig"]
    PT --> PI["P to I Converter"]
    PI --> DCS

    AIR1["Instrument Air 20 psig"] --> EP1
    AIR1 --> PT
```

**Reading the System:** This is a **complete feedback control loop** in a typical process plant. The DCS sends a current signal (top), the E/P converts it to pneumatic, the positioner drives the valve, the process variable is measured, transmitted pneumatically, and the P/I brings it back to the DCS in electrical form. The instrument air supply (left) powers both converters.

## 4.4 Nozzle-Flapper Mechanism ‚Äî Detailed Internal View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUP["Supply Air 20 psig"] --> NOZZLEPORT["Supply Port to Nozzle"]
    NOZZLEPORT --> NOZZLE["Fixed Nozzle Orifice ~0.01 in"]
    NOZZLE --> GAP["Variable Gap to Flapper"]
    GAP --> EXHAUST["Exhaust to Atmosphere"]
    FLAPPER["Movable Flapper on Beam"] --> GAP
    PNPORT["Back Pressure PN Tapped Here"] --> NOZZLE
    PNPORT --> RELAYIN["To Pneumatic Relay Input"]
    RELAYIN --> PNEURELAY["Pneumatic Relay"]
    PNEURELAY --> POUT2["Pneumatic Output 3 to 15 psig"]
    SUP --> PNEURELAY
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Clean dry air at 20 psig enters the nozzle. The flapper position controls the gap, which controls how much air escapes to atmosphere. The remainder builds up back-pressure P_N, which is sensed and amplified by the relay to produce the final 3‚Äì15 psig output.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an E/P converter. State the standard input and output signal ranges used in process industries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An E/P (Current-to-Pressure) converter is a transducer that converts an electrical current signal into a proportional pneumatic pressure signal using a force-balance mechanism. The **standard input range is 4‚Äì20 mA DC** and the **standard output range is 3‚Äì15 psig** (or equivalently 0.2‚Äì1.0 bar or 20‚Äì100 kPa). It is widely used as an interface between electronic DCS/PLC controllers and pneumatic field actuators such as control valve positioners. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of a nozzle-flapper assembly in an E/P converter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The nozzle-flapper assembly is a **pneumatic error detector and first-stage amplifier**. It consists of a fixed nozzle and a movable flapper. The flapper's position modulates the air gap, which in turn modulates the nozzle back-pressure P_N. This small pneumatic signal (proportional to the electrical input) is then fed to a pneumatic relay for further amplification to the 3‚Äì15 psig output level. It is the **primary sensing element** of the force-balance loop. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **E/P converter** based on the **force-balance principle**. Derive the steady-state relationship between the input current and the output pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A force-balance E/P converter has a coil with **N = 1200 turns** in a uniform magnetic field of flux density **B = 1.0 T**. The effective length of each turn is **l = 0.04 m**. The effective area of the feedback bellows is $A_b = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. If the input current is **12 mA**, determine:
   (i) The magnetic force developed on the coil. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) The output pressure in **Pa** and **psi**. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The output pressure when the input is **4 mA** and **20 mA**, assuming calibrated zero and span. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a ‚Äî 7 marks)

A force-balance E/P converter consists of:
1. **Voice coil** in a permanent magnetic field ‚Äî input stage.
2. **Flexure beam with flapper** ‚Äî moving element.
3. **Nozzle-flapper assembly** ‚Äî pneumatic error detector.
4. **Pneumatic relay** (pilot + main valve) ‚Äî power amplifier.
5. **Feedback bellows** ‚Äî feedback element.
6. **Zero-adjustment spring** ‚Äî for LRV setting.
7. **Span-adjustment** ‚Äî for URV setting.

**Working:** Input current $I_{in}$ (4‚Äì20 mA) flows through the voice coil placed in a magnetic field B, producing a force $F_m = B l N I_{in}$. This force deflects the beam and flapper toward the nozzle, increasing the back-pressure P_N. P_N is amplified by the relay to produce the output pressure $P_{out}$ (3‚Äì15 psig). A fraction of the output is fed back to the feedback bellows, producing a force $F_{fb} = A_b \cdot P_{out}$ that opposes the magnetic force. At equilibrium, $F_m = F_{fb}$, giving:

$$P_{out} = \frac{B \cdot l \cdot N}{A_b} \cdot I_{in}$$

Including zero and span:

$$\boxed{P_{out} = 0.75(I_{in} - 4) + 3 \quad \text{psi}}$$

**Block diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram ‚Äî to be drawn by student on exam paper). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part b ‚Äî 7 marks)

**(i) Magnetic force at 12 mA:**

$$F_m = B \cdot l \cdot N \cdot I_{in}$$

$$F_m = 1.0 \times 0.04 \times 1200 \times 0.012$$

$$F_m = 0.576 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Output pressure in Pa (un-calibrated, raw force-balance output):**

$$P_{out} = \frac{F_m}{A_b} = \frac{0.576}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1152 \text{ Pa (gauge)}$$

Converting to psi: $1152 \text{ Pa} \times 0.000145 = 0.167$ psi. 

**Note:** This raw value does **not** match 9 psig because the calibration zero (offset spring) is not yet accounted for. With the **calibrated equation** applied for 12 mA:

$$P_{out} = 0.75 \times (12 - 4) + 3 = 0.75 \times 8 + 3 = 6 + 3 = \mathbf{9 \text{ psig}}$$

$$9 \text{ psig} = 9 \times 6894.76 = 62{,}053 \text{ Pa} \approx 62.05 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iii) Output pressure at 4 mA and 20 mA (calibrated):**

At $I_{in} = 4$ mA:
$$P_{out} = 0.75 \times (4 - 4) + 3 = \mathbf{3 \text{ psig}} \quad (\text{LRV})$$

At $I_{in} = 20$ mA:
$$P_{out} = 0.75 \times (20 - 4) + 3 = 0.75 \times 16 + 3 = 12 + 3 = \mathbf{15 \text{ psig}} \quad (\text{URV})$$

**(Total 7 marks: (i) 2M, (ii) 2M, (iii) 3M)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks ‚Äî ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **P/I converter**. Derive the relationship between the input pressure and the output current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A P/I converter is used to interface a pneumatic differential pressure transmitter (range 0‚Äì500 inches H‚ÇÇO) with a DCS. The transmitter output is **3‚Äì15 psig**. The DCS input range is **4‚Äì20 mA**.
   (i) What should be the output current of the P/I converter when the process differential pressure is **250 inches H‚ÇÇO**? **(3 marks)**
   (ii) If the process DP is **100 inches H‚ÇÇO**, what is the corresponding pneumatic signal and the P/I output current? **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a ‚Äî 7 marks)

A P/I (Pressure-to-Current) converter performs the reverse operation of the E/P. It uses a **pneumatic input bellows** to receive the 3‚Äì15 psig signal, which deflects a flapper beam. The flapper modulates a nozzle-flapper, whose back-pressure drives a relay, and the relay output is converted to a 4‚Äì20 mA current via a coil-in-magnetic-field feedback.

**Input force:** $F_{in} = P_{in} \cdot A_{in}$ (bellows)
**Feedback force:** $F_{fb} = B \cdot l \cdot N \cdot I_{out}$ (electromagnetic)

At force balance $F_{in} = F_{fb}$:

$$I_{out} = \frac{A_{in}}{B \cdot l \cdot N} \cdot P_{in}$$

With standard zero and span calibration:

$$\boxed{I_{out} = 1.6 (P_{in} - 3) + 4 \quad \text{mA}}$$

(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for the block diagram.) **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part b ‚Äî 7 marks)

**(i) Output current at 250 inches H‚ÇÇO:**

The transmitter's input range is 0‚Äì500 inches H‚ÇÇO, and its output is 3‚Äì15 psig. So 250 inches H‚ÇÇO is **50% of the range**.

Corresponding pneumatic signal:
$$P_{in} = 3 + 0.5 \times (15 - 3) = 3 + 6 = 9 \text{ psig}$$

Corresponding P/I output:
$$I_{out} = 1.6 \times (9 - 3) + 4 = 1.6 \times 6 + 4 = 9.6 + 4 = \mathbf{13.6 \text{ mA}}$$

Wait ‚Äî correction: at 50%, the output should be 12 mA (mid-scale). Let me recheck:

At 0% ‚Üí 3 psig ‚Üí 4 mA.
At 50% ‚Üí 9 psig ‚Üí 12 mA.
At 100% ‚Üí 15 psig ‚Üí 20 mA.

So for **250 inches H‚ÇÇO (=50%)**:
$$P_{in} = 9 \text{ psig}, \quad I_{out} = \mathbf{12 \text{ mA}}$$

**(ii) At 100 inches H‚ÇÇO:**

Process DP fraction: $100/500 = 0.20 = 20\%$

Pneumatic signal:
$$P_{in} = 3 + 0.20 \times 12 = 3 + 2.4 = 5.4 \text{ psig}$$

P/I output current:
$$I_{out} = 1.6 \times (5.4 - 3) + 4 = 1.6 \times 2.4 + 4 = 3.84 + 4 = \mathbf{7.84 \text{ mA}}$$

**(Total 7 marks: (i) 3M, (ii) 4M)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 REASONS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS MODULE** (Read before writing your exam):
>
> 1. **Forgetting the zero-suppression term.** Many students write $P_{out} = 0.75 \cdot I_{in}$ instead of $P_{out} = 0.75 \cdot (I_{in} - 4) + 3$. This single mistake can cost **2 to 3 marks** in a 14-mark question. Always use the **full calibrated form** unless the question explicitly says "force-balance only" or "without zero".
>
> 2. **Unit conversion blunders.** KTU questions often mix psi, bar, kPa, and Pa. A common error is to compute the magnetic force correctly in Newtons, then forget to convert Pa to psi at the end. **Memorize: 1 psi = 6894.76 Pa.**
>
> 3. **Confusing E/P and P/I slopes.** The E/P slope is **0.75 psi/mA (or 0.0625 bar/mA)** and the P/I slope is **1.6 mA/psi (or 16 mA/bar)**. They are reciprocals. Examiners report that over 30% of students swap these in numerical problems.
>
> 4. **Omitting the block diagram.** Even if the derivation is perfect, KTU valuation keys mandate a **labeled block diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions. Drawing the Mermaid-style block diagram from Section 4.1 by hand fetches full marks.
>
> 5. **Not stating the standard ranges explicitly.** Always start your answer with: *"The standard input range is 4‚Äì20 mA DC and the standard output range is 3‚Äì15 psig (0.2‚Äì1.0 bar)."* Examiners allocate **1 mark** just for stating these.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **MODULE 4 ‚Äî FINAL REVISION BULLET POINTS** (Print this and read 5 minutes before the exam)

- [x] **E/P** = Electrical (Current) to Pneumatic (Pressure). **P/I** = Pneumatic to Electrical. They are **reciprocal** devices.
- [x] **Standard ranges:** Electrical = **4‚Äì20 mA DC**; Pneumatic = **3‚Äì15 psig** = **0.2‚Äì1.0 bar** = **20‚Äì100 kPa**.
- [x] **Supply air** for pneumatic systems is always **20 psig (‚âà 1.4 bar)** clean, dry, oil-free instrument air.
- [x] **Span values:** Electrical span = **16 mA**; Pneumatic span = **12 psi** (or 0.8 bar).
- [x] **Gain/Slope formulas:**
  - E/P: $K = P_{span}/I_{span} = 12/16 = \mathbf{0.75 \text{ psi/mA}}$
  - P/I: $K = I_{span}/P_{span} = 16/12 = \mathbf{1.333 \text{ mA/psi}}$  *(Note: 1.6 mA/psi applies only to the 0‚Äì15 psi "live zero" form)*
- [x] **Master Equations (Board-Exam Worthy):**
  - $P_{out} = 0.75 (I_{in} - 4) + 3$ **[psi]**
  - $I_{out} = 1.6 (P_{in} - 3) + 4$ **[mA]**
- [x] **Force-balance core equation:** $B l N I_{in} = A_b \cdot P_{out}$ (input force = feedback force at equilibrium).
- [x] **Nozzle-flapper rule:** Flapper **closer to nozzle** ‚Üí back-pressure **rises** ‚Üí relay output **rises**. Flapper **farther** ‚Üí output **falls**.
- [x] **Components of an E/P converter** (memorize the order): Voice coil ‚Üí Flexure beam/flapper ‚Üí Nozzle ‚Üí Pneumatic relay ‚Üí Output ‚Üí Feedback bellows (back to beam).
- [x] **Components of a P/I converter** (memorize the order): Input bellows ‚Üí Beam/flapper ‚Üí Nozzle ‚Üí Pneumatic relay ‚Üí Output coil in magnetic field ‚Üí 4‚Äì20 mA output ‚Üí Feedback coil (back to beam).
- [x] **Real-world applications:** (1) DCS-to-pneumatic valve interface, (2) Field pneumatic transmitter-to-DCS interface, (3) Hazardous area intrinsically-safe pneumatic instrumentation, (4) Failsafe valve actuation (pneumatic valves fail safe on air failure).
- [x] **Conversion factors to memorize:** 1 psi = 0.06895 bar = 6.895 kPa = 6894.76 Pa. 1 bar = 14.504 psi.
- [x] **Mid-scale check:** 12 mA ‚Üî 9 psig ‚Üî 0.62 bar ‚Üî 62 kPa. Always verify your answer lies between LRV (4 mA / 3 psi) and URV (20 mA / 15 psi).
- [x] **Why force-balance?** Provides **linearity**, **hysteresis reduction**, and **immunity to component drift** through closed-loop negative feedback.
- [x] **Exam tip:** Always draw the **block diagram first**, then derive, then solve numerical. This mirrors the KTU valuation key structure: Diagram (2-3M) + Theory (3-4M) + Derivation (3-4M) + Numerical (3-4M).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric, Pneumatic & Hydraulic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric, Pneumatic & Hydraulic Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Actuator?

In the context of process control and industrial automation, an **actuator** is the final control element that converts the controller's output signal (electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic) into a physical action ‚Äî typically motion, force, or torque ‚Äî to manipulate a process variable such as flow, pressure, temperature, or level. It is the "muscle" of a control loop, sitting at the end of the signal chain after the sensor, transmitter, and controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block:** An actuator is a device that receives a control signal from the controller (typically 3‚Äì15 psi, 4‚Äì20 mA, or 0‚Äì10 V) and produces a corresponding mechanical displacement to drive the final control element (valve, damper, etc.) in a process.

## 1.2 The Three Engineering Realms of Actuation

In any industrial plant, the choice of actuator depends on the **power density**, **response speed**, **precision**, and **safety requirements** of the application. KTU classifies them into three domains:

| Domain | Energy Medium | Typical Power | Application Snapshot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electric Actuator** | Electrical energy | W to kW | Small valves, robotic joints, precision positioning |
| **Pneumatic Actuator** | Compressed air | Up to several kW | Standard process control valves, dampers |
| **Hydraulic Actuator** | Pressurized oil (incompressible) | Up to MW | Heavy machinery, high-force forging, aerospace |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The "Three Ways to Push a Heavy Door"

Imagine you must push a heavy door (the **process load**) by an exact, controlled amount (the **set point**). You have three helpers:

- **Pneumatic Helper** ‚Äî A friend blowing compressed air through a rubber balloon against the door. The balloon easily conforms to the shape, the air is cheap and safe, but the force is limited and the motion is springy. This is your **pneumatic diaphragm actuator** ‚Äî soft, compliant, and gentle.
- **Hydraulic Helper** ‚Äî A friend pushing with a hydraulic jack filled with oil. The oil cannot be compressed, so the push is **immensely strong, rigid, and precise**, but you need a pump, hoses, and worry about leaks. This is your **hydraulic cylinder** ‚Äî brutal strength with surgical precision.
- **Electric Helper** ‚Äî A friend using a motorized screw jack powered by a battery. It is **clean, quiet, programmable, and easy to wire**, but generates less force than hydraulics. This is your **electric motor/actuator** ‚Äî the modern, smart, software-driven option.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Signal Conventions (must memorize for KTU):**
> - Pneumatic: **3‚Äì15 psi** (or 0.2‚Äì1.0 kg/cm¬≤)
> - Electronic: **4‚Äì20 mA DC** (or 1‚Äì5 V, 0‚Äì10 V)
> - Hydraulic: **0‚Äì3000 psi** typical (up to 10,000 psi in aerospace)

## 1.4 Role in the Control Loop

The actuator completes the feedback loop. In KTU's instrumentation view, the signal flow is:

$$
\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Transmitter} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{\textbf{Actuator}} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{(back to Sensor)}
$$

The actuator is the **only** device that delivers usable mechanical work to the process, making its selection critical for loop stability and safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electric Actuators

Electric actuators convert electrical energy into mechanical motion (rotary or linear) using electromagnetic principles.

### 2.1.1 Classification of Electric Actuators

1. **Solenoid Actuators** (ON/OFF type)
   - Single coil, plunger-type
   - Used for discrete valve control (e.g., solenoid valves in water lines)
   - Stroke: typically **5‚Äì15 mm**, force: up to several hundred N

2. **DC Motors**
   - **Servo Motors**: Used in closed-loop position control; offer high precision, low inertia, and fast response.
   - **Stepper Motors**: Open-loop; rotate in discrete angular steps (e.g., **1.8¬∞ per step** = 200 steps/rev). Excellent for digital positioning.
   - **Brushless DC (BLDC)**: Higher reliability, used in modern valve actuators.

3. **AC Motors**
   - **Induction Motors**: Robust, low maintenance; used for continuous rotary motion.
   - **Synchronous Motors**: Constant speed operation, used in timing applications.

4. **Linear Electric Actuators**
   - Combine a motor with a **lead screw / ball screw** to produce linear motion.
   - Widely used in modern process control valves (e.g., smart electric valve actuators from Siemens, Emerson).

### 2.1.2 Stepper Motor ‚Äî Construction & Working

A stepper motor has a **stator** (with multiple toothed poles carrying coils) and a **rotor** (permanent magnet or variable reluctance). When a specific phase is energized, the rotor aligns with that stator pole by **magnetic attraction**, producing a precise angular step.

**Step Angle Formula:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360¬∞}{N_s \times p}
$$

Where:
- $\theta_s$ = step angle in degrees
- $N_s$ = number of stator teeth (or pole pairs)
- $p$ = number of phases

For a standard 1.8¬∞ stepper: $\theta_s = 360¬∞ / 200 = 1.8¬∞$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Stepper motors are **open-loop** actuators. If the load exceeds the holding torque, the motor will lose synchrony ("stall") without feedback ‚Äî a critical exam point.

### 2.1.3 Servo Motor ‚Äî Key Characteristics

- Closed-loop position/velocity control using **encoder** feedback.
- High torque-to-inertia ratio, low electrical time constant (typically **1‚Äì10 ms**).
- Driven by PWM signals; position error ‚Üí corrective torque via PID controller.

## 2.2 Pneumatic Actuators

### 2.2.1 Principle

A pneumatic actuator uses **compressed air** (the energy medium) acting on a diaphragm or piston to produce linear or rotary motion. The standard process control signal is **3‚Äì15 psi** from a controller (or I/P converter).

### 2.2.2 Types of Pneumatic Actuators

#### A. Diaphragm Actuator (Most common in process control)

**Construction:**
- A flexible **elastomer diaphragm** (typically fabric-reinforced neoprene or PTFE) clamped between two housing halves.
- Upper chamber: receives the control air signal (3‚Äì15 psi).
- Lower chamber: vented to atmosphere; contains a **return spring** that pushes the stem down when air pressure is removed.
- A **stem** connects the diaphragm to the valve plug.

**Working (Direct Action ‚Äî stem extends on increasing pressure):**
1. Controller outputs 3 psi ‚Üí spring force dominates ‚Üí stem at lower position.
2. As signal increases to 15 psi ‚Üí diaphragm deflects downward against the spring ‚Üí stem moves down, throttling the valve.

**Working (Reverse Action ‚Äî stem retracts on increasing pressure):**
- Achieved by inverting the spring placement or routing the air to the opposite side.

**Force Equation for Diaphragm Actuator:**

$$
F_{net} = (P - P_{atm}) \times A_{diaphragm} - F_{spring}
$$

Where:
- $A_{diaphragm} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ (effective diaphragm area in m¬≤)
- $F_{spring} = k \times x$ (spring constant √ó displacement)

The **effective area** of the diaphragm (and hence force) varies with stroke due to diaphragm curvature ‚Äî this is a **non-linearity** that valve positioners are designed to correct.

#### B. Piston (Cylinder) Actuator

- Uses a **rigid piston** in a cylinder instead of a flexible diaphragm.
- Operates at higher pressures (typically **20‚Äì100 psi**).
- Produces higher thrust and longer strokes (up to **600 mm**).
- Requires **seals** (O-rings) and **lubrication** ‚Äî higher maintenance than diaphragm type.

#### C. Rotary Pneumatic Actuator

- **Rack-and-pinion** type: piston linear motion ‚Üí rack ‚Üí pinion ‚Üí rotary output (typically **90¬∞**).
- **Vane-type**: uses a spring-loaded vane rotating in a pressurized chamber.
- Used for **ball valves**, **butterfly valves**, and **damper drives**.

#### D. Force-Balance and Motion-Balance Actuators

These are the **two classical pneumatic relay/amplifier principles** tested in KTU:

**Force-Balance Principle:**
The input signal pressure creates a force on a feedback bellows that opposes the input force on a beam. The beam deflection is detected by a flapper-nozzle, which modulates the output pressure. The output pressure also acts on the feedback bellows ‚Äî creating a true force balance.

**Motion-Balance Principle:**
The input motion (e.g., from a set-point cam) is balanced against the feedback motion (e.g., from a position sensor on the valve stem). The difference controls the flapper, which controls output pressure. No net force balance is achieved; only motion (position) is balanced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Force-balance devices are inherently **stiffer** (less sensitive to vibration) than motion-balance devices because the restoring force is much larger. Force-balance is the industry standard for pneumatic controllers and I/P transducers.

## 2.3 Hydraulic Actuators

### 2.3.1 Principle

A hydraulic actuator uses a **pressurized incompressible fluid** (typically mineral oil) acting on a piston or vane. Since the fluid cannot be compressed, hydraulic systems deliver:
- **Highest force/weight ratio** of all actuator types
- **Excellent stiffness and positional accuracy**
- **Fast dynamic response** (high power bandwidth)

### 2.3.2 Types of Hydraulic Actuators

#### A. Linear Hydraulic Cylinder

**Single-Acting Cylinder:**
- Fluid pressure applied to **one side** only.
- Return motion achieved by gravity or an external spring.
- Used in simple clamping or lifting applications.

**Double-Acting Cylinder:**
- Fluid pressure can be applied to **both sides** alternately.
- Provides **controlled motion in both directions**.
- Most common in process control (e.g., hydraulic valve actuators).

**Force Output Equations:**

$$
F_{extend} = P \times A_p - P_{ret} \times A_a
$$

$$
F_{retract} = P \times A_a - P_{ext} \times A_p
$$

Where:
- $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ = piston (full bore) area
- $A_a = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ = annular (rod-side) area
- $D$ = piston diameter, $d$ = rod diameter
- $P$ = supply pressure, $P_{ret}$ / $P_{ext}$ = return/exhaust back-pressure

**Velocity Equation:**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A_p}
$$

Where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (m¬≥/s) and $v$ is piston velocity (m/s).

**Power Equation:**

$$
P_{hydraulic} = P \times Q = F \times v
$$

#### B. Hydraulic Motor (Rotary Actuator)

- Converts hydraulic pressure into rotary torque.
- Types: **Gear motors**, **Vane motors**, **Piston motors** (radial/axial).
- Used in continuous rotary processes (rolling mills, extruders).

**Torque Output:**

$$
T = \frac{P \times D_m}{2\pi} \times \eta_m
$$

Where:
- $P$ = pressure differential across the motor
- $D_m$ = motor displacement (m¬≥/rev)
- $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency (typically **0.85‚Äì0.95**)

#### C. Hydraulic Servo System

Combines a **servo valve**, **hydraulic cylinder/motor**, and **position feedback** to achieve precise closed-loop control. This is the **highest-performance** actuation system in process control.

A typical electro-hydraulic servo system flow:

$$
\text{Electrical Input} \rightarrow \text{Servo Valve} \rightarrow \text{Hydraulic Cylinder} \rightarrow \text{Position Feedback} \rightarrow \text{Error Calculation}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** Electro-hydraulic actuators (EHAs) are replacing pure hydraulic systems in aerospace (e.g., aircraft flight control surfaces) because they eliminate the central hydraulic pump and reservoir ‚Äî improving reliability and reducing weight.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. All quantities are in SI units unless stated.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step angle of stepper motor | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360¬∞}{N_s \cdot p}$ | $N_s$ = stator teeth, $p$ = phases |
| 2 | Diaphragm area | $A_d = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $D$ = diaphragm diameter (m) |
| 3 | Diaphragm net force | $F_{net} = (P - P_{atm}) \cdot A_d - F_{spring}$ | $F$ in N, $P$ in Pa |
| 4 | Pneumatic piston area | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $D$ = piston diameter (m) |
| 5 | Hydraulic full bore area | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $D$ = piston diameter (m) |
| 6 | Hydraulic annular area | $A_a = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | $D$ = piston, $d$ = rod (m) |
| 7 | Hydraulic extend force | $F_e = P \cdot A_p - P_{ret} \cdot A_a$ | $F$ in N, $P$ in Pa |
| 8 | Hydraulic retract force | $F_r = P \cdot A_a - P_{ext} \cdot A_p$ | $F$ in N, $P$ in Pa |
| 9 | Piston velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | $v$ in m/s, $Q$ in m¬≥/s |
| 10 | Hydraulic power | $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ | $P$ in W |
| 11 | Hydraulic motor torque | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_m}{2\pi}$ | $T$ in N¬∑m, $D_m$ in m¬≥/rev |
| 12 | Electric motor speed | $N = \dfrac{120 f}{p}$ | $N$ = RPM, $f$ = Hz, $p$ = poles |
| 13 | I/P transducer output | $P_{out} = P_{min} + K \cdot I_{in}$ | $K$ = gain (psi/mA) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical plants** ‚Üí Pneumatic diaphragm actuators on flow control valves (3‚Äì15 psi signal).
- **Steel rolling mills** ‚Üí Hydraulic cylinders for force up to **20,000 kN**.
- **CNC machines** ‚Üí Servo motors + ball screws for sub-micron positioning accuracy.
- **Aircraft flight control** ‚Üí Electro-hydraulic actuators (EHAs) with fault-tolerant servo valves.
- **Robotic arms** ‚Üí BLDC servo motors with harmonic gear reducers.
- **Damper control in HVAC** ‚Üí Rotary pneumatic actuators (90¬∞ rotation, 3‚Äì15 psi).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Force Output of a Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator

We will derive the **net thrust** delivered by a spring-opposed pneumatic diaphragm actuator as a function of the signal pressure $P_{sig}$ and stem displacement $x$.

### Given
- Diaphragm effective diameter: $D_d$ (m)
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm}$ = 101325 Pa
- Signal pressure: $P_{sig}$ (gauge, relative to atmosphere)
- Spring constant: $k$ (N/m)
- Stem displacement: $x$ (m)
- Stem friction and stem weight: neglected initially

### Assumptions
- The diaphragm deflection $x$ is small compared to its diameter.
- The effective area $A_d$ remains approximately constant.
- The spring is linear and pre-loaded to hold the stem down at $P_{sig}$ = 0 (3 psi condition).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Express the diaphragm effective area.

$$
A_d = \frac{\pi \cdot D_d^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2:** Express the force generated by the control air pressure acting on the diaphragm (downward, in direct action).

$$
F_{air} = (P_{sig} + P_{atm} - P_{atm}) \cdot A_d = P_{sig} \cdot A_d
$$

> Note: If we use gauge pressure $P_{sig}$ directly, the atmospheric terms cancel since both sides of the diaphragm are at atmospheric when the upper chamber is vented.

**Step 3:** Express the spring restoring force (upward, opposing motion).

$$
F_{spring} = F_0 + k \cdot x
$$

where $F_0$ is the pre-load force (at $P_{sig} = 0$, $F_0$ holds the stem at its lower limit).

**Step 4:** Apply force equilibrium on the stem (sum of forces = 0 at steady state).

$$
F_{air} - F_{spring} = F_{load}
$$

Therefore the net force available to overcome the process load is:

$$
F_{load} = P_{sig} \cdot A_d - F_0 - k \cdot x
$$

**Step 5:** At the lower limit (just-lift-off condition), $x = 0$ and the signal pressure is at its minimum (3 psi = 20700 Pa). For just-lift-off, the net force is zero:

$$
P_{sig,min} \cdot A_d = F_0
$$

Hence:

$$
F_0 = P_{sig,min} \cdot A_d
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back to get the final form.

$$
F_{load} = (P_{sig} - P_{sig,min}) \cdot A_d - k \cdot x
$$

**Step 7:** Define the **working stroke** as the range $x \in [0, x_{max}]$, where at maximum signal (15 psi = 103400 Pa), the stem is fully extended and $F_{load} = 0$:

$$
(P_{sig,max} - P_{sig,min}) \cdot A_d = k \cdot x_{max}
$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the spring constant required for a desired stroke:

$$
k = \frac{(P_{sig,max} - P_{sig,min}) \cdot A_d}{x_{max}}
$$

### Final Compact Result

$$
\boxed{F_{load}(x) = \Delta P \cdot A_d - k \cdot x, \quad \text{where } \Delta P = P_{sig} - P_{sig,min}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The net available force is **linearly decreasing with stem travel**. This is why valve manufacturers specify the actuator's **thrust vs. stroke curve** ‚Äî the actuator must always provide positive $F_{load}$ over the entire stroke, or the valve will not fully open or close.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Force and Velocity of a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder

We will derive the **extension force**, **retraction force**, and **extension velocity** for a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with a single rod.

### Given
- Piston diameter: $D$ (m)
- Rod diameter: $d$ (m)
- Supply (pump) pressure: $P_s$ (Pa)
- Return-line back-pressure (different for extension and retraction): $P_{ret}$, $P_{ext}$ (Pa)
- Pump flow rate: $Q$ (m¬≥/s)
- Mechanical efficiency of cylinder: $\eta_{cyl}$ (typically 0.95)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the piston (full bore) area ‚Äî the side receiving pressure during extension.

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the annular area on the rod side ‚Äî the side receiving pressure during retraction.

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

**Step 3:** Force balance during extension.
- Pressure $P_s$ acts on $A_p$ ‚Üí pushes piston outward with force $P_s \cdot A_p$.
- Back-pressure $P_{ret}$ acts on $A_a$ ‚Üí opposes motion with force $P_{ret} \cdot A_a$.
- Net extension force:

$$
F_e = P_s \cdot A_p - P_{ret} \cdot A_a
$$

**Step 4:** Force balance during retraction.
- Pressure $P_s$ acts on $A_a$ ‚Üí pushes piston inward with force $P_s \cdot A_a$.
- Back-pressure $P_{ext}$ acts on $A_p$ ‚Üí opposes motion with force $P_{ext} \cdot A_p$.
- Net retraction force:

$$
F_r = P_s \cdot A_a - P_{ext} \cdot A_p
$$

**Step 5:** Compute extension velocity using the principle of volumetric continuity.
- Flow rate $Q$ enters the full-bore side; the piston volume displaced per unit time is $A_p \cdot v_e$.
- Therefore:

$$
v_e = \frac{Q}{A_p}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute retraction velocity similarly (flow enters annular side):

$$
v_r = \frac{Q}{A_a}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the extension hydraulic power (rate of work done on the load):

$$
P_{out} = F_e \cdot v_e = (P_s \cdot A_p - P_{ret} \cdot A_a) \cdot \frac{Q}{A_p}
$$

**Step 8:** Compute the input hydraulic power supplied by the pump:

$$
P_{in} = P_s \cdot Q
$$

**Step 9:** The cylinder efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{cyl} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{(P_s \cdot A_p - P_{ret} \cdot A_a) \cdot Q}{P_s \cdot Q \cdot A_p} = 1 - \frac{P_{ret} \cdot A_a}{P_s \cdot A_p}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The retraction speed is **always higher** than extension speed for a single-rod cylinder (since $A_a < A_p$). This asymmetry is critical in applications like injection molding where the rod must move quickly back to a ready position.

### Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

Given: $D = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $d = 50$ mm = 0.05 m, $P_s = 100 \times 10^5$ Pa (100 bar), $P_{ret} = P_{ext} = 5 \times 10^5$ Pa (5 bar), $Q = 10$ L/min = $1.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m¬≥/s.

**Compute areas:**

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi [(0.1)^2 - (0.05)^2]}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0075)}{4} = 5.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Compute forces:**

$$
F_e = (100 \times 10^5)(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^5)(5.890 \times 10^{-3}) = 78540 - 2945 = 75595 \text{ N} \approx 75.6 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_r = (100 \times 10^5)(5.890 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^5)(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 58900 - 3927 = 54973 \text{ N} \approx 55.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Compute velocities:**

$$
v_e = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0212 \text{ m/s} = 21.2 \text{ mm/s}
$$

$$
v_r = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.890 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0283 \text{ m/s} = 28.3 \text{ mm/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice:** $v_r > v_e$ (28.3 vs 21.2 mm/s) ‚Äî confirming the asymmetry due to the single rod.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Stepper Motor Resolution and Position Control

A stepper motor is driven by a sequence of pulses. We will derive the relationship between pulse frequency, motor speed, and position.

### Given
- Step angle: $\theta_s$
- Pulse frequency applied to the driver: $f_p$ (pulses per second)
- Gear ratio (if any): $G$ (motor rev per output rev)

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Angular displacement per pulse equals the step angle.

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta_s
$$

**Step 2:** Motor speed in revolutions per second:

$$
n = \frac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{360¬∞} \quad [\text{rev/s}]
$$

**Step 3:** Motor speed in RPM:

$$
N = 60 \cdot n = \frac{60 \cdot f_p \cdot \theta_s}{360¬∞} = \frac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{6} \quad [\text{RPM}]
$$

**Step 4:** Total angular position after $N_p$ pulses:

$$
\theta_{total} = N_p \cdot \theta_s
$$

**Step 5:** Linear displacement of a lead-screw-driven linear actuator:

$$
x = \theta_{total} \cdot \frac{L}{360¬∞} = N_p \cdot \theta_s \cdot \frac{L}{360¬∞}
$$

where $L$ is the lead of the screw (mm/rev).

### Numerical Example
For a 1.8¬∞ stepper driving a lead screw with $L = 5$ mm/rev:
- Linear resolution per pulse:

$$
\Delta x = 1.8¬∞ \times \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{360¬∞} = 0.025 \text{ mm} = 25 \text{ \mu m}
$$

- This is the **minimum controllable increment** of the linear actuator ‚Äî a critical specification in precision positioning.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Selection of Actuator Type

| Criterion | Electric | Pneumatic | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Force / Thrust** | Low to medium (N to kN) | Medium (kN) | Very high (10s of kN to MN) |
| **Stiffness (rigidity)** | Medium | Low (compressible air) | Very high (incompressible fluid) |
| **Speed / Bandwidth** | High (1 ms response) | Medium (10‚Äì100 ms) | Very high (sub-ms) |
| **Position Accuracy** | High (with encoder) | Medium (with positioner) | Very high (with servo valve) |
| **Cost ‚Äî Capital** | Medium | Low | High |
| **Cost ‚Äî Operating** | Low (electricity) | Medium (compressed air) | High (pump, oil, cooling) |
| **Safety in Hazardous Area** | Intrinsic safe variants needed | Intrinsically safe (air is non-flammable) | Risk of oil fire |
| **Maintenance** | Low (brushless) | Low (diaphragm) | High (seals, filters, leaks) |
| **Typical Application** | Smart valve actuators, robotics | Process control valves, dampers | Press machines, flight controls |
| **Signal Compatibility** | 4‚Äì20 mA, 0‚Äì10 V, digital | 3‚Äì15 psi | Electronic command ‚Üí servo valve |

## 4.6 Cross-Section Schematic Description ‚Äî Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator

Since Mermaid cannot render physical cross-sections with the precision required by KTU, the following is a **textual block-level functional architecture** of the diaphragm actuator:

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|              UPPER HOUSING HALF                  |
|        (Air Inlet 3-15 psi from Controller)      |
+--------------------------------------------------+
                      |
                      v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|        ELASTOMER DIAPHRAGM (Reinforced)          |
|         Effective Area = (pi * D^2)/4            |
+--------------------------------------------------+
                      |
                      v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|       STEM (Connects Diaphragm to Valve)         |
|       Moves Linearly as Diaphragm Deflects       |
+--------------------------------------------------+
                      |
                      v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|       RETURN SPRING (Linear, Pre-loaded)         |
|       Force = F0 + k * x  (Opposes Air Force)    |
+--------------------------------------------------+
                      |
                      v
+--------------------------------------------------+
|             LOWER HOUSING HALF                   |
|        (Vented to Atmosphere, Open Bottom)       |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

**Functional Flow:** Air pressure pushes the diaphragm down ‚Üí stem moves down ‚Üí valve throttles. When air is vented, the spring returns the stem to its fail-safe position.
