## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]
**Define an actuator. List the three main types of actuators used in industrial process control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An actuator is the final control element in a process control loop that receives a low-energy control signal (3‚Äì15 psi, 4‚Äì20 mA, or 0‚Äì10 V) from the controller and converts it into a proportional mechanical motion (linear or rotary) to manipulate a process variable.

The three main types are:
1. **Electric Actuators** ‚Äî use electrical energy via motors (DC, AC, stepper, servo) or solenoids.
2. **Pneumatic Actuators** ‚Äî use compressed air acting on a diaphragm or piston.
3. **Hydraulic Actuators** ‚Äî use pressurized incompressible oil acting on a piston or vane.

(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing with examples)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]
**Compare diaphragm and piston-type pneumatic actuators on the basis of operating pressure, stroke length, and maintenance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Diaphragm Actuator | Piston Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| Operating pressure | Low (3‚Äì15 psi standard) | Higher (20‚Äì100 psi) |
| Stroke length | Limited (~25‚Äì50 mm) | Long (up to 600 mm) |
| Maintenance | Very low (no seals) | Higher (O-ring seals, lubrication) |

(1 mark per row of comparison ‚Äî 3 marks total)

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice rule: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]

#### Part (a) ‚Äî 7 marks
**Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a pneumatic diaphragm actuator. Derive the expression for net thrust available on the stem.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A pneumatic diaphragm actuator consists of:
1. **Upper housing half** ‚Äî receives the control air signal (3‚Äì15 psi) from the controller or I/P transducer.
2. **Elastomer diaphragm** ‚Äî fabric-reinforced neoprene or PTFE; clamped between the two housing halves.
3. **Stem** ‚Äî rigid rod connecting the diaphragm center to the valve plug.
4. **Return spring** ‚Äî pre-loaded linear spring pushing the stem upward (or downward in reverse action) when air pressure is removed.
5. **Lower housing half** ‚Äî vented to atmosphere; houses the spring.

Refer to the block schematic in Section 4.6 for the cross-section architecture.

**Working (2 marks):**
- At 3 psi (minimum signal): spring force dominates ‚Üí stem is at its lower position (direct action).
- As signal increases to 15 psi: air pressure pushes the diaphragm downward against the spring ‚Üí stem moves downward, throttling the valve.
- The displacement is approximately proportional to the signal pressure, but with some non-linearity due to varying effective diaphragm area.

**Derivation of Net Thrust (2 marks):**

$$
F_{load}(x) = (P_{sig} - P_{sig,min}) \cdot A_d - k \cdot x
$$

where $A_d = \dfrac{\pi D_d^2}{4}$ is the effective diaphragm area and $k \cdot x$ is the spring restoring force. (Full derivation as in Section 3.1.)

#### Part (b) ‚Äî 7 marks
**Explain the force-balance principle in a pneumatic relay. How is it used in a pneumatic controller?**

**Model Solution:**

**Force-Balance Principle (3 marks):**
A force-balance pneumatic relay uses the principle of **null deflection** of a force-summing beam. The components are:
1. **Input bellows** ‚Äî receives the controller signal pressure.
2. **Feedback bellows** ‚Äî receives the output pressure from the relay.
3. **Flapper-nozzle** ‚Äî converts beam deflection into a pneumatic signal.
4. **Relay valve** ‚Äî amplifies the nozzle back-pressure to a high-capacity output.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The input pressure creates a force $F_{in} = P_{in} \cdot A_{b1}$ pushing the beam in one direction.
- The feedback pressure creates a force $F_{fb} = P_{out} \cdot A_{b2}$ pushing in the opposite direction.
- If $F_{in} > F_{fb}$, the beam deflects slightly toward the nozzle, increasing nozzle back-pressure, which increases output pressure $P_{out}$.
- The increased $P_{out}$ increases $F_{fb}$ until $F_{in} = F_{fb}$ ‚Äî equilibrium is reached at a new $P_{out}$ proportional to $P_{in}$.
- The beam deflection is **negligibly small** (a few micrometres), so the system is very stable and immune to vibration.

**Use in Pneumatic Controller (2 marks):**
- In a pneumatic PID controller, the force-balance principle is used in each of the proportional, integral, and derivative stages.
- The integral stage uses a **negative feedback bellows + needle valve** to produce the integral (reset) action.
- The derivative stage uses a **derivative bellows + restriction** to produce the rate (derivative) action.
- Force-balance design gives high accuracy, low hysteresis, and excellent long-term stability ‚Äî making it the industry standard for pneumatic controllers (e.g., Foxboro, Honeywell).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024 ‚Äî Alternative]

#### Part (a) ‚Äî 7 marks
**Explain the construction and working of a double-acting hydraulic actuator. Derive the expressions for extension force, retraction force, and piston velocity.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A double-acting hydraulic actuator (cylinder) consists of:
1. **Cylinder barrel** ‚Äî precision-machined steel tube.
2. **Piston** ‚Äî slides inside the barrel, sealed by O-rings or lip seals.
3. **Piston rod** ‚Äî extends from one side of the piston through a rod seal.
4. **End caps** ‚Äî front (rod side) and rear (cap side), each with a port for hydraulic fluid entry/exit.
5. **Seals** ‚Äî O-rings, wipers, and rod seals to prevent leakage.
6. **Cushions** (optional) ‚Äî deceleration devices at the ends of stroke.

Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 for system-level architecture.

**Working (2 marks):**
- During **extension**: high-pressure oil is admitted through the cap-end port; the oil pushes the piston outward; oil on the rod side is exhausted through the rod-end port back to the tank.
- During **retraction**: high-pressure oil is admitted through the rod-end port; the smaller annular area (due to the rod) receives the pressure, and the cap-end oil is exhausted.
- A 4/3-way **directional control valve (DCV)** governs which port receives pressure.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
F_e = P_s \cdot A_p - P_{ret} \cdot A_a
$$

$$
F_r = P_s \cdot A_a - P_{ext} \cdot A_p
$$

$$
v_e = \frac{Q}{A_p}, \quad v_r = \frac{Q}{A_a}
$$

where $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ and $A_a = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$.

(Full derivation as in Section 3.2.)

#### Part (b) ‚Äî 7 marks
**Compare electric, pneumatic, and hydraulic actuators on any seven parameters. State one typical application of each.**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | Electric Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator | Hydraulic Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy medium | Electricity | Compressed air | Pressurized oil |
| 2 | Power density | Low‚Äìmedium | Low | Very high |
| 3 | Stiffness (rigidity) | Medium | Low | Very high |
| 4 | Response speed | High (1 ms) | Medium (10‚Äì100 ms) | Very high (sub-ms) |
| 5 | Position accuracy | High (with encoder) | Medium (with positioner) | Very high (servo) |
| 6 | Cost (capital) | Medium | Low | High |
| 7 | Maintenance | Low | Low (diaphragm) | High (seals, leaks) |
| 8 | Safety in flammable area | Needs I.S. barrier | Safe (air is inert) | Risk of oil fire |

(7 marks ‚Äî 0.5 marks per row up to 7 rows, 0.5 mark per typical application = 1 mark, total 7 + 1 = covered in 7 marks as per KTU norm)

**Typical Applications (1 mark):**
- **Electric** ‚Äî Smart electric valve actuators in chemical dosing systems; robotic arm joints.
- **Pneumatic** ‚Äî Diaphragm-actuated flow control valves in petrochemical plants.
- **Hydraulic** ‚Äî Hydraulic press for metal forging (forces up to 50,000 kN).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation (Module 4 ‚Äî Actuators):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $A_p$ and $A_a$ in hydraulic cylinders** ‚Äî Students often compute retraction force using the full bore area $A_p$ instead of the annular area $A_a$. This gives the wrong answer and forfeits 2‚Äì3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the spring pre-load term $F_0$** ‚Äî In pneumatic diaphragm derivations, students write $F_{spring} = k \cdot x$ only, omitting the pre-load $F_0$ required to define the lower limit. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Calling stepper motors "closed-loop"** ‚Äî Stepper motors are **open-loop** actuators. If the load exceeds the holding torque, the motor stalls silently without any error signal. Always mention the absence of feedback in stepper answers.
>
> 4. **Mixing up signal and supply pressures in pneumatic actuators** ‚Äî The signal is **3‚Äì15 psi** (from controller); the air supply for positioner/amplifier is **20 psi** (regulated). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the diagram first** ‚Äî KTU evaluators reserve **1‚Äì2 marks** specifically for a "neat labelled diagram" or block diagram. Always include a block diagram, even a hand-drawn one, in long answers.
>
> 6. **Stating "hydraulic systems are incompressible"** ‚Äî Strictly, hydraulic **fluid is** incompressible, but the system has a small bulk modulus. Saying "the system is incompressible" is technically wrong. Use the term "high bulk modulus fluid" or "negligible compressibility" instead.
>
> 7. **Ignoring units in numericals** ‚Äî Always write units in every step. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction in the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ‚Äî Module 4 Actuators:**

- **Actuator** = final control element that converts controller signal to mechanical motion.
- **Three types**: Electric, Pneumatic, Hydraulic ‚Äî each with distinct energy medium and signal convention.
- **Standard signals**: 3‚Äì15 psi (pneumatic), 4‚Äì20 mA (electronic), 0‚Äì3000 psi (hydraulic supply).
- **Diaphragm actuator** uses fabric-reinforced elastomer diaphragm + return spring; limited stroke (~25‚Äì50 mm), low pressure (3‚Äì15 psi), very low maintenance.
- **Piston (pneumatic) actuator** uses rigid piston + seals; higher pressure, longer stroke, more maintenance.
- **Force-balance principle**: input force = feedback force at all times; beam deflection is negligible; high stability and stiffness.
- **Motion-balance principle**: input motion = feedback motion; used in older, less stiff designs; largely superseded by force-balance.
- **Stepper motor** step angle: $\theta_s = 360¬∞ / (N_s \cdot p)$; 1.8¬∞ standard; **open-loop** ‚Äî stalls silently if overloaded.
- **Servo motor**: closed-loop with encoder feedback; high precision; used in CNC and robotics.
- **Linear electric actuator** = motor + lead/ball screw; converts rotary to linear motion; resolution = step angle √ó lead.
- **Double-acting hydraulic cylinder** has two ports ‚Äî one for extension, one for retraction; controlled by a 4/3 DCV.
- **Piston (full bore) area** $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4$ ‚Äî used during extension force calculation.
- **Annular (rod-side) area** $A_a = \pi (D^2 - d^2)/4$ ‚Äî used during retraction force calculation.
- **Hydraulic extend force** $F_e = P_s \cdot A_p - P_{ret} \cdot A_a$ ‚Äî dominant term is $P_s \cdot A_p$.
- **Hydraulic retract force** $F_r = P_s \cdot A_a - P_{ext} \cdot A_p$ ‚Äî always **less than** $F_e$ because $A_a < A_p$.
- **Piston velocity** $v = Q / A$ ‚Äî retract velocity **greater than** extend velocity (same reason).
- **Hydraulic power** $P = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ ‚Äî same formula for fluid power and mechanical power.
- **Hydraulic motor torque** $T = (P \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_m) / (2\pi)$ ‚Äî $D_m$ is motor displacement per revolution.
- **Electro-hydraulic servo system** = electronic command + servo valve + hydraulic cylinder + LVDT feedback; highest performance actuation.
- **Selection rule of thumb**:
  - **Electric** ‚Üí clean, precise, programmable, low-to-medium force.
  - **Pneumatic** ‚Üí safe, cheap, clean (air), low force, low stiffness.
  - **Hydraulic** ‚Üí brute force, very stiff, very fast, but messy and expensive.
- **Always draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers; examiners reward it.
- **Always include units** in numericals; missing units cost marks.
- **Always state assumptions** in derivations; this shows engineering maturity and earns full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes ‚Äî Electric, Pneumatic & Hydraulic Actuators.**
> These notes cover the complete KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCICT503 Module 4. For Module 5, continue with the next topic in the sequence. All formulas, diagrams, and derivations are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme assessment pattern and follow the NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Characteristics. Different Types of Control Valves - Disc, Stem and Seat Design and Applications - Gate Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Valve Characteristics. Different Types of Control Valves - Disc, Stem and Seat Design and Applications - Gate Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Control Valve ‚Äì The Final Control Element

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A **control valve** is a pneumatically, hydraulically, or electrically actuated device used to regulate the flow rate, pressure, temperature, or liquid level of a process fluid by varying the size of the flow passage as directed by a control signal from a controller. It is the most common type of *final control element* in any closed-loop industrial process.

A control valve essentially consists of two main parts:
1. **Actuator** ‚Äì Provides the force/motion to position the valve (pneumatic diaphragm, piston, electric motor, electrohydraulic).
2. **Valve Body (Trim)** ‚Äì The actual throttling device (disc, plug, ball, gate, butterfly) that physically modulates the flow.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äì "The Faucet of a Process Plant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine the water tap in your home. When you rotate the handle, an internal rubber washer (the **seat-disc interface**) lifts up to allow more water, and lowers to restrict it. The handle position is set by your hand (the "controller" deciding how much water you want).
>
> Now, in an industry, instead of your hand, a **PID controller** sends a 4‚Äì20 mA / 3‚Äì15 psi pneumatic signal to a **diaphragm actuator**, which automatically positions the internal disc/plug of the control valve. The plant's "thirst" for fluid is automatically quenched ‚Äî no human turning required.

---

## 1.3 What are "Valve Characteristics"?

The term *valve characteristics* refers to the **mathematical relationship between the valve stem travel (position) and the resulting flow rate through the valve**, under specific and well-defined conditions.

There are **two distinct kinds** of characteristics:

| Type | Definition | Condition |
|------|------------|-----------|
| **Inherent Characteristic** | Flow vs. stem position at *constant* pressure drop across the valve | Laboratory (ideal) |
| **Installed Characteristic** | Flow vs. stem position when the valve is *actually fitted* into a pipeline with fittings, pumps, etc. | Real plant |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **I**nherent = **I**deal (constant ŒîP). **I**nstalled = **R**eal (varying ŒîP with flow).

---

## 1.4 The Four Standard Inherent Characteristics

Every commercial control valve trim is designed to produce one (or a combination) of these four characteristic curves:

1. **Quick-Opening** ‚Äì Maximum flow at very small stem travel. Used for ON/OFF or safety service.
2. **Linear** ‚Äì Flow is *directly proportional* to stem travel. Used for level and some pressure loops.
3. **Equal Percentage (E% or EQ%)** ‚Äì Equal increments of stem travel produce equal *percentage* changes in flow. The most widely used in process control.
4. **Modified Parabolic / Square Root** ‚Äì Intermediate; rarely used standalone.

---

## 1.5 Disc, Stem, and Seat ‚Äì The "Heart Trim"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition of Trim:** The internal wetted parts of a control valve ‚Äî the **disc (or plug)**, the **stem**, and the **seat** ‚Äî together are called the **valve trim**. They determine the characteristic curve, the leakage class, the capacity (Cv), and the chemical/erosion resistance of the valve.

* **Disc / Plug** ‚Äì The moving element that throttles flow. May be contoured (V-port, contoured plug, characterized cage) to shape the inherent flow curve.
* **Stem** ‚Äì Connects the actuator output to the disc. Transmitted force and vibration resistance depend on stem material, diameter, and stroke length.
* **Seat** ‚Äì The fixed annular ring against which the disc seats for shut-off. The seat geometry (flat, contoured, V-notch) co-designs the characteristic with the plug.

---

## 1.6 The Gate Valve ‚Äì A Specialized Member

> [!NOTE]
> **Gate Valve Definition (KTU standard):** A **gate valve** is a linear-motion isolation valve in which a flat or wedge-shaped **gate (disc)** is moved perpendicularly to the flow path by a threaded or rising **stem**, fully opening or fully closing the flow with minimal pressure drop in the open position.

A gate valve is *primarily an ON‚ÄìOFF (isolation) valve*, not a modulating control valve. However, its disc/stem/seat design illustrates the engineering principles of linear-motion trim that form the conceptual basis for *globe valve* control trim.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized Valve Flow Equation

All control valve analyses begin with the **ISA S75.01 / IEC 60534** standardized flow equation for incompressible fluids:

$$Q = C_v \cdot N_1 \cdot F_P \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{\rho / \rho_0}}$$

For liquids, the simplified form used in 90% of KTU problems is:

$$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$$

Where:
* $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate in **GPM (US)** (or m¬≥/h if using $K_v$)
* $C_v$ = **Valve flow coefficient** (GPM of water at 1 psi ŒîP)
* $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across the valve
* $G_f$ = Specific gravity of the fluid (water = 1.0)
* $F_P$ = Piping geometry correction factor (1.0 ideal)
* $N_1$ = Numerical constant (1.0 for SI/GPM)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "$C_v$ = how much *water* flows at a *1 psi* drop." In Europe, $K_v$ uses m¬≥/h at 1 bar drop.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Forms of the Four Inherent Characteristics

Let $y$ = relative stem travel (0 ‚Üí 1) and $\dot{m}$ or $Q$ = relative flow (0 ‚Üí 1 at maximum).

### 2.2.1 Quick-Opening
$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{y}{L}\right)^n \quad ; \quad n \gg 1$$

A high exponent (n ‚âà 5‚Äì7) gives the rapid initial opening.

### 2.2.2 Linear
$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \frac{y}{L}$$

Straight line: 50% travel = 50% flow.

### 2.2.3 Equal Percentage (E%)
$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{\left(\frac{y}{L} - 1\right)}$$

Where $R$ = rangeability (typical 30:1 to 50:1 for EQ% valves).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At 1% stem travel on an EQ% valve with R = 50, the flow is only **0.86% of max** ‚Äî this is why EQ% valves give excellent **turndown** and stable control near shut-off.

### 2.2.4 Modified Parabolic
$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \left(\frac{y}{L}\right)^{1.5 \text{ to } 2}$$

A compromise between linear and equal-percentage.

---

## 2.3 Installed Characteristics ‚Äì The Reality Check

In a real plant, the **system curve** is:

$$\Delta P_{system} = \Delta P_{valve} + \Delta P_{pipe+fittings} = \text{constant (pump head)}$$

As the valve opens, $C_v$ increases ‚Üí flow increases ‚Üí pipe friction $\Delta P$ rises ‚Üí $\Delta P_{valve}$ falls. The installed characteristic therefore *deviates* from the inherent curve:

* **Inherent Linear** ‚Üí Installed becomes **Quick-Opening** at high flows.
* **Inherent Equal Percentage** ‚Üí Installed becomes **nearly Linear** (often desired!).

This is why EQ% trims are preferred in long piping runs.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU-favorite ‚Äî expect at least one in every board exam on this module.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Valve flow (liquid) | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$ | $Q$ in GPM, $\Delta P$ in psi |
| 2 | Valve flow (gas) | $Q = C_v \cdot P_1 \cdot Y \cdot \sqrt{x / (G_g \cdot T \cdot Z)}$ | $P_1$ in psia, $T$ in ¬∞R |
| 3 | Rangeability | $R = Q_{max} / Q_{min-controllable}$ | Dimensionless, typical 30‚Äì50 |
| 4 | Equal Percentage law | $Q / Q_{max} = R^{(y/L - 1)}$ | $R$ is the rangeability |
| 5 | Valve authority | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta P_{v,@Q_{max}}}{\Delta P_{system,@Q_{max}}}$ | Ideal $\beta = 1$; in practice $\beta \geq 0.5$ |
| 6 | Quick-opening exponent | $Q/Q_{max} = 1 - (1 - y/L)^n$ | $n \approx 5\text{‚Äì}7$ |
| 7 | Linear law | $Q/Q_{max} = y/L$ | Trivial 1st-degree |
| 8 | Reynolds correction | $F_R = 1 + \frac{0.33 \cdot F_L^{0.5}}{C_v^{0.25}}$ | For viscous / non-turbulent flow |
| 9 | Seat leakage class | ANSI/FCI 70-2 Class I‚ÄìVI | Class VI = bubble-tight |
| 10 | Stroke speed (pneumatic) | $t = \dfrac{L \cdot A \cdot (1+R_a)}{Q_a \cdot P_s}$ | $A$ = diaphragm area, $P_s$ = supply |

---

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

* **Process Stability** ‚Äì Selecting the correct inherent curve *matches* the process gain. A non-linear process (pH, heat-exchanger) demands an **EQ%** trim to maintain constant loop gain across the operating range.
* **Energy Efficiency** ‚Äì Oversized valves waste pump energy through excessive throttling. Correct $C_v$ sizing minimizes $\Delta P$ across the valve.
* **Safety** ‚Äì Leakage class VI (PTFE soft seat) is mandated for toxic / flammable services (e.g., chlorine, hydrogen).
* **Cavitation & Flashing** ‚Äì When $P_2 \leq P_{vapor}$, the trim geometry (anti-cavitation trims, multi-stage cages) protects the disc and seat from erosion.
* **Rangeability** ‚Äì Determines the lowest controllable flow ‚Äî critical in *turndown* applications like boiler feedwater control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equal Percentage Characteristic from First Principles

The EQ% curve is *defined* as: **equal increments of stem travel produce equal percentage changes in flow.**

### Step 1 ‚Äî Define the differential
Let $Q(y)$ be the flow at stem position $y$, and let a small travel increment $dy$ produce a small *proportional* flow increment:

$$\frac{dQ}{Q} = k \cdot dy$$

where $k$ is a constant of proportionality.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Separate variables and integrate

$$\int_{Q_{min}}^{Q} \frac{dQ}{Q} = \int_{0}^{y} k \, dy$$

Applying the limits:

$$\ln Q - \ln Q_{min} = k \cdot y$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{Q}{Q_{min}}\right) = k \cdot y$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Exponentiate

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{min}} = e^{k y}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Apply the *rangeability* boundary condition

By definition of rangeability, when $y = L$ (full travel), $Q = Q_{max}$:

$$\frac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}} = R = e^{kL}$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \frac{\ln R}{L}$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Final form

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{min}} = e^{\left(\frac{y}{L}\right) \ln R} = R^{\,y/L}$$

Or, normalizing so that $Q/Q_{max}$ ranges from 0 to 1:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{\,y/L \,-\, 1}$$

This is the **standard equal-percentage law** used worldwide and tested in every KTU exam.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A control valve with equal-percentage trim has rangeability $R = 50$ and maximum $C_v = 120$. The system $\Delta P$ at the design flow is **15 psi** and the specific gravity is $G_f = 0.85$. Calculate:
> (a) The flow rate at $y/L = 0.5$ (50% stem position).
> (b) The flow rate at $y/L = 0.8$ (80% stem position).
> (c) The valve authority assuming system $\Delta P$ at zero flow is 25 psi.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Find $C_v$ at the given positions

From $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(y/L - 1)}$:

**(a) y/L = 0.5:**

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = 50^{(0.5 - 1)} = 50^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} = \frac{1}{7.071} = 0.1414$$

$$C_{v,@0.5} = 0.1414 \times 120 = 16.97$$

**(b) y/L = 0.8:**

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = 50^{(0.8 - 1)} = 50^{-0.2}$$

Compute $50^{-0.2}$:

$$\ln(50) = 3.912$$
$$-0.2 \times 3.912 = -0.7824$$
$$e^{-0.7824} = 0.4572$$

$$C_{v,@0.8} = 0.4572 \times 120 = 54.86$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Convert to flow using the liquid flow equation

**(a) At 50% stem position:**

$$Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G_f}} = 16.97 \times \sqrt{\frac{15}{0.85}}$$

$$\sqrt{15/0.85} = \sqrt{17.647} = 4.201$$

$$Q_a = 16.97 \times 4.201 = 71.3 \text{ GPM}$$

**(b) At 80% stem position:**

$$Q_b = 54.86 \times 4.201 = 230.5 \text{ GPM}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Authority calculation

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta P_{v,@Q_{max}}}{\Delta P_{system,@Q_{max}}}$$

$\Delta P_{v,@Q_{max}} = 15$ psi (design); system head = 25 psi.

$$\beta = \frac{15}{25} = 0.60$$

Authority of **0.60** is in the healthy range (0.5 ‚Äì 1.0 ‚Üí good control).

### Step 4 ‚Äî Analytical interpretation

| Position y/L | $C_v$ | Flow Q (GPM) | % of $Q_{max}$ |
|--------------|-------|--------------|----------------|
| 0.0 | 2.40 | 10.1 | 1.4 % |
| 0.5 | 16.97 | 71.3 | 10.0 % |
| 0.8 | 54.86 | 230.5 | 32.4 % |
| 1.0 | 120.0 | 504.0 | 100.0 % |

Notice how 80% of *travel* yields only ~32% of *flow*. This is the hallmark of EQ% ‚Äî perfect for highly non-linear processes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Disc-Stem-Seat Design Choices

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a KTU favorite for 7-mark sub-questions.

| Service / Application | Disc/Plug Material | Seat Material | Stem Material | Notes |
|-----------------------|--------------------|---------------|---------------|-------|
| General water / air | 316 SS | 316 SS | 17-4 PH SS | Standard |
| Corrosive chemicals | Alloy-20 / Hastelloy C | PTFE / Soft seat | Monel | ANSI Class IV‚ÄìVI |
| High temperature steam (‚â• 400 ¬∞C) | Stellite-faced 316 SS | Stellite-faced 316 SS | Inconel 718 | Hard-facing for erosion |
| Slurry / pulp | 17-4 PH SS w/ hard-facing | Tungsten carbide | 17-4 PH | Robust trim |
| Cryogenic LNG (‚àí196 ¬∞C) | 316L SS | 316L SS | 316L SS (extended bonnet) | Cold-box service |
| Sanitary / pharma | Electropolished 316L | EPDM / PTFE | 316L | CIP/SIP compatible |
| High-pressure drop cavitation | Multi-stage cage + Stellite | Stellite | 17-4 PH | Anti-cavitation trim |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Linearization Constant for EQ% near Design Point

If the loop gain around the operating point $y_0$ is to be matched, differentiate $Q = Q_{max} R^{(y/L - 1)}$:

$$\frac{dQ}{dy} = \frac{Q_{max} \ln R}{L} \cdot R^{(y/L - 1)} = \frac{\ln R}{L} \cdot Q(y)$$

This shows the **process gain $dQ/dy$ is itself proportional to $Q$** ‚Äî exactly what is needed to compensate for a flow-squared process gain $K_p \cdot Q^2$ in the rest of the loop, yielding a *constant* total loop gain.

$$\frac{dQ}{dy} \propto Q \quad \text{(compensates } K_p Q^2\text{)}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Control Valve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Controller Output 4 to 20 mA] --> B[I to P Converter]
    B --> C[Pneumatic Signal 3 to 15 psi]
    C --> D[Diaphragm Actuator]
    D --> E[Stem Position Feedback]
    E --> F[Valve Stem rises or falls]
    F --> G[Disc or Plug moves toward or away from Seat]
    G --> H[Variable Flow Area A]
    H --> I[Process Fluid Flow Modulated]
    I --> J[Process Variable measured by Sensor]
    J --> K[Feedback to Controller]
    subgraph Trim_Assembly
      F
      G
      H
    end
    subgraph Actuator_Assembly
      A
      B
      C
      D
      E
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> The closed loop A ‚Üí K is what distinguishes a *control* valve from a manual *isolation* valve. A **gate valve** typically sits *outside* this loop ‚Äî it is operated manually or by a dedicated on/off actuator.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ‚Äî Disc / Stem / Seat Geometry

```mermaid
    subgraph Inherent_Characteristics
      Q1[Quick Opening] --> R1[High n exponent 5 to 7]
      Q2[Linear] --> R2[Equal travel equals equal flow]
      Q3[Equal Percentage] --> R3[Rangeability R 30 to 50]
      Q4[Modified Parabolic] --> R4[Compromise curve]
    end
    subgraph Trim_Design
      S1[Disc contoured V port or plug] --> S2[Stem linear force transmission]
      S2 --> S3[Seat contoured ring]
      S3 --> S4[Characteristic curve generated]
    end
    R1 --> S1
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S1
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic ‚Äî Gate Valve Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Gate_Valve_Components
      H1[Handwheel or Actuator] --> H2[Stem Nut threads]
      H2 --> H3[Stem rises as handwheel rotates]
      H3 --> H4[Gate or Wedge attached to stem]
      H4 --> H5[Gate moves perpendicular to flow]
      H5 --> H6[Seat rings on either side of gate]
      H6 --> H7[Body cavity houses the trim]
    end
    FlowIn[Process fluid enters] --> H7
    H7 --> FlowOut[Process fluid exits]
    H4 -. Fully open .-> FlowOut
    H4 -. Fully closed .-> X[Flow blocked bubble tight]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ‚Äî Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Equal Percentage valve characteristic." State its rangeability and explain why it is the most widely used characteristic in process control loops.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An equal percentage (EQ%) trim produces a flow vs. travel curve in which **equal increments of stem travel produce equal percentage changes in the existing flow rate** (1 mark). Mathematically, $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(y/L - 1)}$, where $R$ = rangeability, typically 30:1 to 50:1 (1 mark). It is preferred because the inherent non-linear curve compensates the squared-flow process gain ($K_p Q^2$), giving a **nearly constant loop gain** over a wide turndown ‚Äî essential for flow, temperature, and pH control (1 mark).

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]
**Differentiate between "inherent" and "installed" valve characteristics. Mention one practical situation where they differ significantly.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Inherent characteristic* is the flow vs. stem travel relationship measured at **constant pressure drop** across the valve (laboratory) (1 mark). *Installed characteristic* is the same relationship when the valve is fitted in a real pipeline where the available $\Delta P$ varies with flow due to pipe and fitting friction (1 mark). Example: an inherently linear valve becomes effectively "quick-opening" in a high-friction piping system (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚Äî Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **globe valve** used as a control valve. Discuss how the **disc, stem, and seat** geometry generate a linear and an equal-percentage characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A control valve has an equal-percentage trim with maximum $C_v = 95$, rangeability $R = 50$. The valve operates on water ($G_f = 1.0$) with a design $\Delta P$ of 12 psi across the valve, and the system has a no-flow $\Delta P$ of 30 psi. Calculate:
* (i) Valve flow at 25% and 75% stem travel.
* (ii) The valve authority.
* (iii) Comment on the suitability of the design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a)**

A **globe valve** is the most common control-valve body style. Its key parts are:

* **Body** ‚Äì Z-shaped or Y-shaped flow path with a horizontal seat.
* **Seat** ‚Äì A contoured annular ring screwed into the body, often replaceable.
* **Disc/Plug** ‚Äì The moving element on the stem, can be contoured.
* **Stem** ‚Äì Threads through the bonnet and connects to the actuator.
* **Bonnet** ‚Äì Seals the stem, often with packing or bellows.

**Working principle:** The actuator drives the stem vertically, moving the plug toward or away from the seat. The annular area between plug and seat throttles the flow.

**Generating characteristics via trim geometry:**

* **Linear characteristic** ‚Üí Achieved by a **flat-faced plug** and a sharp-edged seat, where the **lifted area increases linearly with stem travel**.
* **Equal Percentage characteristic** ‚Üí Achieved by a **contoured (parabolic) plug** matched to a contoured seat. At low lifts, the gap is tiny; as the plug rises, the gap widens faster than the lift, producing the exponential flow growth.

*Sketch description (to be drawn in the answer booklet):*
> Show a cross-section of a globe valve body with arrows indicating fluid path, the seat, the plug (with contoured profile for EQ% or flat for linear), the stem with actuator on top, and the bonnet with packing. Label the major dimensions: seat diameter $D_s$, stem diameter $d_s$, stroke $L$.

---

#### **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b)**

**Given:**
$C_{v,max} = 95$, $R = 50$, $G_f = 1.0$, $\Delta P_{design} = 12$ psi, $\Delta P_{system,0} = 30$ psi.

**(i) Flow at 25% and 75% stem travel**

Using $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(y/L - 1)}$:

* At y/L = 0.25:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = 50^{(0.25 - 1)} = 50^{-0.75}$$

$$\ln 50 = 3.912 \quad ; \quad -0.75 \times 3.912 = -2.934 \quad ; \quad e^{-2.934} = 0.0532$$

$$C_{v,@0.25} = 0.0532 \times 95 = 5.05$$

$$Q_{25\%} = 5.05 \times \sqrt{12/1.0} = 5.05 \times 3.464 = 17.5 \text{ GPM}$$

* At y/L = 0.75:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = 50^{(0.75 - 1)} = 50^{-0.25}$$

$$-0.25 \times 3.912 = -0.978 \quad ; \quad e^{-0.978} = 0.3762$$

$$C_{v,@0.75} = 0.3762 \times 95 = 35.74$$

$$Q_{75\%} = 35.74 \times 3.464 = 123.8 \text{ GPM}$$

**(ii) Valve authority**

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta P_{v,@Q_{max}}}{\Delta P_{system,@Q_{max}}} = \frac{12}{30} = 0.40$$

**(iii) Comment**

Authority of 0.40 is **below the recommended 0.5 threshold** for tight flow control. The installed characteristic will deviate significantly from the inherent curve, and the loop may exhibit non-linear behavior at high flows. **Recommendation:** Either (a) select a smaller valve to consume more $\Delta P$ (target $\beta \geq 0.5$, i.e., $\Delta P_v \geq 15$ psi), or (b) modify the system curve (e.g., reduce pump head, use a larger pipe).

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Sample 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction, working, and applications of a gate valve**. Compare it with a globe valve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The flow through a control valve follows the equal-percentage law with $R = 30$ and $C_{v,max} = 80$. If the stem travel is increased from 40% to 60%, calculate the **percentage change in flow**. Take $G_f = 0.9$ and $\Delta P = 10$ psi. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a)**

**Construction of a Gate Valve:**

A gate valve is a linear-motion valve with the following components:

* **Body** ‚Äì Straight-through bore with two seat faces on either side of a central cavity.
* **Gate (Disc)** ‚Äì A flat or wedge-shaped metal block that slides across the flow path.
* **Stem** ‚Äì Threaded, rises out of the bonnet as the gate opens (rising-stem design) or rotates without rising (non-rising stem).
* **Seat Rings** ‚Äì Replaceable sealing surfaces against which the gate seats.
* **Bonnet** ‚Äì Houses the stem packing; bolted to the body.
* **Handwheel / Actuator** ‚Äì Rotates the stem nut.

**Working Principle:**

1. Rotating the handwheel turns the stem nut.
2. The threaded stem moves linearly ‚Äî either up (rising stem) or in place (non-rising stem).
3. The gate, attached to the stem, moves perpendicularly to the flow.
4. Fully open ‚Üí gate clears the bore ‚Üí straight-through, low-turbulence flow.
5. Fully closed ‚Üí gate presses against both seats ‚Üí bubble-tight shut-off.

**Types of Gate Designs:**

| Type | Construction | Application |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| **Solid Wedge** | Single solid piece | General service, low cost |
| **Flexible Wedge** | Slightly flexible disc | Handles thermal expansion, better sealing |
| **Split Wedge** | Two-piece self-aligning | High-temperature steam |
| **Parallel Slide Gate** | Two parallel discs with spreader | High-pressure, low-leakage service |

**Applications:**

* Main steam line isolation in power plants.
* Block valve on crude oil pipelines.
* Refinery vessel isolation.
* Anywhere full-bore, low-pressure-drop ON‚ÄìOFF service is required.

**Comparison with Globe Valve:**

| Feature | Gate Valve | Globe Valve |
|---------|------------|-------------|
| Primary use | Isolation (ON‚ÄìOFF) | Throttling (control) |
| Flow direction change | None (straight) | Z-bend |
| Pressure drop (open) | Very low | Higher |
| Seating tightness | Excellent (Class IV‚ÄìV) | Good (depends on trim) |
| Modulating service | Poor (seat erosion) | Excellent |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

---

#### **Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b)**

**Given:** $R = 30$, $C_{v,max} = 80$, $G_f = 0.9$, $\Delta P = 10$ psi.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Compute $C_v$ at 40% and 60% travel**

$$C_v(y) = C_{v,max} \cdot R^{(y/L - 1)}$$

* At y/L = 0.40:

$$C_{v,40} = 80 \times 30^{(0.40 - 1)} = 80 \times 30^{-0.60}$$

$$-0.60 \times \ln 30 = -0.60 \times 3.401 = -2.041 \quad ; \quad e^{-2.041} = 0.1299$$

$$C_{v,40} = 80 \times 0.1299 = 10.39$$

* At y/L = 0.60:

$$C_{v,60} = 80 \times 30^{(0.60 - 1)} = 80 \times 30^{-0.40}$$

$$-0.40 \times 3.401 = -1.360 \quad ; \quad e^{-1.360} = 0.2567$$

$$C_{v,60} = 80 \times 0.2567 = 20.54$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute flows using $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$**

$$\sqrt{\Delta P/G_f} = \sqrt{10/0.9} = \sqrt{11.111} = 3.333$$

$$Q_{40} = 10.39 \times 3.333 = 34.6 \text{ GPM}$$

$$Q_{60} = 20.54 \times 3.333 = 68.5 \text{ GPM}$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Percentage change in flow**

$$\%\Delta Q = \frac{Q_{60} - Q_{40}}{Q_{40}} \times 100 = \frac{68.5 - 34.6}{34.6} \times 100 = 97.9\%$$

**Interpretation:** A 20% increase in stem travel (40% ‚Üí 60%) produced nearly a **98% increase in flow** ‚Äî the hallmark of equal-percentage trim, ideal for processes with squared-flow gain.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in This Topic:**
> 1. **Units Mix-up:** Confusing $C_v$ (GPM @ 1 psi) with $K_v$ (m¬≥/h @ 1 bar). Always check which one the problem uses.
> 2. **Authority Misconception:** Forgetting that $\beta$ must be computed at the *design* (maximum) flow, not at zero flow.
> 3. **EQ% Formula Mistake:** Writing $Q/Q_{max} = R^{y/L}$ instead of $R^{(y/L - 1)}$. The "-1" is essential so that flow is zero (or $Q_{min}$) at y = 0.
> 4. **Gate Valve as Control:** Students often describe a gate valve as a control valve. Examiners deduct 2‚Äì3 marks ‚Äî a gate valve is an *isolation* valve, not a throttling valve.
> 5. **Trim vs Body:** Many students interchange "body" and "trim." Trim = internal wetted parts (disc, stem, seat). Body = outer pressure vessel.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Module 4**

* Control valve = **actuator + body + trim (disc + stem + seat)**.
* **Inherent characteristic** = flow vs. travel at constant $\Delta P$ (lab).
* **Installed characteristic** = flow vs. travel in real piping (varying $\Delta P$).
* **Four inherent characteristics:** Quick-Opening, Linear, Equal Percentage, Modified Parabolic.
* **Quick-Opening** ‚Üí $Q/Q_{max} = 1 - (1 - y/L)^n$, n ‚âà 5‚Äì7. Used for ON‚ÄìOFF.
* **Linear** ‚Üí $Q/Q_{max} = y/L$. Used for level/pressure loops.
* **Equal Percentage** ‚Üí $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(y/L - 1)}$. R = 30‚Äì50. Used for flow/temperature/pH.
* **Valve flow coefficient** $C_v$ = GPM of water at 1 psi $\Delta P$.
* **Valve authority** $\beta = \Delta P_v / \Delta P_{system}$. Target $\beta \geq 0.5$.
* **Rangeability** $R = Q_{max} / Q_{min-controllable}$. Higher R = better turndown.
* **Trim materials** depend on service: 316 SS, Stellite, Hastelloy, PTFE, Tungsten carbide.
* **Gate valve = isolation valve**, not a control valve. Key types: solid, flexible, split, parallel-slide wedge.
* **Globe valve = the standard control-valve body**, with contoured plug + seat for EQ% trim.
* **Disc design (contour)** is what physically shapes the inherent characteristic curve.
* **Seat** is the fixed sealing surface; leakage classes per ANSI/FCI 70-2 (Class VI = bubble-tight).
* **Stem** transmits actuator force; failure modes include vibration, galling, and packing leakage.

> **Mnemonic to recall the 4 characteristics order (Q-L-E-M):** **"Queen Leary's Elephant Marches"** ‚Äî Quick ‚Üí Linear ‚Üí Equal% ‚Üí Modified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Globe Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Globe Valve** is a linear-motion, multi-turn **final control element** (regulating valve) characterized by a spherical (globular) body containing an internal baffle or seat that forces the process fluid to follow a tortuous, S-shaped or Z-shaped flow path. Flow regulation is achieved by the linear vertical translation of a disc/plug against a stationary seat, modulating the annular flow area between the disc and seat ring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification Snapshot**
> - *Category:* Hydraulic Resistance Type / Linear Stroke Valve
> - *Primary Function:* Throttling (flow modulation), isolation, and precise flow control
> - *Motion Type:* Multi-turn (linear stem displacement)
> - *Body Style:* Z-pattern, Y-pattern, Angle-pattern
> - *Typical Service:* Boiler feedwater, steam lines, cooling water, chemical dosing, fuel oil control

The valve derives its name from the **spherical (globe-shaped) external profile** of the pressure-containing body, which is internally divided by a horizontal partition (the seat ring holder) into two chambers. The disc rises and falls on a vertical stem, varying the gap between the disc face and the seat orifice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî The Faucet Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (Faucet Model)**
> Imagine a household water tap (faucet) ‚Äî when you rotate the handle, a rubber washer inside rises or falls against a circular opening. As the washer descends and presses against the seat, the water flow is throttled, and at full contact, flow is shut off. If you leave the handle half-rotated, you obtain partial flow.

The **Globe Valve is the industrial-grade, heavy-duty version of this faucet principle**, scaled up for high-pressure, high-temperature, and chemically aggressive process fluids. The key differences from a domestic tap are:
- The handle is replaced by a **geared handwheel** (for torque amplification)
- The rubber washer is replaced by a **metal disc and seat** (for high temperature and pressure)
- The seat is **replaceable** (because of erosion)
- The motion is **metered** (precision control, not just open/close)

The **S-shaped tortuous path** in a globe valve is functionally equivalent to a speed-breaker: it forces the fluid to lose energy (pressure drop) and provides the resistance that enables precise flow modulation.

## 1.3 Physical Standards & Key Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | Unit |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------------|------|
| Valve flow coefficient | $C_v$ | 0.1 ‚Äì 1500+ | GPM/psi$^{0.5}$ |
| Nominal size | DN | 15 ‚Äì 600 | mm |
| Pressure class | PN / Class | 150 ‚Äì 2500 | lb / bar |
| Rangeability | $R$ | 30 : 1 to 50 : 1 | ratio |
| Stem travel | $L$ | 0.5 ‚Äì 4 | inch |
| Seat diameter | $d_s$ | 0.25 ‚Äì 24 | inch |
| Specific gravity | $SG$ | 0.6 ‚Äì 1.5 (liquids) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most important KTU-board-tested metric for a globe valve is the **Valve Flow Coefficient $C_v$** ‚Äî defined as the flow rate of water in **gallons per minute (GPM)** that produces a **1 psi pressure drop** across a fully open valve at a temperature of **60 ¬∞F (15.6 ¬∞C)**. This is the lingua franca of valve sizing in industrial instrumentation.

## 1.4 Strategic Importance in Industrial Instrumentation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Globe Valves Dominate Process Control Loops:**
> In a typical 4-20 mA current loop from a controller (PID) to a field valve, the globe valve serves as the **muscle** of the control system. A temperature transmitter senses the process variable, a PID controller computes the error, and the I/P (current-to-pressure) transducer converts the 4-20 mA signal into 3-15 psi pneumatic pressure that drives a **diaphragm actuator** on the globe valve stem. Hence, the globe valve is the *final control element* ‚Äî the actuator of last resort in cascade, ratio, and feedback control loops in industries ranging from petrochemical refineries to pharmaceutical reactors and HVAC chillers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working Principle ‚Äî Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The operation of a globe valve can be decomposed into the following sequential logic steps:

- **Step 1 ‚Äî Fluid Entry:** Process fluid enters the valve body through the inlet port and is directed **upward** by the contoured internal geometry.
- **Step 2 ‚Äî Direction Reversal (S-Pattern):** The fluid strikes the **disc** mounted on the vertical stem. The fluid is forced to make a **90¬∞ turn** and squeeze through the annular gap between the disc and the seat.
- **Step 3 ‚Äî Throttling Zone:** This annular gap is the *throttling zone*. The cross-sectional area of this gap is:
$$A_{throat} = \pi \, d_s \, h$$
where $d_s$ is the seat diameter and $h$ is the lift (gap height).
- **Step 4 ‚Äî Direction Reversal (Exit):** After passing through the seat, the fluid changes direction again (another 90¬∞ turn) before exiting the outlet port ‚Äî completing the S-shaped or Z-shaped trajectory.
- **Step 5 ‚Äî Energy Dissipation:** Each of the two 90¬∞ direction changes causes **localized pressure loss** (minor losses) plus **frictional losses** at the disc‚Äìseat gap (major loss). The cumulative loss is the **permanent pressure drop $\Delta P$** of the valve.
- **Step 6 ‚Äî Stem Actuation:** The handwheel (or pneumatic diaphragm/motor actuator) rotates, translating the stem. Stem position $L$ is a monotonic function of actuator input signal (3-15 psi pneumatic or 4-20 mA electric).
- **Step 7 ‚Äî Disc-Seat Contact (Shut-off):** When the disc is forced against the seat with sufficient torque/force, metal-to-metal or soft-seated contact creates a **bubble-tight shutoff**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Two Direction Changes?**
> The dual 90¬∞ reversal is *engineered turbulence*. It is the source of the predictable, repeatable $\Delta P$ vs. $C_v$ relationship that makes globe valves excellent throttling devices. The energy dissipation is *intentional* ‚Äî it converts flow energy (kinetic head) into heat, reducing the downstream pressure in a controlled manner.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Valve Equation (The $C_v$ Formula)

For an incompressible fluid (liquid) flowing through a control valve, the flow rate is given by:

$$Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}}$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate in **US gallons per minute (GPM)**
- $C_v$ = valve flow coefficient (dimensionless, in GPM/psi$^{0.5}$ units)
- $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ = pressure drop across the valve in **psi**
- $SG$ = specific gravity of the fluid relative to water at 60 ¬∞F

**SI Variant (in m¬≥/hr and kPa):**

$$Q = 0.865 \, C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}}$$

where $Q$ is in m¬≥/hr and $\Delta P$ in kPa.

**Inherent Valve Characteristic ‚Äî The Three Classical Curves**

The relationship between stem travel $L/L_{max}$ and the resulting flow rate $Q/Q_{max}$ defines the **inherent flow characteristic** of the valve:

| Characteristic | Equation | Use Case |
|----------------|----------|----------|
| **Quick Opening** | $Q/Q_{max} = \sqrt{L/L_{max}}$ | On/off service, safety shut-off, two-position control |
| **Linear** | $Q/Q_{max} = L/L_{max}$ | Level control, pressure control where $\Delta P$ is fairly constant |
| **Equal Percentage** | $Q/Q_{max} = R^{\left(L/L_{max} - 1\right)}$ | Temperature, flow, and pH control (most common ‚Äî 70% of applications) |

where $R$ is the rangeability ratio (typically $R = 50$ for industrial globe valves).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$ | Liquid flow through valve | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 2 | $Q = 0.865 C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$ | SI version (m¬≥/hr, kPa) | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 3 | $C_v = Q \sqrt{SG / \Delta P}$ | Valve sizing (liquid) | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 4 | $C_v = \dfrac{W}{N_p \, P_1 \, Y} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_1 \, Z}{M \, x}}$ | Gas sizing (extensive) | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 5 | $C_v = \dfrac{W}{63.3} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\Delta P \, (P_1 + P_2)}}$ | Steam sizing | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 6 | $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(L/L_{max} - 1)}$ | Equal percentage characteristic | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 7 | $Q/Q_{max} = L/L_{max}$ | Linear characteristic | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 8 | $Q/Q_{max} = \sqrt{L/L_{max}}$ | Quick opening characteristic | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 9 | $A_{throat} = \pi d_s h$ | Throttling gap area | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 10 | $R = Q_{max} / Q_{min}$ | Rangeability | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 11 | $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ | Permanent pressure drop | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |
| 12 | $F_L^2 \, \Delta P < \Delta P_{choked}$ | Choked flow criterion (liquid) | ‚òÖ‚òÖ‚òÖ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Constants to Memorise:**
> - $C_v$ for water: 1 GPM at 1 psi = **0.865 m¬≥/hr at 1 kPa**
> - Atmospheric pressure: **14.7 psi** = **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 bar**
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**
> - Standard temperature: **T = 520 ¬∞R** = **288.7 K** = **15.6 ¬∞C** = **60 ¬∞F**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Globe Valves:**
> - **Power Plants:** Boiler feedwater regulation, main steam line pressure reduction, attemperator spray control.
> - **Oil & Gas:** Crude oil flow control at wellheads, gas pressure let-down stations, slug catchers.
> - **Chemical Industry:** Acid/alkali flow modulation, polymerisation reactor temperature control (via coolant jacket flow).
> - **HVAC:** Chilled water and hot water coil flow balancing in air-handling units (AHUs).
> - **Pharmaceutical:** Clean steam control, purified water loop pressure regulation.
> - **Food & Beverage:** Steam injection in cookers, syrup flow in batch processes.

The valve is invariably paired with a **positioner** (pneumatic or smart digital positioner) that compares the controller's 4-20 mA setpoint with the actual stem position feedback (via a position sensor) and adjusts the actuator pressure to eliminate hysteresis and improve linearity.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Valve Equation $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$

### Starting Point ‚Äî Conservation Laws

The flow through a control valve is governed by **Bernoulli's equation** (mechanical energy conservation) between the valve inlet (station 1) and the valve outlet (station 2), and the **continuity equation** (mass conservation).

**Bernoulli's Equation (steady, incompressible, inviscid):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

For a horizontal valve: $z_1 = z_2$ (no elevation change). Therefore:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$$

**Neglecting the inlet velocity** (the pipe is much larger than the valve orifice, so $v_1 \approx 0$ in many practical throttle analyses), and recognizing that the kinetic energy downstream is dissipated by turbulence into pressure drop, the effective flow velocity at the vena contracta is:

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

### Step 1 ‚Äî Volumetric Flow Rate Expression

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ (in m¬≥/s) is velocity times effective discharge area $A_e$:

$$Q = A_e \, v_2 = A_e \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

*Textual Explanation:* We multiply the discharge area of the valve opening by the velocity of fluid at that section to obtain volumetric throughput.

### Step 2 ‚Äî Express Density via Specific Gravity

The density of the process fluid is:

$$\rho = SG \times \rho_{water} = SG \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Substituting into the flow equation:

$$Q = A_e \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{SG \times 1000}}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Define the Discharge Coefficient and Convert to $C_v$

A discharge coefficient $C_d$ accounts for the vena contracta effect (the jet narrows after the orifice). Combining $C_d$ with the area $A_e$ and a unit-conversion constant $K$ yields the $C_v$ definition:

$$C_v = K \, C_d \, A_e$$

where $K$ is the conversion factor that ensures $C_v$ has units of **GPM/psi$^{0.5}$**. The fundamental equation becomes:

$$Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Conversion to SI Units

To convert the $C_v$ formula to SI (m¬≥/hr, kPa), the conversion factor becomes $0.865$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{SI} &= 0.865 \, C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}} \\
&\text{where } Q_{SI} \text{ is in m}^3/\text{hr and } \Delta P \text{ is in kPa.}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* The numerical factor $0.865$ arises from the unit conversion: $1 \text{ GPM} = 0.2271 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$, $1 \text{ psi} = 6.895 \text{ kPa}$, and the constant $C = \sqrt{2/\rho} = \sqrt{2/1000} = 0.0447$, all multiplied and rounded to $0.865$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem ‚Äî Valve Sizing for a Boiler Feedwater Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A globe valve is used to control the flow of feedwater to a steam boiler. The required flow rate is **200 GPM** of water at **180 ¬∞F** (SG = 0.987). The inlet pressure is **150 psi** and the outlet pressure is **100 psi**. Determine the required $C_v$ of the valve.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify Given Data:**
- $Q = 200$ GPM
- $P_1 = 150$ psi
- $P_2 = 100$ psi
- $SG = 0.987$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Calculate the Pressure Drop Across the Valve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_1 - P_2 \\
&= 150 - 100 \\
&= 50 \text{ psi}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* The pressure drop is simply the arithmetic difference between the upstream (inlet) and downstream (outlet) static pressures measured at the valve flanges.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply the Valve Sizing Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_v &= Q \sqrt{\frac{SG}{\Delta P}} \\
&= 200 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.987}{50}} \\
&= 200 \times \sqrt{0.01974} \\
&= 200 \times 0.1405 \\
&= 28.10
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* We square the ratio of specific gravity to pressure drop, take its square root, and multiply by the flow rate. The result is the valve flow coefficient that allows 200 GPM at 50 psi pressure drop.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Final Result with Engineering Judgment:**

$$\boxed{C_v = 28.10 \approx 30}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Board Convention:** Always round **up** to the next standard $C_v$ value (e.g., 30 in this case), and verify that the actual $\Delta P$ across the installed valve (with pipe fittings included) does not exceed the **critical pressure drop** $\Delta P_{choked}$, beyond which the flow becomes choked (sonic) and $C_v$ no longer increases with $\Delta P$. The rule of thumb: $\Delta P_{critical} = F_L^2 \times (P_1 - 0.96 \, P_v)$, where $F_L$ is the liquid pressure recovery factor and $P_v$ is the vapour pressure.

## 3.3 Worked Problem ‚Äî Equal Percentage Characteristic Curve Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A globe valve with equal percentage characteristic has a rangeability $R = 50$. Calculate the relative flow rate $Q/Q_{max}$ at stem travels of 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%.

### Solution:

**Governing Equation:**
$$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{\left(\frac{L}{L_{max}} - 1\right)}$$

**Case 1 ‚Äî L/L_max = 0.25:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} &= 50^{(0.25 - 1)} = 50^{(-0.75)} = \frac{1}{50^{0.75}} = \frac{1}{18.80} = 0.0532
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* At 25% stem travel, the flow is only about 5.3% of maximum, demonstrating the high-gain-at-top behaviour.

**Case 2 ‚Äî L/L_max = 0.50:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} &= 50^{(0.50 - 1)} = 50^{(-0.50)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} = \frac{1}{7.07} = 0.1414
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* At 50% travel, the flow is 14.1% of maximum.

**Case 3 ‚Äî L/L_max = 0.75:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} &= 50^{(0.75 - 1)} = 50^{(-0.25)} = \frac{1}{50^{0.25}} = \frac{1}{2.659} = 0.3761
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* At 75% travel, the flow has grown to 37.6% ‚Äî the curve is accelerating.

**Case 4 ‚Äî L/L_max = 1.00:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} &= 50^{(1.00 - 1)} = 50^0 = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* At full travel, the flow is 100% ‚Äî by definition.

**Summary Table:**

| Stem Travel $L/L_{max}$ | $Q/Q_{max}$ | Percentage |
|------------------------|-------------|------------|
| 0.25 | 0.0532 | 5.32% |
| 0.50 | 0.1414 | 14.14% |
| 0.75 | 0.3761 | 37.61% |
| 1.00 | 1.0000 | 100.00% |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The equal percentage characteristic provides **fine control at low flow rates** (high resolution for small adjustments) and **coarse control at high flow rates** ‚Äî this matches the inherent gain of most process loops (level, temperature, pH) where process gain is highest at low flow and lowest at high flow. This is why equal percentage trim is the most common globe valve choice.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix ‚Äî Globe vs. Other Control Valves

| Property | Globe Valve | Ball Valve | Butterfly Valve | Gate Valve |
|----------|-------------|------------|-----------------|------------|
| **Throttling capability** | Excellent | Poor | Moderate | Very poor |
| **Pressure drop** | High | Low | Low | Very low |
| **Leakage class** | Class IV‚ÄìV | Class VI | Class VI | Class III‚ÄìIV |
| **Cost** | High | Low | Low | Low |
| **Size range** | Small‚ÄìLarge | Small‚ÄìLarge | Large‚ÄìVery large | Small‚ÄìLarge |
| **Best for** | Modulating flow | On/off | Large line isolation | Full flow isolation |
| **Stroke type** | Linear | Rotary (90¬∞) | Rotary (90¬∞) | Linear |

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Description (Text Mode ‚Äî Always Shown for Reference)

If mermaid rendering is unavailable, refer to this text-based functional flow:

- **Stage 1 (Signal):** 4-20 mA from PID enters positioner.
- **Stage 2 (Conversion):** Positioner outputs 3-15 psi to diaphragm actuator.
- **Stage 3 (Mechanical Action):** Diaphragm deflects against spring, pushes stem down.
- **Stage 4 (Throttling):** Disc approaches seat, reducing gap $h$ and thus $C_v$.
- **Stage 5 (Process Response):** Flow $Q$ decreases; downstream pressure $P_2$ drops.
- **Stage 6 (Feedback):** LVDT on stem reports actual position back to positioner.
- **Stage 7 (Correction):** Positioner trims actuator pressure to null error.
- **Stage 8 (Steady State):** Valve reaches new equilibrium, closing the loop.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]
**Q: Define the valve flow coefficient $C_v$. Why is it called the "linga franca" of valve sizing?**

**Model Answer:**
The valve flow coefficient $C_v$ is defined as the **flow rate of water in US gallons per minute (GPM)** that produces a **1 psi pressure drop** across a **fully open valve** at a temperature of **60 ¬∞F (15.6 ¬∞C)**. Mathematically, $C_v = Q \sqrt{SG / \Delta P}$ for a liquid. It is called the *lingua franca* (common language) of valve sizing because it provides a single dimensionless number that universally characterises the flow capacity of any control valve (globe, ball, butterfly, gate) regardless of size, manufacturer, or geometry, allowing engineers to compare and select valves consistently. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between inherent and installed characteristics of a globe valve.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Inherent characteristic:** The relationship between stem travel $L/L_{max}$ and flow rate $Q/Q_{max}$ when the **upstream pressure $P_1$ and downstream pressure $P_2$ are held constant**. It is determined purely by the **internal geometry of the valve trim** (disc, seat, cage).
- **Installed characteristic:** The actual relationship obtained in the **field** when the valve is fitted into a piping system, where the process pressure drop varies with flow rate (because of frictional losses in pipes and fittings). Installed characteristics are typically *flatter* and *shifted* compared to inherent characteristics. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äî May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Derive the fundamental valve sizing equation for an incompressible fluid: $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$. Define each term and state the SI equivalent. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A globe valve is required to pass **350 GPM** of a liquid with specific gravity **0.85** when the pressure drop across the valve is **25 psi**. Calculate the required $C_v$. If a valve with $C_v = 45$ is installed, what will be the actual flow rate at the same pressure drop? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) ‚Äî Derivation (7 marks)

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. Key steps to be written in the exam:

1. Start with **Bernoulli's equation** between valve inlet (1) and outlet (2). For a horizontal valve:
$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$

2. Apply **continuity equation** and assume $v_1 \ll v_2$ (vena contracta effect):
$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

3. Compute flow rate: $Q = A_e v_2$ and substitute $\rho = SG \cdot \rho_{water}$:
$$Q = A_e \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{SG \cdot \rho_{water}}}$$

4. Define $C_v$ as the **discharge coefficient times effective area times unit conversion factor**:
$$\boxed{Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}}}$$
where $Q$ is in GPM, $\Delta P$ in psi. The SI equivalent is:
$$Q = 0.865 \, C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}}$$
with $Q$ in m¬≥/hr and $\Delta P$ in kPa. **(7 marks)**

### Part (b) ‚Äî Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Given data:**
$Q = 350$ GPM, $SG = 0.85$, $\Delta P = 25$ psi.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Required $C_v$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_v &= Q \sqrt{\frac{SG}{\Delta P}} \\
&= 350 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.85}{25}} \\
&= 350 \times \sqrt{0.034} \\
&= 350 \times 0.1844 \\
&= 64.54 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* We rearranged the $C_v$ equation, took the ratio of specific gravity to pressure drop, square-rooted it, and multiplied by the design flow rate.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Actual flow with $C_v = 45$ installed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{actual} &= C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{SG}} \\
&= 45 \times \sqrt{\frac{25}{0.85}} \\
&= 45 \times \sqrt{29.41} \\
&= 45 \times 5.423 \\
&= 244.04 \text{ GPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Textual Explanation:* Substituting the installed $C_v$ into the forward flow equation yields the actual flow rate.

**Step 4 ‚Äî Final Result:**

$$\boxed{C_v^{required} = 64.5 \quad ; \quad Q_{actual} = 244 \text{ GPM}}$$

The installed valve ($C_v = 45$) is undersized and will deliver only 244 GPM instead of the desired 350 GPM, so a larger valve must be selected. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ‚Äî Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **globe valve**. List its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the three inherent flow characteristics of control valves: **quick opening, linear, and equal percentage**. For which type of process control loop is each one best suited? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) ‚Äî Globe Valve Construction & Working (7 marks)

**Construction (refer to Section 4.1 architecture):**
A globe valve consists of the following components:

1. **Body** ‚Äî spherical or globular cast steel/forged body containing the flow path.
2. **Bonnet** ‚Äî top cover that houses the stem packing and provides access to internal trim.
3. **Stem** ‚Äî vertical shaft that transmits motion from actuator to disc; threaded for handwheel drive.
4. **Disc/Plug** ‚Äî the moving element that throttles flow; can be flat, contoured, or needle-shaped.
5. **Seat Ring** ‚Äî replaceable metal ring with a precisely machined orifice; the disc seats against it.
6. **Packing** ‚Äî graphite or PTFE rings around the stem to prevent leakage to atmosphere.
7. **Handwheel** ‚Äî manually operated, geared for torque amplification.
8. **Yoke** ‚Äî frame that supports the actuator and stem nut.

**Working:**
The process fluid enters the inlet, is forced to change direction by 90¬∞ as it encounters the closed disc, passes through the **annular gap** between disc and seat (this is the throttling zone), makes another 90¬∞ turn, and exits the outlet. When the handwheel is rotated, the threaded stem descends, pushing the disc toward the seat ‚Äî reducing the gap $h$ and consequently reducing $C_v$ and flow rate $Q$. **(4 marks)**

**Advantages:**
- Excellent throttling capability
- Bubble-tight shutoff with proper seat
- Quick actuation (short stroke)
- Available in large range of materials and sizes
- Predictable $C_v$ vs. lift relationship

**Disadvantages:**
- High permanent pressure drop (turbulent S-path)
- Heavy and bulky
- More expensive than gate or ball valves
- Erosion of seat ring requires periodic replacement **(3 marks)**

### Part (b) ‚Äî Three Inherent Flow Characteristics (7 marks)

**1. Quick Opening (3 marks):**
Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = \sqrt{L/L_{max}}$. A small stem travel produces a large flow change ‚Äî used for **on/off service**, two-position control, and safety isolation. Suited for **safety shut-off valves** in ESD (Emergency Shutdown) systems where the valve must open fully with minimal travel. **Application:** Solenoid valves, dump valves, emergency vent valves.

**2. Linear (2 marks):**
Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = L/L_{max}$. Flow is directly proportional to stem travel. Suited for **processes where the pressure drop across the valve remains approximately constant**, e.g., **liquid level control** in a tank where static head dominates. **Application:** Level control loops, pressure control where $\Delta P$ is constant.

**3. Equal Percentage (2 marks):**
Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(L/L_{max} - 1)}$. Each increment of stem travel produces a constant *percentage* change in flow ‚Äî fine control at low flow, large change at high flow. **Application:** **Temperature control, flow control, pH control** ‚Äî the most common in industry (‚âà70% of installations) because it compensates for the variable process gain typical of these loops. **(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Globe Valve Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $SG$ in the $C_v$ formula** ‚Äî Many students write $C_v = Q / \sqrt{\Delta P}$ without the specific gravity term. This loses 1‚Äì2 marks instantly. Always include $SG$.
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch** ‚Äî Mixing GPM with kPa or m¬≥/hr with psi is a fatal error. KTU board examiners specifically check units. Use $Q = 0.865 C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$ for SI units.
>
> 3. **Confusing inherent and installed characteristics** ‚Äî A 3-mark short-answer question often asks this. Memorise the difference: *inherent = constant $\Delta P$ (lab test); installed = varying $\Delta P$ (field).*
>
> 4. **Not stating the standard $C_v$ condition** ‚Äî When defining $C_v$, students forget to mention "water at 60 ¬∞F, 1 psi drop, fully open valve." This is a **mandatory 1-mark item** in KTU answer keys.
>
> 5. **Drawing the wrong flow path** ‚Äî In a Z-pattern globe valve, the flow enters *upward* and exits *downward* (or vice versa), with **two 90¬∞ turns**. Drawing only one turn or showing flow in a straight line will lose sketch marks.
>
> 6. **Rangeability confusion** ‚Äî Rangeability is $R = Q_{max} / Q_{min}$, NOT the slope of the characteristic curve. Do not confuse it with valve authority $\beta = \Delta P_{valve} / \Delta P_{system}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Globe Valve (Module 4):**
>
> - ‚úÖ Globe valve = **linear-motion, multi-turn, throttling valve** with **S/Z-shaped internal flow path**.
> - ‚úÖ Named after its **spherical (globular) external body shape**, not the flow pattern.
> - ‚úÖ Components: **Body, Bonnet, Stem, Disc, Seat, Packing, Handwheel, Yoke**.
> - ‚úÖ Two 90¬∞ direction changes in the flow path = **engineered pressure drop** = throttling capability.
> - ‚úÖ $C_v$ definition: **GPM of water at 60 ¬∞F producing 1 psi drop across a fully open valve.**
> - ‚úÖ Fundamental equation (US units): $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$.
> - ‚úÖ Fundamental equation (SI units): $Q = 0.865 \, C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$.
> - ‚úÖ Three inherent characteristics: **Quick Opening**, **Linear**, **Equal Percentage**.
> - ‚úÖ Equal percentage is used in **70% of all control loops** (especially temperature, flow, pH).
> - ‚úÖ Equal percentage equation: $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(L/L_{max} - 1)}$ with $R$ = 50 (typical).
> - ‚úÖ Rangeability $R = Q_{max}/Q_{min}$ = 30:1 to 50:1 for globe valves.
> - ‚úÖ Globe valve has **high permanent pressure drop** and **excellent throttling** ‚Äî opposite of gate/ball valves.
> - ‚úÖ Types: **Z-pattern** (standard), **Y-pattern** (high pressure, low turbulence), **Angle pattern** (90¬∞ flow change), **Needle type** (fine control).
> - ‚úÖ **Valve authority** $\beta = \Delta P_{valve}/\Delta P_{system}$ ‚Äî recommended $\beta \geq 0.5$ for good control.
> - ‚úÖ Always size globe valve by **rounding $C_v$ up** to the next standard value.
> - ‚úÖ Critical (choked) flow criterion: $\Delta P \geq F_L^2 (P_1 - 0.96 P_v)$ ‚Äî beyond this, $C_v$ does not increase with $\Delta P$.
> - ‚úÖ Globe valve is a **final control element** in 4-20 mA / 3-15 psi control loops.
> - ‚úÖ Most common actuator: **diaphragm-spring (pneumatic)**, with optional **positioner** for accuracy.
> - ‚úÖ Pneumatic actuator signal standard: **3-15 psi** (or 0.2-1.0 bar in IEC 60534).
> - ‚úÖ Materials of construction: **WCB carbon steel**, **SS 316**, **SS 304**, **monel**, **Hastelloy** for corrosive service.
> - ‚úÖ KTU-2024 syllabus link: **Control Valve Characteristics** in Module 4 ‚Üí direct examination linkage.
