> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plug Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# üîå PLUG VALVE ‚Äî Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Plug Valve** is a **quarter-turn rotary motion final control element** used in process industries to start, stop, or throttle fluid flow. It consists of a cylindrical or conically-tapered **plug** with a straight-through **bore**, which rotates about its own axis within the valve body. Rotation of the plug by **90¬∞** aligns or dis-aligns the bore with the pipeline, thereby controlling the flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Definition (Must Use in Answers):**
> *"A plug valve is a rotary motion valve that uses a cylindrical/conical plug with a through-bore, which can be rotated 90¬∞ about its vertical axis to control the flow of fluid. It is classified as a **quarter-turn valve** under rotary motion final control elements."*

### 1.2 Key Design & Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value/Range |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Bore Size | DN | 15 mm to 600 mm |
| Pressure Rating | PN | 1.0 MPa to 4.0 MPa (ANSI 150# ‚Äì 300#) |
| Operating Temperature | T | ‚àí29 ¬∞C to +230 ¬∞C (standard) |
| Quarter-Turn Angle | Œ∏ | 0¬∞ (open) ‚Äì 90¬∞ (closed) |
| Flow Coefficient | C_v | 0.1 to 4000+ (dimensionless-defined) |
| Body Material | ‚Äî | Cast Iron, WCB, SS 316, Alloy-20 |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚Äî "The Tap You Already Use"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
> Think of the **water tap (bibcock) in your kitchen wall**. When the handle is **parallel** to the pipe, water gushes out. When you rotate it **90¬∞ perpendicular**, flow stops. A plug valve is essentially the **industrial-grade, heavy-duty version** of that tap ‚Äî but engineered to handle:
> 1. **High pressure** (up to 40 bar and beyond)
> 2. **Corrosive chemicals** (acids, caustics, hydrocarbons)
> 3. **Solids-laden slurries** (mining, paper-pulp, wastewater)
>
> The "plug" inside the valve behaves like the **gate inside your tap**, while the **stem** is the equivalent of the handle you twist.

### 1.4 Functional Classification (Where it Fits in Process Control)

```
Final Control Element Hierarchy (KTU Module 4 Context):

Control Loop Architecture
        ‚îÇ
        ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Control Valve (Generic Term)
        ‚îÇ       ‚îÇ
        ‚îÇ       ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Linear Motion Valves
        ‚îÇ       ‚îÇ     ‚îú‚îÄ Globe Valve
        ‚îÇ       ‚îÇ     ‚îú‚îÄ Gate Valve
        ‚îÇ       ‚îÇ     ‚îî‚îÄ Diaphragm Valve
        ‚îÇ       ‚îÇ
        ‚îÇ       ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Rotary Motion Valves ‚òÖ (Plug Valve belongs here)
        ‚îÇ             ‚îú‚îÄ Plug Valve ‚óÑ‚óÑ‚óÑ THIS TOPIC
        ‚îÇ             ‚îú‚îÄ Ball Valve
        ‚îÇ             ‚îú‚îÄ Butterfly Valve
        ‚îÇ             ‚îî‚îÄ Disc Valve
        ‚îÇ
        ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Actuator + Positioner (Smart/Pneumatic/Electric)
```

### 1.5 Why Plug Valves are Critical in Instrumentation

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Significance for PCICT503 Students:**
> 1. Used as **on-off and throttling control valves** in **chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, paper, and water treatment** industries.
> 2. Preferred where **frequent operation** (high cycle life) is required.
> 3. They are **final control elements** ‚Äî the "muscle" of any closed-loop **PID control system**.
> 4. Integrated with **pneumatic diaphragm actuators** or **electric actuators** with **smart positioners** (HART/Fieldbus) for precision modulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# üî¨ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy ‚Äî Component-by-Component

A plug valve has **seven critical sub-systems** that work in coordination:

1. **Body** ‚Äî Cast or forged housing with flanged/threaded/socket-weld end connections; houses the plug and seats.
2. **Plug (Cylindrical or Tapered)** ‚Äî The rotatable core component with a rectangular/circular through-bore; the **heart of the valve**.
3. **Stem** ‚Äî Connects the plug to the actuator; transmits torque.
4. **Packing Gland** ‚Äî Prevents leakage along the stem (typically **PTFE/graphite/asbestos-free** packing).
5. **Seat Rings** ‚Äî Provide the sealing surface against the plug; can be **metallic-to-metallic** or **soft-seated (PTFE/RPTFE)**.
6. **Bonnet/Retainer** ‚Äî Holds packing and stem assembly in place.
7. **Lubricant Injection System** (only in lubricated type) ‚Äî Injects sealant grease via a **lubricant fitting (zerk)** to reduce friction and seal leakage paths.

## 2.2 Operational Logic ‚Äî "The Why and The How"

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Operating Principle:**
>
> **Step 1 ‚Äî Initial State (CLOSED):** The through-bore of the plug is oriented **perpendicular** to the pipeline axis. Solid metal of the plug blocks flow.
>
> **Step 2 ‚Äî Command Signal:** A **4‚Äì20 mA / 3‚Äì15 psi** signal is sent from the **PID controller** to the **valve positioner**.
>
> **Step 3 ‚Äî Actuation:** The **pneumatic actuator / electric motor** applies torque to the **stem**, which rotates the **plug** by an angle `Œ∏`.
>
> **Step 4 ‚Äî Flow Modulation:** As the bore rotates from 0¬∞ to 90¬∞, the effective flow area `A_eff` changes as:
>
> $$A_{eff}(\theta) = A_{bore} \times \cos(\theta)$$
>
> where `A_bore` is the cross-sectional area of the through-bore.
>
> **Step 5 ‚Äî Position Feedback:** An **LVDT/potentiometer** or **Hall-effect sensor** reports the actual plug position back to the positioner for closed-loop control.

## 2.3 Types of Plug Valves (Board-Favourite Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 KTU-Recognised Variants:**
>
> | S.No. | Type | Inherent Characteristic | Typical Application |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Lubricated Plug Valve** | Quick-opening | High-pressure steam, hydrocarbon, gas services |
> | 2 | **Non-Lubricated Plug Valve** | Quick-opening | Low-pressure water, air, vacuum services |
> | 3 | **Eccentric Plug Valve** | Modified equal-percentage | Slurry, paper pulp, wastewater ‚Äî solids handling |
> | 4 | **Expanding Plug Valve** | Linear | High-temperature, thermal expansion duty |
> | 5 | **Multi-Port Plug Valve (3-way/4-way)** | Quick-opening | Flow diversion, blending, mixing manifolds |

## 2.4 Inherent Flow Characteristics (Instrumentation Viewpoint)

> [!TIP]
> A plug valve is inherently a **quick-opening** valve ‚Äî meaning a small rotation at the start produces a disproportionately large flow change. This is because the flow area follows a **cosine function** of rotation angle.
>
> **Comparison Table (Very High-Yield for KTU):**
>
> | Valve Type | Inherent Characteristic | Flow vs. Travel Graph Shape |
> |---|---|---|
> | Plug Valve | **Quick-Opening** | Concave-up (very steep at start) |
> | Ball Valve | Modified Equal % | S-shaped |
> | Globe Valve (contoured plug) | Linear | Straight-line |
> | Globe Valve (linear plug) | Equal Percentage | Convex curve |
> | Butterfly Valve (0¬∞‚Äì60¬∞) | Approximately Equal % | Curved |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **must-know** for KTU Semester Examination. Memorise the symbols and units.

| S.No. | Equation (LaTeX) | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}}$ | **Flow coefficient definition** (incompressible) | Dimensionless-defined |
| 2 | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{SG}}$ | Liquid flow rate through valve | gpm |
| 3 | $A_{eff}(\theta) = A_{bore} \cos(\theta)$ | Effective flow area vs. rotation | m¬≤ |
| 4 | $K_v = 0.865 \times C_v$ | Metric flow coefficient conversion | m¬≥/h |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{SG \times Q^2}{C_v^2}$ | Maximum allowable pressure drop | psi |
| 6 | $T_{total} = T_{seat} + T_{dyn} + T_{pack}$ | Total stem torque | N¬∑m |
| 7 | $T_{seat} = \mu_s \cdot d_{plug} \cdot F_{axial}$ | Seating/unseating torque | N¬∑m |
| 8 | $F_{axial} = \Delta P \cdot A_{seat}$ | Axial force on plug | N |
| 9 | $Q = 0.1047 \cdot n \cdot D^3 \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Empirical flow for plug valve (n = constant) | m¬≥/h |
| 10 | $\eta_{valve} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{recovered}}{\Delta P_{supplied}}$ | Valve recovery coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $F_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{choked}}{P_1 - P_{vapour}}}$ | Liquid pressure recovery factor | Dimensionless |

> **NOTE:** All values of $\vert x \vert$ (absolute) wherever used in calculations should be substituted with the appropriate $\vert \cdot \vert$ or `abs()` function in the calculator; in formulas, use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ notation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters Beyond the Exam:**
> 1. **Process Safety:** Plug valves are part of **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** systems ‚Äî their quarter-turn action allows **rapid isolation** of a section in < 2 seconds.
> 2. **Digital Transformation:** Modern plug valves are integrated with **smart positioners (e.g., Fisher DVC6200, Emerson 3661)** providing **HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus PA** communication.
> 3. **Asset Management:** C_v, actuator torque, and travel percentage are now logged in **DCS/PLC-based condition monitoring systems** (Industry 4.0 / IIoT).
> 4. **Sizing Software:** Standards like **ISA 75.01.01, IEC 60534** define computational procedures ‚Äî KTU questions frequently test your grasp of C_v equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# üìê Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚Äî Flow Coefficient C_v for Incompressible Fluid (Liquid Service)

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. We derive it from the **Bernoulli equation** + **continuity equation** + an empirical discharge model.

### Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Upstream and Downstream

For a horizontal valve (z‚ÇÅ = z‚ÇÇ), considering the valve as a flow restriction with head loss `h_L`:

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

Rearranging to isolate the head loss:

$$h_L = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2 - v_2^2}{2g}$$

**Logic:** The pressure drop across the valve is the sum of the **frictional head loss** plus the **kinetic energy change** due to acceleration through the bore.

### Step 2: Express the Head Loss in Terms of Velocity

For a valve with flow area `A_v` (variable based on travel) and pipe area `A_p`:

$$h_L = K_v \cdot \dfrac{v_v^2}{2g}$$

where `K_v` is the **loss coefficient** of the valve (function of geometry and travel).

**Logic:** All valve head losses can be modelled as a non-dimensional coefficient `K_v` multiplied by the velocity head.

### Step 3: Apply Continuity Equation

The mass flow rate is conserved across the valve:

$$\rho \cdot A_v \cdot v_v = \rho \cdot A_p \cdot v_2$$

Therefore, the velocity through the valve bore is:

$$v_v = \dfrac{A_p}{A_v} \cdot v_2$$

**Logic:** Since the bore area is smaller than the pipe, fluid accelerates ‚Äî this is a fundamental control on `v_v`.

### Step 4: Substitute into Bernoulli and Simplify

Combining the above, the volumetric flow rate through the valve is:

$$Q = A_v \cdot v_v = A_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot K_v}}$$

where $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ (pressure drop across the valve).

**Logic:** This is the **fundamental orifice-like equation** for flow through any restriction. The key is the dependence on $\sqrt{\Delta P}$ ‚Äî the source of the square-root inherent characteristic.

### Step 5: Define the Flow Coefficient C_v

By definition, **C_v** is the flow rate of water (SG = 1.0) in **gpm** that produces a pressure drop of **1 psi** across the valve when fully open. Mathematically, the coefficient that absorbs the constants is:

$$C_v = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\Delta P / SG}}$$

Rearranging gives the **standard KTU formula**:

$$\boxed{Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{SG}}}$$

**Logic:** C_v lumps together the geometric area `A_v`, the loss coefficient `K_v`, the fluid density, and the unit conversion constants into a single sizing parameter.

### Step 6: Numerical Example for Practice (Covered in Question Bank)

> See Section 5.2, Question B(b) for a fully worked numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚Äî Effective Flow Area vs. Rotation Angle (The Cosine Law)

### Step 1: Define Geometry

Let the plug bore be a **rectangular slot of width `w` and length `L`**, or a **circular hole of diameter `D_bore`**. The flow area when fully open is:

$$A_{bore} = w \cdot L \quad \text{or} \quad A_{bore} = \dfrac{\pi D_{bore}^2}{4}$$

### Step 2: Project the Bore onto the Pipe Cross-Section

When the plug rotates by angle `Œ∏` from the fully-open position, the projected area visible to the flow is:

$$A_{eff}(\theta) = A_{bore} \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

**Logic:** As the plug rotates, the rectangular bore presents less and less open area to the flow ‚Äî a simple geometric projection.

### Step 3: Derive the Flow vs. Travel Relationship

Substituting into the C_v equation, with the **local C_v(Œ∏)** proportional to the effective area:

$$C_v(\theta) = C_{v,\max} \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

Therefore:

$$Q(\theta) = C_{v,\max} \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{SG}}$$

**Logic:** This is why a plug valve is **quick-opening** ‚Äî at small Œ∏ near 90¬∞, the function $\cos(\theta)$ is still very close to 0, so flow rises steeply. At Œ∏ = 0 (closed), flow is zero; at Œ∏ = 90¬∞ (fully open), flow is maximum.

### Step 4: Plot the Characteristic Curve

| Rotation Œ∏ (deg) | cos(Œ∏) | Normalised Flow Q/Q_max |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 0% (Closed) |
| 15 | 0.966 | 96.6% |
| 30 | 0.866 | 86.6% |
| 45 | 0.707 | 70.7% |
| 60 | 0.500 | 50.0% |
| 75 | 0.259 | 25.9% |
| 90 | 0.000 | 0% (Open? ‚Äî see note) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Clarification:** In practice, the convention is that **0¬∞ = FULLY OPEN** and **90¬∞ = FULLY CLOSED** (or vice-versa). The above table assumes 0¬∞ = closed, 90¬∞ = open, which makes `cos(0) = 1` at Œ∏ = 90¬∞. The KTU board may use either convention ‚Äî **always state your assumption in the answer**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚Äî Total Stem Torque for Actuator Sizing

### Step 1: Identify Torque Components

The total torque the actuator must deliver to rotate the plug against process forces is the sum of three components:

$$T_{total} = T_{seat} + T_{dyn} + T_{pack}$$

**Logic:** Each component represents a different physical resistance to motion.

### Step 2: Derive the Seating Torque T_seat

The seating torque arises from the **friction between the plug and the seat** under the axial seating force `F_axial`:

$$T_{seat} = \mu_s \cdot d_{plug} \cdot F_{axial}$$

where `Œº_s` is the static friction coefficient (‚âà 0.1 to 0.3), and `d_plug` is the effective friction radius.

**Logic:** When the plug first unseats from the seat, the contact force generates frictional resistance ‚Äî this is the "breakaway" torque.

### Step 3: Derive the Dynamic Torque T_dyn

The dynamic torque is due to the **fluid pressure acting on the unbalanced area** of the plug during rotation:

$$T_{dyn} = \dfrac{\mu_d \cdot d_{plug} \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{unbalance}}{2}$$

**Logic:** As the plug rotates, there is always an offset between the upstream and downstream pressure forces ‚Äî this creates a moment that the actuator must overcome.

### Step 4: Derive the Packing Torque T_pack

The packing torque is due to the **friction of the stem against the packing material**:

$$T_{pack} = \mu_{pack} \cdot d_{stem} \cdot L_{pack} \cdot P_{pack}$$

where `Œº_pack` is the packing friction coefficient, `d_stem` is the stem diameter, `L_pack` is the packing length, and `P_pack` is the packing preload.

**Logic:** The packing prevents leakage along the stem, but it also creates a constant frictional drag ‚Äî this must be added to the actuator torque budget.

### Step 5: Safety Factor Application

Industry practice (ISA 75.13) recommends adding a **1.25 to 1.5√ó safety factor**:

$$T_{actuator} = 1.35 \times T_{total}$$

**Logic:** Real-world wear, aging, pressure surges, and process upsets require a margin ‚Äî this is a board-favourite practical point.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚Äî Key Trade-offs at a Glance

| Feature | Plug Valve | Ball Valve | Butterfly Valve | Globe Valve |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Motion Type** | Rotary (¬º turn) | Rotary (¬º turn) | Rotary (¬º turn) | Linear |
| **C_v Capacity** | Medium-High | High | High | Low-Medium |
| **Leakage Class** | Class IV (typical) | Class VI (soft seat) | Class V | Class IV-V |
| **Slurry Service** | Excellent (eccentric) | Poor | Poor | Poor |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Moderate | Low | High |
| **Throttling** | Fair (quick-opening) | Poor | Good (equal %) | Excellent |
| **Size Range** | ¬Ω" to 24" | ¬Ω" to 60" | 2" to 120" | ¬Ω" to 24" |
| **Maintenance** | Lubrication needed (lubricated) | Minimal | Minimal | High |

# üìù KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024]
**"Define a plug valve. List any four advantages and two disadvantages of plug valves."**

**Model Answer:**

A plug valve is a **rotary motion quarter-turn valve** that controls fluid flow by rotating a cylindrical or conically-tapered plug (with a through-bore) by 90¬∞ within the valve body.

**Advantages (Any four):**
1. **Compact and lightweight** design compared to globe valves of equivalent size.
2. **Simple quarter-turn operation** ‚Äî easy to automate with pneumatic/electric actuators.
3. **Low flow resistance** when fully open ‚Äî minimal pressure drop.
4. **Reliable tight shutoff** in lubricated versions with proper sealant injection.
5. **Excellent for slurry and viscous service**, especially the eccentric plug valve variant.

**Disadvantages (Any two):**
1. **High friction** between the plug and seat ‚Üí requires substantial actuator torque.
2. **Frequent lubrication** is required in lubricated plug valves (periodic sealant injection).

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]
**"Explain with a sketch the working of an eccentric plug valve and state its main application."**

**Model Answer:**

In an **eccentric plug valve**, the rotational axis of the plug is **offset (eccentric)** from the centreline of the pipe. When the valve is **fully open**, the plug is positioned such that it is **completely clear of the seat** ‚Äî the through-bore aligns with the pipeline and the plug does not touch the seat at all.

**Working:**
- In the **closed position**, the plug is rotated downward, pressing against the seat to provide a tight seal.
- As the plug rotates from closed to open, it **lifts off the seat** in a cam-like motion, breaking friction with the seat within a few degrees of opening.
- This **"lift-off"** action prevents the seat from being scraped by the plug ‚Äî ideal for **slurries, fibrous fluids, and solids-laden streams**.

**Main Applications:**
- **Paper and pulp industry** (stock lines)
- **Wastewater and sewage treatment**
- **Mining slurries and tailings**
- **Chemical services** with crystallising or polymerising fluids

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ‚ùì Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2024]

**(a) With the help of a neat constructional diagram, explain the working of a lubricated plug valve. Discuss the role of lubricant and its properties.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (refer Section 4.1 diagram):**
A lubricated plug valve consists of:
1. **Body** with flanged/screwed ends
2. **Tapered plug** with a rectangular through-bore
3. **Stem** connected to the plug
4. **Lubricant grooves** machined on the plug's surface (top, bottom, sides)
5. **Lubricant fitting (zerk)** on the body for sealant injection
6. **Packing gland** at the stem entry
7. **Sealant channels** that carry lubricant from the zerk to the plug-seat interface

**Working:**
- The plug is held in place by **mechanical wedge action** (or a spring-loaded ball detent) and rotated by the stem.
- The bore in the plug, when aligned with the pipeline, permits flow.
- The plug is rotated **90¬∞** to bring the solid face across the flow path, achieving shutoff.
- The **lubricant is injected under pressure** through the zerk fitting, flows through the grooves, and forms a **thin sealant film** between the plug and seat.

**Role of Lubricant:**
- **Sealing** ‚Äî fills any micro-leakage paths between plug and seat.
- **Lubrication** ‚Äî reduces friction, lowers operating torque, and extends plug life.
- **Anti-corrosion** ‚Äî provides a protective film on metal surfaces.

**Properties of Lubricant:**
1. **Viscosity-stable** across operating temperature range.
2. **Chemically inert** to the process fluid.
3. **Resistant to water washout**.
4. **Non-toxic and food-grade** (for pharma/food service).
5. **High film strength** to withstand pressure.

---

**(b) Compare lubricated, non-lubricated, and eccentric plug valves. List the industrial applications of each.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lubricated Plug Valve | Non-Lubricated Plug Valve | Eccentric Plug Valve |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Friction reduction** | By injected lubricant | By PTFE sleeve/lining | By offset rotation |
| **Maintenance** | Regular lubrication | Low (PTFE may wear) | Low to moderate |
| **Pressure rating** | Up to ANSI 600# | Up to ANSI 150# | Up to ANSI 300# |
| **Temperature** | Up to 230 ¬∞C | Up to 200 ¬∞C | Up to 200 ¬∞C |
| **Tightness** | Excellent (Class IV) | Good (Class IV) | Good (Class IV) |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower | Moderate |
| **Slurry suitability** | Poor | Poor | **Excellent** |
| **Service life** | Long (with maintenance) | Medium | Long |

**Industrial Applications:**

- **Lubricated:** Hydrocarbon pipelines, gas distribution, high-pressure steam, oil refinery manifolds.
- **Non-Lubricated:** Low-pressure water, air, vacuum, mild chemical service, HVAC.
- **Eccentric:** Paper-pulp, sewage, mining slurries, sugar processing, fly-ash handling.

---

### ‚ùì Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2023]

**(a) Derive the flow coefficient (C_v) equation for an incompressible fluid flowing through a control valve. State clearly the units and assumptions.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Section 3.1, Derivation 1, for the full step-by-step derivation. The condensed exam-style answer is:)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, incompressible flow (liquid).
2. The valve behaves as a variable-area orifice.
3. Pressure energy converts to kinetic energy across the valve with a head loss.
4. Body is horizontal; z‚ÇÅ = z‚ÇÇ.

**Derivation (key steps):**

$$\text{Bernoulli:} \quad \dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

$$\text{Head loss:} \quad h_L = K_v \cdot \dfrac{v_v^2}{2g}$$

$$\text{Continuity:} \quad Q = A_v \cdot v_v$$

Substituting and solving:

$$Q = A_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho K_v}}$$

**Define C_v** as the flow rate (in gpm) of water (SG = 1) that produces ŒîP = 1 psi:

$$\boxed{C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{SG}}}$$

**Units:** Q in gpm, ŒîP in psi, SG dimensionless, C_v dimensionless-defined.

---

**(b) A plug valve has a C_v of 80. It is used to control water (SG = 1.0). The pressure drop across the valve is 36 psi. Calculate:** **(7 marks)**
- **(i) The volumetric flow rate in gpm.**
- **(ii) The flow rate in m¬≥/h, if the metric coefficient K_v is 0.865 √ó C_v.**
- **(iii) Comment on the result if SG of the fluid changes to 0.85 (oil service).**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** C_v = 80, ŒîP = 36 psi, SG_water = 1.0.

**(i) Flow rate in gpm:**

$$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{SG}}$$

$$Q = 80 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{36}{1.0}}$$

$$Q = 80 \cdot 6 = \mathbf{480 \; gpm}$$

**(ii) Conversion to m¬≥/h:**

$$K_v = 0.865 \times C_v = 0.865 \times 80 = 69.2$$

For water (œÅ = 1000 kg/m¬≥), the K_v formula gives:

$$Q = K_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{bar}}{\rho / 1000}}$$

Convert ŒîP: $36 \; psi = 36 \times 0.06895 = 2.482 \; bar$

$$Q = 69.2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2.482}{1.0}} = 69.2 \times 1.575 = \mathbf{109.0 \; m^3/h}$$

**Cross-check:** 480 gpm √ó 0.2271 = 109.0 m¬≥/h ‚úì

**(iii) Effect of changing to oil (SG = 0.85):**

$$Q_{oil} = 80 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{36}{0.85}} = 80 \times 6.508 = \mathbf{520.6 \; gpm}$$

**Comment:** Lowering the specific gravity (lighter fluid) **increases** the flow rate at the same pressure drop ‚Äî because $\sqrt{1/SG}$ increases as SG decreases. This shows why **C_v must be re-evaluated** when the process fluid changes.

---

## 5.3 ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Plug Valve Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the C_v formula direction** ‚Äî The correct form is $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$. Many students wrongly write $Q = C_v \sqrt{SG / \Delta P}$ ‚Äî this gives the **inverse-square** behaviour and is incorrect.
> 2. **Forgetting units** ‚Äî ŒîP must be in **psi** (not bar or kPa) and Q in **gpm** for the C_v formula. Mixing units is a 1‚Äì2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** ‚Äî In derivation questions, omitting the assumption *"incompressible, steady-state, horizontal valve, z‚ÇÅ = z‚ÇÇ"* costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing quarter-turn angle convention** ‚Äî 0¬∞ vs. 90¬∞ as open/closed. Always state your convention in the answer.
> 5. **Drawing a butterfly valve and labelling it as a plug valve** ‚Äî A common mistake. A plug valve has a **cylindrical/conical plug with a through-bore**; a butterfly valve has a **flat disc on a shaft** rotated within the pipe.
> 6. **Skipping the "Application" part** ‚Äî In comparison questions, students describe the design but forget the **industrial application** ‚Äî this loses 2‚Äì3 marks.

---

## 5.4 üìã Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**
>
> - ‚úÖ **Plug valve = quarter-turn rotary valve** with a tapered/cylindrical plug containing a through-bore.
> - ‚úÖ Rotation by **90¬∞** transitions between fully open and fully closed.
> - ‚úÖ Classified as a **rotary motion final control element** (Module 4 topic).
> - ‚úÖ Five major types: **Lubricated, Non-Lubricated, Eccentric, Expanding, Multi-Port**.
> - ‚úÖ **Inherent characteristic: Quick-opening** ‚Äî flow ‚àù $\cos(\theta)$ (or its variant).
> - ‚úÖ **Master formula:** $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$ (C_v in gpm, ŒîP in psi).
> - ‚úÖ **Metric conversion:** $K_v = 0.865 \times C_v$.
> - ‚úÖ **Eccentric plug valve** is the **best choice for slurry and fibrous fluids** (examiner's favourite application question).
> - ‚úÖ **Lubricated plug valve** uses a sealant (zerk fitting) to **reduce friction + seal leakage paths** simultaneously.
> - ‚úÖ **Stem torque** for actuator sizing: $T_{total} = T_{seat} + T_{dyn} + T_{pack}$, multiplied by safety factor **1.35√ó**.
> - ‚úÖ **Standard leakage class** is Class IV (metallic seat) ‚Äî not as tight as Class VI of soft-seated ball valves.
> - ‚úÖ **Preferred applications:** ESD systems, hydrocarbon manifolds, slurry lines, paper-pulp, mining, wastewater.
> - ‚úÖ **Industry standards:** ISA 75.01.01 (sizing), ISA 75.13 (actuator sizing), IEC 60534 (control valve terminology).
> - ‚úÖ **Always include a diagram** in KTU answers ‚Äî it carries 1‚Äì2 marks even in theory questions.
> - ‚úÖ **Mention control loop integration** ‚Äî signal flow from PID ‚Üí positioner ‚Üí actuator ‚Üí plug ‚Üí process.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ball Valve
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ball Valve

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Ball Valve** is a **quarter-turn rotational motion valve** that employs a **hollow, perforated, and pivoting sphere (ball)** ‚Äî typically manufactured from corrosion-resistant alloys such as stainless steel, brass, or PVC ‚Äî to regulate, isolate, or throttle the flow of process fluids in a pipeline. The ball contains a **cylindrical bore (port)** through its centre, oriented along the flow axis when the valve is fully **OPEN** and perpendicular to the flow axis when the valve is fully **CLOSED**. The ball is supported either by **floating** on two seats (sealing rings) or rigidly mounted between two **trunnions**, with the rotational position controlled manually via a lever/handle or automatically via pneumatic, hydraulic, electric, or electro-mechanical **actuators**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 ‚Äî Control Valves & Final Control Elements):**
> Ball valves are classified as **rotary stem-motion control valves**. They form a critical subclass of **final control elements** studied under the broader topic of *Industrial Control Valves*. Examiners frequently test the linkage between valve geometry and **inherent flow characteristics (Cv vs. stem position)**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy ‚Äî The Intuitive Picture

Imagine a **marble (the ball)** with a hole drilled straight through it, sitting inside a **cylindrical pipe (the valve body)**. Now imagine pushing a stick through the side of the pipe so the marble spins on it:

- **Valve OPEN (lever parallel to pipe):** The hole in the marble aligns perfectly with the pipe ‚Üí fluid rushes straight through with almost no obstruction. Think of it as a **sliding door fully open**.
- **Valve CLOSED (lever perpendicular to pipe):** The solid side of the marble now blocks the pipe completely ‚Üí fluid cannot pass. Think of it as a **rotating door slammed shut**.
- **Partially open (lever at 45¬∞):** The fluid is squeezed through a **crescent-shaped opening**, which behaves like a variable-area orifice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Unlike a gate valve that *slides linearly*, a ball valve simply **rotates 90¬∞** to go from fully closed to fully open. This quarter-turn action makes it the **fastest-responding on/off valve** in industrial service.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (KTU 2024 Mandatory Recall)

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Bore Size | **DN** | **15 mm ‚Äì 600 mm** |
| Pressure Rating | **PN / ANSI Class** | **PN 16 ‚Äì PN 100 (Class 150 ‚Äì 600)** |
| Operating Temperature | $T_{op}$ | **-196 ¬∞C to +400 ¬∞C** (metal seated) |
| Flow Coefficient | $C_v$ | **5 ‚Äì 5000** (size dependent) |
| Rotation Angle | $\theta$ | **0¬∞ (closed) ‚Üí 90¬∞ (open)** |
| Seat Leakage Class | **ANSI/FCI 70-2** | **Class IV ‚Äì Class VI** (soft seat) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Component-by-Component Breakdown)

The **functional architecture** of a ball valve is decomposed into six primary sub-assemblies:

1. **Body** ‚Äî The pressure-containing outer shell. May be *one-piece*, *two-piece (split body)*, or *three-piece (bolted body)*. Materials are selected based on **chemical compatibility** (e.g., WCB carbon steel for hydrocarbons, CF8M stainless steel for corrosive chemicals).
2. **Ball** ‚Äî The spherical closure element with a precision-machined **bore**. Surface finish of the ball dictates leakage class.
3. **Seats (Sealing Rings)** ‚Äî Typically **PTFE (Teflon)**, **RPTFE**, **PEEK**, **NYLON**, or **metal-to-metal (Stellite)**. Provide bubble-tight shut-off when the ball is rotated 90¬∞.
4. **Stem** ‚Äî Transmits torque from the actuator to the ball. Sealed externally via **O-rings** or **graphite packing** to prevent atmospheric leakage.
5. **Stem Seal / Packing** ‚Äî Prevents process fluid from escaping along the stem. Critical for **fugitive emission compliance** (API 624, ISO 15848).
6. **Actuator Coupling / Handle** ‚Äî Manual lever (with locking facility) or actuator interface per **ISO 5211** mounting pad standard.

## 2.2 Operational Principle ‚Äî Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The ball valve operates on the principle of **rotational alignment of a cylindrical bore with respect to the pipe axis.**

- **Step 1 (Initial state ‚Äî Closed):** Stem rotates ball such that bore axis $\perp$ pipe axis. Ball's solid face contacts both upstream and downstream seats ‚Üí **zero flow**.
- **Step 2 (Opening stroke, $0¬∞ < \theta < 90¬∞$):** Ball rotates. The exposed flow area $A_f$ increases non-linearly with $\theta$.
- **Step 3 (Fully Open, $\theta = 90¬∞$):** Bore axis $\parallel$ pipe axis. Flow path is essentially a smooth-bore pipe of diameter equal to the ball bore ‚Üí **minimum pressure drop**.
- **Step 4 (Closing):** Reverse rotation. Seats **scrape** against the ball surface, dislodging particulates ‚Üí **self-cleaning action**.

## 2.3 Types of Ball Valves (Classification Matrix)

| Type | Construction | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Floating Ball** | Ball is not fixed; it "floats" downstream under pressure. Seat provides sealing. | Low-pressure, small-bore (‚â§ DN 100), general service. |
| **Trunnion-Mounted** | Ball fixed on top and bottom trunnions. Seats are spring-loaded. | High-pressure, large-bore (‚â• DN 150), oil & gas transmission. |
| **V-Port Ball** | Ball has a 'V'-shaped notch instead of cylindrical bore. | **Throttling service** ‚Äî linear/equal-percentage characteristic. |
| **Cavity-Filler** | Body cavity filled with solid material on the downstream side. | Hygienic/pharma service ‚Äî eliminates fluid stagnation. |
| **Multi-Port (3-way / 4-way)** | L-shaped or T-shaped bore for flow diversion. | Mixing/diverting service in chemical plants. |
| **Top-Entry** | Bonnet detachable from top ‚Üí maintenance without line dismantling. | Critical services where downtime is expensive. |
| **Fully-Welded (Monoblock)** | Body welded to pipe ‚Äî no flanges, no bolted joints. | Buried pipelines, district heating. |

## 2.4 Inherent Flow Characteristic of Ball Valves

The standard full-bore ball valve exhibits a **quick-opening (on/off) characteristic** for the first $\sim$**30¬∞** of rotation, after which flow becomes nearly insensitive to further rotation. This makes it **unsuitable for proportional throttling** unless modified (V-port or characterized ball).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Focus Point:**
> When asked *"Why is a standard ball valve unsuitable for throttling?"* ‚Äî answer must mention:
> 1. Highly **non-linear** inherent characteristic (most of the flow occurs between 0¬∞‚Äì30¬∞).
> 2. **Seat erosion** due to high fluid velocity across partially exposed ball.
> 3. **Cavitation damage** at partial openings.
> **Solution:** Use a **V-port ball valve** or install a **characterizing positioner**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$ | Volumetric flow rate for **incompressible** fluids (liquid) | $Q$ in gpm, $\Delta P$ in psi |
| 2 | $Q = N_1 C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$ | Flow with *N1* dimensionless piping geometry factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $W = 0.865 \, C_v \, \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot P_1 \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)}$ | Mass flow for **gases** at critical pressure drop | $W$ in kg/h |
| 4 | $C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}$ | Rearranged flow coefficient | US gpm |
| 5 | $A_{port} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of the ball bore | m¬≤ |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{port}}$ | **Fluid velocity** through the valve (verify < 3 m/s for throttling) | m/s |
| 7 | $\tau_{br} = \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{d^3}{8}$ | Approximate **break-away torque** for floating ball valve | N¬∑m |
| 8 | $\tau_{act} = K_s \cdot (\Delta P \cdot d^3 + f_s \cdot d^{2.5})$ | Actuator sizing torque empirical formula | N¬∑m |
| 9 | $L_e/d = 3$ | Equivalent length of ball valve (for $K$-factor $= 0.05$ in fully open condition) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $K = f_T \cdot \dfrac{L_e}{D}$ | Resistance coefficient (fully open ball valve $f_T \approx 0.05$) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above: $G_f$ = specific gravity of fluid (water = 1.0), $P_1$ = upstream absolute pressure, $\theta$ = ball rotation angle from closed, $d$ = ball diameter, $f_s$ = seat friction coefficient.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Flow Coefficient ($C_v$) for a Liquid Service

The **flow coefficient** is the fundamental sizing parameter mandated by ISA-75.01 / IEC 60534 standards. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Start with the mechanical energy equation (Bernoulli)

For an incompressible fluid flowing through a restriction (the valve port):

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \Delta P = \dfrac{\rho}{2} (v_2^2 - v_1^2) + \rho g (z_2 - z_1) + h_f
$$

*(Assuming horizontal valve, $z_2 - z_1 = 0$, and neglecting minor losses within the valve itself.)*

### Step 2 ‚Äî Apply continuity equation at the vena contracta

Let $A_v$ = area of the valve port and $C_c$ = coefficient of contraction.

$$
Q = C_c \cdot A_v \cdot v_2
$$

$$
\therefore v_2 = \dfrac{Q}{C_c \cdot A_v}
$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Substitute back into Bernoulli

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho}{2} \left(\dfrac{Q}{C_c \cdot A_v}\right)^2
$$

Rearranging for $Q$:

$$
Q = C_c \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Convert to KTU standard units (US gal/min, psi)

We perform dimensional substitution: $\rho \to G_f \cdot \rho_{water}$, $\Delta P$ in psi, and introduce a dimensionless valve characteristic constant $C_d$ (discharge coefficient).

$$
Q \, [\text{gpm}] = C_d \cdot A_v \, [\text{in}^2] \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P \, [\text{psi}] \cdot 144 \, [\text{in}^2/\text{ft}^2]}{G_f \cdot 62.4 \, [\text{lb/ft}^3]}}
$$

After algebraic simplification (numerical constant evaluates to **29.9**):

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot 29.9 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}}
$$

### Step 5 ‚Äî Define $C_v$ as the standardized flow coefficient

$$
C_v \;\equiv\; C_d \cdot A_v \cdot 29.9
$$

### Step 6 ‚Äî Final Governing Equation

$$
\boxed{\; Q \;=\; C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}} \;}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**

- A valve with $C_v = 1$ will pass **1 US gallon per minute** of water at a **1 psi pressure drop**.
- $C_v$ scales linearly with port area ‚Üí doubling port diameter ‚Üí $C_v$ increases by factor of **4**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem (Module 4 ‚Äî Past Exam Style):**
A ball valve is required to pass $Q = 120$ L/min of an oil with specific gravity $G_f = 0.85$ at a pressure drop $\Delta P = 2.5$ bar. Compute (i) the required $C_v$, and (ii) whether the fluid velocity exceeds the recommended throttling limit of $3$ m/s for a selected valve bore of $d = 25$ mm.

### Part (i) ‚Äî $C_v$ calculation

**Convert units to KTU/US standard:**
- $Q = 120$ L/min $\times 0.2642$ = **31.7 gpm**
- $\Delta P = 2.5$ bar $\times 14.504$ = **36.26 psi**

Apply the $C_v$ equation:

$$
C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}
$$

$$
C_v = 31.7 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.85}{36.26}}
$$

$$
C_v = 31.7 \cdot \sqrt{0.02345}
$$

$$
C_v = 31.7 \cdot 0.1531
$$

$$
\boxed{\; C_v \;\approx\; 4.85 \;}
$$

### Part (ii) ‚Äî Velocity check

**Compute port area:**

$$
A_{port} = \dfrac{\pi \, d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Convert volumetric flow rate to SI:**

$$
Q = 120 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Compute velocity:**

$$
v = \dfrac{Q}{A_{port}} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.07 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
v = 4.07 \text{ m/s} \;>\; 3 \text{ m/s} \quad \therefore \text{ valve is unsuitable for throttling service.}
$$

**Recommendation:** Select a **larger bore valve** (e.g., DN 40) or use a **V-port ball valve** specifically designed for throttling.

---

## 3.3 Actuator Sizing ‚Äî Break-Away Torque Derivation

For a **floating ball valve**, the break-away torque (force required to overcome static friction and initiate rotation) is derived by equating the **pressure-times-area force** on the ball with the **friction force** at the seat:

### Step 1 ‚Äî Pressure force on the ball

$$
F_p = \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d^2}{4}
$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Friction force at the seat contact

$$
F_f = f \cdot F_{seat} = f \cdot \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d^2}{4}
$$

*(Here $f$ = friction coefficient at seat = 0.1 for PTFE, 0.3 for metal.)*

### Step 3 ‚Äî Torque arm

The moment arm from the stem centreline to the seat contact point is approximately $d/2$. Therefore:

$$
\tau_{br} = F_f \cdot \dfrac{d}{2} = f \cdot \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \tau_{br} \;=\; f \cdot \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d^3}{8} \;}
$$

For a stainless-steel-on-PTFE seat combination ($f = 0.1$, $d = 0.05$ m, $\Delta P = 1 \times 10^6$ Pa):

$$
\tau_{br} = 0.1 \times 10^6 \times \dfrac{\pi (0.05)^3}{8} = 0.1 \times 10^6 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \tau_{br} \;\approx\; 4.91 \text{ N¬∑m} \;}
$$

This torque is then multiplied by a **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0** to size the actuator.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ‚Äî Functional Architecture of a Ball Valve Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Control Signal 4-20 mA] --> B[Positioner]
    B --> C[Actuator Pneumatic / Electric / Hydraulic]
    C --> D[Stem Coupling ISO 5211]
    D --> E[Stem Seal Packing]
    E --> F[Ball 90 deg Rotation]
    F --> G{Seats Upstream and Downstream}
    G --> H[Valve Body Pressure Vessel]
    H --> I[Process Pipeline]
    J[Fluid Inlet] --> H
    H --> K[Fluid Outlet]
    style A fill:#FFD580
    style B fill:#B8E0FF
    style C fill:#B8E0FF
    style F fill:#FF9999
    style G fill:#C8E6C9
    style H fill:#D0D0D0
```

## 4.2 Constructional Anatomy ‚Äî Exploded Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BL["BOTTOM HALF OF VALVE BODY"]
        B1[Inlet Flange]
        B2[Lower Seat PTFE]
        B3[Ball with Cylindrical Bore]
        B4[Upper Seat PTFE]
        B5[Outlet Flange]
    end
    subgraph TL["TOP HALF STEM ASSEMBLY"]
        T1[Thrust Washer]
        T2[Stem]
        T3[Stem Packing O-Rings]
        T4[Gland Flange]
        T5[Actuator Mounting Pad ISO 5211]
    end
    B3 <--> T2
    style BL fill:#FFF4E6
    style TL fill:#E6F7FF
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow ‚Äî When to Use a Ball Valve vs. Other Control Valves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Service Requirement]) --> Q1{On-Off or Throttling?}
    Q1 -->|On-Off Isolation| Q2{Working Pressure?}
    Q1 -->|Throttling Modulation| VPort[Use V-PORT Ball Valve]
    VPort --> Done1([Selected V-Port])
    Q2 -->|Less than 40 bar| FloatBall[Floating Ball Valve]
    Q2 -->|Greater than 40 bar| TrunnionBall[Trunnion Mounted Ball Valve]
    FloatBall --> SealMat{Seat Material?}
    SealMat -->|Corrosive| PTFE[PTFE RPTFE Seats]
    SealMat -->|High Temperature| Metal[Metal Seats Stellite]
    TrunnionBall --> SizeCheck{Bore Size?}
    SizeCheck -->|Greater than DN 150| SpringSeat[Spring Loaded Seats]
    PTFE --> End1([Selected Floating Ball PTFE])
    Metal --> End2([Selected Floating Ball Metal])
    SpringSeat --> End3([Selected Trunnion Ball])
    style Start fill:#FFE4B5
    style VPort fill:#90EE90
    style Done1 fill:#90EE90
    style End1 fill:#90EE90
    style End2 fill:#90EE90
    style End3 fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.4 Operating Sequence ‚Äî Bore Alignment vs. Stem Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[CLOSED 0 deg Bore perpendicular] -->|Rotate 45| P2[PARTIALLY OPEN Crescent flow]
    P2 -->|Rotate further 45| P3[FULLY OPEN 90 deg Bore parallel]
    P3 -->|Rotate back 45| P4[PARTIALLY CLOSED]
    P4 -->|Rotate 45| P1
    style P1 fill:#FFB6B6
    style P2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style P3 fill:#90EE90
    style P4 fill:#FFE4B5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (2 √ó 3 Marks)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023]**
*"With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a ball valve. State two industrial applications."*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Construction:** Body, ball with cylindrical bore, two seats (PTFE/metal), stem, packing, actuator/lever. [1 mark]
- **Working:** When stem rotates the ball by 90¬∞, the bore aligns with the pipe (OPEN). Reverse rotation ‚Üí bore perpendicular ‚Üí CLOSED. [1 mark]
- **Two applications:** (i) On/off isolation in oil & gas pipelines; (ii) Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valve in petrochemical plants. [1 mark]

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024]**
*"Compare the inherent flow characteristics of a standard ball valve and a V-port ball valve. Why is the V-port preferred for throttling?"*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Standard ball valve:** Quick-opening (most flow change in first 0¬∞‚Äì30¬∞ rotation). Non-linear ‚Üí unsuitable for throttling. [1.5 marks]
- **V-port ball valve:** Linear or equal-percentage characteristic due to V-notch shaping. [1 mark]
- **Reason for preference in throttling:** Provides proportional control across the full 90¬∞ rotation; reduces cavitation; enables fine modulation. [0.5 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Each question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7** distribution.

---

### ‚ùì Question A **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of ball valves based on ball mounting and seat construction. Compare floating ball and trunnion-mounted ball valves in terms of pressure rating, torque requirement, and typical applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ball valve is to be selected for water service ($G_f = 1.0$) with a design flow rate $Q = 30$ m¬≥/h and allowable pressure drop $\Delta P = 0.5$ bar across the valve. Compute (i) the required $C_v$ value, (ii) the recommended nominal bore (DN) from the standard table below, and (iii) the velocity through the chosen bore. State whether the selection is acceptable for throttling service.

| DN (mm) | $C_v$ |
|---|---|
| 25 | 6 |
| 40 | 16 |
| 50 | 28 |
| 80 | 70 |
| 100 | 110 |

---

#### ‚úÖ Solution to Question A

**(a) Model Answer Outline:**

*Types based on ball mounting:*
1. **Floating Ball Valve** ‚Äî Ball is not fixed by trunnions; it "floats" and seats itself against the downstream seat under line pressure.
2. **Trunnion-Mounted Ball Valve** ‚Äî Ball is rigidly held between two fixed trunnions; seats are spring-loaded and move to seal the ball.

*Types based on seat construction:*
1. **Soft Seat** ‚Äî PTFE / RPTFE / PEEK seats for bubble-tight shut-off (Class VI leakage).
2. **Metal Seat** ‚Äî Stellite or Inconel overlays for high-temperature service (Class IV leakage).
3. **Fire-Safe Seat** ‚Äî Combination of soft primary + metal secondary for fire protection (API 607).

| Parameter | Floating Ball | Trunnion-Mounted |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure Rating | ‚â§ **40 bar (PN 40)** | Up to **ANSI 2500 (~420 bar)** |
| Torque | Lower at small DN, increases sharply with DN | Predictable; spring pre-load adds torque |
| Application | General service, small bore (‚â§ DN 100) | Pipelines, oil & gas, large bore |

*(Note: Allocating 7 marks ‚Üí approx. 3 marks for classification, 2 marks for constructional detail, 2 marks for the comparison table.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert to standard units:**
- $Q = 30$ m¬≥/h $= \dfrac{30 \times 1000}{60}$ L/min $= 500$ L/min
- $Q$ in gpm $= 500 \times 0.2642 = 132.1$ gpm
- $\Delta P = 0.5$ bar $= 0.5 \times 14.504 = 7.252$ psi

**Step 2 ‚Äî Compute required $C_v$:**

$$
C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}} = 132.1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0}{7.252}}
$$

$$
C_v = 132.1 \cdot \sqrt{0.1379} = 132.1 \times 0.3713
$$

$$
\boxed{C_v \approx 49.05}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Select DN from table:**
The next standard $C_v$ value ‚â• 49.05 is **$C_v = 70$ at DN 80 mm**. [Selection ‚Üí DN 80]

**Step 4 ‚Äî Compute velocity in DN 80 bore:**
- Bore area: $A = \dfrac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ m¬≤
- Flow rate: $Q = \dfrac{30}{3600} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3}$ m¬≥/s

$$
v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.66 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Conclusion:**
Since $v = 1.66$ m/s $< 3$ m/s (the recommended throttling limit), the selection is **acceptable for throttling service**.

*(Note: 7 marks ‚Üí 3 marks for $C_v$, 2 marks for DN selection with justification, 2 marks for velocity check and final conclusion.)*

---

### ‚ùì Question B **[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a pneumatic rotary actuator driving a ball valve. Explain how the 4‚Äì20 mA control signal from a DCS is converted into a valve stem rotation of 0¬∞‚Äì90¬∞. Also state the role of a positioner in such a loop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for break-away torque of a floating ball valve. A floating ball valve of bore diameter $d = 100$ mm is used for water service at a differential pressure of $\Delta P = 10$ bar. The seat friction coefficient is $f = 0.12$. Compute (i) the break-away torque, and (ii) the minimum actuator torque if a safety factor of 1.5 is applied.

---

#### ‚úÖ Solution to Question B

**(a) Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Pneumatic Rotary Actuator (Rack & Pinion type):** Pneumatic pressure (typically 20‚Äì100 kPa or 3‚Äì15 psi) is applied to a diaphragm/piston that drives a **rack**, which in turn rotates a **pinion gear** attached to the valve stem. [2 marks]
2. **Signal Conversion Chain:**
   - DCS outputs **4‚Äì20 mA** ‚Üí I/P converter ‚Üí **3‚Äì15 psi pneumatic signal** ‚Üí positioner ‚Üí actuator ‚Üí stem rotates **0¬∞‚Äì90¬∞**. [3 marks]
3. **Role of Positioner:**
   - Compares **setpoint (SP)** vs. **valve position feedback** (via cam/potentiometer).
   - Modulates actuator air pressure to ensure stem position accurately tracks the controller output.
   - **Compensates for:** stem friction, packing friction, process fluid dynamic forces, and opposing spring action. [2 marks]

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚Äî Recall derived formula:**

$$
\tau_{br} = f \cdot \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d^3}{8}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Substitute values (SI units):**
- $f = 0.12$
- $\Delta P = 10 \text{ bar} = 10 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $d = 0.100$ m
- $d^3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m¬≥

**Step 3 ‚Äî Compute break-away torque:**

$$
\tau_{br} = 0.12 \times (1.0 \times 10^6) \times \dfrac{\pi \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})}{8}
$$

$$
\tau_{br} = 0.12 \times 10^6 \times \dfrac{3.1416 \times 10^{-3}}{8}
$$

$$
\tau_{br} = 0.12 \times 10^6 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{br} \approx 47.12 \text{ N¬∑m}}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Apply safety factor:**

$$
\tau_{act,min} = 1.5 \times \tau_{br} = 1.5 \times 47.12
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{act,min} \approx 70.68 \text{ N¬∑m}}
$$

*(Note: 7 marks ‚Üí 3 marks for derivation steps, 2 marks for numerical substitution and torque result, 2 marks for actuator sizing with safety factor.)*

---

## 5.3 ‚öÝÔ∏è KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Repeated Every Year):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors** ‚Äî KTU questions always mix **SI and US units**. Forgetting to convert L/min ‚Üí gpm or bar ‚Üí psi in $C_v$ problems leads to an answer off by an order of magnitude. **Always convert before substituting.**
> 2. **Forgetting the $G_f$ under the square root** ‚Äî The $C_v$ equation requires $G_f$ to be **inside the radical**. Many students wrongly write $C_v = Q \cdot G_f / \sqrt{\Delta P}$.
> 3. **Mis-classifying flow characteristic** ‚Äî Saying a ball valve has a "linear" characteristic is the most common error. It is **quick-opening**, not linear.
> 4. **Confusing floating vs. trunnion torque direction** ‚Äî In a floating ball, *higher pressure increases seat load and torque*. In a trunnion valve, *seat load is decoupled from line pressure* ‚Üí torque is nearly constant.
> 5. **Not specifying "fully open" when using $L_e/d$ values** ‚Äî Resistance coefficients change drastically with stem position.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ‚úÖ Ball valve = **quarter-turn rotary valve** with **hollow, perforated sphere** as closure member.
- ‚úÖ **OPEN** ‚Üî bore **parallel** to pipe; **CLOSED** ‚Üî bore **perpendicular** to pipe.
- ‚úÖ Two main structural types: **Floating Ball** (low pressure, small DN) and **Trunnion-Mounted** (high pressure, large DN).
- ‚úÖ Standard ball valve has **quick-opening** characteristic ‚Äî **not suitable for throttling**; use **V-port** for modulation.
- ‚úÖ Seats: **PTFE/RPTFE** for bubble-tight shut-off; **Stellite/metal** for high temperature.
- ‚úÖ Master formula: $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$ ‚Äî must convert to **gpm, psi** for $C_v$ calculation.
- ‚úÖ Break-away torque for floating ball: $\tau_{br} = f \cdot \Delta P \cdot \dfrac{\pi \, d^3}{8}$ ‚Üí multiply by **safety factor 1.5‚Äì2.0** for actuator sizing.
- ‚úÖ Throttling velocity limit: keep **port velocity < 3 m/s** to avoid seat erosion and cavitation.
- ‚úÖ Self-cleaning action: during rotation, seats scrape the ball surface ‚Äî advantageous in **slurry / dirty service**.
- ‚úÖ Actuator interface must follow **ISO 5211** mounting pad standard for interchangeability.
- ‚úÖ Fire-safe certification per **API 607 / API 6FA** is mandatory in hydrocarbon service.
- ‚úÖ Equivalent length: **$L_e/d \approx 3$** when fully open (resistance coefficient $K \approx 0.05$).
- ‚úÖ Multi-port (L-bore, T-bore) variants enable **flow diversion and mixing** without separate piping.
- ‚úÖ Cavitation in ball valves: occurs downstream when local pressure falls below **vapour pressure**; mitigated by **anti-cavitation trims** or **V-port design**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Butterfly Valve - Diaphragm Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Butterfly Valve - Diaphragm Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Butterfly Valve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A **Butterfly Valve** is a quarter-turn rotary motion control valve that uses a round disc as the flow-control element. The disc rotates about a diametrical axis (the valve stem) perpendicular to the flow direction. When the disc is parallel to the flow, the valve is fully open; when perpendicular, it is fully closed.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **dam gate** placed in the middle of a water pipe. Instead of a heavy wall, picture a thin, round plate (like a **CD/DVD**) mounted on a central axle. To control water, you simply rotate this disc:
- **Disc parallel to flow** ‚Üí Water rushes past freely (Valve OPEN).
- **Disc perpendicular to flow** ‚Üí Water is blocked (Valve CLOSED).
- **Disc at 45¬∞** ‚Üí Partially allowed (THROTTLING).

This is precisely how a butterfly valve works. Because it only needs a **90¬∞ rotation** (quarter-turn) to go from fully open to fully closed, it is incredibly fast, compact, and economical for large pipelines ‚Äî even those exceeding **DN 1200 (48 inches)** in diameter.

**Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Standard Sizes:** DN 50 to DN 3000 (2" to 120").
- **Pressure Ratings:** ANSI Class 150, 300 (typical industrial range up to **20 bar**).
- **Temperature Limit:** Up to **450¬∞C** with metal seats.
- **Flow Characteristic (Inherent):** Approximately **Modified Equal Percentage** between 20¬∞‚Äì70¬∞ of disc rotation.
- **Cv Range:** Very high Cv per dollar of cost ‚Äî most economical for large flow rates.

**Components of a Butterfly Valve:**
1. **Body** ‚Äì Wafer, lug, or flanged type, mounted between pipe flanges.
2. **Disc** ‚Äì The rotating closure element (materials: SS316, Hastelloy, PTFE-lined).
3. **Stem / Shaft** ‚Äì Transmits torque from the actuator to the disc.
4. **Seat** ‚Äì Resilient (EPDM, Viton, PTFE) or metal-to-metal.
5. **Actuator Mounting Pad** ‚Äì For pneumatic, electric, or manual actuators.

---

### 1.2 Diaphragm Valve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A **Diaphragm Valve** is a linear motion control valve in which a flexible, resilient **diaphragm** is pressed against a **weir** (or seat) in the valve body to start, stop, or throttle the flow. The process fluid is completely isolated from the valve bonnet and actuator mechanism.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the **rubber stopper in a syringe**. When you press the plunger, the rubber tip seals against the barrel ‚Äî preventing any fluid from leaking back. A diaphragm valve works on a similar principle:
- A soft, flexible **rubber or PTFE diaphragm** is pressed down by a compressor onto a raised ridge (the **weir**) inside the valve body.
- The weir creates a saddle-shape; the diaphragm squashes onto it like a hand pressing on a balloon ‚Äî sealing the flow.
- Because the process fluid **never touches the stem or actuator**, it is the safest valve for **corrosive, toxic, or sterile fluids**.

**Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Standard Sizes:** DN 15 to DN 400 (¬Ω" to 16").
- **Pressure Ratings:** Typically up to **16 bar** (lower than butterfly).
- **Temperature Limit:** Up to **200¬∞C** (limited by diaphragm material).
- **Flow Characteristic (Inherent):** Approximately **Quick-Opening** for weir type; **Linear** for straight-through (full-bore) type.
- **Body Styles:** **Weir type** (saddle-ridge) and **Straight-through (full-bore)** type.

**Components of a Diaphragm Valve:**
1. **Body** ‚Äì Cast iron, ductile iron, SS, or lined with rubber/PTFE/PVDF.
2. **Diaphragm** ‚Äì Flexible element (EPDM, Viton, PTFE, Natural Rubber) ‚Äî the only wetted moving part.
3. **Bonnet** ‚Äì Encloses the compressor and stem; isolates the process.
4. **Compressor (or Pusher)** ‚Äì A disc that transmits stem force to the diaphragm.
5. **Stem** ‚Äì Connects the actuator handwheel or pneumatic piston to the compressor.
6. **Weir** ‚Äì The raised ridge in the body where the diaphragm seals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always remember that the **diaphragm is a sacrificial/wearing component** and is the *only* part in contact with the process fluid on the moving side. This is a 2-mark favorite in Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle ‚Äî Butterfly Valve

The butterfly valve operates on the principle of **rotary motion flow interruption** using a centrally pivoted disc. Its operation can be broken down into three phases:

**Step 1 ‚Äî Disc Position & Flow Area:**
- At **0¬∞ rotation (disc edge-on to flow)** ‚Üí maximum flow area, Cv is at 100%.
- At **90¬∞ rotation (disc face perpendicular to flow)** ‚Üí zero flow, valve is closed.
- Between 0¬∞ and 90¬∞, the projected area of the disc presented to flow changes **non-linearly** (approximately as a function of $\sin(\theta)$).

**Step 2 ‚Äî Torque Generation (Hydrodynamic Torque):**
- As fluid flows past the disc, a **hydrodynamic torque** acts on the disc. This torque is critical for actuator sizing.
- The hydrodynamic torque $T_h$ depends on the disc diameter, pressure drop, fluid density, and the disc angle $\theta$.
- Near **70¬∞ opening**, the disc experiences a "dynamic torque reversal" ‚Äî the actuator must overcome this to move the disc to full closure.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Stem Sealing:**
- The stem passes through the body and is sealed using **O-rings, packing, or bellows seals** to prevent external leakage.
- Eccentric disc designs (high-performance butterfly valves) use a **double-offset** or **triple-offset** geometry to reduce seat wear and allow metal-to-metal sealing at high temperatures.

---

### 2.2 Operating Principle ‚Äî Diaphragm Valve

The diaphragm valve operates on the principle of **linear compression of a flexible membrane** against a stationary seat (weir).

**Step 1 ‚Äî Linear Stem Motion:**
- The stem moves **linearly downward** (valve closing) or **upward** (valve opening).
- This motion is transmitted to the **compressor**, which in turn pushes the flexible diaphragm.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Diaphragm Flexure:**
- The diaphragm is anchored at its perimeter between the body and bonnet.
- As the compressor descends, the diaphragm bends and presses onto the **weir** in a saddle shape.
- At full closure, the diaphragm fully conforms to the weir contour, providing **bubble-tight shutoff**.

**Step 3 ‚Äî Process Isolation:**
- Because the stem and actuator operate above the diaphragm, the **process fluid is hermetically sealed** from the external environment.
- This makes the diaphragm valve ideal for **pharmaceutical (sanitary), food & beverage, and chemical applications** where zero contamination is mandatory.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Valve Flow Coefficient** | $C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho / \rho_0}{\Delta P}}$ | Universal metric to size valves for given flow $Q$ and pressure drop $\Delta P$. |
| **Inherent Flow Characteristic (Butterfly)** | $C_v(\theta) \approx C_{v,\max} \cdot \sin^2(\theta)$ | Approximates modified equal-percentage behaviour. Used for controller tuning. |
| **Hydrodynamic Torque on Disc** | $T_h = K_t \cdot \Delta P \cdot D^3$ | Used to size the pneumatic/electric actuator. $K_t$ is the torque coefficient (function of $\theta$). |
| **Diaphragm Stress (Hoop Stress)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{2t}$ | Critical for diaphragm material selection. $r$ = effective radius, $t$ = thickness. |
| **Stem Force (Diaphragm Valve)** | $F_{stem} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_s^2}{4} \cdot P_{air} - F_{spring}$ | Used to calculate required actuator air pressure to overcome line pressure. |
| **Rangeability** | $R = \dfrac{C_{v,\max}}{C_{v,\min}}$ | Butterfly: ~30:1; Diaphragm (weir): ~15:1; Diaphragm (straight): ~50:1. |
| **Leakage Class (per FCI 70-2)** | Class I ‚Üí Class VI | Butterfly: Class IV‚ÄìVI; Diaphragm: Class VI (bubble-tight) ‚Äî important in KTU exams. |
| **Characteristic Slope at 63% Travel** | $n = \dfrac{d(C_v/C_{v,\max})}{d(l/L)}$ | $n \approx 1$ ‚Üí Linear; $n > 1$ ‚Üí Equal %; $n < 1$ ‚Üí Quick-opening. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Warning on Markdown Math:** Notice the use of `\vert` in formulas above instead of literal `\|` pipes. This prevents KTU markdown notes from breaking when piped into PDF converters.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Butterfly Valves** dominate the **water and wastewater industry**, **HVAC systems**, **marine pipelines**, and **oil & gas transmission lines** because they handle massive flow rates at low cost.
- **Diaphragm Valves** are irreplaceable in **pharmaceutical manufacturing (FDA-compliant)**, **biotech fermenters**, **semiconductor wet benches**, and **acid/alkali handling** where stem packing failure would be catastrophic.
- Both valves are key components in any **Distributed Control System (DCS)** loop and must be correctly characterized (linear/equal %) for proper PID tuning ‚Äî a frequent topic in KTU Module 4 process control questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Inherent Flow Characteristic ‚Äî Butterfly Valve

The inherent flow characteristic describes how the valve's $C_v$ changes with stem position in the absence of any process pressure drop variations (constant $\Delta P$).

**Starting Point ‚Äî Geometric Flow Area:**
For a butterfly valve with disc diameter $D$ and stem rotation angle $\theta$ (measured from the fully-open position), the **projected flow area** perpendicular to the flow is:

$$
A(\theta) = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Relating Area to Cv:**
Since $C_v$ is directly proportional to the effective flow area for a given valve geometry, we write:

$$
C_v(\theta) = K \cdot A(\theta) = K \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

Here, $K$ is a constant that depends on valve geometry, fluid properties, and the discharge coefficient.

**Step 2 ‚Äî Normalizing to Maximum Cv:**
The maximum $C_v$ occurs at $\theta = 0¬∞$ (fully open):

$$
C_{v,\max} = K \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Forming the Normalized Characteristic Curve:**
Dividing the expression in Step 1 by the maximum value:

$$
\frac{C_v(\theta)}{C_{v,\max}} = \cos(\theta)
$$

However, empirical test data (per ISA 75.11 and IEC 60534-8-3) shows that the actual butterfly valve characteristic is closer to a $\cos^2$ or modified equal-percentage curve. The refined empirical form is:

$$
\frac{C_v(\theta)}{C_{v,\max}} = \sin^2(\theta) \quad \text{for } 0¬∞ \leq \theta \leq 90¬∞
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Verification at Boundary Conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } \theta &= 0¬∞ \text{ (fully open)}: & \frac{C_v}{C_{v,\max}} &= \sin^2(0) = 0 \\
\text{At } \theta &= 90¬∞ \text{ (fully closed)}: & \frac{C_v}{C_{v,\max}} &= \sin^2(90¬∞) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note:** In the convention used by KTU textbooks, $\theta$ is measured from the *closed* position, so $\theta = 0¬∞$ means closed (Cv=0) and $\theta = 90¬∞$ means open (Cv=1). The two conventions are equivalent if you redefine the zero point. Always state your convention explicitly in board exams.

**Step 5 ‚Äî Final General Form:**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{C_v(\theta)}{C_{v,\max}} = \sin^2(\theta) \quad \text{or} \quad \sin^4(\theta) \text{ (more accurate)}}
$$

The $\sin^4$ form is used in high-performance butterfly valves with eccentric discs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Stem Force ‚Äî Diaphragm Valve

For sizing the pneumatic actuator of a diaphragm valve, we need to calculate the **minimum stem force** required to fully close the valve against line pressure.

**Step 1 ‚Äî Identify the Forces:**
Three forces act on the diaphragm-stem system during closure:
1. **Pneumatic force pushing stem down:** $F_{air}$
2. **Line pressure force pushing stem up:** $F_{process}$
3. **Spring force (return spring in actuator):** $F_{spring}$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Express Pneumatic Force:**
The actuator applies a pressure $P_{air}$ on a diaphragm area $A_d = \dfrac{\pi d_d^2}{4}$:

$$
F_{air} = P_{air} \cdot A_d = P_{air} \cdot \frac{\pi d_d^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Express Process Force (acting upward, opposing closure):**
The line fluid at pressure $P_{line}$ acts on the **effective diaphragm sealing area** $A_s$ (not the full diaphragm area ‚Äî because the diaphragm is anchored at its rim):

$$
F_{process} = P_{line} \cdot A_s = P_{line} \cdot \frac{\pi d_s^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Force Balance at Closure (Stem Force Equation):**
For successful closure, the downward force must exceed the upward forces:

$$
F_{air} \geq F_{process} + F_{spring}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{air} \cdot \frac{\pi d_d^2}{4} \geq P_{line} \cdot \frac{\pi d_s^2}{4} + F_{spring}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Solve for Minimum Air Pressure:**

$$
\boxed{P_{air,\min} = \dfrac{4 F_{spring}}{\pi d_d^2} + P_{line} \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_s}{d_d}\right)^2}
$$

This equation is essential for **actuator sizing** in KTU practical/lab viva questions.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | **Butterfly Valve** | **Diaphragm Valve** |
|---|---|---|
| **Motion Type** | Quarter-turn rotary (90¬∞) | Linear (push-down / lift-up) |
| **Closure Element** | Rigid disc on stem | Flexible elastomer diaphragm |
| **Stem-Fluid Isolation** | O-rings / packing (not hermetic) | Hermetic ‚Äî stem isolated from process |
| **Typical Size Range** | DN 50 ‚Äì DN 3000 | DN 15 ‚Äì DN 400 |
| **Pressure Rating** | Up to 20 bar (ANSI 150) | Up to 16 bar |
| **Temperature Limit** | Up to 450¬∞C (metal seat) | Up to 200¬∞C (elastomer limit) |
| **Inherent Characteristic** | Modified equal percentage | Quick-opening (weir) / Linear (straight) |
| **Rangeability** | ~30:1 | ~15:1 (weir) / 50:1 (straight) |
| **Leakage Class (FCI 70-2)** | Class IV ‚Äì VI | Class VI (bubble-tight) |
| **Cv per Cost** | Very high (best for large lines) | Moderate |
| **Best Application** | Water, gas, slurries, large mains | Corrosive, sterile, pharmaceutical fluids |
| **Maintenance** | Replace seat periodically | Replace diaphragm (sacrificial part) |
| **Cost Index (relative)** | 1√ó (baseline) | 1.3√ó ‚Äì 1.8√ó |
| **Cavitation Resistance** | Low (poor at low flow) | High (diaphragm absorbs energy) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Butterfly Valve ‚Äî Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Process Inlet Pipe Flange] --> B1[Valve Body Wafer Type]
    B1 --> C1[Upper Stem Seal O-Ring Packing]
    C1 --> D1[Stem Shaft Vertical Axis]
    D1 --> E1[Rotating Disc 90 deg Quarter Turn]
    E1 --> F1[Resilient Seat EPDM PTFE Viton]
    F1 --> G1[Process Outlet Pipe Flange]
    D1 --> H1[Actuator Mounting Pad ISO 5211]
    H1 --> I1[Pneumatic Piston or Electric Motor]
    I1 --> J1[Positioner 4-20 mA Feedback]
    J1 --> K1[DCS Controller PID Loop]
```

**Component Interaction Map:**
1. The DCS sends a 4‚Äì20 mA signal to the positioner.
2. The positioner commands the pneumatic/electric actuator.
3. The actuator rotates the stem by a fraction of a quarter-turn.
4. The disc rotates to a corresponding angle, modulating flow.
5. The positioner reads back the disc angle to confirm valve position.

---

### 4.2 Diaphragm Valve ‚Äî Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A2[Process Inlet] --> B2[Valve Body with Weir Saddle]
    B2 --> C2[Flexible Diaphragm Elastomer]
    C2 --> D2[Compressor Disc Linear Pusher]
    D2 --> E2[Stem Vertical Linear Motion]
    E2 --> F2[Bonnet Enclosure Sealed]
    F2 --> G2[Handwheel or Pneumatic Piston]
    G2 --> H2[Spring Return Mechanism]
    H2 --> I2[Actuator Positioner 4-20 mA]
    I2 --> J2[DCS Control Room]
    C2 -.->|Presses onto weir| K2[Bubble Tight Shutoff Class VI]
    B2 --> L2[Process Outlet]
```

**Component Interaction Map:**
1. The DCS issues a control signal (3‚Äì15 psi pneumatic or 4‚Äì20 mA electric).
2. The actuator stem moves linearly downward.
3. The compressor pushes the flexible diaphragm onto the weir.
4. The diaphragm seals against the weir, achieving bubble-tight shutoff.
5. On signal reversal, the spring returns the stem, lifting the diaphragm.

---

### 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree for Valve Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Control Valve] --> Q1{Is the fluid corrosive or sterile?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Pressure below 16 bar and temp below 200C?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| DV[Choose Diaphragm Valve]
    Q2 -->|No| BV2[Choose Lined Butterfly Valve]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Pipe size above DN 300?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| BV1[Choose Butterfly Valve]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need bubble tight shutoff?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| DV2[Choose Diaphragm Valve]
    Q4 -->|No| GLV[Choose Globe Valve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Relevance:** This decision tree is a frequently-asked 14-mark question under the topic "Selection of control valves based on application."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A ‚Äî 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì May 2024, Model QP]**
*List any three advantages of a butterfly valve over a globe valve for large-diameter pipelines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Compact and lightweight:** A butterfly valve of DN 600 weighs approximately 80‚Äì100 kg, whereas an equivalent globe valve would weigh over 800 kg ‚Äî a 10√ó saving.
2. **Lower cost per Cv:** Butterfly valves offer the highest flow capacity per unit cost, making them the most economical choice for DN 300 and above.
3. **Faster actuation:** Only 90¬∞ rotation is needed for full closure, allowing quick on-off service with smaller, cheaper actuators.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Why is the diaphragm valve preferred for handling corrosive and sterile fluids?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The **diaphragm** completely isolates the process fluid from the valve stem, bonnet, and packing ‚Äî there is **no path for leakage to the atmosphere**.
2. The **diaphragm is the only wetted moving part**, and it can be manufactured from chemically inert materials (PTFE, Viton, EPDM).
3. It provides **bubble-tight shutoff (FCI 70-2 Class VI)**, essential for pharmaceutical and biotech applications where contamination is unacceptable.

---

### PART B ‚Äî 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students frequently write "butterfly valve is used for high pressure" ‚Äî this is **FALSE**. Butterfly valves are limited to ~20 bar. Always state pressure rating explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *inherent* characteristic (at constant ŒîP) with *installed* characteristic (at varying ŒîP). The KTU answer key differentiates these.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Drawing a diaphragm valve with a "rising stem like a globe valve" ‚Äî the diaphragm is *flexed*, not rigidly pushed. State the flexure explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to specify the **leakage class** when comparing valves. Always quote FCI 70-2 class.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ‚Äî Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** With a neat block-level sketch, explain the construction and working of a **butterfly valve**. Discuss its inherent flow characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A butterfly valve of DN 300 has a maximum $C_v$ of 2400. Calculate the $C_v$ at disc rotations of 30¬∞, 45¬∞, and 60¬∞ using the empirical relation $C_v(\theta)/C_{v,\max} = \sin^2(\theta)$. Comment on the trend. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of Butterfly Valve (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
A butterfly valve consists of the following main parts:
1. **Body** ‚Äî Wafer, lug, or flanged type, sandwiched between pipe flanges.
2. **Disc** ‚Äî A circular plate (typically 316 SS, Hastelloy, or PTFE-lined) that rotates on a central axis.
3. **Stem / Shaft** ‚Äî Connects the disc to the actuator through the body wall, sealed by O-rings.
4. **Seat** ‚Äî A resilient ring (EPDM, Viton, PTFE) or metal seat that provides the sealing surface.
5. **Actuator mounting pad** ‚Äî Standardized as per ISO 5211 for pneumatic/electric actuators.

**Working:**
- The valve operates on **quarter-turn rotary motion**. At **0¬∞**, the disc is parallel to the flow and the valve is fully open (maximum $C_v$).
- At **90¬∞**, the disc is perpendicular to the flow and the valve is fully closed ($C_v = 0$).
- The disc is rotated by an actuator (manual hand lever, pneumatic piston, or electric motor) through a stem that passes through the valve body.

**Inherent Flow Characteristic:**
The inherent characteristic is approximately a **modified equal percentage** curve. The empirical relation is:

$$
\frac{C_v(\theta)}{C_{v,\max}} = \sin^2(\theta)
$$

For small openings (Œ∏ < 20¬∞), the change in $C_v$ is small ‚Äî equal-percentage behavior. For larger openings (Œ∏ > 30¬∞), the curve becomes more linear. This makes the butterfly valve suitable for both **throttling** and **on-off** service.

**Block-level diagram (as drawn above in Section 4.1)** would be presented here for full marks.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

Given:
- $C_{v,\max} = 2400$
- Empirical relation: $C_v(\theta) = C_{v,\max} \cdot \sin^2(\theta)$

**At Œ∏ = 30¬∞:**

$$
C_v(30¬∞) = 2400 \cdot \sin^2(30¬∞) = 2400 \cdot (0.5)^2 = 2400 \cdot 0.25 = 600
$$

**At Œ∏ = 45¬∞:**

$$
C_v(45¬∞) = 2400 \cdot \sin^2(45¬∞) = 2400 \cdot (0.7071)^2 = 2400 \cdot 0.5 = 1200
$$

**At Œ∏ = 60¬∞:**

$$
C_v(60¬∞) = 2400 \cdot \sin^2(60¬∞) = 2400 \cdot (0.8660)^2 = 2400 \cdot 0.75 = 1800
$$

**Trend Analysis:**

| Disc Angle Œ∏ | $C_v$ | % of $C_{v,\max}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0¬∞ | 0 | 0% |
| 30¬∞ | 600 | 25% |
| 45¬∞ | 1200 | 50% |
| 60¬∞ | 1800 | 75% |
| 90¬∞ | 2400 | 100% |

**Comment:** The $C_v$ increases non-linearly with disc rotation. The first 30¬∞ of rotation only yields 25% of the maximum flow, but the second 30¬∞ (from 30¬∞ to 60¬∞) yields 50% ‚Äî confirming the **modified equal-percentage** behavior. This is why butterfly valves are **not recommended for low-flow throttling**: the first 20% of travel is too "dead" for fine control.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚Äî Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **diaphragm valve** with a block-level diagram. Why is it called a "sacrificial-element" valve? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diaphragm valve with a stem diameter of 25 mm and effective sealing diameter of 60 mm is operated by a pneumatic actuator with a diaphragm diameter of 200 mm. The actuator spring exerts a force of 600 N. If the line pressure is 8 bar, calculate the **minimum air pressure** required to fully close the valve. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diaphragm Valve Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
A diaphragm valve consists of:
1. **Body** ‚Äî Cast iron, ductile iron, SS, or rubber/PTFE-lined; contains the weir.
2. **Weir** ‚Äî A raised saddle-shaped ridge in the flow path that acts as the seat.
3. **Diaphragm** ‚Äî A flexible, resilient membrane (EPDM, PTFE, Viton) clamped between the body and bonnet.
4. **Compressor** ‚Äî A rigid disc that pushes the diaphragm onto the weir.
5. **Stem** ‚Äî Connects the compressor to the actuator, sealed within the bonnet.
6. **Bonnet** ‚Äî Sealed enclosure above the diaphragm; keeps external environment isolated.
7. **Actuator** ‚Äî Pneumatic piston or manual handwheel.

**Working:**
- When the stem moves **downward**, the compressor pushes the diaphragm against the weir. The diaphragm flexes and seals against the weir contour ‚Äî providing **bubble-tight shutoff (Class VI)**.
- When the stem moves **upward**, the diaphragm lifts off the weir, allowing flow.
- The process fluid is **completely isolated** from the stem and atmosphere by the diaphragm.

**Why "Sacrificial-Element" Valve:**
The diaphragm is the **only wetted moving part** and is the **primary sealing element**. Over time, it fatigues from repeated flexing, chemical attack, and abrasion. When it eventually fails (leaks or tears), it must be **replaced periodically** ‚Äî much like a sacrificial anode in corrosion protection. The rest of the valve (body, bonnet, stem) usually has a service life of 15‚Äì20 years, but the diaphragm is replaced every 1‚Äì3 years depending on the application.

**Block-level diagram (as in Section 4.2)** would be presented for full marks.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation ‚Äî Minimum Air Pressure (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Stem diameter: $d_s = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Effective sealing diameter: $d_{eff} = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- Actuator diaphragm diameter: $d_d = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.200 \text{ m}$
- Spring force: $F_{spring} = 600 \text{ N}$
- Line pressure: $P_{line} = 8 \text{ bar} = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Calculate the Effective Sealing Area:**

$$
A_{eff} = \frac{\pi d_{eff}^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.060)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Calculate the Process Force (acting upward):**

$$
F_{process} = P_{line} \cdot A_{eff} = (8 \times 10^5) \cdot (2.827 \times 10^{-3}) = 2261.9 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Calculate the Actuator Diaphragm Area:**

$$
A_d = \frac{\pi d_d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.200)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2
$$
