# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Solenoid Force from Maxwell Stress Tensor

**Starting point ‚Äî magnetic energy stored in air gap:**
The energy stored in an air gap of volume \(V_{g} = A \cdot g\) at flux density \(B\) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_m &= \frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}} \cdot V_{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Express the force as the negative gradient of magnetic co-energy with respect to gap length:**
At constant MMF (since current is held during analysis), the force acts to reduce the reluctance ‚Äî i.e., it pulls the plunger toward the pole face. By the principle of virtual work:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= +\frac{\partial W_m}{\partial g}\bigg|_{N I = \text{const}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Differentiate the energy expression with respect to \(g\):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial W_m}{\partial g} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial g} \left( \frac{B^{2} A g}{2 \mu_{0}} \right) = \frac{B^{2} A}{2 \mu_{0}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî State the result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{B^{2} A}{2 \mu_{0}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute \(B = \mu_0 N I / g\) from the simplified reluctance model** \(\mathcal{R} = g / (\mu_0 A)\) so that \(\Phi = NI / \mathcal{R}\) and \(B = \Phi / A\):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{\mu_{0} \cdot N \cdot I}{g} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad B^{2} = \frac{\mu_{0}^{2} N^{2} I^{2}}{g^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî Substitute back into the force equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{1}{2 \mu_{0}} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0}^{2} N^{2} I^{2}}{g^{2}} \cdot A = \frac{\mu_{0} A N^{2} I^{2}}{2 g^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result (Boxed formula):**

$$
\boxed{\,F \;=\; \dfrac{\mu_{0} \cdot A \cdot N^{2} \cdot I^{2}}{2 \cdot g^{2}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Force grows as \(1/g^{2}\). At end-of-stroke (\(g \to 0\)), force becomes very large ‚Äî this is why the stroke is mechanically limited and why solenoid stroke is typically only **2 to 10 mm** for small valves.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sound Pressure Level From Acoustic Intensity

**Starting point ‚Äî sound intensity (acoustic power per unit area):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{p^{2}}{\rho c}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- \(I\) = acoustic intensity (W/m¬≤)
- \(p\) = RMS sound pressure (Pa)
- \(\rho c\) = characteristic impedance of air ‚âà 410 Pa¬∑s/m (at 20 ¬∞C)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Take the ratio of intensity to reference intensity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I}{I_0} &= \frac{p^{2} / (\rho c)}{p_0^{2} / (\rho c)} = \frac{p^{2}}{p_0^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(The characteristic impedance cancels because it is the same at measurement and reference.)

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the definition of sound power level:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_w &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p^{2}}{p_0^{2}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result (Boxed formula):**

$$
\boxed{\,L_p \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Free-Field Hemispherical Radiation

**Starting point ‚Äî sound power is uniformly distributed over the surface of a hemisphere of radius \(r\):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= I \cdot S = I \cdot (2 \pi r^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

(Surface area of a hemisphere = \(2 \pi r^{2}\), not \(4 \pi r^{2}\).)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Write the sound pressure level at the measurement point on the hemisphere surface:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{2 \pi r^{2} I_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Separate the logarithm and rearrange to isolate \(L_w\):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \pi r^{2} I_0}{W_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since \(W_0 / I_0 = 1 \ m^{2}\) by definition (reference power and intensity ratio = 1 m¬≤), the second term simplifies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= L_w - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2 \pi r^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result (Boxed formula):**

$$
\boxed{\,L_w \;=\; L_p \;+\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2 \pi r^{2}\right)\,}
$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For a measured \(L_p = 90\ dB\) at \(r = 1\ m\):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_w &= 90 + 10 \log_{10}(2 \pi \cdot 1) \\
&= 90 + 10 \cdot \log_{10}(6.2832) \\
&= 90 + 10 \cdot 0.798 \\
&= 90 + 7.98 \\
&\approx 97.98\ dB(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

Re-checking at \(r = 10\ m\) ‚Äî should drop by 6 dB (√ó10 distance ‚Üí 6 dB in free field):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p(10) &= L_w - 10 \log_{10}(2 \pi \cdot 100) = 97.98 - 10 \cdot 2.798 \\
&\approx 97.98 - 27.98 = 70.00\ dB(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

That's a drop of **20 dB** when distance goes from 1 m to 10 m ‚Äî consistent with the ‚àí6 dB per doubling (1‚Üí2‚Üí4‚Üí8‚Üí10 ‚âà four doublings ‚âà 24 dB expected, achieved ‚âà 20 dB).

## 3.4 Derivation: Attenuation From Doubling Identical Sound Sources

If two identical machines each produce \(L_p = X\) dB at a point, the **sound pressures add linearly** (incoherent sources) and **sound intensities add linearly** (coherent sources). For independent (incoherent) industrial noise sources, pressures in quadrature add:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{\text{total}}^{2} &= p_{1}^{2} + p_{2}^{2} = 2 p^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 ‚Äî Convert each \(L_p\) back to pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{i} &= p_0 \cdot 10^{L_{p}/20}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Apply the definition to combined source:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{p,\text{total}} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}\, p}{p_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Evaluate \(20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{2})\):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{2}) &= 20 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \log_{10}(2) = 10 \cdot 0.30103 = 3.0103\ dB
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result (Boxed formula):**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta L_p \;=\; +3.01\ dB\ \text{when two identical incoherent sources are added}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** Doubling the number of identical machines increases noise by **+3 dB**. Increasing the number **tenfold** increases noise by **+10 dB**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Noise Reduction Method

| Noise Type | Service | Symptoms | First Mitigation | Final Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Vibration | Liquid (high ŒîP) | Rumbling, pipe shaking | Anti-cavitation trim | Pipe supports + thicker walls |
| Mechanical Vibration | Liquid (ŒîP > 2.5 √ó vapor P) | Cavitation damage, pitting | Multi-stage anti-cavitation cage | Series installation |
| Hydrodynamic | Liquid | Hissing 1‚Äì10 kHz | Low-noise trim | Pipe lagging |
| Aerodynamic | Gas/vapor (subsonic) | Hissing 1‚Äì20 kHz | Quiet trim | Diffuser + lagging |
| Aerodynamic | Gas/vapor (sonic) | Roaring 5‚Äì50 kHz + tones | Multi-stage low-noise trim | In-line silencer + lagging |
| Tonals (shock cell) | High ŒîP gas | High-pitched tone | Increase downstream piping | Replace with low-\(F_L\) trim |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 marks]`
**Define a solenoid and briefly explain its operating principle in an industrial process valve.**

**Model Answer:**
A solenoid is an electromechanical device that converts electrical energy into linear mechanical motion via magnetic flux linkage. In a process valve, a current-carrying coil generates a magnetomotive force \( \mathcal{F} = N \cdot I \) that establishes magnetic flux across an air gap, producing an axial force \(F = \mu_0 A N^{2} I^{2} / (2 g^{2})\) on a ferromagnetic plunger. The plunger seats against (or unseats from) a valve orifice, thereby opening or closing the flow path. When de-energised, a return spring restores the plunger. It acts as a pilot or direct actuator for ON‚ÄìOFF flow control.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 3 marks]`
**Distinguish between mechanical vibration noise and aerodynamic noise in a control valve. Mention one method to reduce each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Mechanical vibration noise** arises from trim and pipe wall vibration driven by cavitation, flashing, or vortex shedding at low frequencies (30 Hz ‚Äì 1 kHz). **Reduction:** use anti-cavitation cage trim and stiffen downstream pipe supports.
- **Aerodynamic noise** arises from turbulent jet mixing, shock cells, and vortex shedding in compressible gas/vapor flow at high frequencies (1 kHz ‚Äì 50 kHz). **Reduction:** use multi-stage low-noise (quiet) trim that keeps flow subsonic at each stage.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each, Module 4 Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the construction and operating principle of a pilot-operated solenoid valve used for ON‚ÄìOFF control of process flow. Clearly distinguish between AC and DC solenoid construction.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the relationship between sound pressure level (\(L_p\)) and sound power level (\(L_w\)) for free-field hemispherical radiation, and hence compute the \(L_w\) of a control valve measured to be 95 dB(A) at a distance of 3 m.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a)

**Construction of pilot-operated solenoid valve:**
A pilot-operated solenoid valve consists of:
1. **Solenoid coil** wound on a bobbin with terminals connected to controller output.
2. **Plunger** (moveable armature) guided axially inside the coil.
3. **Pilot orifice** ‚Äî a small hole that connects the upstream pressure to the top of the main diaphragm/piston chamber.
4. **Main diaphragm or piston** ‚Äî large area membrane that the fluid pressure acts on to open the main seat.
5. **Main valve seat** ‚Äî the actual flow control surface; sized for the required \(C_v\).
6. **Manual override** (optional) ‚Äî allows hand operation if coil fails.
7. **Return spring** ‚Äî sets the fail-safe position (spring-closed or spring-open).
8. **Body** ‚Äî houses the inlet, outlet, and pilot paths.

**Operating principle (block diagram traced through Section 4.5):**
When the controller energises the coil ‚Üí MMF is produced ‚Üí plunger lifts ‚Üí pilot orifice opens. Process pressure now reaches the top of the main diaphragm. Since the diaphragm area is much larger than the pilot seat area, the resulting force overcomes the spring force and the main valve opens. When de-energised, the plunger returns, the pilot closes, pressure equalises across the diaphragm, and the spring recloses the main valve.

**AC vs DC construction difference:**

| Feature | AC | DC |
|---|---|---|
| Shading ring on pole face | **Required** (to eliminate 100 Hz hum) | Not required |
| Stop tube (non-magnetic spacer) | Not required | **Required** (to prevent residual magnetism sticking) |
| Coil thermal design | Must handle inrush = 5‚Äì10√ó holding | Steady-state current only |
| Typical supply | 230 V AC | 24 V DC (or 110/220 V DC) |

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b)

**Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.3 above, for free-field hemispherical radiation over surface \(S = 2 \pi r^{2}\):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= L_w - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2 \pi r^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_w &= L_p + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2 \pi r^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical substitution** with \(L_p = 95\ dB(A)\) and \(r = 3\ m\):

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \pi r^{2} &= 2 \pi \cdot (3)^{2} = 2 \pi \cdot 9 = 18 \pi
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(18 \pi) &= \log_{10}(56.549) \approx 1.7526
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_w &= 95 + 10 \cdot 1.7526 = 95 + 17.53 \approx 112.5\ dB(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** \(L_w \approx 112.5\ dB(A)\).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** List and explain the three principal sources of noise in a control valve. With a neat diagram, describe the IEC 60534-8-3 prediction chain from fluid pressure drop to in-plant sound pressure level at the operator's location.

**(b) [7 marks]** A control valve handles natural gas with inlet pressure \(P_1 = 80\ bar(a)\) and outlet pressure \(P_2 = 30\ bar(a)\). The flow coefficient is \(C_v = 120\), pressure recovery factor \(F_L = 0.9\), and mass flow rate is \(m = 25\ kg/s\). Using the simplified IEC 60534-8-3 relation and the constant \(C_1 = 62\ dB(A)\) for the trim, estimate the sound power level. Comment on whether OSHA action is required at 1 m distance.

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a)

**Three principal sources of control valve noise:**

1. **Mechanical vibration** ‚Äî caused by cavitation, flashing, and trim resonance; produces low-frequency tonal noise (30 Hz ‚Äì 1 kHz) transmitted through pipe walls. Mitigation: anti-cavitation trim, stiffer pipes, supports.

2. **Hydrodynamic noise** ‚Äî in liquid service, turbulent jet mixing and cavitation bubble collapse radiate broadband noise (500 Hz ‚Äì 10 kHz). Mitigation: low-noise trim, pipe lagging.

3. **Aerodynamic noise** ‚Äî in gas/vapor service, subsonic turbulent mixing and supersonic shock cells (when \(P_{2}/P_{1} < x_{T}\)) produce broadband + tonal noise at high frequencies (1 kHz ‚Äì 50 kHz). This is usually the loudest class and the focus of IEC 60534-8-3.

**IEC 60534-8-3 prediction chain (Block diagram traced through Section 4.3):**
- **Step 1:** Compute mass flow rate \(m\) and inlet/outlet pressures \(P_{1}, P_{2}\).
- **Step 2:** Check choked-flow condition \(P_{2}/P_{1} \leq x_{T}\); if yes, use \(P_{2} = x_{T} \cdot P_{1}\).
- **Step 3:** Apply the empirical relation to compute \(L_{w,A}\) (in-band A-weighted sound power level radiated by valve) using trim constants, \(F_{L}\), \(C_v\), and gas properties.
- **Step 4:** Convert to sound pressure at operator location using free-field hemispherical radiation: \(L_p = L_w - 10 \log_{10}(2 \pi r^{2})\).
- **Step 5:** Compare against OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95 limits and apply noise control hierarchy (source ‚Üí path ‚Üí receiver).

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b)

**Step 1 ‚Äî Verify choked flow:**
Terminal pressure ratio for natural gas (‚âà methane, similar to air):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_T &\approx 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} &= \frac{30}{80} = 0.375
\end{aligned}
$$

Since \(0.375 < 0.72\) ‚Üí **choked flow**. Use effective outlet pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2,\text{eff}} &= x_{T} \cdot P_{1} = 0.72 \cdot 80 = 57.6\ bar(a)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ‚Äî Pressure drop across valve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= P_{1} - P_{2,\text{eff}} = 80 - 57.6 = 22.4\ bar
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ‚Äî Apply simplified IEC 60534-8-3 relation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{w,A} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{m \cdot \Delta H \cdot P_{2,\text{eff}}^{2}}{F_{L}^{2} \cdot P_{1} \cdot C_{v}^{2}} \right) + C_{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute values (\(m = 25\), \(\Delta H = 22.4\), \(P_{2,\text{eff}} = 57.6\), \(F_L = 0.9\), \(P_1 = 80\), \(C_v = 120\)):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 25 \cdot 22.4 \cdot (57.6)^{2} \\
&= 25 \cdot 22.4 \cdot 3317.76 \\
&= 25 \cdot 74325.82 \\
&= 1{,}858{,}145.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= (0.9)^{2} \cdot 80 \cdot (120)^{2} \\
&= 0.81 \cdot 80 \cdot 14400 \\
&= 0.81 \cdot 1{,}152{,}000 \\
&= 933{,}120
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{Num}}{\text{Den}} &= \frac{1{,}858{,}145.5}{933{,}120} \approx 1.9915
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 \log_{10}(1.9915) &= 10 \cdot 0.2993 \approx 2.993
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{w,A} &= 2.993 + 62 = 64.99 \approx 65\ dB(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ‚Äî Convert to sound pressure at \(r = 1\ m\):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p(1\ m) &= L_w - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2 \pi \cdot 1^{2}\right) \\
&= 65 - 10 \log_{10}(6.2832) \\
&= 65 - 10 \cdot 0.798 \\
&= 65 - 7.98 \\
&\approx 57.0\ dB(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ‚Äî OSHA compliance comment:**
OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95 sets the **8-hour permissible exposure limit at 90 dB(A)** and the **hearing conservation trigger at 85 dB(A)**.

The estimated \(L_p \approx 57\ dB(A)\) at 1 m is **below both thresholds**, so no hearing protection or noise reduction is required at this distance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚Äî Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply choked-flow correction** when \(P_2/P_1 < x_T\). Always check first.
> 2. **Mixing up units** in the IEC equation ‚Äî \(P_1, P_2, \Delta H\) must be in **consistent pressure units (bar or kPa)**.
> 3. **Using \(P_2\) in dB equations** ‚Äî the term inside log is dimensionless ratio, not pressure.
> 4. **Forgetting the constant \(C_1\)** ‚Äî without it the equation gives physically impossible levels.
> 5. **Stating final answer without units** ‚Äî always write dB(A).
> 6. **Confusing sound pressure with sound power** ‚Äî \(L_p\) depends on distance; \(L_w\) does not.
> 7. **In solenoid questions, missing the shading ring / stop tube distinction** ‚Äî this is the single most-asked AC/DC differentiation point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚Äî Solenoids and Control Valve Noise:**

- A **solenoid** converts electrical energy ‚Üí magnetic force ‚Üí linear motion via MMF \(= N \cdot I\) and air-gap flux.
- Solenoid force: \(F = \mu_{0} A N^{2} I^{2} / (2 g^{2})\) ‚Äî quadratic in \(I\) and \(N\); inversely quadratic in gap.
- **AC solenoids** need **shading rings** (eliminate 100 Hz hum); **DC solenoids** need a **stop tube** (eliminate residual magnetism).
- **Pilot-operated solenoid valves** use the solenoid to vent/pressurise a large diaphragm for high \(C_v\) service.
- **Control valve noise** has three sources: **mechanical vibration, hydrodynamic noise, aerodynamic noise**.
- **Mechanical vibration noise** = 30 Hz ‚Äì 1 kHz ‚Üí causes pipe wall fatigue; fix with stiff supports + anti-cavitation trim.
- **Aerodynamic noise** = 1 kHz ‚Äì 50 kHz ‚Üí governed by IEC 60534-8-3 / ISA S75.01; worst case is choked flow.
- **Sound pressure level**: \(L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_{0})\), with \(p_{0} = 20\ \mu Pa\).
- **Sound power level**: \(L_w = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_{0})\), with \(W_{0} = 10^{-12}\ W\).
- **Free-field hemispherical radiation**: \(L_p = L_w - 10 \log_{10}(2 \pi r^{2})\).
- **Doubling distance** ‚Üí ‚àí6 dB attenuation; **doubling identical sources** ‚Üí +3 dB.
- **Terminal pressure ratio** for air and most gases \(x_{T} \approx 0.72\); below this, flow is choked and noise is maximum.
- **Noise reduction hierarchy**: source (quiet trim, low-\(F_L\), reduce ŒîP) ‚Üí path (silencer, lagging, diffuser) ‚Üí receiver (PPE, distance, barriers).
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95**: PEL = **90 dB(A)** for 8-hr TWA; hearing conservation from **85 dB(A)**.
- **IEC 60534-8-3 prediction** chain: pressures ‚Üí \(L_w\) (with trim constants) ‚Üí \(L_p\) at distance ‚Üí compliance check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrodynamic Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT503 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrodynamic Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrodynamic Noise** is defined as the unwanted, random pressure fluctuations and vibrations generated *within a flowing fluid medium* due to the inherent dynamics of fluid motion, including turbulence, cavitation, vortex shedding, flashing, and phase-change phenomena. Unlike acoustic noise (which propagates primarily through solid pipe walls and structural media), hydrodynamic noise propagates through the **bulk fluid itself**, traveling at or near the local speed of sound in the medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Hydrodynamic noise refers to process-side disturbances that corrupt the signal of flow sensors (orifice plates, Venturi tubes, ultrasonic, Coriolis, vortex shedding meters) by introducing random pressure fluctuations within the flowing fluid.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ‚Äî The Garden Hose & Nozzle:** Imagine holding a garden hose with a partially closed nozzle. As water rushes out, you can *hear* a hissing/whooshing sound ‚Äî that audible component is acoustic noise (radiating from pipe walls). However, if you could feel the *inside* of the water jet, you would detect tiny chaotic pulses, micro-bubbles bursting, and irregular pressure kicks ‚Äî this *internal* turbulence is hydrodynamic noise. Place your thumb at the exit and partially block it: the formation and collapse of vapor cavities (cavitation) creates sharp cracking sounds ‚Äî that is cavitation-induced hydrodynamic noise. Industrial piping behaves identically, only at vastly higher pressures and flow rates.

## 1.3 Classification of Hydrodynamic Noise Sources

| Category | Primary Mechanism | Typical Frequency |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Turbulence Noise** | Random velocity/pressure fluctuations in turbulent flow | 0 ‚Äì 2 kHz |
| **Cavitation Noise** | Formation/collapse of vapor bubbles | 20 kHz ‚Äì 1 MHz |
| **Vortex-Shedding Noise** | Periodic wake oscillations behind bluff bodies | 10 ‚Äì 500 Hz |
| **Flashing Noise** | Sudden vaporization at pressure drops | 1 ‚Äì 10 kHz |
| **Valve Noise (Hydrodynamic)** | High-pressure drop across throttling elements | 500 Hz ‚Äì 20 kHz |
| **Pump/Compressor-Induced** | Impeller blade-pass frequency harmonics | 50 ‚Äì 500 Hz |

## 1.4 Distinguishing Hydrodynamic Noise from Acoustic Noise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Hydrodynamic Noise:** Internal to the fluid, sensed by pressure transducers immersed in flow, decays with distance from source slowly, frequency content depends on flow regime.
> - **Acoustic Noise:** External to the fluid, sensed by microphones/vibration transducers on pipe walls, decays at ~6 dB per distance doubling in free field.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Hydrodynamic noise is a manifestation of **unsteady, incompressible-to-compressible fluid dynamics**. Its analysis rests on three governing pillars:

1. **Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation):**
   $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

2. **Navier‚ÄìStokes Momentum Equation:**
   $$\rho \left(\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}\right) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

3. **Energy Equation (Bernoulli + Dissipation):**
   The conversion between kinetic, potential, and pressure energy with viscous losses defines the energy available for noise generation.

## 2.2 Sources ‚Äî Detailed Logical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Turbulence-Induced Noise
- **Why it happens:** At high Reynolds number ($\text{Re} > 4000$ for pipe flow), fluid parcels exhibit chaotic, multi-scale eddies.
- **How it generates noise:** The non-linear convective term $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}$ couples eddies of different scales, producing broadband pressure fluctuations.
- **Kolmogorov ‚Äì 5/3 Power Law** describes the energy cascade across eddy sizes.

### 2.2.2 Cavitation-Induced Noise
- **Why it happens:** Local static pressure drops below the fluid's vapor pressure, creating vapor cavities.
- **How it generates noise:** When these cavities travel to higher-pressure regions, they collapse violently (in < 1 ms), releasing shock waves with pressures up to **1000 bar** locally.
- **Characteristic:** Extremely high-frequency, impulsive, broadband spectrum.

### 2.2.3 Vortex-Shedding Noise (Bluff Body Oscillation)
- **Why it happens:** Flow past non-streamlined obstacles sheds alternating low-pressure vortices.
- **How it generates noise:** The shedding frequency is governed by the **Strouhal number**, producing a tonal (narrowband) noise component.

### 2.2.4 Flashing
- **Why it happens:** A liquid approaching its saturation temperature passes through a region where pressure drops below saturation pressure.
- **How it generates noise:** Bulk vaporization produces two-phase flow with violent interfacial dynamics.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE for this module. Memorize each with its variable definitions and SI units.

| # | Equation | Symbol Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = density [kg/m¬≥], $V$ = velocity [m/s], $D$ = pipe diameter [m], $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity [Pa¬∑s] | Determines flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent) |
| 2 | $\text{St} = \dfrac{f_{v} D}{V}$ | $\text{St}$ = Strouhal number [‚Äì], $f_{v}$ = vortex shedding frequency [Hz] | Predicts tonal noise frequency |
| 3 | $\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{P_{\text{local}} - P_{v}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2}}$ | $\sigma_{c}$ = cavitation number [‚Äì], $P_{\text{local}}$ = local static pressure [Pa], $P_{v}$ = vapor pressure [Pa] | Predicts onset of cavitation |
| 4 | $f_{v} = \text{St} \cdot \dfrac{V}{D}$ | Same as above (rearranged) | Direct calculation of vortex shedding tone |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho (V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2})$ | Bernoulli pressure recovery across restriction [Pa] | Energy available for noise generation |
| 6 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}}$ | $c$ = speed of sound in fluid [m/s], $K$ = bulk modulus [Pa] | Determines noise propagation speed |
| 7 | $L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{rms}}}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | $L_{p}$ = sound pressure level [dB], $P_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Noise quantification |
| 8 | $G(f) \propto \dfrac{V^{n}}{D^{m}} \cdot f^{-p}$ | $G(f)$ = power spectral density, $n \approx 5\text{‚Äì}7$, $m \approx 1\text{‚Äì}2$, $p \approx 5/3$ (Kolmogorov) | Spectral shape of turbulence noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Strouhal Number for a circular cylinder:** $\text{St} \approx 0.21$ (in the subcritical Reynolds range $10^{3} < \text{Re} < 2 \times 10^{5}$).
> **Typical cavitation threshold:** $\sigma_{c} < 1.0$ indicates incipient cavitation; $\sigma_{c} < 0.5$ indicates severe cavitation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Differential Pressure Flowmeters (Orifice, Venturi):** Hydrodynamic noise corrupts the low-pressure tap signal, causing zero drift and measurement uncertainty.
- **Ultrasonic Flowmeters:** Flow turbulence scatters and Doppler-shifts the ultrasonic beam, reducing SNR.
- **Vortex-Shedding Flowmeters:** The meter *itself* uses vortex shedding; external hydrodynamic noise from upstream valves/pumps can mask the desired signal.
- **Control Valve Sizing:** ANSI/ISA noise prediction standards (IEC 60534-8-3) directly rely on hydrodynamic noise equations.
- **Pipeline Integrity Monitoring:** Hydrodynamic noise is correlated with erosion, pitting, and fatigue damage in pipe walls.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Vortex-Shedding Frequency from the Strouhal Number

We begin with the dimensionless form of the unsteady momentum equation applied to flow past a bluff body. Non-dimensionalizing time by $D/V$ and frequency by $V/D$ yields the Strouhal number.

### Step 1 ‚Äî Define the Non-Dimensional Shedding Frequency

For a bluff body of characteristic dimension $D$ subjected to flow velocity $V$, the characteristic time scale is:

$$\tau = \frac{D}{V}$$

The dimensionless frequency is therefore:

$$f^{*} = f \cdot \tau = \frac{f D}{V} = \text{St}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Apply Reynolds Number Independence in Subcritical Regime

In the subcritical Reynolds range ($10^{3} \leq \text{Re} \leq 2 \times 10^{5}$), experimental data shows $\text{St}$ is approximately constant:

$$\text{St} \approx 0.21 \quad \text{(for a circular cylinder)}$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Solve for the Physical Frequency

Rearranging the Strouhal definition:

$$f_{v} = \text{St} \cdot \frac{V}{D}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Substitute Numerical Values (Worked Example)

**Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m¬≥) flows past a cylindrical bluff body of diameter $D = 0.05$ m at velocity $V = 4$ m/s. Compute the vortex-shedding frequency.

**Solution:**

$$f_{v} = 0.21 \times \frac{4}{0.05}$$

$$f_{v} = 0.21 \times 80$$

$$\boxed{f_{v} = 16.8 \ \text{Hz}}$$

This 16.8 Hz tone is well within the audible range and often resonates with structural modes of piping ‚Äî a critical noise concern.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cavitation Number from Bernoulli's Equation

### Step 1 ‚Äî Start with the Steady Incompressible Bernoulli Equation

$$\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g}$$

### Step 2 ‚Äî Identify the Throat (Local Minimum Pressure) Point

At a restriction (e.g., valve throat), $V_{2} > V_{1}$, so $P_{2} < P_{1}$. The local pressure at the vena contracta is:

$$P_{\text{local}} = P_{1} - \frac{1}{2} \rho (V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2})$$

### Step 3 ‚Äî Define the Cavitation Number as the Ratio of Pressure Margin to Dynamic Head

The cavitation number measures the margin between the local pressure and the vapor pressure, normalized by the kinetic energy per unit volume:

$$\sigma_{c} = \frac{P_{\text{local}} - P_{v}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2}}$$

### Step 4 ‚Äî Interpret Physical Meaning

- $\sigma_{c} \gg 1$ ‚Üí No cavitation (high pressure margin)
- $\sigma_{c} \approx 1$ ‚Üí Incipient cavitation
- $\sigma_{c} \ll 1$ ‚Üí Fully developed cavitation, severe noise

### Step 5 ‚Äî Numerical Illustration

**Given:** Water at $T = 60¬∞\text{C}$, $P_{v} = 19.9$ kPa, $P_{1} = 400$ kPa, $V = 15$ m/s, $\rho = 983$ kg/m¬≥.

**Step (a):** Compute dynamic head:
$$\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 983 \times 15^{2} = 110{,}587 \ \text{Pa} \approx 110.6 \ \text{kPa}$$

**Step (b):** Compute local pressure (assuming $V_{1} \approx 0$):
$$P_{\text{local}} = 400 - 110.6 = 289.4 \ \text{kPa}$$

**Step (c):** Compute cavitation number:
$$\sigma_{c} = \frac{289{,}400 - 19{,}900}{110{,}587} = \frac{269{,}500}{110{,}587}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{c} \approx 2.44}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\sigma_{c} > 1$, cavitation is *not* expected at this operating point. However, reducing inlet pressure below ~130 kPa would drive $\sigma_{c}$ below 1.0 and trigger cavitation noise.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚Äî Noise Sources vs. Mitigation

| Noise Source | Diagnostic Signature | Primary Mitigation Strategy | Effectiveness |
|--------------|---------------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| Turbulence | Broadband, $f^{-5/3}$ roll-off | Flow straighteners, long upstream pipe (‚â• 10D) | High |
| Cavitation | Sharp impulses, high-freq. (kHz‚ÄìMHz) | Avoid $P_{\text{local}} < P_{v}$; anti-cavitation trim | Very High |
| Vortex Shedding | Pure tone at $f_{v} = \text{St} \cdot V/D$ | Streamline geometry, increase D, reduce V | Moderate |
| Flashing | Two-phase broadband | Reduce temperature/pressure drop; multi-stage valves | High |
| Valve Noise | Broadband + tones | Low-noise trim, multi-stage letdown | High |
| Pump-Induced | Blade-pass harmonics | Flexible couplings, pulsation dampeners | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hydrodynamic Noise Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Fluid] --> B[Pipe Inlet]
    B --> C{Pump or Compressor}
    C --> D[Flow Straightening Section]
    D --> E[Primary Sensor Element]
    E --> F[Signal Conditioning Electronics]
    F --> G[Output: Flow Rate]

    H[Hydrodynamic Noise Sources] --> I[Turbulence in Flow]
    H --> J[Cavitation at Restrictions]
    H --> K[Vortex Shedding at Fittings]
    H --> L[Flashing Across Valves]

    I --> M[Pressure Fluctuations in Fluid]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M

    M --> N[Corrupted Pressure Tap Signal]
    M --> O[Ultrasonic Beam Scattering]
    M --> P[Vortex Meter False Counts]

    N --> E
    O --> E
    P --> E

    Q[Mitigation Methods] --> R[Long Straight Pipe Runs]
    Q --> S[Flow Conditioners]
    Q --> T[Pulsation Dampeners]
    Q --> U[Anti-Cavitation Trim Valves]
    Q --> V[Pipe Stiffeners and Supports]

    R --> B
    S --> D
    T --> C
    U --> C
    V --> B

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0066CC
    style H fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style M fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585
    style Q fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow Diagram ‚Äî Noise Injection into a Flow Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FLOW[True Flow Q_true] --> SENSOR[Flow Sensor Element]
    NOISE[Hyrdodynamic Noise N_h] --> SENSOR
    ACOUSTIC[Acoustic Noise N_a] --> SENSOR
    SENSOR --> MIXED[Composite Signal S = f Q_true + N_h + N_a]
    MIXED --> PROC[Signal Processing Filter]
    PROC --> FILTERED[Cleaned Signal Estimate]
    FILTERED --> OUTPUT[Displayed Flow Rate Q_display]

    style FLOW fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style NOISE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style ACOUSTIC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style OUTPUT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In the first diagram, noise sources (orange) feed pressure fluctuations (pink) that contaminate the sensor element (blue path). Mitigation methods (green) act *before* the sensor to reduce noise injection. In the second diagram, true flow and noise sources are summed at the sensor level ‚Äî the cleaner the pre-sensor flow, the higher the SNR at the output.

## 4.3 Frequency Spectrum Map (Schematic Description)

```
Frequency (Hz) ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫
10      100     1k      10k     100k    1M
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
[  VORTEX  ]                              ‚ñ≤

                                          ‚îÇ IMPULSES

[      BROADBAND          ]               
[    0 Hz ‚Äì 2 kHz         ]               ‚îÇ
‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A ‚Äî Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024 Model Question]**

> **Q:** Define hydrodynamic noise. How is it different from acoustic noise?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hydrodynamic noise is the unwanted pressure fluctuation generated *within* a flowing fluid due to turbulence, cavitation, vortex shedding, and flashing. (1 mark)

| Aspect | Hydrodynamic Noise | Acoustic Noise |
|--------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Propagation Medium** | Fluid itself | Pipe walls / surrounding air |
| **Primary Cause** | Flow dynamics | Mechanical vibration |
| **Sensing** | Pressure transducer in flow | Microphone / accelerometer |
| **Frequency Range** | 0 Hz ‚Äì 1 MHz (broadband) | Typically 20 Hz ‚Äì 20 kHz |

(2 marks for the comparison table with at least two distinguishing features)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **Q:** State the Strouhal number and write its significance in hydrodynamic noise prediction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Strouhal number is a dimensionless quantity defined as: (1 mark)

$$\text{St} = \frac{f_{v} \cdot D}{V}$$

It represents the ratio of the characteristic time scale of the flow ($D/V$) to the period of vortex shedding. (1 mark)

**Significance:** It enables prediction of the tonal vortex-shedding frequency for design calculations ‚Äî critical for avoiding resonance with piping structural modes. For a circular cylinder, $\text{St} \approx 0.21$ in the subcritical Reynolds range. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚Äî Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[CHOICE-1]**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì June 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain in detail the various sources of hydrodynamic noise in industrial flow systems. Discuss their typical frequency spectrum. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the cavitation number from Bernoulli's theorem and explain its role in predicting cavitation onset in control valves. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Hydrodynamic noise originates from unsteady flow phenomena in process piping. The principal sources are:

**1. Turbulence (2 marks):**
- Caused by high Reynolds number flow ($\text{Re} > 4000$).
- Random velocity and pressure fluctuations from chaotic eddy motion.
- Broadband spectrum typically 0 Hz ‚Äì 2 kHz.
- Power spectral density follows Kolmogorov's $f^{-5/3}$ decay in inertial subrange.

**2. Cavitation (1.5 marks):**
- Occurs when local static pressure falls below vapor pressure ($P_{\text{local}} < P_{v}$).
- Vapor cavity formation and implosive collapse generate impulsive pressure transients.
- Frequency range: 20 kHz ‚Äì 1 MHz (ultrasonic regime).
- Creates pitting damage and intense audible "marble-in-a-tin" sound.

**3. Vortex Shedding (1.5 marks):**
- Periodic shedding of vortices from bluff bodies (valve stems, orifice plates, pipe bends).
- Tonal noise at $f_{v} = \text{St} \cdot V / D$ (narrowband).
- For $\text{St} \approx 0.21$ and typical process flows: 10 ‚Äì 500 Hz.

**4. Flashing & Valve Noise (1 mark):**
- Flashing: two-phase flow noise when sub-cooled liquid experiences pressure drop below saturation.
- Valve noise: combination of aerodynamic and hydrodynamic mechanisms in throttling.

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from Bernoulli's equation between upstream point 1 and restriction point 2:

$$\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g}$$

Multiply by $\rho g$:

$$P_{1} + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{1}^{2} = P_{2} + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{2}^{2}$$

At a throttling element, $V_{2} \gg V_{1}$. Neglecting $V_{1}$:

$$P_{2} \approx P_{1} - \frac{1}{2} \rho V_{2}^{2}$$

The **cavitation number** is defined as the dimensionless pressure margin:

$$\sigma_{c} = \frac{P_{2} - P_{v}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{2}^{2}}$$

Substituting the expression for $P_{2}$:

$$\sigma_{c} = \frac{P_{1} - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{2}^{2} - P_{v}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{2}^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{c} = \frac{P_{1} - P_{v}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{2}^{2}} - 1}$$

**Application in Control Valves (2 marks):**

- If $\sigma_{c} > 1$: No cavitation expected ‚Üí safe operating region.
- If $0 < \sigma_{c} < 1$: Incipient cavitation ‚Üí noise onset.
- If $\sigma_{c} \leq 0$: Fully developed cavitation ‚Üí severe noise, vibration, and erosion.

Engineers use $\sigma_{c}$ in **valve sizing** (ISA-75.01.01) to ensure that the valve is selected such that the operating $\sigma_{c}$ remains above the incipient-cavitation threshold for the chosen trim type.

### Question B (14 Marks) **[CHOICE-2]**

**[KTU University Exam ‚Äì Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of vortex shedding behind a bluff body. Show that the shedding frequency is governed by the Strouhal number and calculate it for a pipe diameter of 0.1 m, fluid velocity 5 m/s, and $\text{St} = 0.21$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the various methods to mitigate hydrodynamic noise in industrial flow measurement systems, with emphasis on differential pressure and ultrasonic flowmeters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (a) [7 marks]

**Vortex Shedding Explanation (3 marks):**
When fluid flows past a bluff (non-streamlined) body, boundary layer separation occurs asymmetrically, leading to alternating low-pressure vortices shed from each side. This is called the **von K√°rm√°n vortex street**.

The phenomenon is characterized by:

- A periodic shedding frequency $f_{v}$
- A characteristic length scale $D$ (body diameter)
- A characteristic velocity scale $V$

These three parameters combine into the dimensionless Strouhal number:

$$\text{St} = \frac{f_{v} D}{V}$$

**Derivation of Frequency (2 marks):**

Rearranging:

$$f_{v} = \frac{\text{St} \cdot V}{D}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $D = 0.1$ m, $V = 5$ m/s, $\text{St} = 0.21$:

$$f_{v} = \frac{0.21 \times 5}{0.1} = \frac{1.05}{0.1}$$

$$\boxed{f_{v} = 10.5 \ \text{Hz}}$$

This low-frequency tone can excite pipe structural resonances if not addressed.

---

#### Model Solution ‚Äî Part (b) [7 marks]

**Mitigation Strategy 1 ‚Äî Straight Pipe Runs (2 marks):**
- Install ‚â• 10 diameters of straight pipe upstream and ‚â• 5 diameters downstream of the flow meter.
- This allows turbulence to decay and the velocity profile to re-develop.

**Mitigation Strategy 2 ‚Äî Flow Conditioners (1.5 marks):**
- Devices such as **Zanker plates**, **tube bundles**, and **Honeycomb straighteners** eliminate swirl and dampen turbulence.
- Placed upstream of the sensor element.

**Mitigation Strategy 3 ‚Äî Pulsation Dampeners (1 mark):**
- Used near pumps and compressors.
- Absorb pressure pulsations that would otherwise propagate as hydrodynamic noise.

**Mitigation Strategy 4 ‚Äî Anti-Cavitation Valve Trim (1.5 marks):**
- Multi-stage pressure letdown valves avoid $P_{\text{local}} < P_{v}$.
- Specialty trims (e.g., labyrinth, multi-hole cages) reduce cavitation noise by 10‚Äì20 dB.

**Mitigation Strategy 5 ‚Äî Ultrasonic Flowmeter-Specific Measures (1 mark):**
- Use **wetted transducers** to maximize signal coupling.
- Apply **digital signal processing (DSP)** with adaptive filtering.
- Operate at higher ultrasonic frequencies to avoid overlap with turbulence PSD.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing hydrodynamic and acoustic noise** ‚Äî Examiners will deduct up to 2 marks if the student writes "hydrodynamic noise travels through pipes" without clarifying that it travels *inside* the fluid, not through pipe walls.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in Strouhal calculations** ‚Äî Always carry units through to show dimensional consistency. Writing $f_{v} = 10.5$ without units loses 0.5 mark.
>
> 3. **Stating "St = 0.21" as a universal constant** ‚Äî It is valid *only* in the subcritical Reynolds range ($10^{3} \leq \text{Re} \leq 2 \times 10^{5}$). Mentioning the range earns bonus marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping the vapor pressure term** ‚Äî In cavitation-number derivations, students often write $P_{\text{local}}$ instead of $(P_{\text{local}} - P_{v})$. This is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **No diagrams in descriptive answers** ‚Äî KTU 2024 scheme mandates "neat sketches" ‚Äî a missing labeled figure can cost 1‚Äì2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ‚Äî Hydrodynamic Noise**

- ‚úÖ Hydrodynamic noise is **fluid-borne**, not structure-borne; it propagates at the speed of sound in the fluid medium.
- ‚úÖ Three principal sources: **turbulence, cavitation, and vortex shedding** ‚Äî plus flashing and valve noise as secondary sources.
- ‚úÖ Turbulence noise is **broadband** with $f^{-5/3}$ PSD roll-off (Kolmogorov).
- ‚úÖ Cavitation noise is **impulsive**, broadband, in the ultrasonic range (kHz‚ÄìMHz), and is destructive to piping.
- ‚úÖ Vortex shedding noise is **tonal**, governed by $\text{St} \approx 0.21$ for cylinders.
- ‚úÖ Strouhal number: $\text{St} = f_{v} D / V$ ‚Üí rearranged to $f_{v} = \text{St} V / D$.
- ‚úÖ Cavitation number: $\sigma_{c} = (P_{\text{local}} - P_{v}) / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2})$; cavitation begins when $\sigma_{c} \lesssim 1$.
- ‚úÖ Reynolds number $\text{Re} = \rho V D / \mu$ determines whether flow is laminar or turbulent ‚Äî turbulence begins at $\text{Re} \approx 2300$ in pipes.
- ‚úÖ Mitigation methods: straight pipe runs (‚â• 10D upstream), flow conditioners, pulsation dampeners, anti-cavitation trim, DSP filtering.
- ‚úÖ Effects on instruments: zero drift in DP meters, beam scattering in ultrasonic meters, false counts in vortex meters.
- ‚úÖ Standards to know: **IEC 60534-8-3** (control valve noise prediction) and **ISA-75.01.01** (valve sizing).
- ‚úÖ Always show units in numerical answers; mention validity range for $\text{St} \approx 0.21$.
- ‚úÖ Common KTU trick question: "Why is hydrodynamic noise a concern for ultrasonic flowmeters?" ‚Äî Answer: because turbulence scatters the ultrasonic beam, reducing SNR and introducing measurement uncertainty.
